These two addresses are *always* reserved inside every network of every class, irrespective of network size. --- ### (b) Worked Example on `192.168.100.45` [7 marks] **(i) Class Identification** *(1.5 marks)*: $$ \begin{aligned} W &= 192 \\ W_{\text{binary}} &= 11000000 \\ \text{Leading bits} &= \mathbf{110} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Class C} \\ \text{Range check: } 192 \le 192 \le 223 &\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Default Subnet Mask** *(1 mark)*: For Class C, network bits = 24, host bits = 8. $$ \text{Default Mask} = 255.255.255.0 \;\; (\text{or /24}) $$ Binary form: `11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000`. **(iii) Network ID and Host ID** *(1.5 marks)*: $$ \begin{aligned} \;\;\;\;192.\;168.\;100.\;\;45 \\ \text{AND} \;\;255.\;255.\;255.\;\;0 \\ \hline =\;\;\;\;192.\;168.\;100.\;\;\;0 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Network ID} = 192.168.100.0, \quad \text{Host ID} = 45} $$ **(iv) Network & Broadcast Addresses** *(3 marks)*: * **Network address** is obtained by setting *all* host bits to **0**: $$ \text{Network Address} = 192.168.100.\,\mathbf{0} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{192.168.100.0} $$ * **Broadcast address** is obtained by setting *all* host bits to **1** (host portion = `11111111` = 255): $$ \text{Broadcast Address} = 192.168.100.\,\mathbf{255} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{192.168.100.255} $$ **Validation:** Our given address `192.168.100.45` lies strictly between `192.168.100.0` and `192.168.100.255` (i.e., $0 < 45 < 255$), confirming it is a **valid host address** in the Class C network `192.168.100.0/24`. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top reasons students lose marks in Classful Addressing questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $-2$ adjustment.** Many students write $2^{24}$ instead of $2^{24} - 2$ for Class A hosts. *Always* subtract the network address and broadcast address. > 2. **Confusing "Number of Networks" with "Number of Hosts."** Read the question carefully. They have *opposite* power-of-2 exponents. > 3. **Treating Class D and E as unicast classes.** They have **no NetID/HostID split**. Writing $2^{28}$ "networks" for Class D is a common blunder. > 4. **Forgetting the special status of `127.0.0.0/8`.** It is Class A by leading bit, but it is *reserved* — cannot be assigned to a physical network. > 5. **Writing the loopback address as `127.0.0.0` (network) and not mentioning `127.0.0.1` (canonical host).** KTU expects awareness of the practical conventions. > 6. **Mixing up class ranges.** `0` and `127` are *not* valid first octets for assignable Class A networks. The valid range is **1–126**, not 0–127. > 7. **In Part B answers, students often skip the "AND operation" table.** Drawing the AND step *visually* earns full method marks even if a later arithmetic slip occurs. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Classful Addressing** > > ✅ IPv4 = 32 bits, written as four decimal octets (0–255 each). > > ✅ Five classes: A, B, C (unicast), D (multicast), E (experimental). > > ✅ Class is determined by the **leading bits of the first octet**: > * `0` → A, `10` → B, `110` → C, `1110` → D, `1111` → E. > > ✅ First-octet decimal ranges: A = **1–126**, B = **128–191**, C = **192–223**, D = **224–239**, E = **240–255**. > > ✅ Default subnet masks: A = **/8**, B = **/16**, C = **/24**. > > ✅ Network bits / Host bits: A = **8/24**, B = **16/16**, C = **24/8**. > > ✅ Usable hosts per network: $2^{h} - 2$ (subtract network and broadcast addresses). > > ✅ Class A: $16\,777\,214$ hosts; Class B: $65\,534$ hosts; Class C: $254$ hosts. > > ✅ Class A: **126** usable networks; Class B: **16 384**; Class C: **2 097 152**. > > ✅ Special addresses: `0.0.0.0` (this network), `127.0.0.0/8` (loopback), `255.255.255.255` (limited broadcast). > > ✅ Class D → Multicast groups; Class E → Reserved/experimental (no NetID/HostID). > > ✅ Network ID = Address **AND** Default Mask; Host ID = portion *after* the network ID. > > ✅ Network address = host bits all 0; Broadcast address = host bits all 1. > > ✅ Classful addressing was the original Internet scheme (1981–1993); replaced by **CIDR** in 1993 because of inefficient address allocation (Class A and B were grossly under-utilized while Class C was over-demanded).

topics.title: Classless Addressing (Simple Problems on Subnetting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classless Addressing (Simple Problems on Subnetting)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classless Addressing (Classless Inter-Domain Routing - CIDR):** A modern IP addressing architecture that **replaces rigid class boundaries** (Class A, B, C) with a **variable-length prefix** (written as a slash `/n` after the address). The prefix `/n` indicates that the **first `n` bits** of the 32-bit IPv4 address represent the **network ID**, while the remaining `(32 - n)` bits are used for **subnet/host identification**.

In KTU terminology, **Subnetting** is the process of **borrowing bits from the host portion** of a classful or classless address and reassigning them to the network portion, thereby creating multiple smaller logical networks (subnets) within one larger network.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Building Model"

Imagine you own **one large apartment building** with a single postal address (your *Network Address*). Inside, there are **256 rooms** (your *Hosts*). The building management decides to divide it into **4 wings** (4 *Subnets*), each with 64 rooms.

- The **building's outer address** stays the same → **Network address remains unchanged** at the outside.
- A new internal **wing number** is added to every room's address → an extra **subnet bits** are appended.
- The number of rooms per wing decreases → **hosts per subnet reduces**.

This is exactly what subnetting does to an IP address: it **carves up a big network into smaller, manageable sub-networks** without changing the externally visible network identity.

### 1.3 Why Move From Classful to Classless?

> [!NOTE]
> **The Problem with Classful Addressing:**
> - Class C gave only **254 usable hosts** — too few for most organizations.
> - Class B gave **65,534 hosts** — wasteful for organizations needing ~1,000 hosts.
> - Result: **rapid exhaustion of IPv4 address space** and inefficient routing tables.

**CIDR solves this by:**
- Allowing the network boundary to fall **anywhere** between bit 0 and bit 32.
- Supporting **route aggregation (supernetting)** to shrink global routing tables.
- Forming the foundation of modern **Variable Length Subnet Masking (VLSM)**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Value / Notation |
| :--- | :--- |
| Total IPv4 address bits | **32 bits** |
| Dotted-Decimal group size | **8 bits (1 octet)** |
| Default Octet values | **0 – 255** |
| CIDR Notation | **A.B.C.D / n** where `n` = network prefix length |
| Reserved (Network & Broadcast) | **2 addresses per subnet** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Subnetting — Step-by-Step Logic

Subnetting is fundamentally a **bit manipulation exercise**. Here is the structured operational logic every KTU examiner expects:

1. **Identify the IP address and prefix length** `/n`.
2. **Compute the Subnet Mask** — a 32-bit number whose first `n` bits are `1` and remaining `(32-n)` bits are `0`.
3. **Determine bits borrowed for subnetting** — `s = new_prefix - old_prefix` (for subnetting a given network) or simply `s = 32 - n - h` where `h` is host bits.
4. **Compute Number of Subnets** using the borrowed bits.
5. **Compute Number of Hosts per Subnet** using the remaining host bits.
6. **Apply a Boolean AND** between the IP and the Subnet Mask → yields the **Network (Subnet) Address**.
7. **Set all host bits to 1** → yields the **Direct Broadcast Address**.
8. **First usable host** = Network Address + 1.
9. **Last usable host** = Broadcast Address − 1.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why AND operation?** Because the subnet mask's `1` bits *preserve* the network portion while `0` bits *zero out* the host portion, isolating the network ID.
- **Why subtract 2 from hosts?** Because the all-zeros host portion denotes the *subnet itself*, and all-ones denotes the *broadcast* — neither is assignable to a device.
- **Why power of 2?** Because each borrowed bit doubles the addressing space: 1 bit → 2 subnets, 2 bits → 4 subnets, etc.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least one formula from here appears in every KTU exam.**

| Parameter | Formula | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Host bits | $h = 32 - n$ | Bits left after the prefix |
| Number of usable hosts per subnet | $H = 2^{h} - 2$ | Subtract network & broadcast |
| Number of subnets created | $S = 2^{s}$ | Where `s` = bits borrowed |
| Block size (address increment) | $B = 2^{h}$ | Difference between consecutive subnets |
| First usable host | $\text{Network Address} + 1$ | Smallest assignable host |
| Last usable host | $\text{Broadcast Address} - 1$ | Largest assignable host |
| Broadcast address | Network address + $(B - 1)$ | All host bits set to 1 |
| Subnet Mask value | $2^{32-n}$ in binary | First `n` bits = 1, rest = 0 |

### 2.4 Quick Reference — Decimal Values of Common Masks

| Prefix | Subnet Mask (Dotted Decimal) | Total Host Bits | Usable Hosts |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `/8` | 255.0.0.0 | 24 | 16,777,214 |
| `/16` | 255.255.0.0 | 16 | 65,534 |
| `/24` | 255.255.255.0 | 8 | 254 |
| `/25` | 255.255.255.128 | 7 | 126 |
| `/26` | 255.255.255.192 | 6 | 62 |
| `/27` | 255.255.255.224 | 5 | 30 |
| `/28` | 255.255.255.240 | 4 | 14 |
| `/29` | 255.255.255.248 | 3 | 6 |
| `/30` | 255.255.255.252 | 2 | 2 |
| `/31` | 255.255.255.254 | 1 | 0 (point-to-point) |
| `/32` | 255.255.255.255 | 0 | 1 (single host) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Subnetting is the **backbone of enterprise network design**. Every office LAN, data center fabric, and ISP backbone uses subnetting to:
- **Contain broadcast storms** — broadcasts stay inside the subnet.
- **Enforce security policy** — ACLs (Access Control Lists) are typically applied at subnet boundaries.
- **Enable hierarchical routing** — summarization reduces routing table size.
- **Support multi-tenant architectures** — VLSM gives each tenant exactly the address space they need.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following **four worked problems** cover every variant KTU has asked in the last 5 years — from basic subnet identification to designing equal subnets.

---

### **Worked Problem 1 — Single Subnet Identification**

**Problem:** Given the host IP `198.45.16.7 / 28`, find:
- (i) Subnet Mask
- (ii) Network (Subnet) Address
- (iii) First Usable Host, Last Usable Host
- (iv) Direct Broadcast Address
- (v) Number of usable hosts in the subnet

**Step 1 — Identify host bits and block size**

$$n = 28 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = 32 - 28 = 4 \text{ host bits}$$

$$B = 2^{h} = 2^{4} = 16 \text{ (block size)}$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Subnet Mask**

First 28 bits are `1`, last 4 bits are `0`.

$$\text{Subnet Mask} = \underbrace{11111111.11111111.11111111}_{24 \text{ ones}}.\underbrace{1111}_{4 \text{ ones}}0000$$

Convert each octet:
- 11111111 → 255
- 11110000 → 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 = **240**

$$\boxed{\text{Subnet Mask} = 255.255.255.240}$$

**Step 3 — Apply AND operation to get Network Address**

Convert last octet of IP `7` to binary (only last 4 bits matter because mask `240` keeps the first 4 bits intact in the 4th octet — actually mask `240` means first 4 bits of 4th octet preserved).

$$7 = 00000111 \quad \text{AND} \quad 240 = 11110000$$

$$7 \;\text{AND}\; 240 = 00000000 = 0$$

$$\boxed{\text{Network Address} = 198.45.16.0}$$

**Step 4 — Find the Broadcast Address**

Set the last 4 host bits to `1`:

$$00000000 + 16 - 1 = 00001111 = 15$$

$$\boxed{\text{Broadcast Address} = 198.45.16.15}$$

**Step 5 — First and Last Usable Hosts**

$$\text{First Host} = 198.45.16.0 + 1 = 198.45.16.1$$

$$\text{Last Host} = 198.45.16.15 - 1 = 198.45.16.14$$

**Step 6 — Usable Host Count**

$$H = 2^{h} - 2 = 2^{4} - 2 = 16 - 2 = \boxed{14 \text{ usable hosts}}$$

---

### **Worked Problem 2 — Creating Multiple Equal Subnets**

**Problem:** An organization is assigned the block `200.10.20.0 / 24`. The network administrator needs to create **4 equal subnets**. Find the new prefix, subnet mask, and address range of each subnet.

**Step 1 — Bits needed for 4 subnets**

We need $S = 4 = 2^{s}$ subnets.

$$2^{s} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = 2 \text{ bits borrowed}$$

**Step 2 — New Prefix Length**

$$\text{New Prefix} = 24 + 2 = \boxed{/26}$$

**Step 3 — New Subnet Mask**

$$\text{Mask} = 255.255.255.\underbrace{11000000}_{192}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Subnet Mask} = 255.255.255.192}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Block Size**

$$h = 32 - 26 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 2^{6} = 64$$

**Step 5 — Derive All Four Subnets**

| Subnet | Network Address | First Host | Last Host | Broadcast |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Subnet 1 | 200.10.20.0 | 200.10.20.1 | 200.10.20.62 | 200.10.20.63 |
| Subnet 2 | 200.10.20.64 | 200.10.20.65 | 200.10.20.126 | 200.10.20.127 |
| Subnet 3 | 200.10.20.128 | 200.10.20.129 | 200.10.20.190 | 200.10.20.191 |
| Subnet 4 | 200.10.20.192 | 200.10.20.193 | 200.10.20.254 | 200.10.20.255 |

**Verification using AND on Subnet 4 boundary (third octet untouched, fourth octet `128 = 10000000`):**

$$128 \;\text{AND}\; 192 = 10000000 \;\text{AND}\; 11000000 = 10000000 = 128 \;\checkmark$$

---

### **Worked Problem 3 — VLSM-Style Requirement (KTU Favourite)**

**Problem:** Given the network `172.20.0.0 / 16`, design subnets for the following departments:
- **Dept A:** needs 60 hosts
- **Dept B:** needs 30 hosts
- **Dept C:** needs 12 hosts
- **Dept D:** needs 2 hosts (point-to-point link)

Allocate the **largest first** (greedy VLSM method).

**Step 1 — Sort by host requirement (descending)**

| Department | Required Hosts | Nearest $2^{h} - 2$ | Host Bits `h` | New Prefix |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dept A | 60 | $2^{6} - 2 = 62$ | 6 | `/26` |
| Dept B | 30 | $2^{5} - 2 = 30$ | 5 | `/27` |
| Dept C | 12 | $2^{4} - 2 = 14$ | 4 | `/28` |
| Dept D | 2 | $2^{2} - 2 = 2$ | 2 | `/30` |

**Step 2 — Allocate sequentially from `172.20.0.0`**

**Dept A (largest):** Prefix `/26`, block size $2^{6} = 64$.

$$\text{Range: } 172.20.0.0 \;-\; 172.20.0.63$$

Network: `172.20.0.0 / 26`, Broadcast: `172.20.0.63`.

**Dept B:** Prefix `/27`, block size $2^{5} = 32$. Starts at `172.20.0.64`.

$$\text{Range: } 172.20.0.64 \;-\; 172.20.0.95$$

Network: `172.20.0.64 / 27`, Broadcast: `172.20.0.95`.

**Dept C:** Prefix `/28`, block size $2^{4} = 16$. Starts at `172.20.0.96`.

$$\text{Range: } 172.20.0.96 \;-\; 172.20.0.111$$

Network: `172.20.0.96 / 28`, Broadcast: `172.20.0.111`.

**Dept D:** Prefix `/30`, block size $2^{2} = 4$. Starts at `172.20.0.112`.

$$\text{Range: } 172.20.0.112 \;-\; 172.20.0.115$$

Network: `172.20.0.112 / 30`, Broadcast: `172.20.0.115`.

**Step 3 — Validation**

$$64 + 32 + 16 + 4 = 116 \text{ addresses used, which is far less than } 2^{16} = 65536 \;\checkmark$$

This proves **VLSM efficiency** — classful equal subnetting would have wasted vast address space.

---

### **Worked Problem 4 — Finding Subnet from Host IP**

**Problem:** A host has IP `10.45.78.123 / 20`. Determine the subnet it belongs to.

**Step 1 — Compute Subnet Mask**

First 20 bits = 1: `11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000`.

$$\text{Third octet: } 11110000 = 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 = 240$$

$$\text{Mask} = 255.255.240.0$$

**Step 2 — Apply AND**

Third octet: $78 \;\text{AND}\; 240$.

$$78 = 01001110, \quad 240 = 11110000$$

$$78 \;\text{AND}\; 240 = 01000000 = 64$$

**Step 3 — Compute Block Size**

$$h = 32 - 20 = 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 2^{12} = 4096$$

**Step 4 — Network & Broadcast**

$$\text{Network} = 10.45.64.0 \quad (\text{because } 64 \text{ is a multiple of } 16 \text{ and } 4096/256 = 16)$$

$$\text{Broadcast} = 10.45.64.0 + (4096 - 1) \text{ in host portion} = 10.45.79.255$$

**Step 5 — Host Range**

$$\text{Usable Range: } 10.45.64.1 \;-\; 10.45.79.254$$

$$\boxed{\text{Host } 10.45.78.123 \text{ belongs to subnet } 10.45.64.0 / 20}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Subnetting Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given IP A.B.C.D / n] --> B[Compute host bits h = 32 - n]
    B --> C[Compute block size B = 2 to power h]
    C --> D[Construct Subnet Mask: first n bits = 1, rest = 0]
    D --> E[Apply Bitwise AND between IP and Mask]
    E --> F[Result equals Network Address]
    F --> G[Set all h host bits to 1]
    G --> H[Result equals Broadcast Address]
    H --> I[First Host equals Network plus 1]
    I --> J[Last Host equals Broadcast minus 1]
    J --> K[Usable Hosts equals 2 to power h minus 2]
    K --> L[End]

    styleA["A"]:::startNode
    styleL["L"]:::endNode

    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Bit Allocation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Network Bits n]:::nb --> S1[Subnet Bits s]:::sb --> H1[Host Bits h]:::hb

    N2[Original Network ID]:::nb --> S2[Subnets created by borrowing]:::sb --> H2[Hosts inside each subnet]:::hb

    N3[Fixed for entire block]:::nb --> S3[Variable per subnet in VLSM]:::sb --> H3[Variable per subnet in VLSM]:::hb

    classDef nb fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef sb fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef hb fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Classless Subnet Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Original Block 172.20.0.0 / 16]:::root --> A[Dept A / 26 size 64]:::subA
    ROOT --> B[Dept B / 27 size 32]:::subB
    ROOT --> C[Dept C / 28 size 16]:::subC
    ROOT --> D[Dept D / 30 size 4]:::subD
    ROOT --> E[Unused Address Space]:::unused

    A --> A1[172.20.0.0 to 172.20.0.63]:::detail
    B --> B1[172.20.0.64 to 172.20.0.95]:::detail
    C --> C1[172.20.0.96 to 172.20.0.111]:::detail
    D --> D1[172.20.0.112 to 172.20.0.115]:::detail

    classDef root fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef subA fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef subB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef subC fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef subD fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef unused fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Boolean AND Operation — Network Address Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IP[IP Address in Binary 32 bits]:::ip --> ANDOP[Bitwise AND Operation]:::op
    MASK[Subnet Mask in Binary 32 bits]:::mask --> ANDOP
    ANDOP --> RESULT[Network Address in Binary]:::res
    RESULT --> CONV[Convert to Dotted Decimal]:::conv
    CONV --> OUT[Final Network Address A.B.C.D]:::out

    classDef ip fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef mask fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef res fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"What is Classless Addressing? How does it differ from Classful Addressing?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classless Addressing (CIDR)** uses a **variable-length prefix** `/n` to indicate the network portion of an IP address, replacing the rigid 8/16/24-bit boundaries of classful addressing. This allows **efficient allocation** of IP addresses based on actual organizational need, **reduces address wastage**, and enables **route aggregation** to shrink global routing tables.

| Aspect | Classful | Classless (CIDR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Network boundary | Fixed at 8/16/24 bits | Anywhere from 0 to 32 |
| Notation | Implicit (A/B/C class) | Explicit `/n` prefix |
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Supports VLSM | No | Yes |

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — key feature; 1 mark — comparison table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"An organization is assigned the block `200.45.32.0 / 24`. If the prefix length is changed to `/26`, how many subnets are created and how many usable hosts does each subnet have?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Bits borrowed for subnetting:

$$s = 26 - 24 = 2 \text{ bits}$$

Number of subnets:

$$S = 2^{s} = 2^{2} = \mathbf{4 \text{ subnets}}$$

Host bits remaining:

$$h = 32 - 26 = 6 \text{ bits}$$

Usable hosts per subnet:

$$H = 2^{h} - 2 = 64 - 2 = \mathbf{62 \text{ hosts}}$$

*(1 mark — borrowed bits; 1 mark — subnets; 1 mark — usable hosts formula application.)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Given the IP address `192.168.100.0 / 24`, the network administrator needs to create **8 equal subnets**. For the **third subnet**, determine:
- (i) New prefix length and subnet mask. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Network address, first host, last host, and broadcast address. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** A company has been allocated the network `10.0.0.0 / 8`. Design a VLSM scheme for the following departments: Marketing (500 hosts), R&D (120 hosts), Admin (25 hosts), and WAN link (2 hosts). Provide the subnet address, prefix length, and usable host range for each. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — 8 Equal Subnets from /24

**Step 1 — Compute borrowed bits**

$$2^{s} = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = 3 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — New prefix**

$$n_{\text{new}} = 24 + 3 = \boxed{/27}$$

**Step 3 — Subnet mask**

Last octet = `11100000` = 128 + 64 + 32 = **224**.

$$\boxed{\text{Subnet Mask} = 255.255.255.224}$$

**Step 4 — Block size**

$$h = 32 - 27 = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 2^{5} = 32$$

**Step 5 — Third Subnet (index 2, starting from 0)**

$$\text{Network} = 192.168.100.0 + (2 \times 32) = 192.168.100.64$$

$$\text{First Host} = 192.168.100.65$$

$$\text{Broadcast} = 192.168.100.64 + 31 = 192.168.100.95$$

$$\text{Last Host} = 192.168.100.94$$

$$\boxed{\text{3rd Subnet: } 192.168.100.64 / 27, \text{ Hosts } 65 - 94, \text{ Broadcast } 95}$$

---

#### Part (b) — VLSM Design from 10.0.0.0 / 8

**Step 1 — Sort by host requirement**

| Dept | Hosts Needed | $h$ | Prefix | Block Size |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Marketing | 500 | 9 | `/23` | 512 |
| R&D | 120 | 7 | `/25` | 128 |
| Admin | 25 | 5 | `/27` | 32 |
| WAN link | 2 | 2 | `/30` | 4 |

**Step 2 — Allocate sequentially**

- **Marketing:** `10.0.0.0 / 23` → Range `10.0.0.0 – 10.0.1.255` (510 usable).
- **R&D:** `10.0.2.0 / 25` → Range `10.0.2.0 – 10.0.2.127` (126 usable).
- **Admin:** `10.0.2.128 / 27` → Range `10.0.2.128 – 10.0.2.159` (30 usable).
- **WAN:** `10.0.2.160 / 30` → Range `10.0.2.160 – 10.0.2.163` (2 usable).

**Step 3 — Verification**

$$512 + 128 + 32 + 4 = 676 \text{ addresses used; remaining: } 2^{24} - 676 \text{ for future growth} \;\checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Subnetting** with a neat diagram. Given `172.16.0.0 / 16`, divide into **4 equal subnets** and list the network address, broadcast address, and host range for each subnet. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A host with IP address `10.45.78.123 / 20` is connected to your network. Find:
- (i) Subnet mask and the network it belongs to. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) First usable host, last usable host, and broadcast address of that subnet. **(4 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Explanation + 4 Subnets

**Concept (3 marks):** Subnetting is the practice of dividing a large network into smaller logical sub-networks by borrowing bits from the host portion. It improves **security**, **performance** (reduces broadcast domain), and **address utilization**.

**Numerical (4 marks):**

Bits borrowed:

$$s = \log_{2} 4 = 2 \text{ bits}$$

New prefix:

$$n_{\text{new}} = 16 + 2 = /18$$

Block size:

$$h = 32 - 18 = 14 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 2^{14} = 16384$$

Third octet increment = $16384 / 256 = 64$.

| Subnet | Network | First Host | Last Host | Broadcast |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 172.16.0.0 | 172.16.0.1 | 172.16.63.254 | 172.16.63.255 |
| 2 | 172.16.64.0 | 172.16.64.1 | 172.16.127.254 | 172.16.127.255 |
| 3 | 172.16.128.0 | 172.16.128.1 | 172.16.191.254 | 172.16.191.255 |
| 4 | 172.16.192.0 | 172.16.192.1 | 172.16.255.254 | 172.16.255.255 |

---

#### Part (b) — Find Subnet of Given Host

**Step 1 — Subnet Mask**

First 20 bits = 1 → third octet = `11110000` = **240**.

$$\text{Mask} = 255.255.240.0$$

**Step 2 — Network Address**

Third octet: $78 \;\text{AND}\; 240$.

$$78 = 01001110, \quad 240 = 11110000$$

$$78 \;\text{AND}\; 240 = 01000000 = 64$$

$$\boxed{\text{Network} = 10.45.64.0 / 20}$$

**Step 3 — Block & Broadcast**

$$h = 32 - 20 = 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = 4096$$

$$\text{Broadcast} = 10.45.64.0 + 4095 \text{ in host portion} = 10.45.79.255$$

**Step 4 — Usable Hosts**

$$\text{First Host} = 10.45.64.1$$

$$\text{Last Host} = 10.45.79.254$$

$$\boxed{\text{Usable Range: } 10.45.64.1 \;-\; 10.45.79.254}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 2** from $2^{h}$ when computing usable hosts — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using the wrong octet** for AND operation — only the octet(s) where the mask has a non-trivial bit pattern matter. For `/20`, focus on the 3rd octet.
> 3. **Confusing block size with host count** — block size $B$ counts ALL addresses including network & broadcast; usable hosts = $B - 2$.
> 4. **Allocating VLSM in the wrong order** — always allocate the **largest requirement first** (greedy method); doing it smallest-first wastes space.
> 5. **Writing `172.16.0.0 / 26` instead of the correct subnet mask `255.255.255.192`** — both are valid notations but KTU specifically asks for the dotted-decimal mask in subnet mask questions.
> 6. **Not showing AND working** — KTU examiners award a **step-mark** only when the AND operation is explicitly written out.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — must memorize before the exam:**

- [x] **CIDR notation** `/n` means the **first `n` bits** are network bits.
- [x] **Subnet mask formula:** first `n` bits = 1, remaining = 0; convert to dotted decimal.
- [x] **Host bits** $h = 32 - n$.
- [x] **Usable hosts** $H = 2^{h} - 2$ (subtract network & broadcast).
- [x] **Subnets** $S = 2^{s}$ where `s` = borrowed bits.
- [x] **Block size** $B = 2^{h}$ (addresses per subnet, including reserved).
- [x] **Network address** = IP **AND** Mask (bitwise).
- [x] **Broadcast address** = Network address + $(B - 1)$.
- [x] **First usable host** = Network + 1; **Last usable host** = Broadcast − 1.
- [x] **VLSM rule:** Always allocate the **largest subnet first** to avoid fragmentation.
- [x] **/31 and /32** are special: `/31` for point-to-point (RFC 3021), `/32` = single host route.
- [x] **Class A** default mask = /8, **Class B** = /16, **Class C** = /24 — but **CIDR ignores class**.
- [x] **Subnet zero** (subnet 0) and **all-ones subnet** are **valid in CIDR** (RFC 1878) — older textbooks may forbid them; KTU 2024 follows modern RFC.
- [x] **Number of bits to borrow** to get $N$ subnets = $\lceil \log_{2} N \rceil$.
- [x] **Supernetting** is the reverse — combining multiple Class C blocks into one larger block with a shorter prefix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the TCP/IP protocol suite (specifically the **Internet Protocol version 4 – IPv4** and **Internet Protocol version 6 – IPv6**), certain IP addresses are reserved by the **Internet Assigned Numbers Authority (IANA)** and the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** for purposes other than assigning them to individual end hosts or interfaces. These reserved addresses are collectively termed **Special Addresses** (sometimes referred to as *Reserved Addresses*, *Non-host Addresses*, or *Special-Purpose Addresses*).

According to **IETF RFC 5735 (IPv4)** and **RFC 4291 / RFC 6890 (IPv6)**, special addresses serve critical architectural functions such as identifying the network itself, enabling limited/local broadcast, supporting the loopback testing mechanism, providing private addressing scopes (NAT), and facilitating multicast group communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT522 – Module 1):**
> Under KTU 2024 Scheme, students are expected to recognize the binary structure, decimal representation, scope, and routing behavior of every special address. Direct 3-mark and 14-mark questions are frequently framed on this topic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine an apartment building with 1,000 flats. The building has its own postal address (**e.g., "Sunshine Apartments"**), but no individual flat owns it. Now think about a few special situations:

- **"Sunshine Apartments, c/o Postmaster"** – This is a way to send a message to the *whole building* (the **Network/Directed Broadcast Address**).
- **"Building Reception – please announce to all"** – This is a **Limited Broadcast** that never leaves the building.
- **"Building Manager's Office"** – A self-referential internal office used to test the intercom (**Loopback Address**).
- **"Vacant / Not Assigned Yet"** – A placeholder slot reserved for the occupant who hasn't moved in (**0.0.0.0 – This Host on This Network**).
- **"Private Suite (not listed publicly)"** – Apartments reserved for residents only and invisible to the outside post office (**Private IP Addresses**).

Each of these "postal cases" corresponds to a **Special IP Address** in the TCP/IP world.

### 1.3 Classification Overview (IPv4 + IPv6)

| Family | Category | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| IPv4 | Network Address | 192.168.1.0 |
| IPv4 | Directed Broadcast | 192.168.1.255 |
| IPv4 | Limited Broadcast | 255.255.255.255 |
| IPv4 | This Network | 0.0.0.0 |
| IPv4 | Loopback | 127.0.0.1 |
| IPv4 | Private (RFC 1918) | 10.x.x.x, 172.16–31.x.x, 192.168.x.x |
| IPv4 | Link-Local (APIPA) | 169.254.x.x |
| IPv4 | Multicast | 224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255 |
| IPv4 | Reserved/Experimental | 240.0.0.0 – 255.255.255.254 |
| IPv6 | Unspecified | :: |
| IPv6 | Loopback | ::1 |
| IPv6 | Link-Local | fe80::/10 |
| IPv6 | Unique Local (ULA) | fc00::/7 |
| IPv6 | Multicast | ff00::/8 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Exam Stat:** Every special IPv4 address reduces the total usable host pool. For a Class B network, **2 special addresses** (network + directed broadcast) are always non-assignable, leaving $2^{16} - 2 = 65{,}534$ usable hosts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 IPv4 Special Addresses – Detailed Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Network Address (All-Zero Host Bits)

- **Definition:** The lowest address in any subnet where **all host-id bits are set to 0**.
- **Function:** Identifies the *network itself*, not a host.
- **Binary pattern:** `<Network-bits><Host-bits = 0>`
- **Example:** For network 192.168.10.0/26, the network address is **192.168.10.0**.

#### 2.1.2 Directed Broadcast Address (All-One Host Bits)

- **Definition:** The highest address in any subnet where **all host-id bits are set to 1**.
- **Function:** A packet sent here is delivered to **every host** within that specific subnet (it is *routed* to the target network first, then broadcast).
- **Example:** For 192.168.10.0/26, the directed broadcast is **192.168.10.63**.

#### 2.1.3 Limited Broadcast Address

- **Value:** **255.255.255.255**
- **Function:** Restricted to the **local physical network** (never routed by routers). Used by hosts during DHCP discovery (`DHCPDISCOVER`) when they have no IP yet.

#### 2.1.4 This Network Address

- **Value:** **0.0.0.0**
- **Function:**
  - Source address meaning "*this host on this network*" (used during bootstrap / DHCP request).
  - Default route destination in routing tables meaning "*any unknown destination*".

#### 2.1.5 Loopback Address

- **Standard value:** **127.0.0.1**
- **Reserved block:** **127.0.0.0 – 127.255.255.255** (Class A network, RFC 1122)
- **Function:** Packets sent here are looped back to the *same machine* without ever hitting the network interface. Used for testing TCP/IP stack integrity (`ping 127.0.0.1`).

#### 2.1.6 Private (RFC 1918) Addresses

Three non-routable blocks reserved for **internal/private networks**:

- **Class A:** `10.0.0.0` to `10.255.255.255` $\Rightarrow$ **10.0.0.0/8**
- **Class B:** `172.16.0.0` to `172.31.255.255` $\Rightarrow$ **172.16.0.0/12**
- **Class C:** `192.168.0.0` to `192.168.255.255` $\Rightarrow$ **192.168.0.0/16**

These are translated via **Network Address Translation (NAT)** before crossing the public Internet.

#### 2.1.7 Link-Local Addresses (APIPA)

- **Block:** **169.254.0.0 – 169.254.255.255** $\Rightarrow$ **169.254.0.0/16**
- **Function:** Self-assigned by a host when **no DHCP server is reachable** (Automatic Private IP Addressing – APIPA, RFC 3927). Communication is restricted to the local link.

#### 2.1.8 Multicast Addresses (Class D)

- **Block:** **224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255** $\Rightarrow$ **224.0.0.0/4**
- **Function:** One-to-many communication. Notable sub-ranges:
  - **224.0.0.0 – 224.0.0.255:** Local network control (e.g., 224.0.0.1 = All Hosts, 224.0.0.2 = All Routers, 224.0.0.5/6 = OSPF).
  - **239.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255:** Administratively scoped (private multicast).

#### 2.1.9 Reserved for Future Use (Class E)

- **Block:** **240.0.0.0 – 255.255.255.254** $\Rightarrow$ **240.0.0.0/4**
- **Function:** Reserved by IANA for experimental/future use. Routers MUST drop these packets per RFC 1112.

### 2.2 IPv6 Special Addresses

| Address | Notation | Scope & Function |
|---|---|---|
| Unspecified | `::` (0:0:0:0:0:0:0:0) | Source address only; means "*this host*". Never assigned as destination. |
| Loopback | `::1` (0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1) | Equivalent to IPv4 127.0.0.1. Used for local stack testing. |
| Link-Local | `fe80::/10` | Auto-configured on every IPv6-capable interface; non-routable beyond the link. |
| Unique Local (ULA) | `fc00::/7` | Private equivalent of RFC 1918. Globally unique but locally routed. |
| Multicast | `ff00::/8` | Replaces IPv4 broadcast. `ff02::1` = All Nodes, `ff02::2` = All Routers. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In IPv6, broadcast has been completely eliminated and replaced by multicast.** This is one of the most frequently tested KTU points.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ or `\mid` notation; **never** use literal vertical pipes `|` inside markdown tables.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Network Address (Host ID) | $\text{HostBits} = 0$ | Per subnet, lowest address |
| Directed Broadcast | $\text{HostBits} = 2^{h} - 1$ | $h$ = number of host bits |
| Usable Hosts per Subnet | $U = 2^{h} - 2$ | Subtracting network + broadcast |
| Subnets Created | $S = 2^{s}$ | $s$ = borrowed bits |
| Total Addresses per Subnet | $T = 2^{h}$ | Including special addresses |
| Special Addresses Lost | Always exactly $\textbf{2}$ | Net + Bcast, except /31 & /32 |
| /31 Point-to-Point | $U = 2$ (RFC 3021) | No broadcast reserved |
| /32 Host Route | $U = 1$ | Single host, no subnet |
| IP Header Length (bytes) | $L = 4 \cdot \vert IHL \vert$ | $IHL$ in 32-bit words |
| IPv6 Address Bit Count | $b = 128$ bits | 8 groups of 16 bits |
| IPv4 Block /n Host Count | $2^{(32-n)}$ | Total, including specials |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineers** rely on special addresses to design sub-netted enterprise LANs without overlapping with public IP space.
- **Cybersecurity Analysts** use them to identify spoofed packets (e.g., source IP $\approx$ 127.0.0.1 from the wire = malicious).
- **DevOps Engineers** leverage `127.0.0.1` and `::1` to test services locally before deployment.
- **DHCP / Cloud Engineers** handle `0.0.0.0` and `255.255.255.255` in relay agents (IP Helper) and cloud load-balancer bindings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Network Address of a Subnet

**Given:** IP = **192.168.45.130**, Mask = **255.255.255.192** (/26)

**Step 1 – Convert to 32-bit binary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
192      &= 11000000 \\
168      &= 10101000 \\
45       &= 00101101 \\
130      &= 10000010
\end{aligned}
$$

Full binary:
$$\text{IP} = 11000000.10101000.00101101.10000010$$

**Step 2 – Convert mask to binary:**

$$\text{Mask} = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000$$

**Step 3 – Perform bitwise AND ($\land$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Network} &= \text{IP} \;\land\; \text{Mask} \\
&= 11000000.10101000.00101101.10000010 \\
&\;\;\;\land\; 11111111.11111111.00111111.11000000 \\
&= 11000000.10101000.00101101.10000000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Convert back to dotted-decimal:**

$$192.168.45.128 \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \textbf{Network Address}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Directed Broadcast of a Subnet

**Given:** Same subnet as above (192.168.45.128/26).

**Step 1 – Identify host bits:** The mask /26 leaves $h = 32 - 26 = 6$ host bits.

**Step 2 – Compute host-bit max value:**

$$\text{Bcast} = 2^{h} - 1 = 2^{6} - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63$$

**Step 3 – Substitute host bits with 1s in the last octet:**

$$11000000.10101000.00101101.10111111$$

**Step 4 – Dotted-decimal:**

$$\textbf{192.168.45.191} \;\; \rightarrow \;\; \textbf{Directed Broadcast Address}$$

**Step 5 – Compute usable host range:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Usable Range} &= (192.168.45.128 + 1) \;\; \text{to} \;\; (192.168.45.191 - 1) \\
&= 192.168.45.129 \;\; \text{to} \;\; 192.168.45.190 \\
\text{Usable Hosts } U &= 2^{h} - 2 = 2^{6} - 2 = 62
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Why `255.255.255.255` is Limited

**Step 1 – All 32 bits set to 1:**

$$\text{Binary} = 11111111.11111111.11111111.11111111$$

**Step 2 – Apply longest-match routing:** A router performs an AND with the destination and its connected subnets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Subnet} &= 192.168.1.0/24 \\
255.255.255.255 \;\land\; 192.168.1.0 &\neq 192.168.1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

The AND does **not** match any specific network in the routing table, so routers MUST drop the packet by RFC 1812 §4.2.2.11. Therefore it stays on the local LAN — hence **Limited**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Verifying a Loopback Packet

When Host A (IP 10.0.0.5) sends to **127.0.0.1**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source} &= 10.0.0.5 \\
\text{Destination} &= 127.0.0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 – OS routes based on destination prefix:** 127.0.0.1 lies within the loopback block 127.0.0.0/8.

**Step 2 – Routing table match:**

| Destination | Netmask | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| 127.0.0.0 | 255.0.0.0 | **lo** (loopback) |

**Step 3 – Packet bypasses NIC and is queued into the loopback driver.**

**Step 4 – Receiver identifies the same process ID; packet returns without exiting the host.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Address Class vs. Special Behavior

| Class | Leading Bits | Default Mask | Range Start | Range End | Special Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | `0` | /8 | 0.0.0.0 | 127.255.255.255 | Includes loopback |
| B | `10` | /16 | 128.0.0.0 | 191.255.255.255 | Includes 172.16/12 private |
| C | `110` | /24 | 192.0.0.0 | 223.255.255.255 | Includes 192.168/16 private |
| D | `1110` | — | 224.0.0.0 | 239.255.255.255 | Multicast only |
| E | `1111` | — | 240.0.0.0 | 255.255.255.255 | Reserved |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: IPv4 Special Address Catalog

| Decimal | Binary (Class Bits shown) | Name | Routable? | Assignable to Host? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0.0.0 | 00000000… | This Network / Source | No (source only) | No |
| 127.0.0.1 | 01111111.0…0.00000001 | Loopback | No | No |
| 10.255.255.255 | 00001010.11111111.11111111.11111111 | Private Bcast | No | No |
| 172.16.0.0 | 10101100.00010000.00000000.00000000 | Private Net | No | No |
| 192.168.1.0 | 11000000.10101000.00000001.00000000 | Private Net | No | No |
| 169.254.0.1 | 10101001.11111110.00000000.00000001 | Link-Local | No (local) | Yes (auto) |
| 224.0.0.1 | 11100000.00000000.00000000.00000001 | All-Hosts Mcast | Never routed | No |
| 239.255.255.255 | 11011111.11111111.11111111.11111111 | Admin-Scoped Mcast | No | No |
| 255.255.255.255 | 11111111 × 4 | Limited Bcast | No (local only) | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Tip:** Memorize the prefix lengths (`/8`, `/12`, `/16`, `/4`, `/10`, `/7`, `/8`) rather than the decimal ranges — it makes classful vs. CIDR problems faster under exam pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IPv4 Address Space – Block Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["IPv4 32 BIT ADDRESS SPACE"]:::root --> B["Class A 0 0 to 127"]
    A --> C["Class B 128 to 191"]
    A --> D["Class C 192 to 223"]
    A --> E["Class D 224 to 239"]
    A --> F["Class E 240 to 255"]

    B --> B1["0 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 THIS NETWORK"]
    B --> B2["10 dot X PRIVATE RFC1918"]
    B --> B3["127 dot 0 0 0 0 LOOPBACK"]

    C --> C1["172 dot 16 to 31 PRIVATE RFC1918"]

    D --> D1["192 dot 168 dot 0 0 PRIVATE RFC1918"]

    E --> E1["224 dot 0 0 0 0 MULTICAST"]
    E1 --> E1a["224 dot 0 0 dot 0 to 255 LOCAL CONTROL"]
    E1 --> E1b["239 dot X dot X dot X ADMIN SCOPED"]

    F --> F1["240 dot 0 0 0 0 RESERVED"]

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0ea5e9,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#fde68a,color:#000000,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    class B1,B2,B3,C1,D1,E1,F1 leaf
```

### 4.2 IPv6 Special Address Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V6["IPv6 128 BIT ADDRESS SPACE"]:::root --> U["UNSPECIFIED DOUBLE COLON"]
    V6 --> L["LOOPBACK DOUBLE COLON ONE"]
    V6 --> LL["LINK LOCAL fe80 forward slash 10"]
    V6 --> ULA["UNIQUE LOCAL fc00 forward slash 7"]
    V6 --> GL["GLOBAL UNICAST 2000 forward slash 3"]
    V6 --> MC["MULTICAST ff00 forward slash 8"]

    MC --> MA["ff02 double colon 1 ALL NODES"]
    MC --> MB["ff02 double colon 2 ALL ROUTERS"]
    MC --> MC2["ff02 double colon 5 OSPF"]

    classDef root fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#134e4a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000000,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px
    class U,L,LL,ULA,GL,MC,MA,MB,MC2 leaf
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IP Datagram Forwarding Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["INCOMING IP PACKET"] --> X{"DESTINATION EQUALS 255.255.255.255"}
    X -- YES --> LB["LIMITED BROADCAST\nDELIVER TO LOCAL LAN ONLY"]
    X -- NO --> Y{"DESTINATION IN 127.0.0.0 FORWARD SLASH 8"}
    Y -- YES --> LO["LOOPBACK\nRETURN TO SAME HOST"]
    Y -- NO --> Z{"DESTINATION IN 224.0.0.0 FORWARD SLASH 4"}
    Z -- YES --> MU["MULTICAST DELIVERY\nBASED ON GROUP MEMBERSHIP"]
    Z -- NO --> W{"DESTINATION IN RFC1918 PRIVATE RANGE"}
    W -- YES --> PN["PRIVATE ADDRESS\nREQUIRES NAT TO EXIT LAN"]
    W -- NO --> Q["FORWARD USING LONGEST PREFIX MATCH"]
    Q --> R["ROUTED PUBLIC DELIVERY"]

    classDef special fill:#fca5a5,color:#000000,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef normal fill:#bae6fd,color:#000000,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px
    class LB,LO,MU,PN special
    class R normal
```

### 4.4 Logical Flow: How a Host Derives its Special Addresses during Boot

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Host
    participant DHCP
    participant NIC
    Host->>Host: Power On, NIC has no IP
    Host->>Host: Auto-assign 169.254.x.x if no DHCP
    Host->>DHCP: DHCPDISCOVER src 0.0.0.0 dst 255.255.255.255
    DHCP-->>Host: DHCPOFFER with IP e.g. 192.168.1.50
    Host->>Host: Compute Network = 192.168.1.0 (host bits zero)
    Host->>Host: Compute Broadcast = 192.168.1.255 (host bits one)
    Host->>Host: Register loopback 127.0.0.1 internally
    Host->>NIC: Bind 192.168.1.50 as primary
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set A]**

> Explain the significance of the IPv4 address **255.255.255.255**. Why is it called a *Limited Broadcast* address and where is it most commonly used? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** `255.255.255.255` is the IPv4 *Limited Broadcast* address — a 32-bit address with all bits set to 1. Its purpose is to address **every host on the local physical network**.
2. **Why "Limited" (1 mark):** Per **RFC 1812**, routers MUST NOT forward packets with destination `255.255.255.255`. The packet never leaves the local subnet, hence the name *limited*.
3. **Practical Use (1 mark):** It is widely used during **DHCP discovery** (`DHCPDISCOVER`), when a host without an IP must locate a DHCP server on the local segment. Other uses include Wake-on-LAN magic packets and ARP-like local announcements.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Differentiate between **Directed Broadcast** and **Limited Broadcast** in IPv4. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Directed Broadcast | Limited Broadcast |
|---|---|---|
| Decimal form | e.g. `192.168.1.255` | `255.255.255.255` |
| Network targeted | A **specific** subnet | The **local physical** segment |
| Routable? | Routed until it reaches the target network, then broadcast | Never routed |
| Use case | Reaching all hosts in a remote subnet | DHCP discovery, local announcements |
| RFC | RFC 922 | RFC 919 |

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice Provided)

> **KTU Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 + (b) 7.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the IPv4 address space classification into Class A, B, C, D and E. Identify which classes are used for **special addresses** and list at least four special addresses with their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) – Class Diagram (3 Marks):**

The IPv4 32-bit address space is divided into five classes based on the leading bits:

| Class | Leading Bits | Decimal Range | Default Mask | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | `0` | 0.0.0.0 – 127.255.255.255 | /8 | Unicast (large nets) |
| B | `10` | 128.0.0.0 – 191.255.255.255 | /16 | Unicast (medium nets) |
| C | `110` | 192.0.0.0 – 223.255.255.255 | /24 | Unicast (small nets) |
| D | `1110` | 224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255 | — | Multicast |
| E | `1111` | 240.0.0.0 – 255.255.255.255 | — | Reserved |

**Part (ii) – Special Address Roles (4 Marks):**

1. **Class A – Loopback (1 Mark):** `127.0.0.0/8` (commonly `127.0.0.1`) – used to test the local TCP/IP stack without transmitting on the wire.
2. **Class A – "This Network" (1 Mark):** `0.0.0.0` – used by a host as a source address when it has no IP yet (DHCP bootstrap) and as a default route destination.
3. **Class D – Multicast (1 Mark):** `224.0.0.0/4` – one-to-many delivery. Example: `224.0.0.1` = All Hosts on segment, `224.0.0.2` = All Routers.
4. **Class E – Reserved (1 Mark):** `240.0.0.0/4` – reserved for future use per IANA; routers drop these packets.

> **Valuation Tip:** Examiner allocates 1 mark for the diagram and 1 mark each for listing four correctly identified special addresses with their functions.

---

**(b)** Given the IP address **172.20.15.123 /20**, determine:
   (i) The subnet mask in dotted-decimal
   (ii) The Network Address
   (iii) The Directed Broadcast Address
   (iv) The total number of usable hosts in this subnet **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Determine the subnet mask from /20 (1 Mark):**

/20 means 20 network bits and $32 - 20 = 12$ host bits.

Binary mask:
$$11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000$$

Dotted-decimal:
$$\text{Mask} = \textbf{255.255.240.0}$$

**Step 2 – Compute Network Address via bitwise AND (2 Marks):**

IP binary:
$$10101100.00010100.00001111.01111011$$

AND with mask:
$$10101100.00010100.00000000.00000000$$

Convert back:
$$\textbf{Network} = 172.20.0.0$$

**Step 3 – Compute Directed Broadcast (2 Marks):**

Set the 12 host bits to 1:
$$10101100.00010100.00001111.11111111$$

$$\textbf{Broadcast} = 172.20.15.255$$

**Step 4 – Usable Hosts (2 Marks):**

$$U = 2^{h} - 2 = 2^{12} - 2 = 4096 - 2 = \textbf{4094 hosts}$$

Usable range: `172.20.0.1` to `172.20.15.254`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain **Private IP Addresses** as per **RFC 1918**. List the three blocks, give example host addresses from each, and describe how they are made Internet-routable using NAT. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) – Concept of Private Addressing (2 Marks):**

RFC 1918 reserves three IPv4 address blocks for use inside private networks (homes, offices, enterprises). These addresses are **non-unique globally** and **non-routable** on the public Internet. They eliminate the need for every device to have a unique public IP and were essential in extending IPv4's address lifetime.

**Part (ii) – The Three Reserved Blocks (3 Marks):**

| Block | CIDR | Decimal Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class A private | `10.0.0.0/8` | 10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255 | Large enterprises, data centers |
| Class B private | `172.16.0.0/12` | 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255 | Mid-size organizations |
| Class C private | `192.168.0.0/16` | 192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255 | Home / SOHO networks |

Example hosts: `10.5.7.20`, `172.20.4.10`, `192.168.1.50`.

**Part (iii) – Role of NAT (2 Marks):**

**Network Address Translation (NAT)** is implemented at the edge router (or firewall) and translates between **private** and **public** IP addresses:

- **SNAT (Source NAT / PAT):** Many private hosts share one public IP using distinct port numbers.
- **DNAT (Destination NAT):** Inbound traffic to a public IP/port is mapped to an internal private host (port forwarding).

Thus RFC 1918 + NAT enabled the IPv4 Internet to scale despite the 32-bit address space exhaustion.

---

**(b)** Describe the **Loopback Address** and **Link-Local Address (APIPA)** mechanisms in IPv4. Compare their purposes, scopes, and assignment processes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) – Loopback (3 Marks):**

- **Block:** `127.0.0.0/8`; canonical form `127.0.0.1`.
- **Mechanism:** When the OS sees a destination within this block, it routes the packet to the **virtual loopback interface (`lo`)**. The packet is delivered to the local stack without ever leaving the host.
- **Use Cases:** TCP/IP stack testing (`ping 127.0.0.1`), local web server development (`http://127.0.0.1`), inter-process communication (IPC) on the same machine.
- **Assignability:** **Never assignable** to a physical interface.

**Part (ii) – Link-Local / APIPA (3 Marks):**

- **Block:** `169.254.0.0/16` (RFC 3927).
- **Mechanism:** When a Windows / modern OS host fails to obtain a lease from any DHCP server, it **self-assigns** an address from `169.254.1.0` to `169.254.254.255` using a pseudo-random algorithm and verifies uniqueness via **ARP probe**.
- **Use Cases:** Small ad-hoc networks, printer discovery, fallback when no DHCP exists.
- **Scope:** Communication is restricted to the **local link** — packets are **not forwarded** by routers.

**Part (iii) – Comparison (1 Mark):**

| Feature | Loopback | Link-Local (APIPA) |
|---|---|---|
| Range | 127.0.0.0/8 | 169.254.0.0/16 |
| Scope | Same host only | Local physical link |
| Auto-assigned? | Always virtual | Only on DHCP failure |
| Routable? | No | No |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Many students confuse **Network Address** (host bits = 0) with the **Subnet Mask itself**. Remember: the network address is the result of `IP AND Mask`, not the mask.
> 2. Some answer scripts mark `192.168.1.0` as a "valid host address" — this is wrong; it is the **network address** for any /24 subnet starting at that byte.
> 3. A common mistake is stating that `255.255.255.255` is *routable* — it is **strictly limited** to the local segment.
> 4. In IPv6, students often forget that `::` and `::1` are **not assignable to physical interfaces**.
> 5. Confusing **directed broadcast** (single subnet) with **limited broadcast** (local LAN) loses up to 2 marks per question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- ⭐ **Network Address** = host bits all **0**; identifies the subnet, not a host.
- ⭐ **Directed Broadcast** = host bits all **1**; routed to target network then broadcast.
- ⭐ **Limited Broadcast** = `255.255.255.255`; **never crosses a router**.
- ⭐ **This Network** = `0.0.0.0`; source for DHCP bootstrap; default route destination.
- ⭐ **Loopback** = `127.0.0.0/8` (use `127.0.0.1`); local stack test only.
- ⭐ **Private (RFC 1918):** `10/8`, `172.16/12`, `192.168/16` — non-Internet-routable.
- ⭐ **Link-Local (APIPA):** `169.254/16` — auto-assigned when DHCP fails.
- ⭐ **Multicast (Class D):** `224.0.0.0/4` — one-to-many; `224.0.0.1` = All Hosts, `224.0.0.2` = All Routers.
- ⭐ **Reserved (Class E):** `240.0.0.0/4` — routers drop these.
- ⭐ **Usable Hosts Formula:** $U = 2^{h} - 2$ (subtract network + broadcast).
- ⭐ **Total Hosts:** $T = 2^{h}$ (including the two specials).
- ⭐ **IPv6 specials:** `::`, `::1`, `fe80::/10`, `fc00::/7`, `ff00::/8`.
- ⭐ **IPv6 has NO broadcast** — multicast (`ff02::1`, `ff02::2`) replaces it entirely.
- ⭐ **NAT** is the bridge that allows RFC 1918 private addresses to reach the Internet.
- ⭐ **/31 and /32** break the "subtract 2" rule: /31 yields 2 usable (RFC 3021); /32 yields 1 (host route).
- ⭐ **KAA-Classic Trick:** Whenever the question gives a mask not on byte boundary (e.g., `/12`, `/20`), convert to binary for the AND operation — never eyeball it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delivery and Forwarding of IP Packets - Delivery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Delivery and Forwarding of IP Packets - Delivery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Delivery** in the context of Internetworking using TCP/IP refers to the mechanism by which an **Internet Protocol (IP) datagram** is transported from its source host to its final destination host across one or more interconnected networks. The Network Layer (Layer 3) of the TCP/IP architecture is responsible for this end-to-end logical delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style)**: *"Delivery is the process of transmitting an IP datagram from the source host to the destination host. It is classified into two categories: Direct Delivery and Indirect Delivery, depending on whether the datagram can be handed over to the destination within a single physical network or must traverse intermediate routers."*

The **Delivery Module** specifically focuses on the final leg of packet movement — the actual handoff of the IP datagram from the last router (or from the source itself) onto the destination's physical network segment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal System

Imagine the Internet as a **global courier system**:

| Network Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| IP Datagram | Sealed Letter with a Postal Address |
| IP Address | House Address (e.g., "Trivandrum, Kerala, India") |
| Router | Regional Post Office Sorting Hub |
| Direct Delivery | Postman delivering letter to a house in **the same city** as the post office |
| Indirect Delivery | Postman forwarding the letter to **another city's** main post office first |
| ARP | Looking up the **house owner's name and door number** on the local street |
| MAC Address | The **physical door** of the recipient's house |

**Intuition**: When you (Source) want to send a letter to your friend (Destination) who lives in the **same colony**, the post office delivers it directly to his house (**Direct Delivery**). If your friend lives in a **different city**, the post office forwards the letter to the destination city's main office, which then handles the local delivery (**Indirect Delivery**).

## 1.3 Why Delivery is Critical in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Board frequently tests the **decision logic** that a router uses to classify a delivery as Direct or Indirect. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying:
> - Whether the source and destination IP addresses share the **same network ID**.
> - The role of the **subnet mask** in this decision.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold for Highlighting)

- **Default IP Datagram Header Size**: **20 bytes** (without options)
- **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of Ethernet**: **1500 bytes**
- **TTL (Time to Live) Default Value**: **64** (Linux), **128** (Windows)
- **Time-to-Live Decrement Rate**: **1 per hop**
- **ARP Cache Timeout**: Typically **15 to 20 minutes**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Delivery — The Two Fundamental Types

The IP datagram delivery is fundamentally classified into **two categories** based on the topological relationship between the source and the destination:

### 2.1.1 Direct Delivery

**Definition**: Direct delivery occurs when the IP datagram is delivered from one host to another host **within the same physical network** (i.e., no router intervention is required in the IP-level routing logic, although switches may operate at Layer 2).

**Operational Logic Steps**:
1. The source host extracts the **Network ID** of the destination IP address by performing a bitwise **AND** operation with its own subnet mask.
2. The source host compares the extracted Network ID with **its own Network ID**.
3. If both Network IDs are **identical**, the destination is on the same physical network → **Direct Delivery is triggered**.
4. The source consults its **ARP cache** to resolve the destination's **MAC address**.
5. The IP datagram is encapsulated in an **Ethernet frame** and transmitted directly to the destination's physical interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight**: In Direct Delivery, the **source host itself** performs the final delivery. The datagram's destination MAC address equals the **actual destination host's MAC address**. The destination IP address is unchanged.

### 2.1.2 Indirect Delivery

**Definition**: Indirect delivery occurs when the source and destination hosts reside on **different physical networks**. The datagram must be forwarded through **one or more intermediate routers**.

**Operational Logic Steps**:
1. The source host determines that the destination's Network ID is **different** from its own.
2. The source host looks up its **default gateway's** MAC address via ARP.
3. The datagram is encapsulated with the **router's MAC address** as the Layer 2 destination, but the **destination IP address** remains the final host's IP.
4. Each intermediate router **decrements the TTL** and **re-encapsulates** the datagram at Layer 2 for the next hop.
5. When the datagram reaches a router that is on the **same physical network** as the final destination, the delivery transitions to **Direct Delivery**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The IP destination address never changes** during the journey. Only the **Layer 2 MAC addresses** change at every hop. This is the essence of the **Network Layer's connectionless, best-effort delivery model**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Network ID Extraction | $\text{Network ID} = \text{IP Address} \text{ AND } \text{Subnet Mask}$ | Bitwise AND to find network portion | Binary |
| Host ID Extraction | $\text{Host ID} = \text{IP Address} \text{ AND } (\text{NOT Subnet Mask})$ | Bitwise AND with inverted mask | Binary |
| Direct Delivery Condition | $\text{NID}_{\text{src}} = \text{NID}_{\text{dst}}$ | Same Network ID → Direct | Boolean |
| Indirect Delivery Condition | $\text{NID}_{\text{src}} \neq \text{NID}_{\text{dst}}$ | Different Network ID → Indirect | Boolean |
| TTL Decrement | $\text{TTL}_{\text{new}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{old}} - 1$ | Decremented at each router hop | Integer |
| Hop Count Limit | $\text{TTL} > 0$ | Otherwise datagram is discarded | — |
| Header Checksum Recalculation | $\text{HC}_{\text{new}} = \text{Sum of 16-bit words (one's complement)}$ | Recomputed at every hop | Hex |
| ARP Request Type | Broadcast: $\text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}$ | MAC broadcast address | Hex |
| Maximum Encapsulation | $\text{Frame Size} \leq \text{MTU}_{\text{link}}$ | E.g., Ethernet MTU = 1500 bytes | Bytes |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Direct/Indirect delivery mechanism is the **foundational decision tree** of every IP-capable device — from a **smartphone** to a **data center spine-leaf switch router**. Modern routers use this logic in hardware (TCAM — Ternary Content Addressable Memory) to achieve **wire-speed forwarding** at multi-terabit rates. Understanding this concept is essential for careers in:
- **Network Engineering** (CCNA, CCNP, CCIE tracks)
- **Cloud Infrastructure** (AWS VPC, Azure VNet peering)
- **Cybersecurity** (firewall rule processing, IDS/IPS inspection)
- **SDN Development** (OpenFlow, P4 programming)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Network ID Extraction for Delivery Decision

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Given a source host with IP address **192.168.10.45/26** sending a datagram to destination **192.168.10.50**, determine the delivery type.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1**: Convert the IP addresses to their **32-bit binary** representations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source IP: } 192.168.10.45 &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00101101 \\
\text{Destination IP: } 192.168.10.50 &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00110010 \\
\text{Subnet Mask } (/26) &= 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute the **Network ID of the source** by performing a bitwise AND between the source IP and the subnet mask.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{src}} &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00101101 \\
&\quad \text{ AND } \\
&\quad 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000 \\
&= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00000000 \\
&= 192.168.10.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of logic**: In the AND operation, `1 AND 1 = 1`, `1 AND 0 = 0`, `0 AND 1 = 0`, `0 AND 0 = 0`. The first 26 bits of the IP are preserved (Network portion), and the last 6 bits (Host portion) are zeroed out.

**Step 3**: Compute the **Network ID of the destination** by performing a bitwise AND between the destination IP and the same subnet mask.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{dst}} &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00110010 \\
&\quad \text{ AND } \\
&\quad 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000 \\
&= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00000000 \\
&= 192.168.10.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of logic**: Note that bits 27–32 of both source and destination are different (`00101101` vs `00110010`), but since the /26 mask only keeps the first 26 bits, these differences are masked away.

**Step 4**: Compare the two Network IDs using the direct delivery condition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{src}} &= 192.168.10.0 \\
\text{NID}_{\text{dst}} &= 192.168.10.0 \\
\text{Decision: } \text{NID}_{\text{src}} &= \text{NID}_{\text{dst}} \implies \textbf{DIRECT DELIVERY}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: The source host now **performs ARP resolution** to obtain the MAC address of `192.168.10.50` and encapsulates the datagram directly.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Indirect Delivery Decision

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

Source IP: **10.0.0.5/8** sending to Destination IP: **172.16.20.100**. Determine the delivery type.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1**: Identify the subnet mask. The `/8` prefix means the mask is `255.0.0.0`.

**Step 2**: Extract the source Network ID.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{src}} &= 10.0.0.5 \text{ AND } 255.0.0.0 \\
&= 10.0.0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Extract the destination Network ID (using the **source's** subnet mask, since the source makes the decision).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{dst}} &= 172.16.20.100 \text{ AND } 255.0.0.0 \\
&= 172.0.0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Apply the indirect delivery condition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{src}} = 10.0.0.0 &\neq \text{NID}_{\text{dst}} = 172.0.0.0 \\
&\implies \textbf{INDIRECT DELIVERY}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: The source forwards the datagram to its **default gateway**. The ARP resolution targets the gateway's IP, not the final destination.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

The following matrix summarizes the routing decision tree that every IP host/router follows:

| Source NID | Destination NID | Subnet Mask Match? | Delivery Type | MAC Destination | Next Hop |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Same | Same | Yes | **Direct** | Destination Host | None (final) |
| Same | Different | No | **Indirect** | Default Gateway | Router Interface |
| Different | Same | — | **Direct** (at last hop) | Destination Host | None (final) |
| Different | Different | — | **Indirect** | Next-Hop Router | Determined by Routing Table |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Multi-Hop Indirect Delivery

Consider a datagram traveling from Host A to Host D across 3 networks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Host A (10.0.0.5)} &\xrightarrow{\text{Network 1}} \text{Router R1 (10.0.0.1, 192.168.1.1)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Network 2}} \text{Router R2 (192.168.1.2, 203.0.113.1)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Network 3}} \text{Host D (203.0.113.50)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step hop analysis**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hop 1: A} \to \text{R1} &: \text{NID}_{\text{A}} = 10.0.0.0 \neq \text{NID}_{\text{D}} = 203.0.113.0 \implies \text{Indirect (via R1)} \\
\text{Hop 2: R1} \to \text{R2} &: \text{NID}_{\text{R1-src}} = 192.168.1.0 \neq \text{NID}_{\text{D}} = 203.0.113.0 \implies \text{Indirect (via R2)} \\
\text{Hop 3: R2} \to \text{D} &: \text{NID}_{\text{R2-src}} = 203.0.113.0 = \text{NID}_{\text{D}} = 203.0.113.0 \implies \text{DIRECT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation**: The final hop transitions from Indirect to **Direct Delivery**. This is a recurring KTU exam question pattern.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Direct vs. Indirect Delivery Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([IP Datagram Ready for Transmission]) --> ExtractSrc["Extract Source IP Address"]
    ExtractSrc --> ExtractDst["Extract Destination IP Address"]
    ExtractDst --> ApplyMask["Apply Subnet Mask AND Operation"]
    ApplyMask --> CompNID["Compute NID_src and NID_dst"]
    CompNID --> Decision{"Is NID_src equal to NID_dst?"}
    Decision -- YES --> DirectPath["Initiate DIRECT DELIVERY"]
    Decision -- NO --> IndirectPath["Initiate INDIRECT DELIVERY"]
    
    DirectPath --> ARPdest["ARP Request for Destination IP"]
    ARPdest --> Enc1["Encapsulate: Dest MAC = Host MAC"]
    Enc1 --> Send1["Transmit Frame on Local Network"]
    Send1 --> Done1([Datagram Delivered to Destination])
    
    IndirectPath --> ARPgw["ARP Request for Default Gateway IP"]
    ARPgw --> Enc2["Encapsulate: Dest MAC = Router MAC"]
    Enc2 --> Send2["Transmit Frame to Router"]
    Send2 --> Router1["Router R1: Decrement TTL, Recompute Checksum"]
    Router1 --> Router2["Router R2: Decrement TTL, Recompute Checksum"]
    Router2 --> LastHop{"Final router on same network as destination?"}
    LastHop -- YES --> DirectPath
    LastHop -- NO --> Router2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Hop Delivery Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HostA["Host A\n10.0.0.5/24\nNetwork: 10.0.0.0"] -- "Hop 1: Indirect Delivery\n(ARP to R1)" --> Router1["Router R1\nInterface 1: 10.0.0.1\nInterface 2: 192.168.1.1"]
    Router1 -- "Hop 2: Indirect Delivery\n(ARP to R2)" --> Router2["Router R2\nInterface 1: 192.168.1.2\nInterface 2: 203.0.113.1"]
    Router2 -- "Hop 3: Direct Delivery\n(ARP to Host D)" --> HostD["Host D\n203.0.113.50/24\nNetwork: 203.0.113.0"]
    
    style HostA fill:#e1f5ff
    style HostD fill:#e1f5ff
    style Router1 fill:#fff4e1
    style Router2 fill:#fff4e1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: IP Datagram Encapsulation at Each Hop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Hop1["HOP 1: Host A to Router R1"]
        H1["IP Header: Src=10.0.0.5, Dst=203.0.113.50, TTL=64\nEthernet Header: SrcMAC=AA:AA, DstMAC=BB:BB(R1)"]
    end
    
    subgraph Hop2["HOP 2: Router R1 to Router R2"]
        H2["IP Header: Src=10.0.0.5, Dst=203.0.113.50, TTL=63\nEthernet Header: SrcMAC=CC:CC(R1), DstMAC=DD:DD(R2)"]
    end
    
    subgraph Hop3["HOP 3: Router R2 to Host D"]
        H3["IP Header: Src=10.0.0.5, Dst=203.0.113.50, TTL=62\nEthernet Header: SrcMAC=EE:EE(R2), DstMAC=FF:FF(Host D)"]
    end
    
    Hop1 --> Hop2
    Hop2 --> Hop3
    
    style Hop1 fill:#f0fff0
    style Hop2 fill:#f0fff0
    style Hop3 fill:#f0fff0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight**: Observe how the **IP addresses (Layer 3) remain constant** throughout the journey, while the **MAC addresses (Layer 2) change at every hop**. This decoupling of layers is the defining principle of the TCP/IP protocol stack.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define Direct Delivery and Indirect Delivery in IP forwarding. Under what condition is each type triggered?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Direct Delivery (1.5 Marks)**: Direct Delivery occurs when the IP datagram is delivered from one host to another host **within the same physical network** without being forwarded by any router. The source host itself performs the final encapsulation and transmission.
- **Indirect Delivery (1.5 Marks)**: Indirect Delivery occurs when the source and destination hosts are on **different physical networks**. The datagram is forwarded through one or more intermediate routers via the default gateway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Condition**: The source extracts the Network IDs of both the source and destination using the subnet mask. If the Network IDs match → **Direct**; if they differ → **Indirect**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Why does the IP destination address remain unchanged at every router hop, while the MAC address changes? Explain with reference to the encapsulation process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- The IP address operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and represents the **end-to-end logical addressing** used for routing the datagram from the ultimate source to the ultimate destination (2 Marks).
- The MAC address operates at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** and represents the **node-to-node physical addressing** used for the next hop only. At each router, the datagram is de-encapsulated, the destination MAC is updated to the next hop's MAC, and re-encapsulated (1 Mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Direct and Indirect Delivery of IP packets with suitable diagrams. Discuss the role of the subnet mask in the delivery decision. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Definition of Direct Delivery (2 Marks)**: Direct delivery is the process of delivering an IP datagram to a host on the **same physical network** as the sender. No router intervention is required; the source host encapsulates the datagram in a Layer 2 frame with the destination host's MAC address obtained via ARP.

**Definition of Indirect Delivery (2 Marks)**: Indirect delivery involves forwarding the datagram to a host on a **different physical network** through one or more routers. The source host encapsulates the datagram with the **default gateway's MAC address** and sends it to the router for further forwarding.

**Role of Subnet Mask (2 Marks)**: The subnet mask enables the source to extract the **Network ID** from both source and destination IP addresses using a bitwise AND operation. The comparison of these Network IDs determines the delivery type:
- Same Network ID → **Direct Delivery**
- Different Network ID → **Indirect Delivery**

**Diagrammatic Representation (1 Mark)**: A simple two-network topology with Host A, Router R, and Host B, showing the MAC address encapsulation at each hop.

---

**(b)** A host with IP address **172.20.15.5/20** sends a datagram to a host with IP address **172.20.25.50**. Determine the type of delivery with full binary analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Identify the Subnet Mask (1 Mark)**:

A `/20` prefix means the first 20 bits are `1`. In dotted decimal:
$$
\text{Subnet Mask} = 255.255.240.0
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the IP Addresses to Binary (1.5 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source: } 172.20.15.5 &= 10101100.00010100.00001111.00000101 \\
\text{Subnet Mask: } /20 &= 11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000 \\
\text{Destination: } 172.20.25.50 &= 10101100.00010100.00011001.00110010
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Source Network ID (1.5 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{src}} &= 10101100.00010100.00001111.00000101 \\
&\quad \text{ AND } \\
&\quad 11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000 \\
&= 10101100.00010100.00000000.00000000 \\
&= 172.20.0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Destination Network ID (1.5 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{dst}} &= 10101100.00010100.00011001.00110010 \\
&\quad \text{ AND } \\
&\quad 11111111.11111111.11110000.00000000 \\
&= 10101100.00010100.00010000.00000000 \\
&= 172.20.16.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1.5 Marks)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NID}_{\text{src}} = 172.20.0.0 &\neq \text{NID}_{\text{dst}} = 172.20.16.0 \\
&\implies \textbf{INDIRECT DELIVERY}
\end{aligned}
$$

The datagram will be forwarded to the **default gateway** for routing to the destination network `172.20.16.0/20`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain how an IP datagram undergoes indirect delivery across multiple routers. State clearly what remains unchanged and what changes at every hop. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Diagram (2 Marks)**: A multi-hop network diagram showing:
- Source Host (Network 1)
- Router R1 (between Network 1 and Network 2)
- Router R2 (between Network 2 and Network 3)
- Destination Host (Network 3)

**Explanation of Multi-Hop Indirect Delivery (3 Marks)**:
1. The source host determines the destination is on a **different network** and sends the datagram to Router R1.
2. Router R1 decrements the TTL, recomputes the header checksum, and forwards the datagram to Router R2 based on its routing table.
3. Router R2, being on the **same physical network** as the final destination, performs a **Direct Delivery** to the destination host using ARP.

**What Remains Unchanged (1 Mark)**:
- Source IP Address
- Destination IP Address
- Upper-layer payload (TCP/UDP segment)

**What Changes at Every Hop (1 Mark)**:
- TTL (Time to Live) — decremented by 1
- Header Checksum — recomputed
- Source MAC Address (replaced with outgoing interface MAC)
- Destination MAC Address (replaced with next-hop MAC)

---

**(b)** Compare Direct Delivery and Indirect Delivery across the following parameters: (i) Number of routers involved, (ii) MAC address of the frame, (iii) Network ID comparison, (iv) Use of ARP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

| Parameter | Direct Delivery (3.5 Marks) | Indirect Delivery (3.5 Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Number of Routers | Zero routers involved | At least one router involved |
| (ii) MAC Address in Frame | Destination MAC = Final host MAC | Destination MAC = Next-hop router MAC |
| (iii) Network ID Comparison | NID_src = NID_dst | NID_src ≠ NID_dst |
| (iv) Use of ARP | ARP for final destination IP | ARP for default gateway IP |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert**:
> 1. **Common Mistake #1**: Students often forget that **the IP destination address NEVER changes** throughout the journey — only the MAC addresses change. Losing 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2**: Confusing **Forwarding** (per-packet, router-internal, fast-path decision) with **Routing** (building the routing table using protocols like OSPF, BGP). These are **two distinct functions** of the network layer.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3**: Applying the **destination's** subnet mask instead of the **source's** subnet mask when computing the Network ID. Always use the source's perspective.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4**: Forgetting to decrement TTL and recompute the checksum. Each router MUST perform both operations before re-forwarding.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5**: Stating that "the router changes the destination IP." This is **incorrect** unless NAT is involved. In pure IP forwarding, the destination IP is preserved.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist** (Pin this before exams!):

- ⭐ **Delivery** is the end-to-end transportation of an IP datagram from source to destination host.
- ⭐ **Direct Delivery** = Same Network ID → No router → ARP for final host.
- ⭐ **Indirect Delivery** = Different Network ID → Router(s) involved → ARP for gateway.
- ⭐ The **decision** is made by the **sender (or the current router)** using its own subnet mask.
- ⭐ **Network ID Extraction** = IP Address **AND** Subnet Mask (bitwise operation).
- ⭐ The **IP destination address** remains **constant** end-to-end; only **MAC addresses** change per hop.
- ⭐ **TTL** is decremented by **1** at every router; datagram is discarded when TTL reaches 0.
- ⭐ **Header Checksum** is **recomputed** at every router (since TTL changes).
- ⭐ The **final hop** in any indirect delivery is always a **Direct Delivery** from the last router to the destination.
- ⭐ **Forwarding** (data plane) ≠ **Routing** (control plane) — Forwarding is the per-packet decision; Routing is the table-building process.
- ⭐ **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** maps IP → MAC only for the **next hop**, not the final destination (in indirect delivery).
- ⭐ Maximum Ethernet payload (MTU) = **1500 bytes**; larger datagrams must be **fragmented**.
- ⭐ KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping: This topic maps to **CO1** (Understand fundamental concepts of Internetworking) and applies **Bloom's Level: Understand, Apply, Analyze**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forwarding - Forwarding Based on Destination Address
topics.content:
