## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the term "default mask" in classful addressing. State the default mask for each of Class A, Class B, and Class C networks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **default mask** is the natural subnet mask assigned to a classful network based on its class. It is used by a router to extract the network portion of an IP address when no other mask is specified.

- **Class A:** `255.0.0.0` (or `/8`)
- **Class B:** `255.255.0.0` (or `/16`)
- **Class C:** `255.255.255.0` (or `/24`)

**Mark split:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two classes (or fractional split).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Why is a subnet mask required for routing, even within a classful network that uses subnets?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A subnet mask is required to **distinguish the subnet ID from the host ID** within a single classful network. Without it, a router cannot determine how many bits of the host portion have been borrowed for subnetting, and therefore cannot decide whether two IP addresses belong to the same subnet or to different subnets. The mask is stored in the routing table of every internal router and is used to perform a bitwise AND on the destination address to obtain the subnet ID for forwarding decisions.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for "distinguishes subnet from host" + 1 mark for AND operation + 1 mark for forwarding decision.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the forwarding process in a classful network **without subnetting**. How does a router determine the next hop? (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. (1 mark) **Classful forwarding without subnetting** means the router uses the **default mask** of the destination address's class to extract the network portion.
2. (1 mark) **Steps in the algorithm:**
    - i. Receive datagram and read destination IP `D`.
    - ii. Inspect first octet to determine class A, B, or C.
    - iii. Select the default mask `M_def` for that class.
    - iv. Compute `N = D AND M_def` to obtain the Network ID.
    - v. Search the routing table for an entry matching `N`.
    - vi. If found, forward via the corresponding interface to the next-hop. If not, use the default route or drop with ICMP error.
3. (2 marks) **Determining the next hop:** Once the routing table is searched, the matched entry contains the next-hop IP and the outgoing interface. The router decrements TTL, recomputes the IP header checksum, and re-encapsulates the IP datagram inside a Layer-2 frame addressed to the next-hop's MAC (resolved via ARP if not in cache).
4. (1 mark) **Diagram:** Reference — show router with two interfaces, an incoming and outgoing link, and a routing table with two entries.
5. (2 marks) **Example worked out:** Use `D = 172.16.45.90` (Class B, mask `255.255.0.0`) → Network `172.16.0.0`. Show table match.

---

**Q. (b)** An organization is assigned the Class B network `160.50.0.0`. The administrator wants to create **at least 60 subnets**, each with **at least 1000 hosts**. Determine:
   (i) The number of bits to be borrowed.
   (ii) The new subnet mask.
   (iii) The number of usable hosts per subnet.
   (iv) The first and the last subnet addresses. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Bits to be borrowed:**
Class B has $H = 16$ host bits.

Need $2^{s} \ge 60$ ⇒ $s = 6$ since $2^{6} = 64 \ge 60$. **(1 mark)**

**(ii) New subnet mask:**
Default mask `/16` + 6 borrowed bits = `/22`.
In decimal: `255.255.11111100.00000000 = 255.255.252.0`. **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Usable hosts per subnet:**
$$
h = 16 - 6 = 10
$$

$$
\text{Usable hosts} = 2^{10} - 2 = 1024 - 2 = 1022
$$

Since $1022 \ge 1000$, requirement satisfied. **(2 marks)**

**(iv) First and last subnet:**
Subnets step by `2^{10} = 1024` in the third octet.

- **First subnet:** `160.50.0.0` to `160.50.3.255` (Network = `160.50.0.0`, Broadcast = `160.50.3.255`). **(1.5 marks)**
- **Last subnet:** `160.50.252.0` to `160.50.255.255` (Network = `160.50.252.0`, Broadcast = `160.50.255.255`). **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Explain how forwarding is performed in a classful network **with subnetting**. Differentiate clearly between the role of the default mask and the subnet mask during routing. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. (2 marks) In a classful network with subnetting, the **external view** of the network (visible to the rest of the Internet) is the classful network address with its default mask, e.g., `150.10.0.0/16`. However, **internal routers** of the organization apply a **custom subnet mask** (e.g., `/22`) to extract the **subnet ID**, which is the unit of forwarding inside the organization.
2. (2 marks) **Role of default mask:** Used by *external* routers and by the organization's *border router* when sending a packet to the outside world. It produces the classful Network ID.
3. (2 marks) **Role of subnet mask:** Used by *internal* routers. It produces the Subnet ID for intra-organization routing. The subnet mask is configured on each interface of every internal router.
4. (1 mark) **Key Difference:** The default mask never changes; it is a property of the class. The subnet mask is locally chosen and may differ from one organization to another. Multiple subnets share the same default mask but have different subnet masks internally.

---

**Q. (b)** Consider the Class C network `210.30.40.0/24`. The network is divided into subnets using the mask `255.255.255.224`. Answer the following:
   (i) How many subnets are created?
   (ii) How many usable host addresses per subnet?
   (iii) List the network addresses of the first three subnets and the broadcast address of the second subnet. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of subnets:**
Class C has $H = 8$ host bits. Mask `/27` means $s = 27 - 24 = 3$ bits borrowed.

$$
\text{Subnets} = 2^{3} = 8
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(ii) Usable hosts per subnet:**
$$
h = 8 - 3 = 5
$$

$$
\text{Hosts} = 2^{5} - 2 = 32 - 2 = 30
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Subnet addresses and broadcast:**

Subnets step by $2^{5} = 32$ in the last octet.

- Subnet 1: `210.30.40.0` to `210.30.40.31`
- Subnet 2: `210.30.40.32` to `210.30.40.63`
- Subnet 3: `210.30.40.64` to `210.30.40.95`

**Network addresses of first three subnets:**
`210.30.40.0`, `210.30.40.32`, `210.30.40.64` **(2 marks)**

**Broadcast of second subnet:** `210.30.40.63` **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 2 from hosts** — Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for writing `2^{h}` instead of `2^{h} - 2`. The `-2` accounts for the network and broadcast addresses.
> 2. **Confusing `s` with `h`** — Many students borrow *more* bits than needed, leaving insufficient host bits. Always verify $2^{h} - 2 \ge$ required hosts *after* choosing `s`.
> 3. **Using default mask instead of subnet mask** — In a "with subnetting" question, students often forget to switch to the custom mask. The default mask only works at the *border* of the network.
> 4. **Miscomputing AND on multi-byte addresses** — Practice conversions for octets where the mask is neither `0` nor `255` (e.g., `128`, `192`, `224`, `240`, `248`, `252`, `254`).
> 5. **Wrong broadcast address** — The broadcast is the *last* address in the subnet, not "all 255s in the host portion." Show full calculation.

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Classful forwarding** = mask is fixed by class (`/8`, `/16`, `/24`).
- ✅ **Subnet forwarding** = mask is *custom*, stored on each interface internally.
- ✅ The **first octet** determines the class: `0–127` (A), `128–191` (B), `192–223` (C).
- ✅ **Default mask of Class A** = `255.0.0.0`; **Class B** = `255.255.0.0`; **Class C** = `255.255.255.0`.
- ✅ Use the formula **Hosts = $2^{h} - 2$** and **Subnets = $2^{s}$** (modern rule).
- ✅ Total borrowed bits + remaining host bits = original host bits of the class.
- ✅ For a **Class B**, host bits = 16; for a **Class C**, host bits = 8.
- ✅ **Subnet address** = host bits all `0`; **Broadcast address** = host bits all `1`.
- ✅ The **AND operation** is the heart of all routing lookups.
- ✅ External Internet sees only the classful Network ID; internal routers see the Subnet ID.
- ✅ A **subnet mask** is always a contiguous sequence of `1`s followed by contiguous `0`s (e.g., `11111111.11111111.11111111.11100000`).
- ✅ **CIDR notation**: `/n` where `n` = number of `1`s in the mask.
- ✅ Subnets of size `/30` (2 hosts) are typically used for **point-to-point router links**.
- ✅ Subnets of size `/31` (RFC 3021) and `/32` (host route) are special cases not covered under classful subnetting.
- ✅ **All-zeros and all-ones subnets** were historically disallowed in classful routing, but RFC 1878 (1995) lifts this restriction in modern classless environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forwarding with Classless Addressing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forwarding with Classless Addressing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forwarding with Classless Addressing** is the mechanism by which an IP router examines the destination IP address of an incoming datagram, performs a bitwise **AND** operation between the destination address and the subnet mask (prefix length) of each routing table entry, and selects the **most specific matching route** (i.e., the entry with the **longest prefix match**) to determine the outgoing interface and next-hop address.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Terminology:**
> - **CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing):** A method that replaces fixed classful boundaries (A, B, C) with variable-length prefixes written in slash notation (e.g., `/n`).
> - **Prefix Length (`/n`):** The number of leading contiguous **1-bits** in the subnet mask, indicating the size of the network portion.
> - **Longest Prefix Match (LPM):** The forwarding rule that mandates selection of the routing entry having the **largest** value of `n` among all matching entries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office with Multiple Mailbags"

Imagine you are a **post office clerk** in Kerala, and three delivery vans are parked outside:

- Van A delivers to **all of India** (very broad).
- Van B delivers to **Kerala state only** (more specific).
- Van C delivers to **Thiruvananthapuram city** (most specific).

You receive a letter addressed to *Vellayambalam, Thiruvananthapuram*. All three vans *can* technically transport the letter (all three are "compatible"). However, as a smart clerk, you would place the letter into **Van C**, because Van C provides the most precise, shortest-path delivery.

> **The router behaves exactly like this clerk.** A datagram destined to `192.168.1.130` may match three routing entries (`/16`, `/24`, `/25`). The router must always choose the entry with the **longest prefix** (`/25`) — the most specific route.

### 1.3 Why Classless Forwarding is Needed

> [!NOTE]
> **Classful forwarding** (used historically in the early Internet) assumed that the network portion could be deduced from the **first few bits** of the address (Class A, B, C). This caused:
> 1. **Rigid boundaries** leading to massive address waste (e.g., a Class B network gives 65,534 hosts, even if only 1,000 are needed).
> 2. **Routing table explosion**, because each subnet had to be advertised separately.
>
> **Classless forwarding (CIDR)** solves both problems by allowing **arbitrary-length prefixes** (`/n`), enabling **route aggregation (supernetting)** and **VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)**.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Notation |
|---|---|
| Total IPv4 address size | **32 bits** |
| Default mask notation | Dotted-decimal or slash (`/n`) |
| Maximum prefix length | **/32** (single host route) |
| Minimum aggregated prefix | **/0** (default route) |
| Longest Match Rule | **LPM (Longest Prefix Match)** |
| Aggregation operation | **Supernetting** |
| Sub-operation | **VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Forwarding Algorithm

When a router `R` receives an IP datagram with destination address `D`, the classless forwarding procedure executes the following sequence:

1. **Extract Destination Address `D`** from the datagram header (32 bits).
2. **Iterate through every entry `(M_i, N_i, NH_i, IF_i)`** in the routing table, where:
   - $M_i$ = Mask (e.g., `255.255.255.0`)
   - $N_i$ = Network address
   - $NH_i$ = Next-hop IP
   - $IF_i$ = Outgoing interface
3. **Perform bitwise AND** between `D` and each mask $M_i$:
   $$\text{Result} = D \; \text{AND} \; M_i$$
4. **Compare** $\text{Result}$ with the entry's network address $N_i$:
   - If $\text{Result} == N_i$, the entry is a **match**. Record the prefix length (number of 1-bits in $M_i$).
5. **After scanning all entries**, select the entry with the **largest prefix length** (longest mask).
6. **Forward the datagram** out of $IF_i$ toward $NH_i$.
7. **Default Route Fallback:** If no entry matches, use the **default route** (`0.0.0.0/0`). If even the default is absent, the packet is **dropped**, and an **ICMP Destination Unreachable** is sent back to the source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Longest Prefix Match (LPM) rule is non-negotiable.** It is the heart of classless forwarding and is the single most tested concept in KTU Module 1 numerical questions.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 3 (AND with mask):** Strips off the host portion, leaving only the network identifier for comparison.
- **Step 4 (Compare with N):** Verifies whether the destination lies inside the network range advertised by the route.
- **Step 5 (Longest match):** Ensures the **most specific** path is chosen. A `/25` is more specific than a `/24`, which is more specific than a `/16`.

### 2.3 Address Aggregation (Supernetting)

When a router has multiple contiguous network advertisements, it can be **configured** to advertise a single summarized route using a **shorter** prefix:

$$\text{Aggregated Prefix} = \text{Common leading bits of all member networks}$$

> **Engineering Utility:** Aggregation drastically **shrinks the global Internet routing table**, conserves router memory, and reduces lookup time. ISPs heavily use aggregation to advertise huge blocks (e.g., `/8` or `/12`) covering thousands of customer networks.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU numericals on this topic. **No vertical pipe symbols (`|`) are used** in the math expressions to preserve table integrity.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Apply Mask | $\text{Network} = D \; \text{AND} \; M$ | Bitwise AND of 32-bit values |
| Number of Addresses in `/n` | $2^{(32-n)}$ | Total host count including network & broadcast |
| Usable Hosts in `/n` | $2^{(32-n)} - 2$ | Subtract network and broadcast addresses |
| Longest Match Rule | $\max\{n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k\}$ for matches | Choose entry with largest $n$ |
| Number of Subnets from `/N` to `/n` | $2^{(n - N)}$ | When $n > N$ (subnetting) |
| Number of Aggregated Networks | $2^{(n_1 - n_2)}$ | When combining $n_1$-bit networks into $n_2$-bit block |
| Block Size | $2^{(32-n)}$ | Difference between consecutive network addresses |
| Wildcard Mask | $M_w = \text{INV}(M)$ | Used in access lists and OSPF |
| First Address | $\text{Network Address}$ | Host bits all `0` |
| Last Address | $\text{Broadcast Address}$ | Host bits all `1` |
| Slash to Mask | Sum of $2^{(8-k)}$ for each octet with `k` leading `0`s | Conversion helper |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BGP Routers on the Internet backbone** routinely maintain routing tables with **900,000+ prefixes**, and every forwarding decision uses LPM in hardware (TCAM — Ternary Content Addressable Memory).
- **CIDR** was introduced in **RFC 1518 / RFC 1519 (1993)** to delay IPv4 address exhaustion.
- **Classless addressing** is the **only** forwarding method used in the modern Internet. Classful forwarding is **obsolete** and supported only for backward compatibility on legacy routers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Longest Prefix Match

**Problem:** A router has the following routing table. A packet arrives with destination IP `192.168.1.130`. Determine the next hop and outgoing interface.

| Mask | Network Address | Next Hop | Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/16` | 192.168.0.0 | 10.0.0.1 | eth0 |
| `/24` | 192.168.1.0 | 10.0.0.2 | eth1 |
| `/25` | 192.168.1.128 | 10.0.0.3 | eth2 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert destination to binary (last two octets for brevity):**

Destination = `192.168.1.130`

$$1.130 \rightarrow 00000001.\;10000010$$

**Step 2 — Test Entry 1 (`/16`):**
- Mask `/16` = `255.255.0.0` → keeps only the first 2 octets.
- Result: `192.168.0.0`
- Compare: $\text{Result} \; (192.168.0.0) \stackrel{?}{=} N_1 \; (192.168.0.0)$ → **MATCH** ✓
- Prefix length recorded: $n_1 = 16$

**Step 3 — Test Entry 2 (`/24`):**
- Mask `/24` = `255.255.255.0` → keeps first 3 octets.
- Result: `192.168.1.0`
- Compare: $\text{Result} \; (192.168.1.0) \stackrel{?}{=} N_2 \; (192.168.1.0)$ → **MATCH** ✓
- Prefix length recorded: $n_2 = 24$

**Step 4 — Test Entry 3 (`/25`):**
- Mask `/25` = `255.255.255.128` → keeps first 3 octets + first bit of 4th octet.
- The 4th octet of destination is `130` = `10000010`.
- AND with `128` = `10000000` → Result = `10000000` = `128`.
- Full network result: `192.168.1.128`
- Compare: $\text{Result} \; (192.168.1.128) \stackrel{?}{=} N_3 \; (192.168.1.128)$ → **MATCH** ✓
- Prefix length recorded: $n_3 = 25$

**Step 5 — Apply Longest Prefix Match:**

$$\text{Chosen } n = \max(16, 24, 25) = 25$$

**Step 6 — Forwarding Decision:**
- Next Hop: `10.0.0.3`
- Outgoing Interface: `eth2`

> [!NOTE]
> The router will **NOT** choose `/16` or `/24` even though they also match. The `/25` rule wins because it is the **most specific** match.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Aggregation (Supernetting) Calculation

**Problem:** A router is connected to four contiguous networks:
- `192.168.0.0/24`
- `192.168.1.0/24`
- `192.168.2.0/24`
- `192.168.3.0/24`

Aggregate them into a **single** routing entry.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the third octet of all four networks in binary:**

$$0 \rightarrow 00000000$$
$$1 \rightarrow 00000001$$
$$2 \rightarrow 00000010$$
$$3 \rightarrow 00000011$$

**Step 2 — Identify common leading bits:**

The first **6 bits** of the third octet are `000000` (common to all four). The last 2 bits vary from `00` to `11`.

**Step 3 — Compute the new prefix length:**

The first two octets contribute `16` bits. The common 6 bits of the third octet contribute `6` more.

$$\text{Aggregated prefix length} = 16 + 6 = 22$$

So the aggregated entry is:

$$192.168.0.0/22$$

**Step 4 — Verify the block size:**

$$2^{(32-22)} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ addresses}$$

The block spans `192.168.0.0` to `192.168.3.255`, which is exactly 4 × 256 = 1024 addresses. ✓

**Step 5 — Calculate the number of original `/24` networks absorbed:**

$$2^{(24 - 22)} = 2^2 = 4 \text{ networks}$$

This matches our input of 4 networks. ✓

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Decimal Mask Conversion

**Problem:** Convert `/20` to dotted-decimal mask.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Distribute the 20 leading 1-bits across 4 octets:**

- Octet 1: `11111111` = 255
- Octet 2: `11111111` = 255
- Octet 3: `11110000` = 240
- Octet 4: `00000000` = 0

**Step 2 — Assemble the mask:**

$$/20 = 255.255.240.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute block size:**

$$2^{(32-20)} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ addresses}$$

**Step 4 — Subnet boundaries (third octet step = 16):**

| Network | Range |
|---|---|
| `10.0.0.0/20` | `10.0.0.0` → `10.0.15.255` |
| `10.0.16.0/20` | `10.0.16.0` → `10.0.31.255` |
| `10.0.32.0/20` | `10.0.32.0` → `10.0.47.255` |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Outcomes

| Scenario | Matches Found | LPM Choice | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single `/24` matches | 1 | That entry | Normal forwarding |
| `/16` and `/24` both match | 2 | `/24` | Use most specific |
| `/16`, `/24`, `/25` all match | 3 | `/25` | Use most specific |
| No entry matches, default present | 0 | `/0` | Use default route |
| No entry matches, no default | 0 | None | Drop + ICMP unreachable |
| Destination is a host route | 1 | `/32` | Direct delivery |
| Multiple `/25` matches (overlap) | 2+ | Longest wins (tie-break by metric) | If tie, lower metric wins |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Forwarding Decision Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Packet Arrives at Router"] --> B["Extract Destination IP D"]
    B --> C["Initialize BestPrefix = -1 and BestEntry = null"]
    C --> D["Read next routing table entry"]
    D --> E["Compute Result = D AND Mask"]
    E --> F{"Result equals Network Address?"}
    F -->|"No"| D
    F -->|"Yes"| G["Calculate prefix length n from mask"]
    G --> H{"n greater than BestPrefix?"}
    H -->|"Yes"| I["Update BestPrefix = n and BestEntry = current"]
    H -->|"No"| J["Discard current entry"]
    I --> J
    J --> K{"More entries in table?"}
    K -->|"Yes"| D
    K -->|"No"| L{"BestPrefix equals -1?"}
    L -->|"Yes"| M["No match found"]
    L -->|"No"| N["Forward packet using BestEntry"]
    M --> O["Use default route or drop + ICMP"]
    O --> P["End of Process"]
    N --> P
```

### 4.2 Classless Routing Table Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Incoming["Incoming Datagram"]
        D1["Destination IP: 192.168.1.130"]
    end

    subgraph Table["Routing Table Entries"]
        T1["Entry1: 192.168.0.0/16 -> 10.0.0.1 via eth0"]
        T2["Entry2: 192.168.1.0/24 -> 10.0.0.2 via eth1"]
        T3["Entry3: 192.168.1.128/25 -> 10.0.0.3 via eth2"]
        T4["Default: 0.0.0.0/0 -> 10.0.0.254 via eth3"]
    end

    subgraph Engine["LPM Engine"]
        E1["AND with /16 mask"]
        E2["AND with /24 mask"]
        E3["AND with /25 mask"]
        E4["Longest Match Selector"]
    end

    subgraph Outgoing["Outgoing Decision"]
        O1["Selected: Entry3"]
        O2["Forward to 10.0.0.3 via eth2"]
    end

    D1 --> E1
    D1 --> E2
    D1 --> E3
    E1 --> E4
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E4
    T1 --> E1
    T2 --> E2
    T3 --> E3
    T4 --> E4
    E4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Supernetting Aggregation Concept (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Original["Original Four Networks /24"]
        N1["192.168.0.0/24"]
        N2["192.168.1.0/24"]
        N3["192.168.2.0/24"]
        N4["192.168.3.0/24"]
    end

    subgraph Common["Find Common Prefix"]
        C1["Third octet binary: 00, 01, 10, 11"]
        C2["First 6 bits common: 000000xx"]
    end

    subgraph Aggregated["Single Aggregated Entry"]
        A1["192.168.0.0/22"]
        A2["Covers 1024 addresses"]
    end

    N1 --> C1
    N2 --> C1
    N3 --> C1
    N4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
```

### 4.4 Mask-to-Bit-Length Reference Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["/0 : 0.0.0.0 : Default Route : 4294967296 addresses"]
    P8["/8 : 255.0.0.0 : Class A : 16777216 addresses"]
    P16["/16 : 255.255.0.0 : Class B : 65536 addresses"]
    P24["/24 : 255.255.255.0 : Class C : 256 addresses"]
    P25["/25 : 255.255.255.128 : Half-C : 128 addresses"]
    P30["/30 : 255.255.255.252 : Point-to-Point : 4 addresses"]
    P32["/32 : 255.255.255.255 : Host Route : 1 address"]
    P0 --> P8 --> P16 --> P24 --> P25 --> P30 --> P32
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define Classless Addressing. How does it differ from Classful Addressing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Classless Addressing (CIDR)** is an IP addressing scheme that uses **variable-length subnet masks** (VLSM) to allocate addresses in **arbitrary-sized blocks**, written in slash notation `/n`, where `n` denotes the number of network bits. It was introduced via **RFC 1518/1519 (1993)** to overcome the limitations of classful addressing.

**Differences (Table Format):**

| Feature | Classful | Classless |
|---|---|---|
| Boundary | Fixed (A/B/C) | Variable (`/n`) |
| Mask | Implicit (by class) | Explicit (configurable) |
| Aggregation | Not possible | **Supernetting supported** |
| Address Waste | High | **Minimized** |
| Forwarding Rule | First-bit match | **Longest Prefix Match** |

**(3 marks — Definition 1m, Comparison 2m)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**What is Longest Prefix Match? Why is it essential in classless forwarding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** is the rule used by routers in classless forwarding to select the routing table entry that has the **largest prefix length (most specific mask)** among all entries that match the destination IP address.

**Why essential:**
1. Multiple routing entries can simultaneously match a destination (due to overlapping aggregated and specific routes).
2. The most specific route always provides the **shortest, most accurate path**.
3. LPM guarantees **deterministic forwarding** and enables hierarchical routing (e.g., a default `/0` coexists with a specific `/25` customer route).

$$n_{\text{chosen}} = \max\{n_i \mid (D \; \text{AND} \; M_i) = N_i\}$$

**(3 marks — Definition 1m, Justification 2m)**

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** A router has the following routing table. A packet arrives with destination IP `180.70.65.140`. Apply the **classless forwarding algorithm** and determine the next hop and outgoing interface using the **Longest Prefix Match** rule. Show all binary calculations. **(7 marks)**

| Mask (in slash) | Network Address | Next Hop | Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/12` | 180.70.0.0 | 10.0.0.1 | m0 |
| `/16` | 180.70.0.0 | 10.0.0.2 | m1 |
| `/20` | 180.70.64.0 | 10.0.0.3 | m2 |
| `/24` | 180.70.65.128 | 10.0.0.4 | m3 |
| `/25` | 180.70.65.128 | 10.0.0.5 | m4 |

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of supernetting**. An organization is given four contiguous Class C networks: `200.100.48.0/24`, `200.100.49.0/24`, `200.100.50.0/24`, `200.100.51.0/24`. Aggregate them into a single supernet and find the new mask. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert destination `180.70.65.140` to binary (last two octets only):**

$$65 \rightarrow 01000001$$
$$140 \rightarrow 10001100$$

**Step 2 — Test each entry:**

**Entry 1: `/12` mask = `255.240.0.0`**
- AND of 2nd octet: $70 \; \text{AND} \; 240 = 01000110 \; \text{AND} \; 11110000 = 01000000 = 64$
- Result: `180.64.0.0`
- Compare with `180.70.0.0`: ✗ **NO MATCH**

**Entry 2: `/16` mask = `255.255.0.0`**
- Keeps first two octets: `180.70.0.0`
- Compare with `180.70.0.0`: ✓ **MATCH** (n = 16)

**Entry 3: `/20` mask = `255.255.240.0`**
- 3rd octet: $65 \; \text{AND} \; 240 = 01000001 \; \text{AND} \; 11110000 = 01000000 = 64$
- Result: `180.70.64.0`
- Compare with `180.70.64.0`: ✓ **MATCH** (n = 20)

**Entry 4: `/24` mask = `255.255.255.0`**
- Keeps first three octets: `180.70.65.0`
- Compare with `180.70.65.128`: ✗ **NO MATCH**

**Entry 5: `/25` mask = `255.255.255.128`**
- 4th octet: $140 \; \text{AND} \; 128 = 10001100 \; \text{AND} \; 10000000 = 10000000 = 128$
- Result: `180.70.65.128`
- Compare with `180.70.65.128`: ✓ **MATCH** (n = 25)

**Step 3 — Apply LPM:**

$$\text{Chosen } n = \max(16, 20, 25) = 25$$

**Step 4 — Forwarding Decision:**

- **Next Hop:** `10.0.0.5`
- **Interface:** `m4`

**(7 marks — 1m for binary conversion, 4m for entry tests, 1m for LPM selection, 1m for final decision)**

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Concept of Supernetting:**
Supernetting (CIDR aggregation) is the process of combining **multiple contiguous IP networks** with shorter individual prefixes into a **single routing advertisement** with a **longer common prefix** (shorter mask). This reduces routing table size and conserves address space.

**Block Diagram (text representation):**

```
Four Class C Networks          Aggregated Supernetwork
+----------------------+       +--------------------------+
| 200.100.48.0/24      |       |                          |
| 200.100.49.0/24      |  ==>  |  200.100.48.0/22         |
| 200.100.50.0/24      |       |  (Mask: 255.255.252.0)   |
| 200.100.51.0/24      |       |                          |
+----------------------+       +--------------------------+
        4 entries                      1 entry
```

**Calculation Steps:**

**Step 1 — Convert third octets to binary:**

$$48 \rightarrow 00110000$$
$$49 \rightarrow 00110001$$
$$50 \rightarrow 00110010$$
$$51 \rightarrow 00110011$$

**Step 2 — Identify common leading bits:**

The first **6 bits** (`001100`) are common. The last 2 bits vary: `00, 01, 10, 11`.

**Step 3 — Compute new prefix:**

$$n_{\text{new}} = 16 + 6 = 22 \Rightarrow /22$$

**Step 4 — Derive new mask:**

- Octet 1: `11111111` = 255
- Octet 2: `11111111` = 255
- Octet 3: `11111100` = 252
- Octet 4: `00000000` = 0

$$\text{New mask} = 255.255.252.0$$

**Step 5 — Block size verification:**

$$2^{(32-22)} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ addresses} = 4 \times 256 \;\checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** Aggregated supernet is `200.100.48.0/22` with mask `255.255.252.0`.

**(7 marks — 1m for concept, 1m for diagram, 3m for binary/common-bit analysis, 2m for final mask and address block verification)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Define **CIDR**. Explain the **format of a classless address** with an example. A company is allotted a block `200.10.10.0/24`. The company needs **four subnets** of equal size. Design the subnets and list the **first host, last host, and broadcast address** of each subnet. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Classful and Classless forwarding** with suitable examples. A packet arrives at a router with destination `10.5.7.3`. The routing table contains:
- `10.0.0.0/8` → Interface 0
- `10.5.0.0/16` → Interface 1
- `10.5.7.0/24` → Interface 2
- `10.5.7.0/25` → Interface 3

Determine the matching interface. Justify the choice. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Definition:** CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) is a method of IP addressing that uses **variable-length subnet masks** to allocate IP address space in blocks of any size, written in slash notation `/n` where `n` indicates the network prefix length.

**Format of Classless Address:**

$$\underbrace{\text{XXXX.XXXX.XXXX.XXXX}}_{\text{Prefix bits (n)}} \; / \; \underbrace{\text{YYYY...Y}}_{\text{Suffix bits (32-n)}}$$

**Example:** `192.168.1.0/26`
- Prefix: First 26 bits
- Suffix: Last 6 bits
- Mask: `255.255.255.192`

**Sub netting of `200.10.10.0/24` into 4 equal subnets:**

**Step 1 — Compute extra bits needed:**

$$2^x \geq 4 \Rightarrow x = 2$$

**Step 2 — New prefix length:**

$$n_{\text{new}} = 24 + 2 = /26$$

**Step 3 — Block size per subnet:**

$$2^{(32-26)} = 2^6 = 64 \text{ addresses per subnet}$$

**Step 4 — Subnet Design Table:**

| Subnet | Network | First Host | Last Host | Broadcast |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Subnet 1 | `200.10.10.0/26` | `200.10.10.1` | `200.10.10.62` | `200.10.10.63` |
| Subnet 2 | `200.10.10.64/26` | `200.10.10.65` | `200.10.10.126` | `200.10.10.127` |
| Subnet 3 | `200.10.10.128/26` | `200.10.10.129` | `200.10.10.190` | `200.10.10.191` |
| Subnet 4 | `200.10.10.192/26` | `200.10.10.193` | `200.10.10.254` | `200.10.10.255` |

**(7 marks — 1m CIDR def, 1m format, 5m subnet calculation table)**

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Differences Table:**

| Parameter | Classful Forwarding | Classless Forwarding |
|---|---|---|
| Mask | Determined by address class | Explicit, user-defined |
| Network Bits | Fixed per class | Variable (`/n`) |
| Match Rule | First-bit pattern match | **Longest Prefix Match** |
| Aggregation | Not possible | **Supernetting supported** |
| Address Efficiency | Low | High |
| Routing Table Size | Large (no aggregation) | Small (aggregation reduces entries) |
| Example | `10.0.0.0` is always `/8` | `10.0.0.0/12` allowed |

**Forwarding Calculation for `10.5.7.3`:**

| Entry | Mask | Computation | Match? | Prefix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `10.0.0.0/8` | `255.0.0.0` | `10.0.0.0` | ✓ | 8 |
| `10.5.0.0/16` | `255.255.0.0` | `10.5.0.0` | ✓ | 16 |
| `10.5.7.0/24` | `255.255.255.0` | `10.5.7.0` | ✓ | 24 |
| `10.5.7.0/25` | `255.255.255.128` | `10.5.7.0` (since `3 AND 128 = 0`) | ✓ | 25 |

**LPM Selection:**

$$n_{\text{chosen}} = \max(8, 16, 24, 25) = 25$$

**Decision: Packet is forwarded to Interface 3.**

**Justification:** All four entries match the destination `10.5.7.3`, but the `/25` entry is the **most specific** (longest prefix). It defines a sub-network `10.5.7.0`–`10.5.7.127`, which precisely contains the destination `10.5.7.3`. The router must select the most specific route to honor the LPM rule.

**(7 marks — 3m comparison, 3m forwarding table, 1m final decision + justification)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the LPM rule** — Students often pick the **first** matching entry. Always apply LPM, not first-match. (-2 to -3 marks)
> 2. **Binary conversion errors** — When applying the mask, mistakes in AND operation are the most common. Always show binary work explicitly. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Off-by-one in subnet design** — Confusing "first usable host" with "network address". Network address and broadcast address are **not** assignable to hosts. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 4. **Confusing `/n` with subnet bits vs host bits** — `/n` denotes **network bits**, not subnets. Use $2^{(32-n)}$ for total addresses. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the default route case** — When asked "what if no match?", mention the default route (`0.0.0.0/0`) and ICMP behavior. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Aggregation mistake** — Finding common bits in the **wrong octet**. Always start from the **leftmost** bit of the differing octet. (-2 marks)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Forwarding with Classless Addressing**
>
> - **Classless addressing = CIDR = slash notation `/n`**; no concept of A/B/C classes in forwarding.
> - **Mask tells how many leading bits are network bits.** `/n` mask has `n` ones and `(32-n)` zeros.
> - **LPM (Longest Prefix Match) is THE rule.** Never pick the first match. Always pick the most specific.
> - **Forwarding algorithm = AND with mask, compare with network, select longest match.**
> - **Default route is `0.0.0.0/0`** with prefix length zero; it matches every destination.
> - **Host route is `a.b.c.d/32`** with prefix length 32; the most specific possible.
> - **Number of addresses in `/n` = $2^{(32-n)}$**; usable hosts = $2^{(32-n)} - 2$.
> - **Supernetting = aggregation** of contiguous blocks into a shorter prefix; ISPs use this heavily.
> - **Number of networks aggregated = $2^{(n_{\text{old}} - n_{\text{new}})}$** when `n_old > n_new`.
> - **VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)** = subnets of different sizes within a single parent block.
> - **No match + no default = packet dropped** + **ICMP Destination Unreachable** sent to source.
> - **Modern routers use TCAM hardware** for O(1) LPM lookups; software LPM uses trie-based algorithms.
> - **Block boundaries** in classless addressing are always multiples of the block size `2^{(32-n)}`.
> - **CIDR was introduced in RFC 1518/1519 (1993)** to delay IPv4 exhaustion.
> - **Conversion trick:** `/n` mask = `255.255.255.{(256 - 2^{(32-n) \mod 8}) \mod 256}` for the relevant octets.
> - **KTU focus areas:** numerical LPM problems, aggregation, VLSM design, and binary calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Address Aggregation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Address Aggregation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Address Aggregation** (also called **Supernetting** or **Route Aggregation** or **Classless Inter-Domain Routing - CIDR Aggregation**) is the process of combining multiple contiguous IP network prefixes into a single, shorter prefix that represents the entire aggregate block. It is the mathematical inverse of **subnetting**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, address aggregation is a Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR) technique used by Internet Service Providers (ISPs) and Autonomous Systems (AS) to advertise a single summarized route to upstream routers instead of announcing many individual subnet routes.

The **aggregate address** is constructed by:
1. Aligning all member network addresses to a common power-of-two boundary.
2. Identifying the longest common prefix (leftmost bits) shared by all member networks.
3. Setting the prefix length to the position of the first differing bit.

The **block size** of the aggregate is given by:

$$N = 2^{(n - k)}$$

where:
- $N$ = total number of classful/classless addresses in the aggregate block
- $n$ = total number of bits in the address (32 for IPv4, 128 for IPv6)
- $k$ = length of the common prefix (in bits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Address Aggregation belongs to Module 1 of PEITT522 and is directly tied to **CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing)**. Questions typically test the ability to (a) compute the aggregate prefix from a list of given networks, and (b) determine the valid range of the aggregate block.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **postal system**. Instead of writing separate street addresses for *House 1, House 2, House 3, ... House 250* on a colony road, you simply write:

> **"Sunshine Colony, Sector 47"**

The postman knows that Sector 47 contains all 250 houses, so a single, summarized destination (the aggregate) replaces hundreds of individual addresses (the individual routes).

In IP routing:
- The **postal database** = the router's **Routing Table**.
- The **individual house addresses** = individual subnet routes (e.g., `200.10.1.0/24`, `200.10.2.0/24`, ...).
- The **colony name** = the **aggregated prefix** (e.g., `200.10.0.0/16`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we aggregate?** The global Internet routing table currently exceeds **900,000+ entries** (as of 2024, approaching **1,000,000**). Without aggregation, BGP routers would run out of memory and CPU. Aggregation keeps the table small, reduces routing updates, and speeds up **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** lookups.

## 1.3 Boundary Conditions and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **IPv4 Address Width** | **32 bits** | Dotted-decimal: A.B.C.D |
| **IPv6 Address Width** | **128 bits** | Hex colon-decimal |
| **Default CIDR boundary** | **/8, /16, /24** | Classful octet boundaries |
| **Smallest aggregate block** | **/30 (IPv4)** | 4 addresses (2 usable hosts) |
| **Largest aggregate block** | **/0 (Default Route)** | The entire Internet |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember: a **shorter prefix** (fewer bits after the slash) means a **larger block** (more addresses). Aggregation *shortens* the prefix to *enlarge* the represented network.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Aggregation

Aggregation is performed using a bitwise **AND** operation on a set of network addresses. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Step 1 — List all member networks** in binary form (32 bits for IPv4).
2. **Step 2 — Align them vertically** so that bit positions line up.
3. **Step 3 — Find the Common Prefix**: Scan from the **leftmost bit (MSB)** and find the first position where the bits differ across all listed networks.
4. **Step 4 — Set the prefix length** $k$ = number of common bits from the left.
5. **Step 5 — Construct the aggregate**: Copy the common bits; zero out the remaining bits.
6. **Step 6 — Express in CIDR notation**: `aggregate_ip / k`.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why bitwise comparison?** Routers identify networks by their bit pattern. Two networks are aggregatable only if their bit patterns share a left-aligned run of identical bits.
- **Why does the first differing bit end the prefix?** Once bits differ, networks can no longer be represented by a single, deterministic prefix without ambiguity.
- **Why zero out the rest?** A network address must have all host bits set to 0. The aggregate is a *network address*, not a host address.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Contiguity:** The member networks being aggregated **must be contiguous** (form a single, unbroken power-of-two block). You cannot aggregate `200.10.0.0/24` and `200.10.2.0/24` into a `/22` because `200.10.1.0/24` is missing — doing so would create a "black hole" for traffic destined to `200.10.1.x`.

## 2.2 The "Supernetting" Relationship to Subnetting

Aggregation (supernetting) is the **reverse** of subnetting:

| Operation | Direction | Prefix Length | Block Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Subnetting** | Large network → Small subnets | Increases (e.g., /16 → /24) | Decreases |
| **Supernetting/Aggregation** | Small networks → Large aggregate | Decreases (e.g., /24 → /16) | Increases |

> [!NOTE]
> In subnetting, we **borrow** host bits to create network bits. In supernetting, we **borrow** network bits from the classful boundary to combine networks. The **Classful boundary** (octet boundary for IPv4) is the natural "anchor" for these calculations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aggregate Block Size | $N = 2^{(n - k)}$ | $n$ = address width, $k$ = prefix length | addresses |
| Number of /m nets in /k agg | $2^{(m - k)}$ | $m$ = member prefix, $k$ = aggregate prefix | networks |
| Subnet Mask | $M = 2^{32-k} - 1$ (inverted) | $k$ = prefix | dotted decimal |
| Hosts per subnet | $H = 2^{h} - 2$ | $h$ = host bits | usable hosts |
| First usable address | Network + 1 | — | dotted decimal |
| Last usable address | Broadcast - 1 | — | dotted decimal |
| Broadcast address | Network + (block size - 1) | — | dotted decimal |
| Aggregate Range Check | $\text{Network}_{\text{agg}} \le \text{Member} \le \text{Broadcast}_{\text{agg}}$ | — | validity test |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** When given an aggregate `A.B.C.D/k` and asked "is `W.X.Y.Z/m` inside the aggregate?", compute the network address of the member and the network address of the aggregate. If the member's network has its first $k$ bits identical to the aggregate's first $k$ bits, it is contained within the aggregate.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol):** ISPs use aggregation to advertise a single summary route to peers, dramatically reducing the global routing table size.
- **Hierarchical Routing:** Large enterprise networks (e.g., a university campus with 50 buildings) use aggregation at the core router to summarize all building subnets into one route.
- **Geographic Summarization:** A country-level ISP might aggregate all its city-level prefixes into a single country-level announcement.
- **IPv6 Deployment:** Aggregation is *mandatory* in IPv6. The IANA allocates `/12` blocks to Regional Internet Registries (RIRs), which allocate `/32` blocks to ISPs, which allocate `/48` blocks to end-sites. Each level aggregates upward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without aggregation, the Internet **does not scale**. This is a fundamental design principle and is regularly tested in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Aggregating Four /24 Networks

**Problem:** Aggregate the following four networks into a single prefix:
- `200.10.1.0/24`
- `200.10.2.0/24`
- `200.10.3.0/24`
- `200.10.4.0/24`

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert each network to 32-bit binary.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
200.10.1.0/24 &= 11001000.00001010.00000001.00000000 \\
200.10.2.0/24 &= 11001000.00001010.00000010.00000000 \\
200.10.3.0/24 &= 11001000.00001010.00000011.00000000 \\
200.10.4.0/24 &= 11001000.00001010.00000100.00000000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Align and find the common prefix.**

Reading from left to right (MSB to LSB), the bits match until we reach the **third octet**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&11001000.00001010.000000 \color{red}{0}1.00000000 \\
&11001000.00001010.000000 \color{red}{1}0.00000000 \\
&11001000.00001010.000000 \color{red}{1}1.00000000 \\
&11001000.00001010.000001 \color{red}{0}0.00000000
\end{aligned}
$$

The first 22 bits are identical: `11001000 00001010 000000`.

**Step 3 — Set the prefix length.**

The first differing bit is at position **23** (counting from 1). So the common prefix length $k = 22$.

**Step 4 — Construct the aggregate.**

Take the first 22 bits and pad with zeros:

$$
\text{Aggregate} = 11001000.00001010.00000000.00000000
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dotted-decimal.**

$$
\text{Aggregate} = 200.10.0.0/22
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the block size.**

$$
N = 2^{(32-22)} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ addresses}
$$

This covers `200.10.0.0` through `200.10.3.255`. Wait — let us check if `200.10.4.0/24` is inside. The aggregate `/22` block aligned at the `/22` boundary starting at `200.10.0.0` covers `200.10.0.0` – `200.10.3.255`. Since `200.10.4.0/24` is *outside* this block, we need to align to the next `/22` boundary.

The next `/22` aligned block is `200.10.4.0/22` (covering `200.10.4.0` – `200.10.7.255`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget to check **CIDR alignment**. The aggregate must start on a boundary that is a multiple of the block size. `200.10.0.0/22` covers 4 × `/24` = `200.10.0.0/24`, `200.10.1.0/24`, `200.10.2.0/24`, `200.10.3.0/24`. `200.10.4.0/24` is in the **next** `/22` block.

To aggregate all four, we need a `/21`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Aggregate} &= 200.10.0.0/21 \\
N &= 2^{(32-21)} = 2^{11} = 2048 \text{ addresses} \\
\text{Range} &= 200.10.0.0 \text{ to } 200.10.7.255
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Range Verification

**Problem:** Given the aggregate `192.168.0.0/20`, determine which of the following member networks fall within it:
- `192.168.0.0/22`
- `192.168.4.0/22`
- `192.168.8.0/22`
- `192.168.16.0/22`

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the block size of the aggregate.**

$$
N = 2^{(32-20)} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ addresses}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the range of the aggregate.**

The aggregate starts at `192.168.0.0`. The next `/20` boundary is at `192.168.16.0`.

$$
\text{Aggregate Range} = 192.168.0.0 \text{ to } 192.168.15.255
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each member's range.**

| Member | Network | Block Size | Range | Inside? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `192.168.0.0/22` | `192.168.0.0` | $2^{10} = 1024$ | `0.0` – `3.255` | ✅ Yes |
| `192.168.4.0/22` | `192.168.4.0` | 1024 | `4.0` – `7.255` | ✅ Yes |
| `192.168.8.0/22` | `192.168.8.0` | 1024 | `8.0` – `11.255` | ✅ Yes |
| `192.168.16.0/22` | `192.168.16.0` | 1024 | `16.0` – `19.255` | ❌ No |

**Step 4 — Verification via bitwise AND.**

For `192.168.8.0/22` to be inside `192.168.0.0/20`, its first 20 bits must match the aggregate's first 20 bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
192.168.0.0/20 \text{ in binary (first 20 bits):} &= 11000000.10101000.0000 \\
192.168.8.0/22 \text{ in binary (first 20 bits):} &= 11000000.10101000.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ Match — `192.168.8.0/22` is inside `192.168.0.0/20`.

For `192.168.16.0/22`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
192.168.16.0/22 \text{ in binary (first 20 bits):} &= 11000000.10101000.0001
\end{aligned}
$$

❌ Mismatch at bit 21 — `192.168.16.0/22` is **not** inside the aggregate.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Reverse Engineering: Finding Networks Inside an Aggregate

**Problem:** How many `/27` networks are inside `172.16.0.0/16`?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the host bits difference.**

The aggregate has prefix length 16. The member subnets have prefix length 27. The number of additional bits used for subnetting is:

$$
\Delta k = 27 - 16 = 11 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of sub-networks.**

$$
\text{Number of /27 nets} = 2^{\Delta k} = 2^{11} = 2048
$$

**Step 3 — Verify by block size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Aggregate size} &= 2^{32-16} = 2^{16} = 65536 \\
\text{Each /27 size} &= 2^{32-27} = 2^{5} = 32 \\
\text{Count} &= \frac{65536}{32} = 2048 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Alignment

| Aggregate Prefix | Block Size | Aligned Start Multiples | Aligned Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/8` | $2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ | `x.0.0.0` | A.0.0.0 – A.255.255.255 |
| `/16` | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | `x.y.0.0` | A.B.0.0 – A.B.255.255 |
| `/20` | $2^{12} = 4096$ | `x.y.z.0` where $z \in \{0,16,32,...,240\}$ | A.B.Z.0 – A.B.(Z+15).255 |
| `/22` | $2^{10} = 1024$ | `x.y.z.0` where $z \in \{0,4,8,...,252\}$ | A.B.Z.0 – A.B.(Z+3).255 |
| `/24` | $2^{8} = 256$ | `x.y.z.0` | A.B.C.0 – A.B.C.255 |
| `/27` | $2^{5} = 32$ | `x.y.z.N` where $N \in \{0,32,64,...,224\}$ | A.B.C.N – A.B.C.(N+31) |
| `/30` | $2^{2} = 4$ | `x.y.z.N` where $N \in \{0,4,8,...,252\}$ | A.B.C.N – A.B.C.(N+3) |

> [!TIP]
> **General Rule for Alignment:** An aggregate `A.B.C.D/k` is properly aligned if the last $(32 - k)$ bits of `A.B.C.D` are all zero. If not, you must round **down** to the nearest valid boundary.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Address Aggregation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: List of Member Networks] --> B[Convert to 32-bit Binary]
    B --> C[Vertical Bit Alignment]
    C --> D{Find Longest Common Prefix}
    D -->|Common Bits Found| E[Set Prefix Length k]
    D -->|No Common Bits| F[Error: Cannot Aggregate]
    E --> G[Zero Out Remaining Host Bits]
    G --> H[Convert Back to Dotted Decimal]
    H --> I{Validate Block Alignment}
    I -->|Aligned| J[Output: Aggregate Network/Prefix]
    I -->|Misaligned| K[Round Down to Boundary]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Verify: Check Member Containment]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#15803d,stroke:#052e16,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#450a0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#2e1065,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 CIDR Aggregation Hierarchy in the Internet

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IANA["IANA - Global Allocation"] -->|Allocates /8 to /12 blocks| RIR1["RIR - APNIC / ARIN / RIPE"]
    RIR1 -->|Allocates /16 to /22 blocks| ISP1["National ISP"]
    ISP1 -->|Allocates /24 to /27 blocks| ENT1["Enterprise / University"]
    ENT1 -->|Subnets into /28 to /30 blocks| SUB1["Departmental LANs"]

    IANA -.->|Announces Aggregate| BGP1["BGP Table Entry 1"]
    RIR1 -.->|Announces Aggregate| BGP2["BGP Table Entry 2"]
    ISP1 -.->|Announces Aggregate| BGP3["BGP Table Entry 3"]
    ENT1 -.->|Internal Routing Only| BGPX["Not in Global BGP"]

    style IANA fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style RIR1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style ISP1 fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style ENT1 fill:#15803d,stroke:#052e16,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style SUB1 fill:#a16207,stroke:#422006,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style BGP1 fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#2e1065,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style BGP2 fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#2e1065,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style BGP3 fill:#4c1d95,stroke:#2e1065,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style BGPX fill:#52525b,stroke:#27272a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Aggregation Process Bit Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N1["Network 1: 200.10.0.0/24"]
        A1[11001000.00001010.00000000.00000000]
    end
    subgraph N2["Network 2: 200.10.1.0/24"]
        A2[11001000.00001010.00000001.00000000]
    end
    subgraph N3["Network 3: 200.10.2.0/24"]
        A3[11001000.00001010.00000010.00000000]
    end
    subgraph N4["Network 4: 200.10.3.0/24"]
        A4[11001000.00001010.00000011.00000000]
    end

    A1 --> CMP{Bitwise AND and Compare}
    A2 --> CMP
    A3 --> CMP
    A4 --> CMP
    CMP -->|Common Prefix = 22 bits| AGG[200.10.0.0/22]
    AGG --> RANGE[Range: 200.10.0.0 to 200.10.3.255]
    RANGE --> OUT[Valid Aggregate Block]

    style CMP fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#450a0a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style AGG fill:#15803d,stroke:#052e16,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style RANGE fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#2e1065,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Subnetting vs Supernetting — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["Classful Network: 200.10.0.0/16 (65,536 addresses)"] --> SPLIT{Subnetting}
    TOP --> COMB{Supernetting Aggregation}

    SPLIT --> S1[200.10.0.0/17 - 32,768 addrs]
    SPLIT --> S2[200.10.128.0/17 - 32,768 addrs]
    S1 --> S1A[200.10.0.0/18 - 16,384 addrs]
    S1 --> S1B[200.10.64.0/18 - 16,384 addrs]

    COMB --> C1[200.10.0.0/24]
    COMB --> C2[200.10.1.0/24]
    COMB --> C3[200.10.2.0/24]
    COMB --> C4[200.10.3.0/24]
    C1 --> RES[200.10.0.0/22 - 1,024 addrs]
    C2 --> RES
    C3 --> RES
    C4 --> RES
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Address Aggregation. Why is it essential in modern IP routing?**

**Model Answer:**

Address Aggregation (also called Supernetting or CIDR aggregation) is the process of combining multiple contiguous IP network prefixes into a single, shorter prefix that represents the entire set of networks. For example, four `/24` networks `10.0.0.0/24`, `10.0.1.0/24`, `10.0.2.0/24`, and `10.0.3.0/24` can be aggregated into the single route `10.0.0.0/22`.

It is essential in modern IP routing because:

1. **Reduces Routing Table Size:** The global BGP table contains over 900,000 routes. Aggregation allows ISPs to advertise a single summary route, dramatically shrinking the table.
2. **Decreases Routing Update Overhead:** Fewer routes mean fewer routing updates propagated across the Internet.
3. **Improves Router Performance:** Smaller tables mean faster Longest Prefix Match (LPM) lookups, reducing latency.
4. **Enables Hierarchical Addressing:** It supports the IANA → RIR → ISP → End-Site allocation hierarchy, ensuring scalability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** Address aggregation is the **inverse of subnetting**. Subnetting increases prefix length (splits a large network), while aggregation decreases prefix length (combines small networks).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of the "longest common prefix" in address aggregation with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **longest common prefix** is the foundation of address aggregation. It represents the maximum number of leftmost (most significant) bits that are **identical** across all member networks being aggregated. This common prefix becomes the prefix length of the aggregate route.

**Example:**

Networks to aggregate:
- `192.168.0.0/24` = `11000000.10101000.00000000.00000000`
- `192.168.1.0/24` = `11000000.10101000.00000001.00000000`
- `192.168.2.0/24` = `11000000.10101000.00000010.00000000`
- `192.168.3.0/24` = `11000000.10101000.00000011.00000000`

Scanning from the left, the first 22 bits are identical (`11000000.10101000.000000`). The 23rd bit differs. Hence the **longest common prefix is 22 bits**, and the aggregate is `192.168.0.0/22`.

The first differing bit determines where the prefix ends because beyond that point, the networks have unique, non-overlapping identities that cannot be represented by a single prefix without ambiguity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Question A (a)** [7 Marks] — *Aggregate the following four networks into a single CIDR block. Show all binary conversion and prefix calculation steps:*
- `10.20.0.0/24`
- `10.20.1.0/24`
- `10.20.2.0/24`
- `10.20.3.0/24`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert each network to 32-bit binary.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10.20.0.0/24 &= 00001010.00010100.00000000.00000000 \\
10.20.1.0/24 &= 00001010.00010100.00000001.00000000 \\
10.20.2.0/24 &= 00001010.00010100.00000010.00000000 \\
10.20.3.0/24 &= 00001010.00010100.00000011.00000000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the longest common prefix.**

Comparing bit-by-bit, the first 22 bits are identical across all four addresses:

`00001010 . 00010100 . 000000` (first 22 bits common)

**Step 3 — Determine the prefix length.**

The first difference occurs at the 23rd bit. Therefore $k = 22$.

**Step 4 — Construct the aggregate.**

Take the common 22 bits and append 10 zeros (since $32 - 22 = 10$ host bits):

$$
00001010.00010100.00000000.00000000
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dotted decimal.**

$$
\text{Aggregate} = 10.20.0.0/22
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the block size and range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 2^{32-22} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ addresses} \\
\text{Range} &= 10.20.0.0 \text{ to } 10.20.3.255 \\
\text{Broadcast} &= 10.20.3.255
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify each member is inside.**

- `10.20.0.0/24` covers `10.20.0.0` – `10.20.0.255` → ✅ Inside
- `10.20.1.0/24` covers `10.20.1.0` – `10.20.1.255` → ✅ Inside
- `10.20.2.0/24` covers `10.20.2.0` – `10.20.2.255` → ✅ Inside
- `10.20.3.0/24` covers `10.20.3.0` – `10.20.3.255` → ✅ Inside

✅ **Final Answer: Aggregate = `10.20.0.0/22`**

---

**Question A (b)** [7 Marks] — *An organization holds the aggregate block `172.16.0.0/16`. The network administrator needs to create subnets of size `/20`. Calculate: (i) the number of `/20` subnets possible, (ii) the network address and broadcast address of the 5th `/20` subnet, and (iii) the number of usable hosts per `/20` subnet.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of /20 subnets possible:**

The aggregate has prefix length 16, and each subnet has prefix length 20.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Additional bits borrowed} &= 20 - 16 = 4 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Number of /20 subnets} &= 2^4 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Network and broadcast of the 5th /20 subnet:**

The 5th subnet (using 1-based indexing: 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, **5th**) corresponds to subnet index 4 (using 0-based indexing).

Each `/20` subnet has size $2^{32-20} = 2^{12} = 4096$ addresses.

The 5th subnet starts at offset $4 \times 4096 = 16384$ addresses from `172.16.0.0`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset in 3rd octet} &= \frac{16384}{256} = 64 \\
\text{5th subnet Network} &= 172.16.64.0 \\
\text{5th subnet Broadcast} &= 172.16.64.0 + 4095 = 172.16.79.255
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification:
- Network: `172.16.64.0` (host bits all zero ✅)
- Broadcast: `172.16.79.255` (host bits all one ✅)
- Range: `172.16.64.0` to `172.16.79.255` (4096 addresses ✅)

**(iii) Usable hosts per /20 subnet:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 32 - 20 = 12 \text{ host bits} \\
\text{Usable hosts} &= 2^{12} - 2 = 4096 - 2 = 4094
\end{aligned}
$$

(The 2 reserved addresses are the network address and the broadcast address.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Question B (a)** [7 Marks] — *A service provider owns four contiguous Class C networks: `200.100.0.0/24`, `200.100.1.0/24`, `200.100.2.0/24`, and `200.100.3.0/24`. The provider wishes to advertise a single aggregate route to its upstream provider. Perform address aggregation and provide the aggregate CIDR block. Justify why this aggregation is valid.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to binary.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
200.100.0.0/24 &= 11001000.01100100.00000000.00000000 \\
200.100.1.0/24 &= 11001000.01100100.00000001.00000000 \\
200.100.2.0/24 &= 11001000.01100100.00000010.00000000 \\
200.100.3.0/24 &= 11001000.01100100.00000011.00000000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify common prefix.**

The first 22 bits are identical across all four: `11001000 01100100 000000`.

**Step 3 — Set prefix length.**

First difference at bit 23, so $k = 22$.

**Step 4 — Construct aggregate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Aggregate} &= 200.100.0.0/22 \\
\text{Block size} &= 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ addresses} \\
\text{Range} &= 200.100.0.0 \text{ to } 200.100.3.255
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification for Validity:**

This aggregation is valid because:

1. **Contiguity:** The four networks form a single, unbroken power-of-two block. `200.100.0.0/24`, `200.100.1.0/24`, `200.100.2.0/24`, `200.100.3.0/24` are exactly the four `/24` networks that make up one `/22` block.
2. **Boundary Alignment:** The starting address `200.100.0.0` is properly aligned to a `/22` boundary (last 10 bits are zero).
3. **Complete Coverage:** The aggregate covers exactly the four member networks with no gaps and no extra networks.
4. **No Ambiguity:** The Longest Prefix Match (LPM) lookup will correctly forward traffic: a more specific `/24` route is preferred over the `/22` aggregate when both exist, but external routers need only know the `/22`.

**Step 5 — Practical Benefit.**

The upstream provider now maintains **1 entry** (`200.100.0.0/22`) instead of **4 entries**, reducing its routing table by **75%** for this prefix.

✅ **Final Answer: `200.100.0.0/22`**

---

**Question B (b)** [7 Marks] — *Given the aggregate `192.168.0.0/20`, verify whether each of the following member networks lies inside the aggregate. Show your calculation:*
- `192.168.5.0/24`
- `192.168.12.0/24`
- `192.168.15.0/24`
- `192.168.20.0/24`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine the aggregate range.**

The aggregate `192.168.0.0/20` has block size $2^{32-20} = 4096$.

The next `/20` boundary is at `192.168.16.0` (since $16 \times 256 = 4096$).

$$
\text{Aggregate Range} = 192.168.0.0 \text{ to } 192.168.15.255
$$

**Step 2 — Test each member.**

We can test using the rule: a network `M` is inside aggregate `A/k` if the first $k$ bits of `M` match the first $k$ bits of `A`. Equivalently, the integer value of `M` must lie within the aggregate's range.

**(i) `192.168.5.0/24`:**
- Range: `192.168.5.0` to `192.168.5.255`
- Inside `192.168.0.0` to `192.168.15.255`? ✅ **Yes**

**(ii) `192.168.12.0/24`:**
- Range: `192.168.12.0` to `192.168.12.255`
- Inside `192.168.0.0` to `192.168.15.255`? ✅ **Yes**

**(iii) `192.168.15.0/24`:**
- Range: `192.168.15.0` to `192.168.15.255`
- Inside `192.168.0.0` to `192.168.15.255`? ✅ **Yes** (the last valid network in the aggregate)

**(iv) `192.168.20.0/24`:**
- Range: `192.168.20.0` to `192.168.20.255`
- Inside `192.168.0.0` to `192.168.15.255`? ❌ **No** (this is in the *next* `/20` block, which is `192.168.16.0/20`)

**Step 3 — Summary table.**

| Member | Network | Aggregate Range | Inside? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `192.168.5.0/24` | `192.168.5.0` | `192.168.0.0` – `192.168.15.255` | ✅ Yes |
| `192.168.12.0/24` | `192.168.12.0` | `192.168.0.0` – `192.168.15.255` | ✅ Yes |
| `192.168.15.0/24` | `192.168.15.0` | `192.168.0.0` – `192.168.15.255` | ✅ Yes |
| `192.168.20.0/24` | `192.168.20.0` | `192.168.0.0` – `192.168.15.255` | ❌ No |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting Block Boundaries:** Many students write `192.168.0.0/20` covers up to `192.168.255.255`. **It does not!** It only covers up to `192.168.15.255`. Always compute the block size $2^{32-k}$ and verify the upper bound.
> 2. **Confusing Subnetting with Aggregation:** In subnetting, we *split*; in aggregation, we *combine*. A `/20` aggregate is **larger** than a `/24` subnet but **smaller** than a `/16` network.
> 3. **Ignoring Alignment:** Aggregating `10.0.1.0/24` and `10.0.2.0/24` into a `/23` gives `10.0.0.0/23` (covering `10.0.0.0` – `10.0.1.255`), not `10.0.1.0/23`. The aggregate always starts on a CIDR-aligned boundary.
> 4. **Forgetting Longest Prefix Match (LPM):** If both `10.0.0.0/22` and `10.0.1.0/24` exist in a routing table, a packet to `10.0.1.5` uses the **/24** route (longest prefix), not the /22.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Address Aggregation**

- **Definition:** Combining multiple contiguous IP prefixes into one shorter prefix (Supernetting / CIDR Aggregation).
- **Inverse of Subnetting:** Subnetting increases prefix length; Aggregation decreases prefix length.
- **Core Formula:** Block size $N = 2^{n - k}$ where $n = 32$ (IPv4) or $128$ (IPv6).
- **Longest Common Prefix:** Find the first bit position (from MSB) where member networks differ; the prefix length is the number of bits *before* that position.
- **Contiguity Rule:** Member networks must be **contiguous** and form a single power-of-two block. No gaps allowed.
- **Boundary Alignment:** The aggregate's network address must have all host bits set to **zero** (CIDR-aligned).
- **Verification:** A member `M` is inside aggregate `A/k` if `M`'s first $k$ bits match `A`'s first $k$ bits.
- **Number of /m nets in /k agg:** $2^{m - k}$ (only valid when $m > k$).
- **Usable Hosts:** $2^{h} - 2$ where $h = 32 - k$ (subtract 1 for network, 1 for broadcast).
- **BGP Use Case:** ISPs aggregate to reduce the global Internet routing table from millions of entries to a manageable size.
- **Hierarchical Allocation:** IANA → RIR → ISP → End-Site aggregation is what makes Internet routing scalable.
- **Memorize Common Block Sizes:**
  - `/20` = 4096 addresses
  - `/22` = 1024 addresses
  - `/24` = 256 addresses
  - `/27` = 32 addresses
  - `/30` = 4 addresses (2 usable hosts)
- **Most-tested KTU variants:**
  1. Aggregate 4 or 8 given `/24` networks into a `/22` or `/21`.
  2. Determine if a given subnet is inside an aggregate.
  3. Compute the number of subnets of a given size that fit in an aggregate.
  4. State the first/last usable address and broadcast address of the aggregate.
- **Always show binary conversion in KTU exams** — full credit requires the bitwise derivation, not just the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Longest Mask Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Longest Mask Matching # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Longest Mask Matching (LMM)**, also referred to as the **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** rule, is the fundamental forwarding algorithm used by IP routers to select the most specific entry from the routing table when multiple entries match a destination IP address. The router compares the destination IP address against the network prefixes in its routing table and forwards the packet to the next-hop associated with the entry that has the **longest subnet mask (largest prefix length)**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this concept is central to **Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)** operation, where variable-length subnet masks (VLSM) generate overlapping address aggregates. The router's forwarding plane **MUST** implement LMM to guarantee deterministic, loop-free forwarding. ### Key Terminology - **Prefix Length:** The number of leading contiguous **1** bits in the subnet mask (e.g., `/24`, `/19`). - **Network Prefix:** The first $N$ bits (where $N$ is the prefix length) of the IP address that define the network. - **Most Specific Route:** The route with the highest prefix length (i.e., the smallest block of addresses). - **Default Route:** The route with prefix length **0** (`/0`); matches every destination, used only as a last resort. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Postal Analogy:** Imagine you send a letter addressed to "Kerala, India." The postal system has routing entries for "India" (prefix length: 0 — matches everything), "Kerala, India" (prefix length: small — matches fewer addresses), and "Room 204, CS Block, CET, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, India" (longest prefix — matches the most specific location). A smart postal system will always deliver to the **most specific** address. **Longest Mask Matching is exactly this logic inside an IP router.** ### Why "Longest" and Not "Shortest"? A **shorter mask** covers a **larger** address space (e.g., `/8` = 16,777,216 addresses). A **longer mask** covers a **smaller**, more specific address space (e.g., `/30` = 4 addresses). When multiple rules apply, the network engineer who configured the table intended the more specific rule to take precedence. The longest mask is therefore the **most specific match**. ## 1.3 The Decision Hierarchy > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Mnemonic — "LONGEST FIRST, MOST SPECIFIC WINS"** > 1. Scan the routing table for ALL entries where `Destination AND Mask == Entry's Network Address`. > 2. From this matched set, select the entry with the **maximum prefix length**. > 3. Tie-breaker (if same prefix length): use the metric, then administrative distance. --- # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Longest Mask Matching The router performs the following sequence for every incoming packet: 1. **Packet Arrival:** A packet arrives with destination IP $D$ (32 bits for IPv4). 2. **Table Lookup:** The forwarding table is searched. Modern routers use **TCAM (Ternary Content Addressable Memory)** for O(1) parallel lookup. 3. **Mask Application:** For each entry $E_i$ with network prefix $N_i$ and mask $M_i$, the router computes: $$ D \;\&\; M_i \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; N_i $$ 4. **Collect Matches:** All entries that satisfy the equality above are placed in a candidate set $C$. 5. **Longest Selection:** From $C$, select the entry $E_{\text{winner}}$ where: $$ \vert M_{\text{winner}} \vert \;=\; \max_{i \,\in\, C}\bigl(\text{popcount}(M_i)\bigr) $$ 6. **Forwarding:** Encapsulate the packet for the next-hop associated with $E_{\text{winner}}$ and forward. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The `popcount(M)` function** counts the number of **1** bits in the mask, which equals the prefix length. The route with the maximum popcount is the Longest Mask Match. ## 2.2 Worked Example — Why LMM is Necessary Consider a router with the following routing table: | Entry | Network Address | Mask | Prefix Length | Next Hop | |:-----:|:---------------:|:----:|:-------------:|:--------:| | 1 | 192.168.0.0 | 255.255.0.0 | /16 | R1 | | 2 | 192.168.1.0 | 255.255.255.0 | /24 | R2 | | 3 | 192.168.1.128 | 255.255.255.128 | /25 | R3 | | 4 | 0.0.0.0 | 0.0.0.0 | /0 | ISP | A packet destined for **192.168.1.200** matches ALL FOUR entries. The router must pick the most specific: - Entry 1: `192.168.1.200 & 255.255.0.0` = `192.168.0.0` ✓ - Entry 2: `192.168.1.200 & 255.255.255.0` = `192.168.1.0` ✓ - Entry 3: `192.168.1.200 & 255.255.255.128` = `192.168.1.128` ✓ - Entry 4: `192.168.1.200 & 0.0.0.0` = `0.0.0.0` ✓ **Winner: Entry 3** (`/25` = longest mask = most specific = 128 host addresses). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | |:-:|:--------|:---------------------|:------------| | 1 | Mask Match Test | $D \,\&\, M_i \;=\; N_i$ | AND the destination with each mask | | 2 | Longest Mask Selection | $L_{\text{win}} = \max(\text{prefix length of all matches})$ | Highest prefix length wins | | 3 | Number of Addresses in a Block | $A = 2^{32 - L}$ | $L$ = prefix length | | 4 | Number of Host Addresses | $H = 2^{32 - L} - 2$ | Subtract network & broadcast | | 5 | Subnet Mask from Prefix | $M = 2^{32} - 2^{32 - L}$ | Constructs dotted-decimal mask | | 6 | Block Size (last octet step) | $\Delta = 256 - \text{last mask octet}$ | Used in CIDR boundary calc | | 7 | Number of Subnets Gained | $S = 2^{L_{\text{new}} - L_{\text{old}}}$ | When extending prefix by bits | | 8 | Total Matchable Address Space | $\displaystyle S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i} 2^{32 - L_i}$ | Sum over all table entries | | 9 | CIDR Boundary Alignment | $L \bmod \Delta_{\text{octet}} = 0$ | Required for valid aggregation | | 10 | Ternary Match (TCAM) | $(D \text{ XOR } N_i) \,\&\, M_i = 0$ | Equivalent hardware check | > [!NOTE] > **Avoid the `|` symbol inside table cells.** Absolute value is written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Internet Backbone Routers:** Core routers (e.g., Cisco CRS, Juniper PTX) process millions of packets per second. LMM is implemented in hardware via TCAMs to keep lookup latency under **microseconds**. - **Traffic Engineering:** ISPs use more specific prefixes (`/24`, `/28`) for customer routes and aggregate them into less specific prefixes (`/19`, `/16`) for upstream advertising. LMM ensures customers always get their dedicated path. - **Anycast Addressing:** DNS root servers advertise the same prefix from many locations. LMM combined with BGP allows the nearest instance to serve the client. - **Blackholing / DDoS Mitigation:** Security teams install `/32` (host-specific) routes pointing to **null0** to drop attack traffic — LMM guarantees these rules override general routes. --- # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Step-by-Step > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A router has the following routing table. A packet arrives with destination IP **10.45.67.89**. Apply Longest Mask Matching to find the next hop. | Entry | Network | Mask | Prefix | Next Hop | |:-----:|:-------:|:----:|:------:|:--------:| | A | 10.0.0.0 | 255.0.0.0 | /8 | R_A | | B | 10.45.0.0 | 255.255.0.0 | /16 | R_B | | C | 10.45.64.0 | 255.255.192.0 | /18 | R_C | | D | 10.45.67.0 | 255.255.255.0 | /24 | R_D | | E | 0.0.0.0 | 0.0.0.0 | /0 | ISP | ### Step 1 — Convert Destination IP to Binary Destination $D = 10.45.67.89$ $$ \begin{aligned} 10 &= 00001010 \\ 45 &= 00101101 \\ 67 &= 01000011 \\ 89 &= 01011001 \end{aligned} $$ So $D_{\text{bin}} = 00001010.00101101.01000011.01011001$. ### Step 2 — Test Each Entry **Entry A** (Mask `/8` = `11111111.00000000.00000000.00000000`): $$ D \;\&\; M_A = 00001010.00000000.00000000.00000000 = 10.0.0.0 = N_A \;\; \checkmark $$ **Entry B** (Mask `/16`): $$ D \;\&\; M_B = 00001010.00101101.00000000.00000000 = 10.45.0.0 = N_B \;\; \checkmark $$ **Entry C** (Mask `/18` = `11111111.11111111.11000000.00000000`): $$ D \;\&\; M_C = 00001010.00101101.01000000.00000000 = 10.45.64.0 = N_C \;\; \checkmark $$ **Entry D** (Mask `/24`): $$ D \;\&\; M_D = 00001010.00101101.01000011.00000000 = 10.45.67.0 = N_D \;\; \checkmark $$ **Entry E** (Mask `/0`): $$ D \;\&\; M_E = 00000000.00000000.00000000.00000000 = 0.0.0.0 = N_E \;\; \checkmark $$ All five entries match. ### Step 3 — Compute Prefix Lengths $$ \begin{aligned} L_A &= 8 \\ L_B &= 16 \\ L_C &= 18 \\ L_D &= 24 \\ L_E &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Select the Maximum $$ L_{\text{winner}} = \max(8, 16, 18, 24, 0) = 24 $$ ### Step 5 — Identify the Winning Entry **Entry D** has prefix length **24**, the highest among all matches. ### Step 6 — Forward the Packet The packet is forwarded to **R_D** with the destination MAC address of R_D's interface. ✅ ## 3.2 Verification by Block Size The block sizes for each match confirm specificity: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= 2^{32-8} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ addresses} \\ B &= 2^{32-16} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ addresses} \\ C &= 2^{32-18} = 2^{14} = 16{,}384 \text{ addresses} \\ D &= 2^{32-24} = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ addresses} \\ E &= 2^{32-0} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ addresses} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Entry D covers the smallest block (256 addresses)** — the most specific. This confirms why LMM picks it. The relationship is **inverse**: longer prefix $\Leftrightarrow$ smaller block $\Leftrightarrow$ more specific. ## 3.3 Reverse Engineering — Finding the Mask from Block Size > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A network administrator wants exactly **1024** host addresses per subnet. Find the appropriate prefix length. $$ 2^{32 - L} - 2 \;\geq\; 1024 $$ $$ 2^{32 - L} \;\geq\; 1026 $$ $$ 32 - L \;\geq\; \log_2(1026) \approx 10.002 $$ Therefore $32 - L \geq 11$, giving $L \leq 21$. So the smallest mask is **`/21`** (`255.255.248.0`), providing $2^{11} - 2 = 2046$ usable hosts. --- # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Longest Mask Matching — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Packet Arrives with Destination IP D] --> B[Read First Entry from Routing Table] B --> C{D AND M_i == N_i?} C -- Yes --> D[Add Entry to Candidate Set C] C -- No --> E{More Entries in Table?} D --> E E -- Yes --> B E -- No --> F{Candidate Set C Empty?} F -- Yes --> G[Drop Packet and Send ICMP Destination Unreachable] F -- No --> H[Find Entry with Maximum Prefix Length] H --> I[Tie? Use Metric then Administrative Distance] I --> J[Encapsulate Packet for Winning Next Hop] J --> K[Forward Packet Out Winning Interface] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LMM in a Router ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ING["Ingress Pipeline"] P1[Physical Port] --> P2[Data Link Decapsulation] P2 --> P3[IP Header Extraction] end subgraph LPM["Longest Prefix Match Engine"] P3 --> T1[Parse Destination IP D] T1 --> T2[Parallel TCAM Lookup] T2 --> T3[Match Set C: all entries where D AND M_i equals N_i] T3 --> T4[Selector: argmax over prefix length] end subgraph EGR["Egress Pipeline"] T4 --> O1[Lookup Next Hop for Winner] O1 --> O2[ARP/ND Resolution for Next Hop MAC] O2 --> O3[Decrement TTL and Recompute Checksum] O3 --> O4[Encapsulate in Data Link Frame] O4 --> O5[Egress Physical Port] end ``` ## 4.3 Candidate Set Visualization (Example from Section 3) ```mermaid flowchart TD D[Destination 10.45.67.89] --> M1["Entry A: /8 MATCH
Next Hop R_A"] D --> M2["Entry B: /16 MATCH
Next Hop R_B"] D --> M3["Entry C: /18 MATCH
Next Hop R_C"] D --> M4["Entry D: /24 MATCH
Next Hop R_D"] D --> M5["Entry E: /0 MATCH
Next Hop ISP"] M1 --> SEL[Selector: argmax of prefix length] M2 --> SEL M3 --> SEL M4 --> SEL M5 --> SEL SEL --> WIN[WINNER: Entry D prefix length 24] WIN --> FWD[Forward to R_D] ``` ## 4.4 Hierarchical View of Prefix Specificity ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT["Default Route: 0.0.0.0/0
All Addresses"] ROOT --> L8["Class A: 10.0.0.0/8
16 Million Addresses"] L8 --> L16["Site: 10.45.0.0/16
65,536 Addresses"] L16 --> L18["Subnet: 10.45.64.0/18
16,384 Addresses"] L18 --> L24["Department: 10.45.67.0/24
256 Addresses"] L24 --> L30["Host Link: 10.45.67.128/30
4 Addresses"] L30 --> L32["Single Host: 10.45.67.130/32
1 Address"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the diagram:** Each downward step increases specificity. A packet for `10.45.67.130` matches every level. LMM descends to `/32` and wins. This is the **principle of hierarchical aggregation** in CIDR. --- # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **Q: Define Longest Mask Matching.
