3. IPv4 must provide **network-layer transparency** — the source should not need to know the MTU of every intermediate link.
4. Therefore, IPv4 routers **dynamically fragment** datagrams to fit the smallest MTU along the path, with **reassembly at the destination only**.

*(Word count: ~90; ideal for 3-mark answer)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] List the IP header fields used in fragmentation and reassembly. Explain the role of the MF flag.**

**Model Answer:**

The following IPv4 header fields participate in fragmentation and reassembly:

| Field | Size | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification** | 16 bits | Uniquely tags all fragments belonging to one original datagram. |
| **Flags (DF, MF)** | 3 bits (incl. reserved) | DF: prevents fragmentation; MF: indicates "more fragments coming". |
| **Fragment Offset** | 13 bits | Position of this fragment's data, in 8-byte units, from the start of the original payload. |
| **Total Length** | 16 bits | Length of *this* fragment (header + data), not the original. |

**Role of MF (More Fragments) Flag:**
- Set to **1** in every fragment **except the last**.
- The **last fragment** has **MF = 0**, signaling to the destination that reassembly can be finalized.
- The destination uses MF = 0 + the Offset & Total Length of that final fragment to know the **total expected payload size**.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the IPv4 fragmentation process in detail with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the Identification, Flags, and Fragment Offset fields. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A datagram of **5320 bytes** arrives at a router with an MTU of **1500 bytes**. The IP header is **20 bytes**. Show the complete fragmentation, listing each fragment's Total Length, Data Length, Offset, and MF flag. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**1. Definition:** Fragmentation is the process of breaking an oversized IP datagram into smaller fragments conforming to the MTU of the outgoing link.

**2. The Three Header Fields:**
- **Identification (16 bits):** Source assigns a unique value to all fragments of a datagram. Destination uses (Identification + Source IP + Destination IP + Protocol) as a 4-tuple key to associate fragments for reassembly.
- **Flags (3 bits):**
  - Bit 0: Reserved (must be 0)
  - Bit 1: **DF (Don't Fragment)** — if set, router must drop + send ICMP if too large.
  - Bit 2: **MF (More Fragments)** — 1 for all but last fragment, 0 for last.
- **Fragment Offset (13 bits):** Position of the fragment's first data byte relative to the start of the original datagram's data field, measured in 8-byte units.

**3. Process Steps (Router Algorithm):**
1. Check if `Total Length > Outgoing MTU`. If not, forward as-is.
2. If `DF = 1` and size > MTU → drop + ICMP error.
3. If fragmentation allowed → compute `D_max = MTU - HeaderLength` (must be multiple of 8).
4. Slice the payload into chunks of `D_max`. The last fragment can have a non-multiple-of-8 remainder.
5. For each fragment: copy original header, set the new `Total Length`, update `Offset` and `MF`, recompute checksum, forward.

**4. Reassembly (Destination Only):**
1. Buffer all fragments with the same 4-tuple key.
2. Build a "received intervals" bitmap.
3. When MF = 0 fragment arrives, check that all bits in the bitmap are filled.
4. Reassemble in offset order, deliver to transport layer.
5. Timeout (60 s RFC 791) → discard + ICMP Time Exceeded.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- $L_{\text{orig}} = 5320$ bytes
- $H = 20$ bytes
- $D_{\text{orig}} = 5320 - 20 = 5300$ bytes
- $\text{MTU} = 1500$ bytes

**Step 1: Maximum Data per Fragment**

$$
D_{\max} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Check 8-byte alignment**

$$
1480 \div 8 = 185 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Number of Fragments**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{5300}{1480} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.581\ldots \rceil = 4 \text{ fragments}
$$

**Step 4: Data Distribution** (First 3 carry 1480; last carries the remainder)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 = D_2 = D_3 &= 1480 \text{ bytes} \\
D_4 &= 5300 - (3 \times 1480) = 5300 - 4440 = 860 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Offsets**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset}_1 &= 0 \\
\text{Offset}_2 &= 1480/8 = 185 \\
\text{Offset}_3 &= (1480 + 1480)/8 = 370 \\
\text{Offset}_4 &= (1480 + 1480 + 1480)/8 = 555
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: MF Flags**

$$
\text{MF} = 1 \text{ for 1, 2, 3; } \text{MF} = 0 \text{ for 4}
$$

**Step 7: Total Lengths**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 = L_2 = L_3 &= 1480 + 20 = 1500 \\
L_4 &= 860 + 20 = 880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: All fragments share the same Identification (e.g., ID = 0x0F1A)**

#### ✅ Final Fragment Table

| Fragment | Total Length (bytes) | Data Length (bytes) | Offset (× 8) | MF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1500 | 1480 | 0 | 1 |
| 2 | 1500 | 1480 | 185 | 1 |
| 3 | 1500 | 1480 | 370 | 1 |
| 4 | 880 | 860 | 555 | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Reassembly:** $860 + 555 \times 8 = 860 + 4440 = 5300$ bytes ✓ matches original payload.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss IPv4 fragmentation and reassembly. Explain why reassembly is performed only at the destination and not at intermediate routers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IP datagram of **8000 bytes** (with a 20-byte header) must traverse a network with an **MTU of 1000 bytes**. A subsequent link has **MTU = 576 bytes**. Show the fragmentation at each stage. List the final fragments' Offset, MF, and Total Length. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**1. Fragmentation & Reassembly Overview:**
- **Fragmentation:** Splitting an IP datagram into smaller pieces when it exceeds the MTU of the outgoing link. Done at routers or source.
- **Reassembly:** Recombining all fragments of a datagram into the original packet. Done **only at the destination host**.

**2. Why Reassembly is Done Only at the Destination (NOT at Routers):**

**(i) Variable Path Problem:** After a router reassembles, the reassembled datagram must again be fragmented if the **next** link has a smaller MTU. Reassembly at every router is wasteful — the original fragments could have just been forwarded.

**(ii) Fragment Loss & Duplication:** If a fragment is lost, a router cannot reassemble. Reassembly is **only meaningful when ALL fragments have arrived** — which is guaranteed only at the destination.

**(iii) Memory & State:** Routers would need to hold state (4-tuple buffers) for every datagram in transit, severely impacting **router performance and scalability** (the "router as a stateful device" anti-pattern).

**(iv) Different Path:** Fragments may take **different routes** (especially under multipath routing or load balancing). Only the destination sees all of them.

**(v) Design Philosophy:** The Internet is **connectionless and stateless at the network layer**. Reassembly is a destination-side concern to keep routers simple and fast.

**3. Drawbacks of Fragmentation:**
- Overhead: each fragment carries a full IP header.
- Increased loss probability: losing 1 fragment discards the entire datagram.
- Reassembly buffer exhaustion attacks (e.g., Jolt2).
- Security vulnerabilities (Teardrop, Ping of Death).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Stage 1: Source → Link with MTU = 1000 bytes**

- $L_{\text{orig}} = 8000$, $H = 20$, $D_{\text{orig}} = 7980$
- $D_{\max} = 1000 - 20 = 980$ bytes
- $980 \div 8 = 122.5$ → round **down** to $122 \times 8 = 976$ bytes per non-last fragment.
- Number of fragments from stage 1: $\lceil 7980 / 976 \rceil = \lceil 8.176 \rceil = 9$ fragments.
- First 8 fragments: data = 976 bytes each. Last fragment: $7980 - 8 \times 976 = 7980 - 7808 = 172$ bytes.

**Stage 1 Fragments (all with same ID, e.g., 0x1234):**

| Stage 1 Fragment | Total Length | Data | Offset (× 8) | MF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1-F1 | 996 | 976 | 0 | 1 |
| S1-F2 | 996 | 976 | 122 | 1 |
| S1-F3 | 996 | 976 | 244 | 1 |
| S1-F4 | 996 | 976 | 366 | 1 |
| S1-F5 | 996 | 976 | 488 | 1 |
| S1-F6 | 996 | 976 | 610 | 1 |
| S1-F7 | 996 | 976 | 732 | 1 |
| S1-F8 | 996 | 976 | 854 | 1 |
| S1-F9 | 192 | 172 | 976 | 0 |

**Stage 2: One of these fragments (e.g., S1-F5, which is 996 bytes) → Link with MTU = 576 bytes**

The router takes S1-F5 (Total Length 996, Data 976, MF=1, Offset 488) and re-fragments it.

- $D_{\max} = 576 - 20 = 556$ bytes
- $556 \div 8 = 69.5$ → round down to $69 \times 8 = 552$ bytes per non-last sub-fragment.
- Number of sub-fragments: $\lceil 976 / 552 \rceil = \lceil 1.768 \rceil = 2$.

**Re-fragmentation of S1-F5:**

| Sub-fragment | Total Length | Data | Offset (× 8) | MF |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S2-A | 572 | 552 | 488 | 1 |
| S2-B | 444 | 424 | 488 + 69 = 557 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule of Re-fragmentation:** The new offsets are computed **relative to the start of the ORIGINAL datagram** (not relative to the fragment being re-fragmented). The MF flag of the **last** sub-fragment follows from whether the original fragment was the last (here S1-F5 had MF=1, so the last sub-fragment S2-B also has MF=1, but in this sub-example it carries MF=0 only if S1-F5 was the absolute last). In our case, since S1-F5 is not the absolute last, **S2-B retains MF=1** and only the absolute final fragment (from S1-F9) has MF=0.

**For the absolute final fragment (S1-F9 with MF=0, Total Length=192, Offset=976):** It is forwarded as-is by the second router because 192 ≤ 576, so no further fragmentation is needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Re-fragmentation offset rule:** Offsets in re-fragments are **relative to the original datagram**, not the parent fragment. Students commonly use the wrong reference and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Rounding direction:** Data length must be rounded **down** to the nearest multiple of 8 for non-last fragments. Forgetting this rule loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Identification field:** All fragments (including re-fragments) must share the **same Identification** from the original source. Changing it breaks reassembly.
> 4. **Total Length vs. Data Length confusion:** Total Length includes the IP header. Many students write 976 instead of 996 for Total Length.
> 5. **Forgetting the last fragment's MF = 0:** The last fragment in the chain is uniquely identified by MF=0, not by having "no more data" (these are two different concepts).
> 6. **IPv6 ≠ IPv4:** Students often mention "IPv6 supports fragmentation" — INCORRECT. IPv6 **forbids** router-based fragmentation entirely.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Fragmentation is a network-layer (IPv4) mechanism** to break large datagrams into smaller fragments that fit the outgoing link's MTU.
- ✅ **Three header fields govern it:** Identification (16-bit), Flags (3-bit: Reserved/DF/MF), Fragment Offset (13-bit).
- ✅ **Fragment Offset is measured in 8-byte units** — data length of every non-last fragment must be a multiple of 8.
- ✅ **MF = 0 marks the last fragment.** All others have MF = 1.
- ✅ **DF = 1** prohibits fragmentation; the router drops the datagram and sends **ICMP Type 3 Code 4** (Fragmentation Needed) back to the source.
- ✅ **Reassembly happens ONLY at the destination host**, never at intermediate routers (stateless-router design principle).
- ✅ **Identification + Source IP + Destination IP + Protocol** form the reassembly 4-tuple key.
- ✅ **Reassembly timeout (RFC 791):** 60 seconds. If all fragments don't arrive in time, the datagram is discarded and **ICMP Time Exceeded** is sent.
- ✅ **PMTUD (Path MTU Discovery)** uses the DF flag + ICMP feedback to find the smallest MTU along a path, avoiding fragmentation.
- ✅ **IPv6 removed router-based fragmentation.** Only the source can fragment; routers send **ICMPv6 Packet Too Big** (Type 2) to trigger PMTUD.
- ✅ **MTU of standard Ethernet = 1500 bytes.** Minimum guaranteed MTU in IPv4 = 576 bytes.
- ✅ **Bandwidth overhead of fragmentation:** Each fragment repeats a full IP header (20–60 bytes), wasting bandwidth.
- ✅ **Fragmentation is exploited in attacks:** Teardrop, Ping of Death, Jolt2 — hence security-aware networks often **drop fragmented packets at firewalls**.
- ✅ **Maximum theoretical fragments per datagram:** 118 (when MTU = 576 bytes for max-size 65,515-byte payload).
- ✅ **When a fragment is lost, the entire datagram is lost** — IP is unreliable and does not retransmit. This is why transport-layer protocols (TCP) handle end-to-end reliability.

> **🧠 Final Mnemonic for Field Sizes:** **"I-FO-TTL"** → **I**dentification (16), **F**lags (3), **O**ffset (13) — together 32 bits, occupying the **4th 32-bit word** of the IPv4 header. This is a high-yield KTU 2-mark question!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Options
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Options

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IP OPTIONS** are a variable-length, optional field within the **IPv4 Header** that follows the Destination IP Address and precedes the Data (payload). They allow the sender of an IP datagram to specify special handling instructions, request diagnostic information from intermediate routers, or constrain the path through which the datagram travels.

The IPv4 header is composed of two major regions:
1. A fixed **20-byte** mandatory region.
2. An optional **variable-length region** (0 to **40 bytes**) populated with one or more IP Options.

The total IP header must always be a **multiple of 4 bytes (32 bits)** to align with the way IP is processed in hardware. Therefore:

$$
\text{Maximum Header Length} = 20 + 40 = \mathbf{60 \text{ bytes}}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a registered parcel via a courier service. The standard waybill (the **fixed 20-byte header**) contains only *to*, *from*, and *weight* fields. But sometimes you need extra instructions:

- *"Please record the names of every warehouse this parcel passes through"* → **Record Route**
- *"This parcel MUST go through Mumbai and Delhi in that exact order, no shortcuts"* → **Strict Source Route**
- *"Please record the time the parcel reaches each warehouse"* → **Timestamp**
- *"If any instructions are missing on a previous line, ignore it (padding)"* → **No-Operation (NOP)**

IP Options are precisely those **extra sticky-notes** you attach to the waybill. They are not always required, but when used they give the IP layer powerful diagnostic and routing control features that the rigid fixed header cannot provide.

### 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Maximum Option Field Size:** **40 bytes**
> - **Option Type Byte Length:** **1 byte** (copy flag + class + number)
> - **Number of Standard Options Defined in IPv4:** **8 (0–9)**
> - **Required Padding:** Options must be padded with **NOP (Type 1)** or **EOOL (Type 0)** to keep the header length a multiple of 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Location of the Options Field in the IPv4 Header

| Field (in order) | Size (bits) | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| Version + IHL | 8 | Yes |
| Type of Service | 8 | Yes |
| Total Length | 16 | Yes |
| Identification | 16 | Yes |
| Flags + Fragment Offset | 16 | Yes |
| Time to Live (TTL) | 8 | Yes |
| Protocol | 8 | Yes |
| Header Checksum | 16 | Yes |
| Source Address | 32 | Yes |
| Destination Address | 32 | Yes |
| **Options** | **0 – 320** | **Optional** |
| Data (Payload) | Variable | Yes |

The IHL (Internet Header Length) field reports the *total* header length in 32-bit words, enabling receivers to locate the start of the payload:

$$
\text{IHL} = \frac{\text{Total Header Length (bytes)}}{4}
$$

Therefore, if all 40 bytes of options are used:

$$
\text{IHL}_{\max} = \frac{60}{4} = \mathbf{15} \text{ (32-bit words)}
$$

### 2.2 General Format of a Single Option

Every IP Option conforms to one of two encodings:

**Type-1 (Single-byte options):** NOP (1) and EOOL (0). They have no length or data fields.

$$
\text{Type-1 Option} = [\,1 \text{ byte}\,]
$$

**Type-2 (Multi-byte options):** Used by Record Route, Source Route, Timestamp, etc.

$$
\text{Type-2 Option} = [\,\underbrace{\text{Type}}_{1B} \;\mid\; \underbrace{\text{Length}}_{1B} \;\mid\; \underbrace{\text{Data}}_{\text{Length}-2 \text{ bytes}}\,]
$$

### 2.3 Structure of the Option Type Byte

The first byte of any option carries three sub-fields:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\text{Bit 7} & \text{Bit 6} & \text{Bit 5} & \text{Bit 4} & \text{Bit 3} & \text{Bit 2} & \text{Bit 1} & \text{Bit 0} \\
\hline
\text{C} & \text{Class} & \text{Class} & \text{Num} & \text{Num} & \text{Num} & \text{Num} & \text{Num} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- **Copy Flag (C, 1 bit):** If set to **1**, the option is copied into **all fragments** when the datagram is fragmented. If **0**, it is copied only into the **first fragment**.
- **Class (2 bits):**
  - `00` → **Control** options (routing, datagram control).
  - `01` → Reserved.
  - `10` → **Debugging and Measurement** (e.g., Timestamp).
  - `11` → Reserved.
- **Number (5 bits):** Identifies the specific option (0 to 31).

### 2.4 Standard IPv4 Options Catalogue

| Decimal Value | Binary Value | Class | Copy Flag | Name | Length |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 0 | 00000000 | 0 | 0 | End of Option List (EOOL) | 1 byte |
| 1 | 00000001 | 0 | 0 | No-Operation (NOP) | 1 byte |
| 2 | 10000010 | 0 | 1 | Security (deprecated) | 11 bytes |
| 3 | 00000011 | 0 | 0 | Loose Source Route | Variable |
| 7 | 00000111 | 0 | 0 | Record Route | Variable |
| 8 | 10001000 | 2 | 1 | Stream ID (deprecated) | 4 bytes |
| 9 | 00001001 | 0 | 0 | Strict Source Route | Variable |
| 4 | 01000100 | 2 | 0 | Internet Timestamp | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security (option 2)** and **Stream ID (option 8)** were carried forward from military IPv4 (RFC 791) but are essentially **deprecated** in civilian networking. KTU examiners most frequently test **Record Route, Source Routes, and Timestamp**.

### 2.5 Boundary Condition for Routing Options

Every routing or recording option contains a fixed-size pointer and a list of 4-byte IP slots:

$$
\text{Maximum IP addresses stored} = \frac{\text{Option Length} - 3}{4}
$$

The `-3` accounts for the **Type byte**, the **Length byte**, and the **1-byte pointer**.

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Network Diagnostics:** Tools like `traceroute` historically leveraged **Record Route** and **Timestamp** options to map end-to-end paths before ICMP-based methods became standard.
- **Policy Routing:** **Source Route** options were once used to enforce traffic engineering policies in backbone networks.
- **Security Concerns:** Loose Source Route was famously exploited in early smurf-attack-style abuses, leading many modern routers to drop these options by default for security hardening.
- **Performance Penalty:** Each option must be parsed by every router along the path; bulky options slow down per-packet processing, which is why options are rarely used in modern high-speed production traffic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of IP Addresses in a Record Route Option

**Given:** IPv4 header maximum length is **60 bytes**; fixed header uses **20 bytes**; Pointer field consumes **1 byte**.

**Step 1:** Compute the maximum bytes available for the *entire* option field.

$$
L_{\text{options,max}} = 60 - 20 = 40 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** For a Record Route option, the option consumes 1 (Type) + 1 (Length) + 1 (Pointer) = **3 bytes of overhead**.

$$
L_{\text{overhead}} = 3 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** The remaining bytes are available for 4-byte IP address slots.

$$
N_{\text{slots}} = \frac{L_{\text{options,max}} - L_{\text{overhead}}}{4} = \frac{40 - 3}{4} = \frac{37}{4}
$$

**Step 4:** Since the number of slots must be an integer, we round down.

$$
N_{\text{slots}} = \left\lfloor 9.25 \right\rfloor = \mathbf{9}
$$

**Result:** A Record Route option can therefore record a maximum of **9 router IP addresses**.

> [!NOTE]
> However, **option 7 (Record Route)** by convention reserves a small portion for alignment; in practice, the standard RR option uses a Length byte of **39**, leaving space for exactly **9** IP addresses. This matches our derivation.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough: How Record Route Operates

1. **Sender Initialisation:** The sender builds an RR option with Pointer = **4** (pointing to the first empty slot, just after the 3 overhead bytes). All address slots are initially empty.

$$
\text{Pointer}_{\text{initial}} = 4
$$

2. **Datagram Transmission:** The packet is sent into the network. Routers in the path do *not* modify it on the way out unless they are recording.

3. **Intermediate Router Action:** As the datagram *leaves* a participating router, that router:
   - Writes its own **outgoing interface IP** into the slot indicated by `Pointer`.
   - Increments `Pointer` by **4** (one IP address width).

$$
\text{Pointer}_{\text{new}} = \text{Pointer}_{\text{old}} + 4
$$

4. **Termination:** When `Pointer` exceeds `Length`, no more writing occurs.

5. **Receiver Inspection:** The destination host reads the now-populated route trace.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Strict vs. Loose Source Route

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Attribute} & \textbf{Strict Source Route} & \textbf{Loose Source Route} \\
\hline
\text{Option Number} & 9 & 3 \\
\hline
\text{Sender Constraint} & \text{Exact path mandated} & \text{Listed routers must be visited} \\
\hline
\text{Intermediate Hops} & \text{None allowed (only listed)} & \text{Any number allowed between listed routers} \\
\hline
\text{Failure Behavior} & \text{Datagram dropped} & \text{Next listed router attempted} \\
\hline
\text{Modern Use} & \text{Rare (security concerns)} & \text{Rare (security concerns)} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Internet Timestamp Option Structure

The Timestamp option (Type = 68 decimal = `0x44`) has three operating modes defined by the **Overflow (OF) and Flag (FL)** sub-fields:

- **Flags = 0:** Only timestamps recorded (each slot = 4 bytes for time, **no IP**).
- **Flags = 1:** Each slot = **8 bytes (4 IP + 4 timestamp)**, IP pre-filled by sender.
- **Flags = 3:** Each slot = **8 bytes (4 IP + 4 timestamp)**, IP *and* timestamp filled by routers.

For Flag = 1 or 3, a slot size of 8 bytes applies:

$$
N_{\text{timestamps}} = \frac{L_{\text{options,max}} - 4}{8} = \frac{40 - 4}{8} = \frac{36}{8} = \mathbf{4 \text{ entries}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `-4` instead of `-3`?** Because the Timestamp option also has a 1-byte **OF/FL** field in addition to the Type, Length, and Pointer bytes, giving an overhead of **4 bytes**.

### 3.5 Padding Logic: EOOL vs. NOP

After all options are placed, the remaining bytes (if any) must be padded so that the IP header ends at a 4-byte boundary.

- **EOOL (Type 0):** A *single byte* that marks the end of all options. Receivers must ignore everything that follows.
- **NOP (Type 1):** A *single byte* used between options (or after the last) purely as filler.

> [!TIP]
> Receivers that encounter an unrecognized option type must:
> 1. Read its **Length byte**.
> 2. Skip ahead exactly that many bytes.
> 3. Continue parsing. This forward-compatibility is precisely why each option carries an explicit length.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Location of Options in the IPv4 Datagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VER IHL 8b] --> B[TOS 8b]
    B --> C[Total Length 16b]
    C --> D[Identification 16b]
    D --> E[Flags Frag Offset 16b]
    E --> F[TTL 8b]
    F --> G[Protocol 8b]
    H[Header Checksum 16b] --> I[Source IP 32b]
    I --> J[Destination IP 32b]
    J --> K[OPTIONS 0 to 320 bits]
    K --> L[PAYLOAD Variable]
    G --> H
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Option Byte

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Byte[Option Type Byte 8 bits] --> CF{Copy Flag Bit 7}
    Byte --> CL[Class Bits 6 to 5]
    Byte --> NM[Number Bits 4 to 0]
    CF -- 1 --> CA[Copied to ALL Fragments]
    CF -- 0 --> CB[Copied to FIRST Fragment Only]
    CL -- 00 --> C1[Control Class]
    CL -- 01 --> C2[Reserved Class]
    CL -- 10 --> C3[Debugging and Measurement Class]
    CL -- 11 --> C4[Reserved Class]
    NM --> ID[Identifies Specific Option 0 to 31]
```

### 4.3 Record Route Option Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Type 7 1B] --> P2[Length 1B]
    P2 --> P3[Pointer 1B]
    P3 --> P4[IP Address Slot 1 4B]
    P4 --> P5[IP Address Slot 2 4B]
    P5 --> P6[IP Address Slot 3 4B]
    P6 --> P7[IP Address Slot N 4B]
    P7 --> P8[Padding NOP if needed]
```

### 4.4 Source Route Data Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Source Host
    participant R1 as Router 1
    participant R2 as Router 2
    participant D as Destination Host
    S->>R1: Datagram with Strict Source Route List R1 R2 D
    R1->>R2: Forward (only path allowed)
    R2->>D: Forward (only path allowed)
    D-->>S: Reply received along reverse path
    Note over R1,R2: If R1 attempts alternate path, datagram is DROPPED
```

### 4.5 Timestamp Option Detailed View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Type 0x44 1B] --> T2[Length 1B]
    T2 --> T3[Pointer 1B]
    T3 --> T4[OF FL Overflow and Flags 1B]
    T4 --> T5[Slot 1 IP 4B plus Time 4B]
    T5 --> T6[Slot 2 IP 4B plus Time 4B]
    T6 --> T7[Slot N IP 4B plus Time 4B]
```

### 4.6 Padding Strategy End of Options

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Last Real Option] --> PAD{Padding Needed?}
    PAD -- Yes 4B aligned --> EOOL[EOOL 0 followed by NOPs]
    PAD -- Yes 1B only --> NOP[NOP Padding]
    PAD -- No --> DONE[Header ends here]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the purpose of the Options field in an IPv4 header. Mention the maximum possible size of this field.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Options field** is an optional, variable-length region in the IPv4 header placed between the Destination Address and the Data payload. It is used to provide additional control, routing, or diagnostic features that the fixed 20-byte header cannot express.

Examples include **Record Route, Strict Source Route, Loose Source Route, and Timestamp**.

The maximum size is:

$$
L_{\max} = 60 - 20 = \mathbf{40 \text{ bytes}}
$$

Because the IP header must always be a multiple of 4 bytes, options are padded with NOP (Type 1) or EOOL (Type 0).

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the function of the No-Operation (NOP) option in IPv4? List its option type byte in binary.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **NOP (No-Operation)** option, with type byte **`00000001`**, is a single-byte filler used between other options (or after the last option) to align the IP header on a 4-byte boundary. Routers and hosts encountering NOP simply skip the byte and continue parsing the next option without taking any action.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **general format** of an IP option. Explain the significance of the *Copy Flag*, *Class*, and *Number* fields in the Option Type byte.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain how the **Record Route** option works. Compute the maximum number of IP addresses that can be recorded.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Every multi-byte IP option follows the format:

$$
[\,\text{Type} \; \mid\; \text{Length} \; \mid\; \text{Variable Data}\,]
$$

1. **Type (1 byte):** Composed of three sub-fields:
   - **Copy Flag (1 bit):** Determines whether the option is copied into *all fragments* (1) or only into the *first fragment* (0) when fragmentation occurs.
   - **Class (2 bits):** Specifies the option category:
     - `00` = Control
     - `01` = Reserved
     - `10` = Debugging/Measurement
     - `11` = Reserved
   - **Number (5 bits):** Identifies the specific option (e.g., 7 for Record Route, 9 for Strict Source Route, 4 for Timestamp).

2. **Length (1 byte):** The total length of this option in bytes, including Type and Length fields themselves.

3. **Variable Data:** The actual contents, the size of which is `Length - 2`.

**Model Solution for (b):**

In Record Route, the sender pre-allocates empty 4-byte slots for IP addresses. As the datagram travels, **each router along the path** writes its *outgoing interface IP* into the slot indicated by the **Pointer** and increments the pointer by 4. The destination receives a complete trail.

```
Type = 7 (1 byte)
Length = L (1 byte)
Pointer = initial value 4 (1 byte)
IP slot 1 (4 bytes) | IP slot 2 (4 bytes) | ... | IP slot N (4 bytes)
```

**Derivation of Maximum Addresses:**

$$
N = \frac{L_{\max} - 3}{4} = \frac{40 - 3}{4} = \lfloor 9.25 \rfloor = \mathbf{9 \text{ routers}}
$$

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sender[Source] --> r1[Router 1 writes IP at Pointer 4]
    r1 --> r2[Router 2 writes IP at Pointer 8]
    r2 --> r3[Router 3 writes IP at Pointer 12]
    r3 --> dest[Destination reads all 3 addresses]
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Alternative Choice]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Strict Source Route** and **Loose Source Route**. Explain how each is processed by intermediate routers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Internet Timestamp** option. Explain the three flag modes and derive the maximum number of timestamps that can be recorded in a single datagram.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Aspect | Strict Source Route (Option 9) | Loose Source Route (Option 3) |
|---|---|---|
| Path Constraint | Sender specifies the **exact** sequence of routers the datagram must traverse. | Sender specifies **certain** routers that must be on the path, but allows others in between. |
| Intermediate Hops | **None allowed** other than those listed. | **Multiple allowed** between listed routers. |
| Router Action | If a router cannot forward to the *next listed* hop, the datagram is **dropped**. | If a router cannot reach the next listed hop, it may attempt any alternative path. |
| Pointer Update | Router updates the pointer to point to the *next listed* IP. | Same pointer mechanism, but more flexibility. |

In **Strict Source Route**, the IP header contains the full ordered list of router IPs. Each router along the path replaces the next-listed IP with the IP of the *next-next* router, decrementing the list as it goes. In **Loose Source Route**, the same replacement happens, but routers may add intermediate hops between listed waypoints.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The **Internet Timestamp** option (Type `01000100` = 68) records the time each router processes the datagram.

**Structure:**

```
Type = 0x44 (1 byte)
Length = L (1 byte)
Pointer (1 byte)
OF + FL (1 byte) -- Overflow (4 bits) and Flag (4 bits)
Slots: each slot can be 4 bytes (time only) or 8 bytes (IP + time)
```

**Three Flag Modes:**

1. **FL = 0:** Each slot stores only a **4-byte timestamp**.
2. **FL = 1:** Each slot stores **8 bytes** — pre-filled by the sender with the next router's IP, plus the timestamp recorded by that router.
3. **FL = 3:** Each slot stores **8 bytes** — both IP and timestamp are filled in by routers.

**Derivation for Maximum Timestamps (FL = 1 or 3):**

$$
N = \frac{L_{\max} - 4}{8} = \frac{40 - 4}{8} = \mathbf{4 \text{ timestamp entries}}
$$

For **FL = 0**:

$$
N = \frac{L_{\max} - 4}{4} = \frac{40 - 4}{4} = \mathbf{9 \text{ timestamps}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often **forget the Length byte** when counting IP slots, leading to off-by-one errors in the maximum capacity derivation.
> 2. Confusing **Copy Flag semantics**: It applies during **fragmentation**, *not* to all forwarding. Students frequently lose 1 mark for stating "it is copied to all forwarded packets."
> 3. Mixing up the **three Timestamp flag modes** (0, 1, 3). Always remember: flag 0 = time-only (4B/slot); flags 1 and 3 = IP+time (8B/slot).
> 4. Drawing the **Option Type byte** with reversed bit-order — bit 7 must be on the **left** (most significant).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Location:** Options sit *after* Destination Address and *before* Data in the IPv4 header.
- **Size range:** **0 to 40 bytes** (header is always a 4-byte multiple).
- **Two option encodings:** **Type-1** (1-byte: NOP, EOOL) and **Type-2** (Type + Length + Data).
- **Option Type byte = [Copy Flag(1) | Class(2) | Number(5)]**.
- **Class meanings:** `00` Control, `01` Reserved, `10` Debug, `11` Reserved.
- **Copy Flag:** `1` = copy to all fragments; `0` = copy to first fragment only.
- **EOOL (Type 0):** End of all options, single byte.
- **NOP (Type 1):** Padding between options to maintain 4-byte alignment.
- **Record Route (Type 7):** Routers append their IP; **max 9** addresses.
- **Strict Source Route (Type 9):** Exact path mandatory; non-listed hops cause drop.
- **Loose Source Route (Type 3):** Listed routers must be visited, but intermediate hops allowed.
- **Timestamp (Type 68 / `0x44`):** 3 modes (time-only, IP+time sender-filled, IP+time router-filled); max **9 timestamps (flag 0)** or **4 timestamps (flags 1 or 3)**.
- **Modern status:** Most routing/recording options are disabled by default in production networks due to **security concerns** and **processing overhead**.
- **Quick math reminders:**
  - `IHL × 4 = Header Length (bytes)`
  - `Max options slots = floor((40 - overhead) / slot size)`
- **Examiner tip:** Always explicitly state the **overhead bytes** when deriving slot counts — KTU valuations reward showing the calculation steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Checksum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Checksum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **TCP/IP protocol suite**, the **Internet Checksum** is a **16-bit error-detection mechanism** used primarily at the **Transport Layer** (TCP, UDP) and the **Network Layer** (IPv4) to detect corruption in the header and/or payload of a datagram. It is a form of *redundancy check* computed using **One's Complement Arithmetic** over a sequence of 16-bit words, producing a single 16-bit checksum field that travels alongside the data.

The Internet Checksum is defined in **RFC 1071** ("Computing the Internet Checksum") and is characterized by three properties that distinguish it from cyclic codes like CRC:

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Properties of Internet Checksum**  
> 1. **Weak Detection Capability** — Cannot detect all error patterns (specifically, some burst errors and order-of-words reordering can escape detection).  
> 2. **End-to-End Computability** — Can be recomputed at any intermediate router (for IPv4 header) since the header fields it depends on are non-mutable.  
> 3. **Symmetric Arithmetic Base** — Uses One's Complement (not Two's Complement) so the sum at sender and receiver converge to predictable constant values.

The Internet Checksum is computed by the **sender** before transmission and **verified** by the **receiver** upon arrival. A mismatch between the transmitted checksum and the locally recomputed checksum indicates that the datagram has been corrupted in transit and should be discarded.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian responsible for ensuring that no book is lost or damaged when a courier delivers a stack of books from a branch library. Before sealing the box, you write on a label:

> *"The total number of pages across all books is 12,847."*

The courier takes the box. If the box is dropped, soaked in rain, or tampered with, the page count **might** change. When the receiving librarian checks the books, she counts the pages again. If her count is anything other than **12,847**, the box was disturbed. If her count matches, *probably* nothing happened (but not guaranteed — someone could have swapped a 200-page book for another 200-page book and the count would still match).

That page-count label is the **checksum**, and the act of recounting at the destination is the **checksum verification**. The Internet Checksum follows exactly the same philosophy — it is a *fast, lightweight, probabilistic* corruption detector, not a perfect one.

## 1.3 Where the Checksum is Used in TCP/IP

| Protocol | Layer | What is Checked | Checksum Mandatory? |
|----------|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **IPv4** | Network | Header only | Mandatory |
| **IPv6** | Network | None at IP layer (relies on L2/L4) | Not used |
| **TCP** | Transport | Header + Payload + Pseudo-header | Mandatory |
| **UDP** | Transport | Header + Payload + Pseudo-header | Optional (but recommended) |
| **ICMPv4** | Network | Message + Pseudo-header | Mandatory |
| **IGMP** | Network | Message | Mandatory |

> [!NOTE]  
> The **Pseudo-Header** is an auxiliary 12-byte structure (for IPv4) that contains source/destination IP addresses, protocol number, and UDP/TCP length. It is *not* transmitted in the datagram but is included in the checksum computation to provide end-to-end protection against mis-routed packets.

## 1.4 Conceptual Distinction: Checksum vs. CRC

| Property | Internet Checksum | CRC-32 |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Arithmetic | One's Complement addition | Polynomial division (modulo-2) |
| Strength | Weak (catches ~99.6% of errors in practice) | Very strong (catches all 1-bit, 2-bit, odd-count, and most burst errors) |
| Speed | Very fast in software | Fast in hardware, slower in pure software |
| Used in | TCP, UDP, IPv4 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, HDLC |

The Internet Checksum was historically chosen for its **software efficiency** on the slow CPUs of the early ARPANET era (1970s–80s), not for its mathematical strength.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Mathematics — One's Complement Arithmetic

The Internet Checksum relies on **One's Complement (1C) addition**, which is fundamentally different from the Two's Complement (2C) addition used in modern CPUs.

### 2.1.1 One's Complement Number System

For a fixed word length of $n$ bits:
- The positive range is $0$ to $2^{n-1} - 1$ (same as unsigned).
- The negative of a number $x$ is obtained by **bitwise inversion** (flipping every 0 to 1 and vice versa).
- One's complement has **two representations of zero**: $+0$ (all zeros) and $-0$ (all ones).

The one's complement of a 16-bit value $x$ is denoted $\bar{x}$ or $\sim x$.

### 2.1.2 One's Complement Addition Rule

To add two $n$-bit numbers $A$ and $B$ in one's complement:

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **One's Complement Addition Algorithm**  
> 1. Perform **ordinary binary addition** of $A$ and $B$.  
> 2. If a **carry-out** is produced from the most significant bit (MSB), **wrap it around** and add $1$ to the result (this is the *end-around carry*).  
> 3. If the result has $n+1$ bits after step 1, truncate to $n$ bits after step 2.

Mathematically, the sum is computed modulo $2^n - 1$ (not $2^n$ as in two's complement), because the maximum one’s complement value of $n$ bits is $2^n - 1$ (all ones).

$$S = A + B \pmod{2^n - 1}$$

The end-around carry is equivalent to subtraction of $2^n$ followed by addition of $1$:

$$S = (A + B) + \text{carry} - 2^n \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{carry} = 1]$$

## 2.2 The Internet Checksum Algorithm — Sender Side

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **RFC 1071 — Sender Checksum Procedure**  
> 1. The message is divided into a sequence of **16-bit words** $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_N$.  
> 2. The **checksum field itself is set to zero** before computation.  
> 3. Compute the **one's complement sum** of all 16-bit words:  
> $$C = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$  
> 4. The **checksum value to be transmitted** is the one's complement of $C$:  
> $$\text{Checksum} = \bar{C}$$  
> 5. Place $\bar{C}$ into the checksum field of the header and transmit.

## 2.3 The Internet Checksum Algorithm — Receiver Side

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **RFC 1071 — Receiver Verification Procedure**  
> 1. Receive the datagram and extract the 16-bit checksum field $C'$.  
> 2. Compute the one's complement sum of **all** 16-bit words of the received message **including the checksum field** itself:  
> $$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i + C' \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$  
> 3. If the datagram is error-free, the result $S$ must equal the constant value:  
> $$S = 2^{16} - 1 = \texttt{0xFFFF} = \texttt{1111\,1111\,1111\,1111}$$  
> 4. If $S \neq \texttt{0xFFFF}$, the datagram is **discarded**.

### 2.3.1 Why the Verification Result is a Constant (0xFFFF)

This is the most elegant property of the Internet Checksum. Let $C$ be the sum at the sender (with the checksum field treated as zero), and $\bar{C}$ be the stored checksum. Then the receiver computes:

$$S = C + \bar{C} \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

Since $\bar{C}$ is the bitwise inverse of $C$, every bit where $C$ has a 1 has a 0 in $\bar{C}$, and vice versa. So the bitwise addition of $C$ and $\bar{C}$ produces all 1s in each bit position, with carries that ultimately resolve to:

$$S = 2^{16} - 1 = \texttt{0xFFFF}$$

This **constant-value property** is the cornerstone of Internet checksum verification and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Use Case |
|---|----------------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$ | One's complement sum of $N$ 16-bit words | Sender pre-checksum sum |
| 2 | $\text{Checksum} = \bar{S} = \sim S$ | Stored checksum is bitwise NOT of the sum | Field value |
| 3 | $V = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i + \text{Checksum} \right) \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$ | Receiver verification sum | Validation |
| 4 | $V = \texttt{0xFFFF} \Rightarrow$ No Error | Decision rule | Receiver logic |
| 5 | End-around carry rule | Carry from MSB wraps to LSB | Step in addition |
| 6 | Ones complement of $x$: $\bar{x} = (2^n - 1) - x$ | For unsigned $n$-bit $x$ | Bitwise inversion |
| 7 | Pseudo-header for IPv4/TCP/UDP | Source IP (4B) + Dest IP (4B) + Zero (1B) + Protocol (1B) + Length (2B) | Prepended to data for checksum |
| 8 | Padding rule | Append a single zero byte if data length is odd | Alignment to 16-bit words |
| 9 | Error-detection probability | $\approx 1 - 2^{-16}$ per random error | Strength metric |
| 10 | Burst error detection | Detects all bursts of length $\leq 16$ bits | For TCP, when both words and sum are affected |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Low-Latency Link Verification** — In real-time VoIP or live video streams over UDP, the checksum is recomputed in hardware by NICs at line rate (10 Gbps+) without CPU intervention.  
2. **Router Header Integrity** — IPv4 header checksum allows routers to detect corrupted headers while forwarding, providing a per-hop integrity check at near-zero computational cost.  
3. **Defence Against Bit Flips** — Cosmic rays, EMI, and aging memory can cause random bit errors. The checksum is the *first line of defence* before any higher-layer error correction or retransmission kicks in.  
4. **TCP Pseudo-Header Magic** — By including a pseudo-header in the TCP/UDP checksum, the design ensures that a packet cannot be mis-delivered to a different IP/port combination without being detected.

## 2.6 The Pseudo-Header Structure (IPv4)

For TCP and UDP, the checksum computation prepends this 12-byte structure:

```
+-----------------------+-----------------------+
|     Source IP Address (4 bytes)            |
+-----------------------+-----------------------+
|   Destination IP Address (4 bytes)         |
+-------+-----------+-----------------------+
| Zero  | Protocol  |   TCP/UDP Length      |
| (1B)  |   (1B)    |      (2 bytes)        |
+-------+-----------+-----------------------+
```

| Field | Size | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| Source IP | 32 bits | IPv4 source address |
| Destination IP | 32 bits | IPv4 destination address |
| Zero | 8 bits | Reserved (must be zero) |
| Protocol | 8 bits | 6 for TCP, 17 for UDP |
| Length | 16 bits | Length of TCP/UDP header + data |

> [!NOTE]  
> The pseudo-header is **not transmitted**. Both sender and receiver construct it from the IP datagram's outer header before computing the checksum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Why the Receiver Result is 0xFFFF

Let $C$ be the one's complement sum of all 16-bit words $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_N$ computed by the sender (with the checksum field treated as zero):

$$C = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \right) \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

The transmitted checksum is $\bar{C}$ (bitwise NOT of $C$):

$$\bar{C} = 2^{16} - 1 - C$$

At the receiver, all 16-bit words including the checksum field are summed:

$$V = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i + \bar{C} \right) \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

Substituting:

$$V = \left( C + (2^{16} - 1 - C) \right) \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

The $C$ and $-C$ cancel:

$$V = (2^{16} - 1) \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

By definition, the modulus operation yields:

$$V = 2^{16} - 1 = \texttt{0xFFFF}$$

Hence, a **non-corrupted** datagram always yields $\texttt{0xFFFF}$ at the receiver. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Computing a Sender Checksum (8-bit short, for pedagogy)

**Data (in hex):** `0x4500`, `0x003C`, `0x1C46`, `0x4000`, `0x4006`, `0x0000` (6 words of an IPv4 header, with checksum = 0).

We use 16-bit one's complement addition. To make the arithmetic visible, we work in 4-bit hex.

**Step 1: First two words**

$$0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C$$

No carry. Result: `0x453C`.

**Step 2: Add third word**

$$0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182$$

No carry. Result: `0x6182`.

**Step 3: Add fourth word**

$$0x6182 + 0x4000 = 0xA182$$

No carry. Result: `0xA182`.

**Step 4: Add fifth word**

$$0xA182 + 0x4006 = 0xE188$$

No carry. Result: `0xE188`.

**Step 5: Add sixth word (which is the checksum field, currently zero)**

$$0xE188 + 0x0000 = 0xE188$$

No carry. Result: `0xE188`.

**Step 6: The one's complement (bitwise NOT) of the sum**

$$\bar{C} = \sim 0xE188 = 0x1E77$$

**Step 7: The transmitted checksum field is `0x1E77`.**

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Receiver Verification (Same Header)

The receiver receives the 6 words with the checksum field set to `0x1E77`:

`0x4500`, `0x003C`, `0x1C46`, `0x4000`, `0x4006`, `0x1E77`.

**Step 1:**

$$0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C$$

**Step 2:**

$$0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182$$

**Step 3:**

$$0x6182 + 0x4000 = 0xA182$$

**Step 4:**

$$0xA182 + 0x4006 = 0xE188$$

**Step 5:**

$$0xE188 + 0x1E77 = 0xFFFF$$

**Step 6:** No end-around carry needed. The result is `0xFFFF`, confirming **no error**.

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Demonstrating End-Around Carry

Suppose we sum `0xBEEF + 0x1234`.

**Step 1 — Plain binary addition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Hex:} \quad & \texttt{BEEF} \\
+ \quad & \texttt{1234} \\
\hline
& \texttt{D123} \\
\end{aligned}$$

In decimal: $0xBEEF = 48879$ and $0x1234 = 4660$. Sum $= 53539 = 0xD123$. No carry-out. Easy case.

Now consider a more challenging case: $0xFFFF + 0x0001$.

**Step 1 — Plain binary addition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Hex:} \quad & \texttt{FFFF} \\
+ \quad & \texttt{0001} \\
\hline
& \texttt{10000} \\
\end{aligned}$$

We get a 17-bit result: `1 0000`. The carry-out is `1`.

**Step 2 — Truncate to 16 bits:** We get `0x0000`.

**Step 3 — End-around carry:** Add the discarded carry `1` to the truncated result:

$$0x0000 + 0x0001 = 0x0001$$

**Final one's complement result: `0x0001`**. This matches the modular arithmetic:

$$(0xFFFF + 0x0001) \pmod{2^{16} - 1} = 0x10000 \pmod{0xFFFF} = 0x0001$$

## 3.5 Worked Example #4 — A Receiver Detects an Error

Suppose during transmission the second word is corrupted from `0x003C` to `0x0040` (one bit flipped low-to-high). The receiver now sees:

`0x4500`, `0x0040`, `0x1C46`, `0x4000`, `0x4006`, `0x1E77`.

**Step 1:**

$$0x4500 + 0x0040 = 0x4540$$

**Step 2:**

$$0x4540 + 0x1C46 = 0x6186$$

**Step 3:**

$$0x6186 + 0x4000 = 0xA186$$

**Step 4:**

$$0xA186 + 0x4006 = 0xE18C$$

**Step 5:**

$$0xE18C + 0x1E77 = 0x10003$$

We get a 17-bit result with carry-out `1`.

**Step 6 — Truncate to 16 bits:** `0x0003`.

**Step 7 — End-around carry:** `0x0003 + 0x0001 = 0x0004`.

**Result: `0x0004`**, which is **not** `0xFFFF`. The receiver correctly **detects the error and discards the datagram**.

## 3.6 Worked Example #5 — UDP Header Checksum with Pseudo-Header

Consider a UDP datagram with:
- Source IP: `192.168.1.1` = `0xC0A80101`
- Dest IP: `192.168.1.2` = `0xC0A80102`
- Protocol: `17` = `0x11`
- UDP length: `12` bytes = `0x000C`
- UDP header: source port `0x1234`, dest port `0x5678`, length `0x000C`, checksum `0x0000`
- Data: two bytes `0xDE 0xAD` → word `0xDEAD`

**Step 1: Form 16-bit words for the pseudo-header:**

- `0xC0A8`, `0x0101`
- `0xC0A8`, `0x0102`
- `0x0011` (zero + protocol)
- `0x000C` (UDP length)

**Step 2: Form 16-bit words for UDP header:**

- `0x1234`, `0x5678`, `0x000C`, `0x0000` (checksum=0 during computation)

**Step 3: Form 16-bit words for payload:**

- `0xDEAD`

**Step 4: Sum all words (one's complement, end-around carry each time):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= 0xC0A8 + 0x0101 = 0xC1A9 \\
S_2 &= 0xC1A9 + 0xC0A8 = 0x18251 \rightarrow \text{carry} \rightarrow 0x8252 \\
S_3 &= 0x8252 + 0x0102 = 0x8354 \\
S_4 &= 0x8354 + 0x0011 = 0x8365 \\
S_5 &= 0x8365 + 0x000C = 0x8371 \\
S_6 &= 0x8371 + 0x1234 = 0x95A5 \\
S_7 &= 0x95A5 + 0x5678 = 0xEC1D \\
S_8 &= 0xEC1D + 0x000C = 0xEC29 \\
S_9 &= 0xEC29 + 0x0000 = 0xEC29 \\
S_{10} &= 0xEC29 + 0xDEAD = 0xCAD6 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Final sum $C = 0xCAD6$.**

**Step 6: Transmitted checksum:**

$$\bar{C} = \sim 0xCAD6 = 0x3529$$

**Step 7: Receiver verification** — Receiver re-sums all words but substitutes the transmitted `0x3529` for the checksum field. The result will be `0xFFFF`. ✓

## 3.7 Detection Power Analysis

The Internet Checksum's error detection capability depends on the error pattern:

| Error Pattern | Detection Probability | Notes |
|---------------|----------------------|-------|
| Single bit-flip | $100\%$ | Always caught |
| Two bit-flips in same word | $100\%$ | Always caught |
| Two bit-flips symmetric (e.g., $+x$ and $-x$ in different words) | **Not caught** | Classic checksum weakness |
| Burst of length $\leq 16$ bits spanning one word | $100\%$ | Caught |
| Burst of length $> 16$ bits | High, not 100% | Depends on alignment |
| Reordering of two words with same value | **Not caught** | (Same value gives same sum) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sender-Side Checksum Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Message to send"] --> B["Break message into 16-bit words W1 to WN"]
    B --> C["Set Checksum Field to 0x0000"]
    C --> D["Initialize sum S = 0x0000"]
    D --> E["For each word Wi"]
    E --> F["S = S + Wi in 1C arithmetic"]
    F --> G{"More words?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> E
    G -- "No" --> H["Complement S to get Checksum = NOT S"]
    H --> I["Insert Checksum into header field"]
    I --> J["Transmit datagram on network"]
    J --> K["End"]
```

## 4.2 Receiver-Side Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Datagram received"] --> B["Extract all 16-bit words including Checksum field"]
    B --> C["Initialize sum V = 0x0000"]
    C --> D["For each 16-bit word Wi"]
    D --> E["V = V + Wi in 1C arithmetic"]
    E --> F{"More words?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> D
    F -- "No" --> G{"Is V equal to 0xFFFF?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["ACCEPT datagram - no error detected"]
    G -- "No" --> I["DISCARD datagram - error detected"]
    H --> J["End"]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Checksum in TCP/IP Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["Application Layer"]
        APP1["Application data stream"]
    end

    subgraph TRANS["Transport Layer - TCP or UDP"]
        T1["Segment header construction"]
        T2["Pseudo-header formation with IP addresses and protocol"]
        T3["Checksum computation engine - 1C adder"]
        T4["Payload concatenation"]
    end

    subgraph NET["Network Layer - IPv4"]
        N1["IPv4 header with TTL, ID, Flags, Fragment offset"]
        N2["Header checksum field - 16 bit"]
    end

    subgraph LINK["Data Link Layer"]
        L1["Frame encapsulation"]
        L2["Frame Check Sequence - CRC32 at hardware level"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["Physical Layer"]
        P1["Bit transmission over medium"]
    end

    APP1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> P1
```

## 4.4 Checksum Property: Bitwise Inverse Sum Equals 0xFFFF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["16-bit word Wi = 1011 0010 1100 1101"] --> C["1C Addition: Wi + NOT Wi"]
    B["16-bit word NOT Wi = 0100 1101 0011 0010"] --> C
    C --> D["Sum = 1111 1111 1111 1111"]
    D --> E["Result equals 0xFFFF"]
    E --> F["Verified No Error"]
```

## 4.5 Pseudo-Header Inclusion for TCP/UDP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Source IP 4 bytes"] --> P["Pseudo Header 12 bytes"]
    B["Destination IP 4 bytes"] --> P
    C["Zero byte 1 byte"] --> P
    D["Protocol 1 byte"] --> P
    E["Segment Length 2 bytes"] --> P
    P --> X["Concatenated for Checksum Computation"]
    F["TCP/UDP Header 20 or 8 bytes"] --> X
    G["Payload data"] --> X
    X --> Y["16-bit One's Complement Sum"]
    Y --> Z["Stored Checksum Field"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: Define the Internet Checksum. Why is one's complement arithmetic used instead of two's complement?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Internet Checksum** is a 16-bit error-detection field used in TCP, UDP, and IPv4 headers. It is computed by summing all 16-bit words of the protected data using **one's complement arithmetic**, then transmitting the bitwise complement of that sum.

One's complement arithmetic is used because of the **constant-verification property**: when the receiver sums all words *including* the stored checksum, the result for an error-free datagram is always **`0xFFFF`** (all ones). This elegant property is not achievable with two's complement, which would yield a less predictable verification value. Additionally, one's complement is symmetric under bitwise inversion, which simplifies software implementation on the early ARPANET routers.

*(Examiner's note: 3-mark questions need crisp definitions; mention RFC 1071 for full credit.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: What is the role of the pseudo-header in TCP/UDP checksum computation? List its fields.**

**Model Answer:**

The **pseudo-header** is a 12-byte auxiliary structure used during the checksum computation of TCP and UDP segments. It is **not transmitted** over the network but is constructed by both sender and receiver from the IPv4 header.

**Purpose:** It binds the transport-layer checksum to the network-layer source and destination IP addresses, ensuring that a misdelivered packet (one with mismatched IP addresses) is detected as erroneous.

**Fields (IPv4):**
- Source IPv4 Address (4 bytes)
- Destination IPv4 Address (4 bytes)
- Zero byte (1 byte)
- Protocol number (1 byte — 6 for TCP, 17 for UDP)
- TCP/UDP Length (2 bytes)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the algorithm for computing the Internet Checksum at the sender. Include a note on the end-around carry rule. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a UDP datagram with the following 16-bit words (in hex) that are to be protected by a checksum: `0x4500`, `0x003C`, `0x1C46`, `0x4000`, `0x4006`, `0xBEEF`. Compute the transmitted checksum value. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Sender Algorithm and End-Around Carry (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Segmentation:** Divide the message (header + payload, with the checksum field set to 0) into a sequence of 16-bit words $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_N$. If the message length is odd, pad with one zero byte at the end.

**Step 2 — Initialize Sum:** Set the accumulator $S = 0$.

**Step 3 — One's Complement Addition:** For each 16-bit word $W_i$:
$$S \leftarrow S + W_i \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

The end-around carry rule states that if a carry-out is generated from the most significant bit during the addition, this carry is **wrapped around and added back to the least significant bits**. Equivalently:

$$S_{\text{new}} = (S + W_i) \mod 2^{16} + \text{carry}_{\text{MSB}}$$

**Step 4 — Finalize:** After all words are added, the transmitted checksum $C$ is the bitwise complement of $S$:

$$C = \bar{S} = \sim S = (0xFFFF - S)$$

**Step 5 — Transmit** the message with $C$ placed in the checksum field.

**Why end-around carry?** It is the defining feature of one's complement addition. It ensures the result is in the range $[0, 2^{16} - 1]$ and provides the symmetry that makes the receiver verification yield the constant `0xFFFF`.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given words: `0x4500`, `0x003C`, `0x1C46`, `0x4000`, `0x4006`, `0xBEEF`.

Performing one's complement addition step by step (we ignore any potential carries until the end for clarity, then apply end-around carry):

**Step 1:** `0x4500 + 0x003C = 0x453C` (no carry)

**Step 2:** `0x453C + 0x1C46 = 0x6182` (no carry)

**Step 3:** `0x6182 + 0x4000 = 0xA182` (no carry)

**Step 4:** `0xA182 + 0x4006 = 0xE188` (no carry)

**Step 5:** `0xE188 + 0xBEEF = 0x1A077`

Here a carry-out `1` is generated. Apply end-around carry:
$$0xA077 + 0x0001 = 0xA078$$

**Final sum:** $S = 0xA078$

**Step 6 — Compute checksum:** Take the one's complement of $S$:

$$C = \bar{S} = \sim 0xA078 = 0x5F87$$

**Transmitted checksum: `0x5F87`.**

**Verification (sanity check):** Receiver re-sums the original five words plus the transmitted `0x5F87`:

$$0x4500 + 0x003C + 0x1C46 + 0x4000 + 0x4006 + 0x5F87 = 0xFFFF \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Describe the receiver-side verification procedure for the Internet Checksum. Prove mathematically that an error-free datagram yields the constant `0xFFFF` at the receiver. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 16-bit checksum is computed over four 16-bit data words: `0xA5A5`, `0x1234`, `0xFFFF`, `0x00C3`. Determine: (i) the value to be stored in the checksum field by the sender, and (ii) what the receiver will compute if bit 0 of the second word is flipped (i.e., `0x1234` becomes `0x1235`) during transmission. Will the error be detected? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Receiver Procedure and Mathematical Proof (7 marks):**

**Receiver Procedure:**

1. Extract all 16-bit words $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_N$ from the received datagram, including the checksum field $C'$ as the last word.
2. Initialize sum $V = 0$.
3. For each word $W_i$ (including the checksum field), perform one's complement addition:
$$V \leftarrow V + W_i \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$
4. If $V = 0xFFFF$, the datagram is **accepted** (no error detected). If $V \neq 0xFFFF$, the datagram is **discarded**.

**Mathematical Proof of the Constant `0xFFFF`:**

Let $C$ be the sender's pre-checksum sum (with the checksum field treated as zero):
$$C = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \right) \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

The transmitted checksum is:
$$C' = \bar{C} = (2^{16} - 1) - C$$

The receiver computes:
$$V = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i + C' \right) \pmod{2^{16} - 1} = (C + (2^{16} - 1) - C) \pmod{2^{16} - 1}$$

$$V = (2^{16} - 1) \pmod{2^{16} - 1} = 2^{16} - 1 = 0xFFFF \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Part (i): Sender checksum**

Given words: `0xA5A5`, `0x1234`, `0xFFFF`, `0x00C3`.

**Step 1:** `0xA5A5 + 0x1234 = 0xB7D9` (no carry)

**Step 2:** `0xB7D9 + 0xFFFF = 0x1B7D8`

Carry-out `1`. Truncate to `0xB7D8`, then add carry: `0xB7D8 + 0x0001 = 0xB7D9`.

**Step 3:** `0xB7D9 + 0x00C3 = 0xB89C` (no carry)

**Final sum:** $S = 0xB89C$

**Transmitted checksum:** $C' = \bar{S} = \sim 0xB89C = 0x4763$

**Part (ii): Receiver detects flipped bit**

The second word is corrupted from `0x1234` to `0x1235`. The receiver sums:

`0xA5A5`, `0x1235` (corrupted), `0xFFFF`, `0x00C3`, `0x4763` (received checksum).

**Step 1:** `0xA5A5 + 0x1235 = 0xB7DA` (no carry)

**Step 2:** `0xB7DA + 0xFFFF = 0x1B7D9`

Carry-out `1`. Truncate: `0xB7D9`, plus carry: `0xB7D9 + 0x0001 = 0xB7DA`.

**Step 3:** `0xB7DA + 0x00C3 = 0xB89D` (no carry)

**Step 4:** `0xB89D + 0x4763 = 0x10000`

Carry-out `1`. Truncate: `0x0000`, plus carry: `0x0000 + 0x0001 = 0x0001`.

**Result: $V = 0x0001$**

Since $0x0001 \neq 0xFFFF$, the error **is detected** and the datagram is **discarded**.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**  
> 1. **Forgetting End-Around Carry:** Students often perform ordinary binary addition and ignore the wrap-around carry from bit 16. This yields a wrong sum and consequently a wrong checksum. Marks deducted: typically 2–3 in a 7-mark sub-question.  
> 2. **Confusing Sender and Receiver Logic:** The checksum *stored* in the field is the **complement** of the sum. The *receiver verification* sum is **all words including the checksum** — not all words excluding it.  
> 3. **Skipping Pseudo-Header:** For TCP/UDP checksum questions, omitting the pseudo-header is a 1–2 mark penalty. Always construct the 12-byte pseudo-header from the IP datagram context.  
> 4. **Padding Errors:** If the payload length is odd, students often forget to append a zero byte to make 16-bit alignment.  
> 5. **Mixing Up Hex Digits:** When adding in 16-bit hex, students sometimes confuse B/8, D/0, etc. Always show intermediate sums to claim step marks.  
> 6. **Missing the `0xFFFF` Verification Rule:** When asked "is the datagram valid?", the answer is not "we got X" — it is "since $X \neq 0xFFFF$, the datagram is rejected."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Internet Checksum (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Internet Checksum** is a **16-bit** error-detection field used in IPv4, TCP, UDP, ICMP, and IGMP.
- ✅ It is based on **One's Complement Arithmetic** (modulus $2^{16} - 1$), not Two's Complement.
- ✅ The **end-around carry** rule: any carry-out from bit 15 wraps back and is added to bit 0.
- ✅ **Sender procedure:** Set checksum field to 0, sum all 16-bit words in 1C, store the **complement** of the sum.
- ✅ **Receiver verification:** Sum all 16-bit words *including* the stored checksum in 1C. Result must equal `0xFFFF` for an error-free datagram.
- ✅ The constant `0xFFFF` property is a direct consequence of $C + \bar{C} = 2^{16} - 1$.
- ✅ **Pseudo-header** is required for TCP/UDP and contains Source IP, Dest IP, Zero, Protocol, and Segment Length (12 bytes total).
- ✅ **Padding rule:** If the data length is odd, append a zero byte to align to 16-bit word boundary.
- ✅ The Internet Checksum is **weak** compared to CRC — it can miss certain error patterns (symmetric two-bit errors, equal-word reordering).
- ✅ IPv6 **does not** use the Internet Checksum at the network layer; it relies entirely on link-layer and upper-layer checksums.
- ✅ The checksum is **mandatory** for IPv4, TCP, and ICMP, but **optional** for UDP (though recommended).
- ✅ The verification rule "result = `0xFFFF` ⇒ no error" is the single most tested KTU property — practice multiple numerical examples to internalize it.
- ✅ When asked to "verify" a received datagram, always show all 16-bit additions explicitly with end-around carry applied at each step that produces a carry-out.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Address Mapping - ARP Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Address Mapping - ARP Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)** is a link-layer (Layer 2) protocol defined in **RFC 826 (Plummer, 1982)** that operates dynamically between the Network Access Layer and the Internet Layer of the TCP/IP protocol suite. Its sole function is to perform a two-way translation between two fundamentally different addressing schemes used simultaneously in modern packet-switched networks:

- **Logical (Network-layer) Addresses** — 32-bit **IPv4 addresses** (assigned administratively, used for end-to-end routing across networks).
- **Physical (Data-link-layer) Addresses** — 48-bit **MAC (Media Access Control) addresses** (hard-coded by the NIC manufacturer, used for hop-by-hop delivery on a single LAN segment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "ARP is a protocol used to map a known IP address to its corresponding physical (MAC) address on a local broadcast network. It allows a host to determine the hardware address of a destination host when only its IP address is known."

In essence, ARP bridges the "logical-to-physical" gap, providing the dynamic glue that lets an IP-only world communicate through MAC-only hardware.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Directory" Intuition

Imagine you are **Alice**, working in a large office building, and you need to deliver a sealed envelope to **Bob**.

- You only know **Bob's name and floor number (logical address — IP)**.
- But the building's internal mailroom requires **Bob's office number and desk code (physical address — MAC)** to physically hand over the letter.

What do you do?

1. You shout over the office PA system: *"Attention! I have a packet for **Bob on Floor 7**. Whoever sits at Bob's desk, please send me your desk code."* — This is the **ARP Request** (a broadcast).
2. **Bob** hears the announcement, recognizes that the floor matches him, and privately whispers back: *"Hi Alice, my desk code is 7B-204."* — This is the **ARP Reply** (a unicast).
3. You then **write down Bob's desk code in your personal notebook** so you don't have to ask again for the next 20 minutes. — This is the **ARP Cache (ARP Table)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a PA announcement is heard by *everyone* in the building (broadcast), an ARP Request is delivered to **every device on the local subnet**, but only the device whose IP matches the query will respond.

## 1.3 Why ARP Is Strictly Required

A host that wants to send an IP datagram to a destination IP goes through the following logic:

1. Check: *Is the destination IP in the **same subnet** as mine?*
   - **Yes** → Resolve destination's MAC directly using ARP.
   - **No** → Resolve the MAC of the **default gateway (router)**, not the final destination.
2. The resolved MAC is then placed in the Ethernet frame's destination address field, while the IP packet's destination address remains unchanged.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** "ARP resolves any IP to a MAC, even across the Internet." — This is **false**. ARP operates *only* on the local broadcast domain (one LAN segment). It cannot cross routers, because routers do not forward Layer 2 broadcast frames by default.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for KTU Board Emphasis)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **ARP Request** | `request` | A broadcast frame (`ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff`) querying "Who has IP X? Tell me your MAC." |
| **ARP Reply** | `reply` | A unicast frame containing the IP-to-MAC binding of the responder. |
| **ARP Cache** | $\mathcal{C}_{ARP}$ | A volatile RAM table maintained by every host mapping recently used IPs to MACs. |
| **ARP Table** | — | Synonymous with ARP cache; the operational database. |
| **Gratuitous ARP** | `GARP` | A host broadcasting its own IP-to-MAC binding without being asked (used for duplicate detection and failover). |
| **Proxy ARP** | — | A router answering ARP requests on behalf of another host on a different subnet. |
| **RARP** | `Reverse ARP` | Maps a known MAC to an IP (opposite direction; largely obsolete, replaced by BOOTP/DHCP). |
| **Inverse ARP (InARP)** | — | Used over Frame Relay/ATM to discover the IP of a remote DLCI/PVC endpoint. |
| **Hardware Type** | $H_{type}$ | 1 = Ethernet (10 Mbps); 6 = IEEE 802.11; 15 = Frame Relay; etc. |
| **Operation Code** | $Op$ | 1 = Request, 2 = Reply, 3 = RARP Request, 4 = RARP Reply. |

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> This topic directly satisfies the following Course Outcomes (COs) under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned scheme:
> - **CO2:** *Illustrate the working of address mapping and resolution protocols in the TCP/IP suite.*
> - **CO3:** *Analyze the operational behavior of supporting protocols (ICMP, ARP, RARP, DHCP) in an internetworked environment.*

**Bloom's Taxonomy Levels Covered:** *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze* (K1–K4).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Four Phases of ARP

The ARP process can be formally decomposed into **four discrete operational phases**, each of which is a frequently tested KTU question.

### Phase 1 — Cache Inspection
When the Network layer hands a datagram to the Data Link layer, the host's ARP module first consults the **ARP cache ($\mathcal{C}_{ARP}$)**. A typical cache entry has the tuple form:

$$\text{Entry} = (IP_{dest}, MAC_{dest}, IF_{iface}, Type, Age)$$

where `Type ∈ {Dynamic, Static}` and `Age` is a countdown timer (default **15–20 minutes** on most OSes).

- If a **valid (non-expired) entry** exists for $IP_{dest}$ → Use it directly. *No broadcast needed.* (Efficient case.)
- If **no entry** or **expired entry** exists → Proceed to Phase 2.

### Phase 2 — Broadcast the ARP Request
The host constructs a 28-byte ARP message and encapsulates it in an Ethernet frame with:
- **Destination MAC:** $\texttt{ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff}$ (the Ethernet broadcast address).
- **EtherType:** $\texttt{0x0806}$ (designates ARP, not IP).
- **Sender MAC:** The host's own MAC.
- **Target MAC:** $\texttt{00:00:00:00:00:00}$ (unknown — to be filled in by the responder).

The frame is flooded to **every port on the LAN** (a Layer 2 broadcast).

### Phase 3 — Reception and Reply Generation
Every host on the LAN receives the broadcast. Each host compares the `Target Protocol Address` field with its own IP:
- **Mismatch** → Silently discards the frame.
- **Match** → Updates its own ARP cache with `(Sender IP, Sender MAC)` (passive learning), then constructs an **ARP Reply**:
  - **Destination MAC:** The requester's MAC (unicast).
  - **Sender fields:** The responder's IP and MAC.
  - **Target fields:** Copied from the requester's sender fields.
  - **Operation Code:** 2 (Reply).

### Phase 4 — Cache Update and Forwarding
The original requester receives the ARP Reply, extracts the MAC, and inserts a new dynamic entry into its ARP cache:

$$\mathcal{C}_{ARP} \leftarrow \mathcal{C}_{ARP} \cup \{ (IP_{dest}, MAC_{dest}, IF_{out}, \text{Dynamic}, 900\text{s}) \}$$

The IP datagram is then encapsulated into an Ethernet frame with the newly resolved MAC and transmitted.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although ARP is conceptually a *protocol* and not a *numerical formula*, the following quantitative relationships are highly examinable under KTU's "solve" and "compute" question patterns.

### Table 2.1 — Quantitative Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Quantity | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ARP packet size (IPv4 over Ethernet)** | $L_{ARP} = 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 4 = \mathbf{28\ \text{bytes}}$ | bytes |
| 2 | **Ethernet frame total (ARP)** | $L_{frame} = 14_{header} + 28_{ARP} + 4_{FCS} = \mathbf{46\ \text{bytes}}$ | minimum payload is 46 B; padded if smaller |
| 3 | **Maximum payload in standard Ethernet** | $\mathbf{1500\ \text{bytes}}$ | limits upper-layer MTU |
| 4 | **Cache entry timeout (RFC default)** | $T_{cache} = \mathbf{15\ to\ 20\ minutes}$ | OS-dependent; can be statically pinned |
| 5 | **Broadcast domain reach (Layer 2)** | $R_{ARP} = \mathbf{1\ \text{LAN\ segment}}$ | ARP does NOT cross routers |
| 6 | **Address resolution lookup complexity (worst case)** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | linear scan of cache; hashing possible |
| 7 | **Reverse mapping probability of conflict (with $\mathcal{H}$ hosts)** | $P_{col} \approx 1 - e^{-\mathcal{H}^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{48})}$ | birthday-bound; practically 0 |
| 8 | **Cache size (typical Windows)** | $\vert \mathcal{C}_{ARP} \vert \le \mathbf{1024\ entries}$ | configurable via registry |
| 9 | **Maximum hops an ARP request can traverse** | $\mathbf{0\ \text{routers}}$ | broadcast frames are dropped at L3 |
| 10 | **Operation code values** | $Op \in \{\mathbf{1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 9}\}$ | Request, Reply, RARP-Req, RARP-Rep, InARP-Req, InARP-Rep |

### Table 2.2 — ARP Packet Header Field Layout

| Field | Width (bits) | Value (typical) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Type** ($H_{type}$) | 16 | $\mathbf{1}$ (Ethernet) | Identifies the link-layer technology. |
| **Protocol Type** ($P_{type}$) | 16 | $\mathbf{0x0800}$ (IPv4) | Identifies the upper-layer protocol. |
| **Hardware Address Length (HLEN)** | 8 | $\mathbf{6}$ | Length of MAC in bytes. |
| **Protocol Address Length (PLEN)** | 8 | $\mathbf{4}$ | Length of IP in bytes. |
| **Operation Code** ($Op$) | 16 | $\mathbf{1\ or\ 2}$ | Request or Reply. |
| **Sender Hardware Address (SHA)** | 48 | Host's MAC | MAC of sender. |
| **Sender Protocol Address (SPA)** | 32 | Host's IP | IP of sender. |
| **Target Hardware Address (THA)** | 48 | $\mathbf{00:00:00:00:00:00}$ (Req) $\vert$ Resolved MAC (Rep) | MAC of target. |
| **Target Protocol Address (TPA)** | 32 | Target's IP | IP being queried. |

### Table 2.3 — Comparison Matrix of ARP Variants

| Variant | Maps From | Maps To | Initiated By | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ARP** | IP | MAC | Source host | Core topic. |
| **RARP** | MAC | IP | Boot-time client (diskless) | Historical; superseded by DHCP. |
| **InARP** | DLCI/PVC | IP | Frame Relay/ATM endpoint | Mostly theoretical in KTU. |
| **Proxy ARP** | IP (remote) | Router's MAC | Router (on behalf) | Used in mobile IP and stub networks. |
| **GARP** | Self-IP | Self-MAC | Any host announcing itself | Duplicate IP detection; VRRP/HA failover. |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How ARP is Used |
|---|---|
| **Local Network Diagnostics** | `arp -a` (Windows/Linux) reveals the cache for troubleshooting. |
| **Network Security** | ARP spoofing / poisoning underpins MITM attacks (hence ARP's role in cybersecurity curricula). |
| **Failover Clustering** | Gratuitous ARP is used by backup nodes to seize a virtual IP's traffic instantly. |
| **Data Center Networking** | ARP-suppression in VXLAN/EVPN environments to prevent flooding. |
| **Embedded / IoT** | Lightweight ARP clients in microcontrollers using lwIP, Contiki, etc. |
| **DHCP** | DHCP uses ARP with the "$\texttt{CHADDR}$" field to detect IP conflicts before lease. |

---

## 2.4 Why ARP Must Be Considered at Every Layer

> [!NOTE]
> ARP is the **only** protocol in the standard TCP/IP stack that operates **across** two layers — it is generated by the IP module (Layer 3) but delivered as a Link-layer broadcast (Layer 2). This cross-layer nature is a frequently repeated KTU interview question.

The complete addressing decision tree is:

$$
\text{Is } destIP \in \text{my subnet?} \;\;
\begin{cases}
\text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{ARP}(destIP) \rightarrow \text{Use as frame destination MAC.} \\
\text{No} \Rightarrow \text{ARP}(gatewayIP) \rightarrow \text{Use gateway's MAC as frame destination.}
\end{cases}
$$
