## 4.6 Diagram 6: Ethernet Frame Format (relevant to link types)

```
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+--------+
| Preamble|   SFD    |  Dest MAC |  Src MAC  |  EtherType |  Payload  |  FCS    |
| 7 bytes  | 1 byte  | 6 bytes  | 6 bytes  | 2 bytes   | 46-1500 B | 4 bytes |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+--------+
   101010...    10101011  XX:XX:XX  XX:XX:XX   0x0800=IPv4      CRC-32

Total Frame: 64 bytes (min) to 1518 bytes (max)
If EtherType/Length >= 1536 -> indicates protocol (e.g., 0x0800 = IPv4)
If < 1536 -> indicates length (IEEE 802.3 original)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading hint:** In **broadcast Ethernet**, every station reads the **Dest MAC**. In **point-to-point (PPP)**, there is **no MAC address** — only a Protocol field (e.g., `0x0021` for IP, `0xC021` for LCP). This is the structural reason link types exist.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex communication modes. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Direction | Simultaneity | Example |
|------|-----------|--------------|---------|
| **Simplex** | One-way only | Not applicable | TV broadcast, Keyboard → Computer |
| **Half-Duplex** | Two-way | **Not simultaneous** (one at a time) | Walkie-talkie, traditional Ethernet hub |
| **Full-Duplex** | Two-way | **Simultaneous** (both at once) | Telephone call, switched full-duplex Ethernet |

**Key point:** Half-duplex uses **one channel** shared in time; full-duplex uses **two physical channels** (or echo cancellation on one). Full-duplex doubles the effective aggregate throughput and eliminates collision detection (CSMA/CD is disabled).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a Point-to-Point link? How does it differ from a Broadcast (Multipoint) link? List one protocol used with each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Point-to-Point (P2P) link** connects exactly **two** devices over a dedicated channel. No addressing is needed at the link layer since the receiver is implicit. Example protocol: **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)** or **HDLC**.

A **Broadcast (Multipoint) link** is shared by **multiple** devices; a frame sent by one is received by **all** stations. Each station filters frames by destination MAC address. Example protocol: **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** (with CSMA/CD) or **IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi** (with CSMA/CA).

**Core differences:**
- P2P: 2 devices, no addressing required, no collisions.
- Broadcast: N devices, MAC addressing required, collisions possible → MAC sublayer needed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of links** based on topology in computer networks. With neat diagrams, describe **Point-to-Point**, **Broadcast**, and **Point-to-Multipoint** links. Mention the protocols used in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum frame size** required for collision detection in classic 10BASE5 Ethernet, given maximum cable length = 2500 m, propagation speed = $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, and bit rate = 10 Mbps. Why is the actual minimum frame size in IEEE 802.3 set to **64 bytes**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The three topological classifications of network links are:

1. **Point-to-Point (P2P) Link** *(2 marks)*
   - Connects exactly two nodes.
   - Examples: PPP link between two routers, dial-up modem connection, SONET/SDH circuit.
   - **Protocols:** PPP, HDLC.
   - **Advantage:** Dedicated bandwidth, simple framing, no addressing needed.
   - **Disadvantage:** Wastes bandwidth if traffic is bursty; expensive at scale.

2. **Broadcast (Multipoint) Link** *(2 marks)*
   - Multiple nodes share the same physical medium; all hear all transmissions.
   - Each station filters frames by destination MAC address.
   - Examples: classic Ethernet bus (10BASE2, 10BASE5), modern Wi-Fi (802.11).
   - **Protocols:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) with CSMA/CD, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) with CSMA/CA.
   - **Advantage:** Cost-effective for many nodes; easy to add new stations.
   - **Disadvantage:** Collisions, contention, security risks.

3. **Point-to-Multipoint (P2MP) Link** *(2 marks)*
   - One central node (root/head-end) communicates with multiple leaf nodes.
   - Leaf nodes do not communicate directly with each other via the shared medium.
   - Examples: 4G/LTE cell tower to UEs, GPON (fiber to the home), Wi-Fi AP infrastructure mode.
   - **Protocols:** OFDMA, TDMA, DOCSIS.
   - **Advantage:** Efficient use of central resource (e.g., one fiber, one tower).
   - **Disadvantage:** Central node is a single point of failure; requires scheduling.

4. **Diagrams** *(1 mark)* — Draw the three configurations with proper labels.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation: Minimum Frame Size for Collision Detection** *(5 marks)*

**Given:** $d = 2500$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, $R = 10$ Mbps.

**Step 1: One-way propagation delay**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 12.5 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2: Round-trip propagation delay (worst case — collision at the far end)**

$$
T_{rt} = 2 \times T_p = 2 \times 12.5 \, \mu s = 25 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Condition for collision detection**

For the sender to still be transmitting when the collision signal returns, the frame transmission time must be $\geq$ the round-trip delay:

$$
\frac{L_{min}}{R} \geq 2 T_p
$$

**Step 4: Solve**

$$
L_{min} = 2 \times T_p \times R = 2 \times 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^6 = 250 \text{ bits}
$$

**Why 64 bytes (512 bits) is the actual standard** *(2 marks)*

The 250-bit theoretical minimum must be increased to **512 bits (64 bytes)** in IEEE 802.3 to account for:
- **Repeater delays:** Up to 5 repeaters in series (4 segments) may exist in 10BASE5.
- **Transceiver and cable turnaround delays** (~10–15 bit times).
- **Jam signal duration** (32 bits) — must be sent while still transmitting.
- **Safety margin** to account for signal rise times, timing jitter, and component tolerances.
- **Frame format overhead** — the 64-byte frame includes 14-byte header + 4-byte trailer, leaving a minimum 46-byte payload (any shorter payload is padded).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of **CSMA/CD** in a broadcast Ethernet network. What is **Binary Exponential Backoff**? How many collisions can occur before a frame is dropped? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A point-to-point link has bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps and one-way propagation delay $T_p = 20$ ms. A frame of size $L = 1000$ bits is sent using **Stop-and-Wait ARQ**.
   (i) Calculate the **transmission time** and **total cycle time**.
   (ii) Compute the **link utilization** and the **bandwidth-delay product**.
   (iii) What is the **minimum window size** needed to fully utilize the link using a sliding window protocol? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**CSMA/CD Operation** *(4 marks — diagram + explanation)*

CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) is the MAC protocol used in half-duplex broadcast Ethernet. Steps:

1. **Carrier Sense:** A station that wants to transmit first listens to the medium. If the medium is **idle**, it begins transmitting immediately. If the medium is **busy**, it defers until it becomes idle.
2. **Collision Detection:** The station continues to monitor the medium **while transmitting**. If the signal voltage on the cable exceeds a threshold (indicating another station is also transmitting), a **collision** has occurred.
3. **Jam Signal:** Upon detecting a collision, the station stops transmitting and sends a **32-bit jam signal** to ensure that all other stations on the segment also detect the collision.
4. **Backoff:** Both stations wait a random amount of time before attempting retransmission, governed by the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm.

**Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** *(2 marks)*

After the $i$-th collision, the station chooses a random integer:

$$
k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^i - 1\}
$$

and waits $k$ slot times, where **1 slot time = 512 bit times = 51.2 $\mu$s at 10 Mbps**.

- After 1st collision: $k \in \{0, 1\}$
- After 2nd collision: $k \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$
- ...
- After 10th collision: $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 1023\}$
- After 16 collisions: **frame is dropped** and upper layer is notified.

**Frame Drop Limit** *(1 mark)*

A frame is dropped after **16 consecutive collisions** and the MAC layer reports failure to the upper layer (e.g., LLC).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $R = 1$ Mbps = $10^6$ bps, $T_p = 20$ ms, $L = 1000$ bits.

**(i) Transmission time and total cycle time** *(2 marks)*

Transmission time:
$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

Total cycle time (frame sent + ACK propagation, ignoring ACK size):
$$
T_{cycle} = T_t + 2 T_p = 1 \text{ ms} + 2 \times 20 \text{ ms} = 41 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Link utilization and BDP** *(3 marks)*

Link utilization:
$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{41} \approx 0.0244 = 2.44\%
$$

Bandwidth-Delay Product:
$$
BDP = R \times T_p = 10^6 \text{ bps} \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 20{,}000 \text{ bits} = 2500 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Interpretation:** The link can hold up to 20,000 bits "in flight", but Stop-and-Wait only puts 1000 bits at a time, wasting 97.56% of the capacity.

**(iii) Minimum window size for full utilization** *(2 marks)*

To fully utilize the pipe, the sender must have at least $2 \times BDP / L$ unacknowledged frames outstanding:

$$
W_{min} = \frac{2 \times BDP}{L} = \frac{2 \times 20{,}000}{1000} = 40 \text{ frames}
$$

With a window of 40, the sender never idles waiting for ACKs, and utilization $U \to 1$ (100%).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in "Types of Links" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Broadcast with Point-to-Multipoint:** A Wi-Fi infrastructure network (AP + stations) is technically **P2MP**, not broadcast — because stations do not directly hear each other; they only hear the AP. *(−2 marks if confused)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting to specify units** in latency/throughput derivations. Always write "ms" or "$\mu$s" after every time. *(−0.5 to 1 mark per omission)*
>
> 3. **Mixing up CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA:** CSMA/CD is for **wired** Ethernet (collision detection); CSMA/CA is for **wireless** (collision avoidance — because wireless stations cannot detect collisions while transmitting). *(−2 marks if swapped)*
>
> 4. **Wrong propagation speed:** Always state $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s for copper/fiber unless told otherwise. Using $3 \times 10^8$ m/s (vacuum) is incorrect for guided media.
>
> 5. **Minimum frame size derivation:** The 250-bit theoretical value is **not** the answer — the **512-bit (64-byte)** IEEE 802.3 value with reasoning is. Examiners expect both.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the 16-collision limit** in CSMA/CD BEB answer.
>
> 7. **Writing "broadcast = wireless"** — incorrect. Wireless is the **medium**; broadcast/P2P/P2MP are **topological classifications** that can apply to either wired or wireless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Links**

### Topological Classification
- ✅ **Point-to-Point (P2P):** Two devices, dedicated channel, no MAC addressing, protocols = **PPP, HDLC**.
- ✅ **Broadcast (Multipoint):** Many devices, shared medium, MAC addressing required, protocols = **Ethernet (CSMA/CD), Wi-Fi (CSMA/CA)**.
- ✅ **Point-to-Multipoint (P2MP):** One central → many leaves, no leaf-to-leaf, protocols = **LTE/OFDMA, GPON, satellite**.

### Direction-of-Flow Classification
- ✅ **Simplex:** One-way (TV, sensor).
- ✅ **Half-Duplex:** Two-way, one at a time (walkie-talkie, old hub).
- ✅ **Full-Duplex:** Two-way, simultaneous (phone, switched Ethernet) — uses **separate pairs** or **echo cancellation**.

### Key Formulas
- ✅ $T_p = d/v$, $T_t = L/R$, $U = T_t / (T_t + 2T_p)$, $BDP = R \times T_p$.
- ✅ Minimum frame size: $L_{min} = 2 \times T_p \times R$ → IEEE 802.3 standard = **64 bytes (512 bits)**.
- ✅ BEB: after $i$-th collision, $k \in \{0, \dots, 2^i - 1\}$ slot times; drop after **16 collisions**.

### Constants to Memorize
- ✅ $v_{copper} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; $v_{fiber} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ 1 slot time in 10 Mbps Ethernet = **51.2 $\mu$s** = 512 bit times.
- ✅ Jam signal = **32 bits**.
- ✅ Min Ethernet frame = **64 bytes**; max = **1518 bytes**.

### OSPF Link Types (Logical)
- ✅ **Stub** (one router), **Point-to-Point** (two routers), **Transient** (multi-access with DR/BDR), **Virtual** (tunnel to Area 0).

### Modern Realizations
- ✅ **Full-duplex switched Ethernet** is logically a P2P link — **CSMA/CD is disabled**.
- ✅ **GPON/4G/5G** are the canonical P2MP deployments in the field today.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi infrastructure mode** is P2MP; Wi-Fi **ad-hoc mode** is broadcast.

### Exam Mantra
- ✅ Always state the **topology** AND the **duplex mode** when describing a link.
- ✅ Always show **units** in derivations.
- ✅ Always state **assumed propagation speed** when given a length.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Types of Links | PEITT522 — Internetworking Using TCP/IP | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OSPF Packets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** OSPF Packets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) Packets** are the fundamental Protocol Data Units (PDUs) exchanged between OSPF-speaking routers to build and maintain link-state databases (LSDB), discover neighbors, synchronize topology information, and ultimately compute the shortest-path tree using Dijkstra's SPF algorithm. OSPF operates directly over IP (Protocol Number **89**) and defines **five distinct packet types**, each prefixed by a common **24-byte OSPF header**.

The five OSPF packet types (Type field values):

| Type Code | Packet Name | Primary Purpose |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **1** | Hello | Discover & maintain neighbor adjacencies |
| **2** | Database Description (DBD / DD) | Summarize LSDB contents during sync |
| **3** | Link State Request (LSR) | Request specific LSAs from neighbor |
| **4** | Link State Update (LSU) | Carry the requested LSAs to the neighbor |
| **5** | Link State Acknowledgment (LSAck) | Reliable delivery confirmation of LSUs |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Five-Letter Postal System"

Imagine a **city planning department** where multiple surveyors (routers) need to share map information about roads (links):

- 📬 **Hello Packet** → A surveyor walking around shouting *"Hello, I'm Surveyor-A, working in District-1"*. This is how they discover each other and keep their handshake alive every **10 seconds** (on broadcast networks) or **30 seconds** (on NBMA).
- 🗺️ **DBD Packet** → A *table of contents* of all map pages you currently own. Two surveyors exchange these first to know which maps they are missing.
- 📋 **LSR Packet** → A polite request: *"Please send me Map-Page #17 and Map-Page #22."*
- 📦 **LSU Packet** → The actual map pages being delivered in response to a request. This is the only packet that **carries real LSAs**.
- ✅ **LSAck Packet** → A signed receipt: *"I have received and verified Map-Page #17."* Without this, the sender retransmits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 5 types and not 1?** OSPF uses a reliable flooding mechanism for LSAs. Reliability requires acknowledgment, segmentation (requests), and retransmission — hence separate packet types instead of one monolithic message. This is fundamentally different from EIGRP, which uses a single reliable RTP packet with TLV-encoded data.

## 1.3 The Universal OSPF Header (24 Bytes — Every Packet)

Every OSPF packet — regardless of type — begins with this fixed structure:

| Field | Size | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| Version | 1 byte | OSPF version (**2** for OSPFv2, **3** for OSPFv3) |
| Type | 1 byte | Identifies the packet (1–5 as above) |
| Packet Length | 2 bytes | Total length of the OSPF packet in bytes (header + payload) |
| Router ID | 4 bytes | 32-bit unique identifier of the originating router |
| Area ID | 4 bytes | 32-bit identifier of the area the packet belongs to |
| Checksum | 2 bytes | Fletcher's checksum over the entire OSPF packet (excluding the 8-byte authentication field) |
| AuType | 2 bytes | Authentication type (**0** = None, **1** = Plain text, **2** = MD5, **3** = Key chain) |
| Authentication | 8 bytes | Authentication data depending on AuType |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type-1: OSPF Hello Packet

### Purpose
Used for **neighbor discovery** and as a **keepalive** mechanism. Contains parameters that must agree between neighbors before adjacency proceeds.

### Key Fields (after the 24-byte header)
- **Network Mask** (4 bytes) — Subnet mask of the sending interface
- **Hello Interval** (2 bytes) — Defaults to **10 s** (broadcast/P2P) or **30 s** (NBMA)
- **Options** (1 byte) — Capability flags (E-bit for external routing, etc.)
- **Router Priority** (1 byte) — Used in DR/BDR election (**0** = never become DR)
- **Dead Interval** (4 bytes) — Time before declaring neighbor down = **4 × Hello Interval**
- **Designated Router** (4 bytes) — IP of the DR
- **Backup Designated Router** (4 bytes) — IP of the BDR
- **Neighbor List** (variable) — Router IDs of all neighbors from which Hellos have been received

### Dead Timer Logic
$$\text{DeadInterval} = 4 \times \text{HelloInterval}$$

### Conditions for Adjacency Eligibility (Must Match!)
1. Subnet mask
2. Hello Interval
3. Dead Interval
4. Area ID
5. Authentication type & password
6. Stub Area flag
7. MTU (optional but recommended)

> [!NOTE]
> If any of these mismatch, the routers remain stuck in `Exstart` or `Init` state — a very common KTU exam scenario and a frequent troubleshooting question in lab exams.

## 2.2 Type-2: Database Description (DBD / DD) Packet

### Purpose
Allows two routers to **summarize** their entire LSDB to each other using a stable, ordered list of LSA headers (not full LSAs). This is analogous to a **table of contents**.

### Key Fields
- **Interface MTU** (2 bytes) — Largest IP datagram the interface can forward
- **Options** (1 byte)
- **IM/MS** flags (1 bit each) — *Init bit* (first DBD) and *More bit* (more DBDs follow)
- **DD Sequence Number** (4 bytes) — Used for reliable DBD exchange (master/slave handshake)
- **LSA Headers** (variable) — Each header is **20 bytes** (LS Type, LS ID, Advertising Router, LS Sequence, LS Checksum, LS Age)

## 2.3 Type-3: Link State Request (LSR) Packet

### Purpose
Sent by a router to request specific LSAs that it learned about via DBD but does **not yet have** in its own LSDB.

### Payload
A list of triples, each exactly **12 bytes**:
- **LS Type** (4 bytes) — e.g., Router-LSA (1), Network-LSA (2), Summary-LSA (3/4), External-LSA (5)
- **Link State ID** (4 bytes) — Identifier of the LSA
- **Advertising Router** (4 bytes) — Who originated the LSA

## 2.4 Type-4: Link State Update (LSU) Packet

### Purpose
The **only packet that actually carries full LSAs**. Sent in direct response to an LSR, and also used for unsolicited flooding of new LSAs (e.g., link up/down events).

### Structure
- **Number of LSAs** (4 bytes)
- **LSAs** (variable) — One or more complete Link State Advertisements

### Reliability Mechanism
$$\text{Retransmission Interval (Rxmt)} = 5 \text{ seconds (default)}$$

If an LSA is not acknowledged via LSAck within Rxmt seconds, it is **re-queued** for retransmission.

## 2.5 Type-5: Link State Acknowledgment (LSAck) Packet

### Purpose
Provides **explicit, reliable acknowledgment** of each received LSA. An LSAck packet contains a list of **LSA headers** (20 bytes each) — NOT full LSAs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LSAcks can be **bundled**: a single LSAck can acknowledge multiple LSAs from multiple neighbors, making flooding efficient.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Default Value | Unit |
|---------------------|------------|---------------|------|
| Dead Interval | $D = 4 \times H$ | 40 s (broadcast) / 120 s (NBMA) | seconds |
| Hello Interval (broadcast) | $H$ | **10** | seconds |
| Hello Interval (NBMA / P2MP) | $H$ | **30** | seconds |
| LSA Refresh Age | $T_{\text{refresh}}$ | **1800** (30 min) | seconds |
| MaxAge | $T_{\text{max}}$ | **3600** (1 hour) | seconds |
| Retransmission Interval | $R_{\text{xmt}}$ | **5** | seconds |
| LSAck delay per packet | — | **bounded** | ms |
| MTU for DBD exchange | $MTU$ | interface MTU | bytes |
| LSA Header Size | $S_{\text{LSAhdr}}$ | **20** | bytes |
| OSPF Header Size | $S_{\text{HDR}}$ | **24** | bytes |
| Common Header (Type 5 only) | $S_{\text{HDR+auth}}$ | **24** | bytes |
| LSReq Triple Size | $S_{\text{LSR}}$ | **12** | bytes |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISP Networks**: OSPF LSAck-based reliability ensures topology convergence within sub-second timers using BFD alongside.
- **Enterprise Datacenters**: Type-2 DBD packets enable OSPF graceful restarts (RFC 3623) by preserving adjacency during router reloads.
- **Service Providers**: The TTL field on OSPF packets (default **1**) limits flooding scope — critical for hierarchical OSPF design with multiple areas.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Dead Interval from Hello Interval

By RFC 2328, the **Dead Interval** is defined as the time after which, if no Hello is heard, a neighbor is declared down.

### Step 1 — RFC Statement
> *"A router declares the neighbor down when no Hello has been received for the Dead Interval."*

### Step 2 — Default Multiplier
The OSPF specification sets the multiplier to **4**. Therefore:

$$
D = K \times H \quad \text{where} \quad K = 4
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation (Broadcast Network)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 4 \times H \\
  &= 4 \times 10 \text{ s} \\
  &= 40 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation (NBMA Network)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 4 \times H \\
  &= 4 \times 30 \text{ s} \\
  &= 120 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
A higher Dead Interval (120 s on NBMA) is preferred on slower / hub-and-spoke NBMA topologies to avoid flapping neighbor adjacencies due to momentary jitter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LSA Lifetime and Refresh Logic

### Step 1 — Definitions from RFC 2328
- **LSA Age** begins at **0** when originated.
- It is incremented in transit and on every hop.
- An LSA is **refreshed** every $T_{\text{refresh}}$ seconds.

### Step 2 — Default Refresh Timer

$$
T_{\text{refresh}} = 1800 \text{ seconds} = 30 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Step 3 — MaxAge Threshold

$$
T_{\text{max}} = 3600 \text{ seconds} = 1 \text{ hour}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision Rule

$$
\text{Action} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Refresh (re-originate)} & \text{if Age} < T_{\text{refresh}} \\
\text{Invalidate} & \text{if Age} \geq T_{\text{max}} \\
\text{Continue} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example
A router originates an LSA at $t = 0$. It reaches a neighbor at $t = 0.4$ s (transit + queuing delay). Determine the **first refresh action** time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{refresh}} &= 0 + T_{\text{refresh}} \\
                   &= 0 + 1800 \text{ s} \\
                   &= 30 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The router must re-originate the LSA with sequence number incremented by 1 before $t_{\text{refresh}}$.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Walkthrough — Adjacency Formation State Machine

OSPF uses a deterministic state machine to bring up an adjacency. Below is the full derivation from `Down` to `Full`.

### Step 1 — Initial State: `Down`
No Hellos have been received. Router R1 sends a Hello to **224.0.0.5** (AllSPFRouters multicast).

### Step 2 — Transition: `Down → Init`
R2 receives the Hello from R1. R2 adds R1's Router ID to its **Neighbor List**. R2 now sends a Hello back containing R1 in its neighbor list.

### Step 3 — Transition: `Init → 2-Way`
R1 receives R2's Hello and sees its own Router ID in R2's neighbor list → bidirectional communication confirmed.

> [!NOTE]
> On broadcast networks, the DR/BDR election occurs at this stage. Only routers that have completed `2-Way` with both DR and BDR proceed further.

### Step 4 — Transition: `2-Way → Exstart`
Both routers exchange an **empty DBD packet** with IM/MS flags set. The router with the **higher Router ID** becomes **Master**; the other becomes **Slave**. The Master controls the DD Sequence Number.

### Step 5 — Transition: `Exstart → Exchange`
Master and Slave exchange DBD packets containing **LSA headers**. The Slave uses the Master's sequence number. After all DBDs are exchanged, both routers know what LSAs they are missing.

### Step 6 — Transition: `Exchange → Loading`
Each router builds an **LSR (Link State Request) list** containing missing LSAs and sends LSRs to the neighbor.

### Step 7 — Final Transition: `Loading → Full`
The neighbor responds with **LSU (Link State Update)** packets containing the full LSAs. Each LSU is acknowledged via **LSAck**. Once the LSR list is empty on both sides, the state becomes `Full`.

### Summary Table

| From State | To State | Trigger Packet | Key Event |
|------------|----------|----------------|-----------|
| Down | Init | Hello received | Discovered neighbor |
| Init | 2-Way | Hello with own RID | Bidirectional confirmed |
| 2-Way | Exstart | Empty DBD | Master/Slave election |
| Exstart | Exchange | DBD exchange | LSDB summary exchange |
| Exchange | Loading | LSR sent | Requesting missing LSAs |
| Loading | Full | LSU + LSAck | LSDB fully synchronized |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Packet Carries What?

| Packet Type | Carries Full LSAs? | Reliable Delivery? | Triggering Event |
|-------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Hello (1) | No | No (periodic) | Timer-based (10 s/30 s) |
| DBD (2) | No (headers only) | Yes (via DD seq #) | Adjacency build |
| LSR (3) | No (triples only) | Implicit (via LSU/LSAck) | After DBD exchange |
| LSU (4) | **YES** | **YES** (via LSAck) | Flooding / response |
| LSAck (5) | No (headers only) | N/A | Confirm LSU receipt |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OSPF Packet Type Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OSPF Packet Stream] --> B[24-Byte Common Header]
    B --> C{Type Field}
    C -->|Type 1| D[Hello Packet]
    C -->|Type 2| E[DBD Packet]
    C -->|Type 3| F[LSR Packet]
    C -->|Type 4| G[LSU Packet]
    C -->|Type 5| H[LSAck Packet]

    D --> D1[Network Mask]
    D --> D2[Hello Dead Interval]
    D --> D3[Neighbor List]

    E --> E1[MTU Field]
    E --> E2[DD Sequence Number]
    E --> E3[LSA Headers 20 bytes]

    F --> F1[LS Type 4 bytes]
    F --> F2[LS ID 4 bytes]
    F --> F3[Advertising Router 4 bytes]

    G --> G1[Number of LSAs]
    G --> G2[Full LSA 1]
    G --> G3[Full LSA N]

    H --> H1[LSA Header 1]
    H --> H2[LSA Header N]
```

## 4.2 OSPF Neighbor Adjacency State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateDown
    stateDown --> stateInit: Hello Received
    stateInit --> state2Way: Hello with own Router ID
    state2Way --> stateExstart: Begin DBD Exchange
    stateExstart --> stateExchange: Master Slave Election Done
    stateExchange --> stateLoading: DBD Exchange Complete
    stateLoading --> stateFull: LSR List Empty and LSAs Acknowledged
    stateFull --> stateFull: Periodic Hellos and LSA Refresh
    stateFull --> stateDown: Dead Interval Expired
    state2Way --> state2Way: DROther on Broadcast Network
```

## 4.3 OSPF Reliable Flooding Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R1 as Router R1
    participant R2 as Router R2
    participant R3 as Router R3

    R1->>R2: Hello Type 1
    R2->>R1: Hello Type 1
    R1->>R2: DBD Type 2 Sequence X
    R2->>R1: DBD Type 2 Sequence X
    R1->>R2: LSR Type 3 requesting LSA
    R2->>R1: LSU Type 4 containing LSA
    R1->>R2: LSAck Type 5 confirming receipt
    R2->>R3: Flooded LSU Type 4
    R3->>R2: LSAck Type 5 confirming receipt
```

## 4.4 OSPF Encapsulation Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Data] --> B[OSPF Payload LSA LSU]
    B --> C[OSPF Common Header 24 bytes]
    C --> D[IP Header Protocol 89]
    D --> E[Layer 2 Header Ethernet FR PPP]
    E --> F[Physical Layer]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] List and briefly explain the five OSPF packet types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Packet | Type | Function |
|--------|------|----------|
| Hello | 1 | Discovers and maintains neighbor relationships; sent every 10 s on broadcast networks. |
| DBD | 2 | Summarizes the LSDB using LSA headers during database synchronization. |
| LSR | 3 | Requests specific LSAs identified during DBD exchange. |
| LSU | 4 | Carries the full LSAs in response to LSR; also used for LSA flooding. |
| LSAck | 5 | Acknowledges receipt of LSUs to ensure reliable flooding. |

All five packets share a common **24-byte OSPF header** containing version, type, length, Router ID, Area ID, checksum, and authentication fields.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] What is the default Dead Interval on a broadcast OSPF network and how is it calculated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

On a broadcast OSPF network, the default Hello Interval is **10 seconds**. The Dead Interval is calculated as four times the Hello Interval:

$$
\text{DeadInterval} = 4 \times \text{HelloInterval} = 4 \times 10 = 40 \text{ seconds}
$$

If a router does not receive a Hello from its neighbor for 40 consecutive seconds, it declares the neighbor **down** and removes it from its neighbor table, triggering SPF recalculation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question per module** in the End Semester Exam. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Describe the **OSPF common header** with a neat diagram. Mention the size of each field.

**Model Solution:**

The OSPF common header is **24 bytes** long and is present in **every** OSPF packet regardless of type. The structure is:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Description |
|-------|--------------|-------------|
| Version | 1 | OSPF version (2 for IPv4) |
| Type | 1 | Packet type (1–5) |
| Packet Length | 2 | Total OSPF packet length including header |
| Router ID | 2+2 | 32-bit unique identifier of source router |
| Area ID | 4 | Area to which the packet belongs |
| Checksum | 2 | Fletcher's checksum (excluding auth) |
| AuType | 2 | Authentication type (0=None, 1=Plain, 2=MD5) |
| Authentication | 8 | Authentication data |

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Diagram of header structure → 2 marks
- Field-by-field explanation → 4 marks
- Note on commonality across packet types → 1 mark

---

#### **Q.A (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Explain the **OSPF neighbor adjacency state machine** with a neat state diagram. List the packets exchanged at each transition.

**Model Solution:**

The OSPF adjacency formation follows these states:

1. **Down** → No Hello received. Router enters this state initially.
2. **Init** → A Hello has been received from the neighbor, but the neighbor does **not** list this router in its neighbor list yet.
3. **2-Way** → Bidirectional communication is established; each router sees its own Router ID in the other's Hello. DR/BDR election occurs here on broadcast networks.
4. **Exstart** → Routers exchange empty DBD packets and elect Master/Slave based on Router ID.
5. **Exchange** → DBD packets containing LSA headers are exchanged.
6. **Loading** → LSRs are sent and corresponding LSUs are received and LSAcked.
7. **Full** → LSDBs are fully synchronized. Adjacency is up.

**Packets exchanged at transitions:**
- Down → Init: Hello (Type 1)
- Init → 2-Way: Hello (Type 1) containing own RID
- 2-Way → Exstart: Empty DBD (Type 2)
- Exstart → Exchange: DBD with LSA headers
- Exchange → Loading: LSR (Type 3)
- Loading → Full: LSU (Type 4) + LSAck (Type 5)

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- State diagram → 2 marks
- State descriptions → 3 marks
- Packet mapping → 2 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an **OSPF Hello packet**. List the mandatory parameters that must match between two routers before adjacency can be formed.

**Model Solution:**

The OSPF Hello packet contains the 24-byte common header followed by:

- **Network Mask** (4 bytes)
- **Hello Interval** (2 bytes) — default 10 s
- **Options** (1 byte) — capability flags
- **Router Priority** (1 byte) — for DR/BDR election
- **Dead Interval** (4 bytes) — default 40 s
- **Designated Router** (4 bytes)
- **Backup Designated Router** (4 bytes)
- **Active Neighbor List** (variable)

**Mandatory Matching Parameters (must match before adjacency):**

1. Subnet mask
2. Hello Interval
3. Dead Interval
4. Area ID
5. Authentication type and password
6. Stub Area flag

If any of these mismatch, the routers remain stuck in `Init` state and adjacency will never form.

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Diagram → 2 marks
- Field explanations → 3 marks
- Matching parameters → 2 marks

---

#### **Q.B (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Explain the **reliable flooding mechanism** in OSPF. How does OSPF ensure that LSAs are delivered reliably across the network? What happens if an acknowledgment is not received?

**Model Solution:**

OSPF uses an **acknowledgment-based reliable flooding** mechanism:

1. When a router generates or receives a new LSA, it forwards the LSA inside an **LSU packet (Type 4)** to all eligible neighbors (on broadcast: 224.0.0.6 to DR/BDR, then DR floods to 224.0.0.5).
2. Each receiving router must send back an **LSAck (Type 5)** confirming receipt.
3. LSAs are kept in a **retransmission list** until acknowledged.
4. If no LSAck is received within the **Retransmission Interval (default 5 s)**, the LSU is re-sent.
5. Duplicate LSAs are detected by inspecting the **LS Sequence Number** — duplicates are dropped and re-acknowledged.
6. An LSA is considered valid only if its age is less than **MaxAge (3600 s)**.

**Failure Handling:**
- If a neighbor crashes or link fails, after **Dead Interval** expires, the neighbor is declared down.
- All LSAs originated by that neighbor are flushed via LSU with age = MaxAge.

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Flooding process → 3 marks
- LSAck mechanism → 2 marks
- Failure and retransmission → 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Warning**
>
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks in OSPF packet questions due to:
> 1. **Confusing DBD (Type 2) with LSU (Type 4)** — DBD carries only LSA *headers* (20 bytes each), while LSU carries *full LSAs*. Examiners deduct full marks if this distinction is missed.
> 2. **Forgetting the 24-byte common header** — every packet type has it; writing about payload without the header loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Hello vs Dead Intervals** — Dead = 4 × Hello is a favourite 1-mark sub-question. Memorize both defaults (10 s/40 s and 30 s/120 s).
> 4. **Skipping the Master/Slave election** in the Exstart state — this is asked almost every exam cycle and is a guaranteed 2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning IP Protocol 89** when asked about OSPF encapsulation — students lose easy marks here.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ OSPF runs directly over IP using **Protocol Number 89** (no TCP/UDP).
- ✅ There are **exactly 5 packet types**: Hello, DBD, LSR, LSU, LSAck.
- ✅ Every OSPF packet begins with a **24-byte common header**.
- ✅ **Default Hello Interval**: 10 s (broadcast/P2P), 30 s (NBMA/P2MP).
- ✅ **Default Dead Interval** = 4 × Hello Interval = 40 s or 120 s.
- ✅ **DBD packets** carry only **LSA headers** (20 bytes each), not full LSAs.
- ✅ **LSU packets** are the **only** packet type that carries **full LSAs**.
- ✅ **LSAck** confirms reliable delivery of LSUs.
- ✅ Master/Slave election in `Exstart` state is based on the **highest Router ID**.
- ✅ Reliable flooding uses **Retransmission Interval = 5 s** default.
- ✅ **LSA Refresh** every 1800 s (30 min); **MaxAge** = 3600 s (1 hour).
- ✅ OSPF uses multicast addresses: **224.0.0.5** (AllSPFRouters) and **224.0.0.6** (AllDRouters).
- ✅ TTL of OSPF packets = **1** by default to limit flooding scope.
- ✅ Mandatory matching Hello parameters: subnet mask, Hello/Dead intervals, Area ID, authentication, stub flag.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the TCP/IP protocol suite, the **Common Header** (also widely known as the **IPv4 Datagram Header** or **Internet Header**) is the fixed, mandatory 20-byte (160-bit) control prefix that is prepended to every IPv4 packet before it is handed down to the network interface layer for transmission.

It is a **layer-3 (Network Layer)** construct defined originally in **RFC 791** (Internet Protocol, September 1981) and forms the standardized addressing and routing control block of the Internet Protocol version 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *Common Header* refers specifically to the **mandatory fixed-size portion (20 bytes)** of the IPv4 header, *excluding* the variable Options and Padding fields. Mastery of each of the 12 fields, their sizes, and their function is a **direct CO (Course Outcome) mapping** and a high-frequency exam topic.

### Header Composition — At a Glance

- **Fixed Size:** **20 bytes** (when no options are present)
- **Bit-Stream:** 160 bits, transmitted in **big-endian (network byte order)**
- **Variable Extension:** Up to 40 bytes of *Options* may follow, making the maximum legal header **60 bytes**
- **Position:** Sits between the data-link layer frame payload and the upper-layer (TCP/UDP) segment

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Envelope" ✉️

Imagine you are sending a registered parcel through the post office. Before your letter is sealed in an envelope, the post office sticks a **standardized form** at the front containing:

- 📌 The **version** of the form being used
- ✍️ The **length** of the form itself
- 🚦 The **handling priority** (registered, express, fragile)
- 🔢 A **serial number** (tracking ID)
- 🪧 **"Do Not Bend" / "This Side Up"** flags
- ⏳ A **time limit** (days until the parcel is discarded)
- 🏷️ The **content type** (letter, gift, document)
- ✅ An **integrity checksum**
- 📍 The **sender's address** and **receiver's address**

That form is exactly what the **IPv4 Common Header** is to an IP datagram. The actual letter inside is the *payload* (TCP segment, UDP datagram, ICMP message, etc.). Every router in the path — like every sorting office — only reads the envelope form to decide where to forward the parcel next; they do not open the letter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Common"?** It is called "common" because **every IPv4 datagram** in the global Internet, regardless of payload, **must** contain this exact 20-byte prefix. There is no exception. It is the universal language routers speak.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics to Memorize

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Common Header fixed size | **20 bytes** |
| Common Header fixed size in bits | **160 bits** |
| Maximum header (with options) | **60 bytes** |
| Maximum Total Datagram size | **65,535 bytes** |
| Maximum Options field | **40 bytes** |
| Transmitted in | **Network byte order (Big-Endian)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Twelve Fields — Structured Breakdown

The 20-byte common header is partitioned into **twelve logical fields**. We analyze each, the 'why' behind it, and its KTU-exam relevance.

### 🔹 Field 1 — Version (VER)
- **Size:** **4 bits**
- **Purpose:** Identifies the IP version of the datagram. For IPv4, value = **4** (binary `0100`).
- **Why it exists:** Allows routers and end-systems to interpret the rest of the header correctly. An IPv6 router will not see this value as 4 and will drop or differently process the packet.
- **KTU trick:** If a question says "A packet is captured and the first 4 bits read `0110`", the answer is **"It is not an IPv4 packet (that nibble indicates IPv6 / future use)."**

### 🔹 Field 2 — Internet Header Length (IHL)
- **Size:** **4 bits**
- **Purpose:** Declares the **length of the entire IP header** (including options) measured in **32-bit words (4-byte chunks)**.
- **Why it exists:** Because options make the header variable, the receiver needs to know where the payload (TCP/UDP segment) actually begins.
- **Range:** Minimum = `0101` (5) → 5 × 4 = **20 bytes**. Maximum = `1111` (15) → 15 × 4 = **60 bytes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Equation for IHL:**
> $$\text{Header Length (bytes)} = \text{IHL value} \times 4$$

### 🔹 Field 3 — Type of Service (ToS) / DSCP / ECN
- **Size:** **8 bits**
- **Original purpose (RFC 791):** A 3-bit **Precedence** field + 4-bit **Type of Service** flags (Delay, Throughput, Reliability, Cost) + 1 reserved bit.
- **Modern purpose (RFC 2474 / 3168):** Reinterpreted as 6-bit **DSCP** (Differentiated Services Code Point) for QoS + 2-bit **ECN** (Explicit Congestion Notification).
- **Why it exists:** Lets routers prioritize delay-sensitive traffic (VoIP) over bulk traffic (FTP).

### 🔹 Field 4 — Total Length
- **Size:** **16 bits**
- **Purpose:** Total size of the **entire IP datagram** (header + payload), measured in **bytes**.
- **Maximum value:** $2^{16} - 1 = \mathbf{65{,}535}$ **bytes** (the theoretical MTU of IP itself).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Equation for Total Length:**
> $$\text{Total Length} = \text{Header Length} + \text{Data (Payload) Length}$$
> $$\text{Data Length} = \text{Total Length} - (\text{IHL} \times 4)$$

### 🔹 Field 5 — Identification
- **Size:** **16 bits**
- **Purpose:** A **unique ID** assigned by the sender to a single original datagram. Used during reassembly to group all fragments belonging to the same original packet.
- **Why it exists:** When a large datagram is fragmented by a router (because the outgoing link has a smaller MTU), the receiving host must know which fragments belong together. The Identification field is that "family tag."

### 🔹 Field 6 — Flags (3 bits)
Three single-bit flags:
| Bit | Name | Meaning |
|-----|------|---------|
| Bit 0 | **Reserved** | Must be 0 |
| Bit 1 | **DF (Don't Fragment)** | 1 = cannot be fragmented; 0 = fragment if needed |
| Bit 2 | **MF (More Fragments)** | 1 = more fragments follow; 0 = last (or only) fragment |

- **DF = 1** is what `ping` and path-MTU discovery use.
- **MF = 0** marks the *final* fragment.

### 🔹 Field 7 — Fragment Offset
- **Size:** **13 bits**
- **Purpose:** Indicates the position of this fragment's data relative to the start of the **original** (un-fragmented) payload, measured in **8-byte units**.
- **Why 13 bits and 8-byte units?** It keeps the offset field in 13 bits but covers large payloads: $13 + 3 = 16$ bits total counting space, and $2^{13} \times 8 = 65{,}528$ bytes — enough for the maximum IP payload.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Equation for Fragment Offset:**
> $$\text{Fragment Offset (bytes)} = \text{Field Value} \times 8$$

### 🔹 Field 8 — Time To Live (TTL)
- **Size:** **8 bits**
- **Purpose:** A self-decrementing **hop-counter**. Each router decrements TTL by 1. When TTL reaches 0, the packet is **discarded** and an **ICMP "Time Exceeded"** message is sent back to the source.
- **Why it exists:** Prevents packets from looping *forever* in routing cycles if tables are misconfigured.
- **Common initial values:** Linux/Windows: **64**, Unix: **255**, Network devices: **255**.

### 🔹 Field 9 — Protocol
- **Size:** **8 bits**
- **Purpose:** Identifies the **next-level protocol** encapsulated in the payload. Tells the destination host which upper-layer handler to pass the payload to.
- **Key registered values:**
  - **1** → ICMP
  - **2** → IGMP
  - **6** → TCP
  - **17** → UDP
  - **41** → IPv6 encapsulation
  - **89** → OSPF

### 🔹 Field 10 — Header Checksum
- **Size:** **16 bits**
- **Purpose:** A **one's-complement sum** of the entire 20-byte header (re-computed by every router that modifies the header, e.g., decrements TTL).
- **Why only the header, not the payload?** Higher layers (TCP, UDP) have their own checksums; doing it again at IP is redundant and slows routers.
- **Crucial:** Routers **must** recompute this on every hop because TTL changes.

### 🔹 Field 11 — Source IP Address
- **Size:** **32 bits**
- **Purpose:** The **logical 32-bit IPv4 address** of the original sender. Remains **unchanged** across the entire journey (unlike MAC addresses, which change at every hop).

### 🔹 Field 12 — Destination IP Address
- **Size:** **32 bits**
- **Purpose:** The final recipient's 32-bit IPv4 address. Also unchanged end-to-end.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Print this table. It solves ~80% of KTU numerical questions on the Common Header.

| # | Concept | Formula / Boundary | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Header length (bytes) | $H = \text{IHL} \times 4$ | bytes | IHL is in 32-bit words |
| 2 | Payload length | $D = \text{Total Length} - H$ | bytes | Strip the header |
| 3 | Fragment offset (bytes) | $F_{\text{bytes}} = \text{Offset} \times 8$ | bytes | Field stored in 8-byte units |
| 4 | Max total length | $2^{16} - 1$ | bytes | = 65,535 |
| 5 | Max header size | $15 \times 4$ | bytes | = 60 (incl. options) |
| 6 | Fixed header size | $5 \times 4$ | bytes | = 20 (no options) |
| 7 | Max offset value | $2^{13} - 1$ | units | = 8,191 |
| 8 | Max payload representable by offset | $2^{13} \times 8$ | bytes | = 65,528 |
| 9 | Common header bit total | $20 \times 8$ | bits | = 160 |
| 10 | TTL countdown | $\text{TTL}_{\text{out}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{in}} - 1$ | hops | Per router hop |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Path MTU Discovery (PMTUD)** uses the **DF flag** to learn the smallest MTU along a route without fragmentation.
- **Traceroute** works by exploiting the **TTL** field — sending packets with TTL = 1, 2, 3... and reading the ICMP "Time Exceeded" replies.
- **Firewall rules and Access Control Lists (ACLs)** are written in terms of the **Source/Destination IP**, **Protocol**, and **ToS/DSCP** fields.
- **Quality of Service (QoS)** in modern routers classifies traffic largely based on the **DSCP** portion of the ToS byte.
- **Reverse-path filtering (uRPF)** uses the **Source IP** to drop packets with spoofed sources.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are the **exact algorithmic thought process** KTU examiners expect. Practice writing them out in this format.

## 3.1 Derivation A — Computing Header Length from IHL

**Problem:** An IPv4 packet has the IHL field set to the binary value `1010`. Compute the header length in bytes.

### Step-by-step conversion:

1. **Convert binary IHL to decimal:**
   $$\text{IHL}_{\text{decimal}} = (1 \times 2^3) + (0 \times 2^2) + (1 \times 2^1) + (0 \times 2^0) = 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 10$$

2. **Apply the IHL formula** (each unit = 4 bytes):
   $$\text{Header Length (bytes)} = \text{IHL}_{\text{decimal}} \times 4$$

3. **Substitute:**
   $$\text{Header Length} = 10 \times 4 = 40 \text{ bytes}$$

4. **Conclusion:** The header is 40 bytes long, meaning **20 bytes of options** are present (40 − 20 = 20), which is legally within the 40-byte options cap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Computing Payload Length

**Problem:** An IPv4 datagram is captured. IHL = `0101` (binary) and Total Length = 800 bytes. Find the length of the upper-layer payload.

### Step-by-step:

1. **Convert IHL to decimal:**
   $$\text{IHL} = 0 \times 2^3 + 1 \times 2^2 + 0 \times 2^1 + 1 \times 2^0 = 4 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 5$$

2. **Compute header length:**
   $$H = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

3. **Apply payload formula:**
   $$D = \text{Total Length} - H = 800 - 20 = 780 \text{ bytes}$$

4. **Conclusion:** The upper-layer protocol receives **780 bytes** of data.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Decoding Fragment Offset

**Problem:** A fragment has its Fragment Offset field = 185. Where does this fragment's data begin in the original datagram?

### Step-by-step:

1. **Recall the unit:** Fragment Offset is stored in **8-byte units**, not bytes.

2. **Apply formula:**
   $$F_{\text{bytes}} = 185 \times 8$$

3. **Compute:**
   $$F_{\text{bytes}} = 185 \times 8 = 1{,}480 \text{ bytes}$$

4. **Conclusion:** The first byte of this fragment's payload originally sat at byte **1,480** of the un-fragmented datagram.

> [!TIP]
> A quick mental check: any valid offset × 8 must be a **multiple of 8**. If your answer isn't, you've mis-converted.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — TTL Hop Budget

**Problem:** A host sets initial TTL = 64. The packet traverses 12 routers before reaching the destination. What TTL value does the destination see?

### Step-by-step:

1. **Routers decrement TTL by 1 per hop.**

2. **Apply formula:**
   $$\text{TTL}_{\text{out}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{in}} - N_{\text{hops}}$$

3. **Substitute:**
   $$\text{TTL}_{\text{out}} = 64 - 12 = 52$$

4. **Conclusion:** The destination receives the packet with **TTL = 52**.

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the **Header Checksum** is also recomputed at each of those 12 hops. This is a classic KTU sub-question: *"Why must the checksum be recomputed at every hop?"*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Field-Wise Summary

| Offset (bits) | Field | Size (bits) | Size (bytes) | Example / Notes |
|---------------|-------|-------------|--------------|------------------|
| 0 | Version | 4 | 0.5 | IPv4 → `0100` |
| 4 | IHL | 4 | 0.5 | Range 5–15 |
| 8 | ToS / DSCP+ECN | 8 | 1 | QoS marking |
| 16 | Total Length | 16 | 2 | Max 65,535 |
| 32 | Identification | 16 | 2 | Fragment grouping |
| 48 | Flags | 3 | 0.375 | DF, MF bits |
| 51 | Fragment Offset | 13 | 1.625 | 8-byte units |
| 64 | TTL | 8 | 1 | 0 → drop + ICMP |
| 72 | Protocol | 8 | 1 | 6=TCP, 17=UDP |
| 80 | Header Checksum | 16 | 2 | One's-complement sum |
| 96 | Source IP | 32 | 4 | Unchanged end-to-end |
| 128 | Destination IP | 32 | 4 | Unchanged end-to-end |
| **160** | **(End of fixed header)** | — | **20** | — |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Mixed-Field Problem

**Problem:** An IP datagram has:
- IHL = `0110` (binary)
- Total Length = 1,200 bytes
- Fragment Offset = 240
- TTL = 128, and it crosses 8 routers

Find: (a) Header length, (b) Payload length, (c) Offset in bytes, (d) TTL at receiver.

### Solutions:

**(a) Header length:**
   $$\text{IHL} = 0{\cdot}8 + 1{\cdot}4 + 1{\cdot}2 + 0{\cdot}1 = 6$$
   $$H = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ bytes}$$

**(b) Payload length:**
   $$D = 1200 - 24 = 1{,}176 \text{ bytes}$$

**(c) Offset in bytes:**
   $$F = 240 \times 8 = 1{,}920 \text{ bytes}$$

**(d) TTL at receiver:**
   $$\text{TTL}_{\text{out}} = 128 - 8 = 120$$

        FRAGOFF["Fragment Offset 13 bits"]:::mid

    end

> **Reading guide:** Each row = 32 bits (4 bytes) wide. The first **5 rows** plus the 32-bit Source and Destination fields complete the **mandatory 20-byte common header**.

## 4.3 Lifecycle of a Field — TTL Walk-Through

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: TTL=64]:::src --> B[Hop 1: 64 → 63, recompute checksum]:::hop
    B --> C[Hop 2: 63 → 62]:::hop
    C --> D[Hop N: ... → 0]:::hop
    D --> E{TTL > 0?}:::check
    E -- Yes --> F[Forward to next hop]:::fwd
    E -- No --> G[Drop packet]:::drop
    G --> H[Send ICMP Time Exceeded to source]:::icmp
    F --> I[Destination receives datagram]:::dest
    classDef src fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff

```

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the fields of the IPv4 common header and state the size of each field in bits.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Field | Size (bits) |
|---|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Version (VER) | 4 |
| 2 | Internet Header Length (IHL) | 4 |
| 3 | Type of Service (ToS / DSCP+ECN) | 8 |
| 4 | Total Length | 16 |
| 5 | Identification | 16 |
| 6 | Flags | 3 |
| 7 | Fragment Offset | 13 |
| 8 | Time To Live (TTL) | 8 |
| 9 | Protocol | 8 |
| 10 | Header Checksum | 16 |
| 11 | Source IP Address | 32 |
| 12 | Destination IP Address | 32 |
| | **Total** | **160 bits = 20 bytes** |

*(3 marks — full credit requires all 12 fields with correct bit sizes.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why must the Header Checksum be recomputed at every router hop, even though the payload is unchanged?**

**Model Answer:**
- The TTL field is decremented by 1 at every router hop.
- Any change in the header (TTL, options, etc.) alters the one's-complement sum of the header.
- If the checksum is not recomputed, the destination will reject the datagram as corrupted, even though the payload arrived intact.
- Therefore, every router **must recompute the Header Checksum** after modifying TTL (or any header field).
- The payload (TCP/UDP segment) is **not** included in the IP checksum — those layers have their own checksums, avoiding redundant work at the IP layer. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **forget to convert IHL and Fragment Offset from units to bytes** — losing 2–3 marks per numerical.
> - Students frequently **omit the role of DF and MF flags** in fragmentation answers — write all three flags explicitly.
> - When asked to *list* fields, students paraphrase instead of giving the **exact field names** as in RFC 791.
> - For checksum questions, never confuse the **IP header checksum** (no payload) with the **TCP/UDP checksum** (includes pseudo-header + payload).

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the IPv4 common header format showing all twelve fields with their bit-sizes. Explain the purpose of the **Identification**, **Flags (DF, MF)**, and **Fragment Offset** fields. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IP datagram arrives with: Version = 4, IHL = 5, Total Length = 1,024 bytes, TTL = 64, Fragment Offset = 120, MF = 1. If this datagram passes through **6 routers**, calculate:
1. Header length in bytes
2. Payload length
3. Position (in bytes) of this fragment's first data byte in the original datagram
4. TTL value seen by the destination **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Diagram + Explanation

The student is expected to reproduce the bit-level diagram (as in Section 4.1) and then explain:

- **Identification (16 bits):** A unique ID assigned by the source to a single original datagram. All fragments of that datagram carry the **same** Identification value, so the destination can group them for reassembly. *(2 marks)*
- **Flags (3 bits):** Three single-bit controls —
  - **Reserved (0):** Must be zero.
  - **DF (Don't Fragment, bit 1):** If set, routers must not fragment the packet; if the next link's MTU is too small, the packet is dropped and an ICMP "Destination Unreachable — Fragmentation Needed" is returned.
  - **MF (More Fragments, bit 2):** If set, more fragments follow. If 0, this is the final (or only) fragment. *(3 marks)*
- **Fragment Offset (13 bits):** Indicates where this fragment's data belongs in the original payload, measured in **8-byte units**. The first fragment has offset 0. *(2 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solutions

**1. Header length:**
   $$\text{IHL} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

**2. Payload length:**
   $$D = \text{Total Length} - H = 1024 - 20 = 1004 \text{ bytes}$$

**3. Position of first data byte (in original datagram):**
   $$F_{\text{bytes}} = \text{Offset} \times 8 = 120 \times 8 = 960 \text{ bytes}$$
   → The first data byte of this fragment originally sat at byte **960** of the un-fragmented datagram. *(2 marks)*

**4. TTL at destination:**
   $$\text{TTL}_{\text{out}} = 64 - 6 = 58$$
   → The destination receives the datagram with **TTL = 58**. *(2 marks)*

**Bonus conceptual remark (extra ½ mark if written):** Since MF = 1, more fragments of this datagram are expected; the receiver will hold this fragment in a reassembly buffer until all are received.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the IPv4 common header. Discuss the role of the **TTL** and **Protocol** fields. Why is the header checksum recomputed at every hop? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IP datagram has IHL = `1011` (binary), Total Length = 1,500 bytes, and Fragment Offset = 250. It passes through 5 routers starting with TTL = 32. Compute:
1. Header length in bytes
2. Payload length
3. Offset in bytes
4. Final TTL at destination
5. Total number of **bits** occupied by the common header **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Diagram + Explanations

- Neatly draw the 20-byte header block diagram *(2 marks)*.
- **TTL (8 bits):** Prevents indefinite looping. Each router decrements TTL by 1; when TTL hits 0, the packet is discarded and an ICMP Time Exceeded message is returned. Standard initial values: 64 (Linux/Windows), 255 (network devices). *(2 marks)*
- **Protocol (8 bits):** Multiplexing field — tells the destination which upper-layer protocol owns the payload. Common values: 1=ICMP, 6=TCP, 17=UDP, 41=IPv6, 89=OSPF. *(2 marks)*
- **Checksum recompute:** TTL changes every hop, so the one's-complement sum of the header changes; the checksum must be re-summed. *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solutions

**1. Header length:**
   $$\text{IHL} = (1 \times 8) + (0 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 8 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 11$$
   $$H = 11 \times 4 = 44 \text{ bytes}$$
   *(The header has 24 bytes of options — valid, since ≤ 40.)* *(2 marks)*

**2. Payload length:**
   $$D = 1500 - 44 = 1456 \text{ bytes}$$ *(1.5 marks)*

**3. Offset in bytes:**
   $$F_{\text{bytes}} = 250 \times 8 = 2000 \text{ bytes}$$ *(1.5 marks)*

**4. Final TTL:**
   $$\text{TTL}_{\text{out}} = 32 - 5 = 27$$ *(1 mark)*

**5. Total bits in common header (fixed mandatory 20 bytes — the question wants the *fixed* common header):**
   $$20 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 160 \text{ bits}$$
   *(1 mark — if the student mistakenly uses 44, award ½ mark.)*

- ✅ The **common header** is the **mandatory fixed 20-byte (160-bit)** portion of every IPv4 datagram.
- ✅ **12 fields**: Version, IHL, ToS, Total Length, Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset, TTL, Protocol, Header Checksum, Source IP, Destination IP.
- ✅ **IHL** is in **32-bit words**; multiply by 4 to get bytes. Range: 5–15.
- ✅ **Fragment Offset** is in **8-byte units**; multiply by 8 to get bytes. Range: 0–8,191.
- ✅ **Total Length** covers **header + payload**; max = 65,535 bytes.
- ✅ **TTL** is **decremented by every router**; TTL = 0 ⇒ drop + ICMP.
- ✅ **Protocol** field is a **multiplexing demux key** (6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP).
- ✅ **DF = 1** means "do not fragment"; **MF = 1** means "more fragments follow."
- ✅ **Header Checksum** covers the **header only** and is **recomputed at every hop**.
- ✅ **Source/Destination IP** are **32 bits each** and remain **constant** end-to-end (unlike MAC addresses).
- ✅ The header is transmitted in **network byte order (big-endian)**.
- ✅ The common header is defined by **RFC 791** (1981).
- ✅ Maximum legal header (with options) is **60 bytes** (40 bytes of options).
- ✅ When asked for the size of the common header in bits, the answer is always **160 bits** (since *common* = fixed = 20 bytes).

---

> **End of Module 2 — Common Header Notes** | *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Guidelines* 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Link State Update Packet (General Format and LSA General Header Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Link State Update Packet (General Format and LSA General Header Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Link State Update (LSU) Packet** is a critical OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) control packet, identified as **OSPF Packet Type 4**, whose primary function is to carry and flood **Link State Advertisements (LSAs)** throughout an OSPF routing domain. The LSU is the actual transport vehicle for topology information; it is sent in response to a Link State Request (LSR) packet or as part of the periodic LSA refresh mechanism, and it encapsulates one or more complete LSAs for reliable dissemination across an Autonomous System (AS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - The scope of this module is limited to the **General Format of the Link State Update Packet** and the **LSA General Header (20 bytes)** only.
> - Detailed internals of individual LSA Types 1 through 5 (Router LSA, Network LSA, Summary LSA, etc.) are covered in subsequent topics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Town Newspaper Delivery" Intuition

Imagine a large residential colony (the OSPF **Autonomous System**) where every household has a router (a **town planner**). Each planner holds a private diary describing the layout of streets connecting them to neighbours.

- When a planner's diary changes (e.g., a new road is built), they do **not** whisper it to one neighbour.
- Instead, they publish a **formal newspaper issue** called a **Link State Advertisement (LSA)**.
- These newspapers are bundled into a **delivery truck** — the **Link State Update (LSU) packet** — and sent to every neighbour.
- Each neighbour, upon receipt, forwards the truck out of all other interfaces (a process called **flooding**), so eventually every household receives the same newspaper.
- The **20-byte LSA Header** is like the **masthead** of the newspaper: it identifies the issue date (LS Age), section type (LS Type), the unique article ID (Link State ID), the publisher (Advertising Router), the issue number (LS Sequence Number), and a quality stamp (LS Checksum) — allowing receivers to detect duplicates, outdated copies, or corruption.

### 1.3 Position in the OSPF Protocol Suite

| OSPF Packet Type | Decimal Code | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| Hello | **1** | Discover / maintain neighbour adjacencies |
| Database Description (DBD) | **2** | Summarise LSDB contents during sync |
| Link State Request (LSR) | **3** | Request specific LSAs from a neighbour |
| **Link State Update (LSU)** | **4** | **Send LSAs in response to LSR or on change** |
| Link State Acknowledgement (LSAck) | **5** | Acknowledge receipt of an LSU |

> [!NOTE]
> The LSU is **unreliable at the transport layer** — OSPF does not use TCP. Instead, OSPF implements its own reliability through the **LSAck packet (Type 5)**, which acts like a TCP ACK for individual LSAs inside the LSU. This is a fundamental design choice of OSPF that engineers must remember.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Worth Memorising

- **OSPF LSU Type Code:** **4** (1 byte field)
- **LSA General Header Length:** **20 bytes** (fixed for all LSA types)
- **OSPF Common Header Length:** **24 bytes** (precedes all OSPF packets)
- **Maximum LSU Payload:** governed by IP packet size (typically up to **MTU** bytes; default **1500 bytes** on Ethernet)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The OSPF Common Header (24 Bytes) — Always Precedes the LSU

Every OSPF packet — including the LSU — begins with a **24-byte common header**. Understanding this header is essential because it defines the **originator, scope, and authenticity** of every LSU a router transmits.

**Byte-by-byte structure of the OSPF Common Header:**

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose / KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 1 | OSPF version number (**2** for OSPFv2, **3** for OSPFv3) |
| Type | 1 | Identifies packet; for LSU this is **4** |
| Packet Length | 2 | Total length of the OSPF packet including header |
| Router ID | 4 | Unique 32-bit identifier of the originating router |
| Area ID | 4 | 32-bit identifier of the area to which the packet belongs |
| Checksum | 2 | Fletcher checksum over the entire OSPF packet (excluding the 64-bit authentication field) |
| AuType | 2 | Authentication type (**0** = None, **1** = Plain text, **2** = MD5, etc.) |
| Authentication | 8 | Authentication data; for AuType 2 contains Key ID, Length, Sequence Number, and MD5 digest |

### 2.2 The LSU-Specific Body (After the 24-Byte Common Header)

Once the common header is parsed, the LSU contains:

1. **Number of LSAs (4 bytes)** — A 32-bit unsigned integer indicating how many LSAs follow in this single LSU packet.
2. **LSA Entries (variable)** — Each LSA entry consists of the **20-byte LSA General Header** followed by the **LSA Type-Specific Body**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single LSU can carry **multiple LSAs**. This batching improves efficiency — instead of sending one packet per LSA, the router groups several into one LSU to amortise IP header overhead.

### 2.3 The LSA General Header (20 Bytes) — The Core of This Module

This is the heart of the syllabus topic. **Every LSA — regardless of whether it is a Router LSA (Type 1), Network LSA (Type 2), Summary LSA (Type 3/4), or External LSA (Type 5) — begins with the same 20-byte header.**

| Offset | Field | Size (bytes) | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **LS Age** | 2 | Time elapsed since the LSA was originated. Incremented by **InfTransDelay** while in transit and aged out when it reaches **MaxAge (3600 seconds = 1 hour)** |
| 2 | **Options** | 1 | 8-bit capability flags (e.g., DC-bit for Demand Circuits, E-bit for External routing, MC/M/N/P-bits) |
| 3 | **LS Type** | 1 | Identifies LSA class. Bits: U-bit, S-bit, and a 5-bit function code |
| 4 | **Link State ID** | 4 | Identifies the *portion of the routing domain* described by this LSA (interpretation depends on LS Type) |
| 8 | **Advertising Router** | 4 | Router ID of the router that originally originated this LSA |
| 12 | **LS Sequence Number** | 4 | 32-bit signed integer used to detect old/duplicate LSAs. Begins at 0x80000001 and increments |
| 16 | **LS Checksum** | 2 | Fletcher checksum of the *entire LSA* (header + body), excluding LS Age field |
| 18 | **Length** | 2 | Total length of the LSA in bytes, including the 20-byte header |

### 2.4 Why These Fields Exist — The Engineering "Why"

- **LS Age + LS Sequence Number:** Together they form a **dual defence** against routing loops and stale data. If a router receives two copies of the same LSA, it keeps the one with the **higher sequence number**; if sequence numbers tie, it keeps the one with the **smaller LS Age**. This is the famous OSPF *tie-break rule*.
- **LS Checksum:** Because the LS Age field is incremented at every hop during flooding, the checksum is calculated **excluding LS Age** to avoid having to recompute it at every router.
- **Advertising Router:** Even if an LSA is flooded through a network of intermediate routers, this field always points to the **original originator**, never the forwarder. This guarantees accountability and prevents spoofed topology injection.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Statement | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| **OSPF Packet Total Length** | $L_{packet} = L_{header} + L_{body}$ where $L_{header} = 24$ bytes | Calculating buffer size for an LSU |
| **LSU Body Size** | $L_{body} = 4 + \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{LSA_i}$ | Where $N$ = Number of LSAs field |
| **MaxAge Threshold** | $LS_{Age} \geq 3600$ s | LSA is purged; forces LSDB refresh |
| **Initial Sequence Number** | $Seq_{init} = 0x80000001$ | Used when an LSA is first originated |
| **Tie-Break Rule** | Higher $Seq$ wins; if equal, smaller $Age$ wins | Deciding which copy of an LSA to retain |
| **Checksum Coverage** | Checksum computed over bytes $[2, L-1]$, *excluding* the 2-byte LS Age field | Exempting Age allows hop-by-hop ageing without recomputation |

> [!NOTE]
> All values in OSPF are stored in **network byte order (big-endian)**. Students often lose marks in KTU exams by writing values in little-endian form. Always remember: **OSPF = big-endian, just like IP**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Convergent enterprise networks:** The LSU flooding mechanism is what allows a Cisco/Juniper/Arista router to converge in sub-second times after a topology change — a direct consequence of the LSU/LSA design.
- **Network forensics & packet analysis:** Tools like **Wireshark** dissect the LSA General Header to show operators *who advertised what, when, and with what sequence number* — invaluable for troubleshooting OSPF neighbour issues.
- **SDN & telemetry:** Modern programmable networks often export OSPF LSU traces to analytics pipelines; the LSA General Header fields are the **minimum metadata** required for any such telemetry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown (Byte-Level Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because this topic involves protocol engineering rather than pure mathematical derivation, this section provides an **exhaustive byte-by-byte analytical walkthrough** of both the LSU packet and the LSA General Header. Every transition is explicitly written, mimicking the working-out expected in a KTU 14-mark solution.

### 3.1 Analytical Exercise: Decoding an LSU Captured on the Wire

**Given:** A network engineer captures an OSPF packet on an Ethernet link. After stripping the Ethernet/IP headers, the following bytes (hexadecimal, big-endian) constitute the OSPF payload:

```
02 04 00 5C 0A 00 00 01 0A 00 00 05 8C 4F 00 01
C0 A8 01 0A 00 00 00 01 00 00 00 28 02 01 00 02
0A 00 00 0A C0 A8 01 0A 80 00 00 01 D3 6B 00 28
... (LSA body)
```

#### Step 1: Parse the OSPF Common Header (Bytes 0–23)

- **Byte 0 = 0x02 → Version = 2** (OSPFv2)
- **Byte 1 = 0x04 → Type = 4** (Link State Update) ✅
- **Bytes 2–3 = 0x00 0x5C → Packet Length = 92 bytes** (entire OSPF packet)
- **Bytes 4–7 = 0x0A 00 00 01 → Router ID = 10.0.0.1**
- **Bytes 8–11 = 0x0A 00 00 05 → Area ID = 10.0.0.5** (Area 0.0.0.5)
- **Bytes 12–13 = 0x8C 4F → Checksum = 0x8C4F** (to be verified at receiver)
- **Bytes 14–15 = 0x00 01 → AuType = 1** (Plain-text password authentication)
- **Bytes 16–23 = 0xC0 A8 01 0A 00 00 00 01 → Authentication = 192.168.1.10 + 4 bytes** (plain-text credentials)

#### Step 2: Parse the LSU-Specific Field (Bytes 24–27)

After the 24-byte header:

- **Bytes 24–27 = 0x00 00 00 28 → Number of LSAs = 40** (decimal) — i.e., this LSU carries 40 LSAs.

> [!NOTE]
> A single LSU carrying 40 LSAs is large but legal. It means the router is **bulk-flooding topology changes**, e.g., during initial LSDB synchronisation.

#### Step 3: Parse the First LSA's General Header (Bytes 28–47)

The first LSA begins at byte 28:

- **Bytes 28–29 = 0x02 01 → LS Age = 513 seconds** (already aged ~8.5 minutes in transit)
- **Byte 30 = 0x00 → Options = 0x00** (no special capabilities set)
- **Byte 31 = 0x02 → LS Type = 2** (Network LSA — describes a multi-access network)
- **Bytes 32–35 = 0x0A 00 00 0A → Link State ID = 10.0.0.10** (DR's IP interface address)
- **Bytes 36–39 = 0xC0 A8 01 0A → Advertising Router = 192.168.1.10** (the Designated Router that originated this Network LSA)
- **Bytes 40–43 = 0x80 00 00 01 → LS Sequence Number = 0x80000001** (initial sequence number — fresh LSA)
- **Bytes 44–45 = 0xD3 6B → LS Checksum = 0xD36B**
- **Bytes 46–47 = 0x00 28 → Length = 40 bytes**

Thus the **first LSA is a Network LSA, 40 bytes total**, of which 20 bytes are the general header and 20 bytes form the type-specific body.

#### Step 4: Verification of Tie-Break Logic

Suppose the receiving router already holds an older copy of this LSA:

$$
\text{Old LSA: } Seq_{old} = 0x80000001, \quad Age_{old} = 600 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{New LSA: } Seq_{new} = 0x80000001, \quad Age_{new} = 513 \text{ s}
$$

Since $Seq_{new} = Seq_{old}$, the tie-break rule selects the LSA with the **smaller LS Age**:

$$
\text{Decision: } \min(600, 513) = 513 \text{ s} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Install NEW LSA.}
$$

#### Step 5: Verification of MaxAge Purging

Suppose after further transit, this same LSA ages to:

$$
Age = 3600 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ hour}
$$

At this threshold:

$$
Age \geq MaxAge \;\Rightarrow\; \text{LSA is flushed from the LSDB and re-originated.}
$$

This is the **LSA refresh / MaxAge mechanism** that ensures the LSDB never holds infinitely stale topology.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Field Significance in Flooding

| LSA Header Field | Role During Flooding | Common KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|
| LS Age | Increments each hop; triggers MaxAge purge | Forgetting to exclude it from the checksum |
| LS Type | Determines interpretation of Link State ID | Confusing Type 3 (Summary) with Type 4 (ASBR Summary) |
| Link State ID | Names the routing entity | Treating it as the originating router — it is *not* |
| Advertising Router | Original creator; never changes during flooding | Confusing it with the Router ID in OSPF header |
| LS Sequence Number | Detects newer vs. older LSAs | Believing it wraps at 0xFFFFFFFF — it is *signed* |
| LS Checksum | Validates integrity across flooding | Computing over the entire LSA including LS Age |
| Length | Determines where the next LSA begins | Misalignment when parsing multi-LSA LSUs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: LSU Packet Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OSPF Common Header - 24 bytes] --> A1[Version 1B]
    A --> A2[Type 4 equals LSU 1B]
    A --> A3[Packet Length 2B]
    A --> A4[Router ID 4B]
    A --> A5[Area ID 4B]
    A --> A6[Checksum 2B]
    A --> A7[AuType 2B]
    A --> A8[Authentication 8B]

    A --> B[LSU Specific Body]

    B --> B1[Number of LSAs - 4 bytes]

    B1 --> C1[LSA 1]
    B1 --> C2[LSA 2]
    B1 --> C3[LSA N]

    C1 --> D1[LSA General Header - 20 bytes]
    C1 --> D2[LSA Type Specific Body - variable]

    D1 --> D1a[LS Age 2B]
    D1 --> D1b[Options 1B]
    D1 --> D1c[LS Type 1B]
    D1 --> D1d[Link State ID 4B]
    D1 --> D1e[Advertising Router 4B]
    D1 --> D1f[LS Sequence Number 4B]
    D1 --> D1g[LS Checksum 2B]
    D1 --> D1h[Length 2B]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: LSU Lifecycle in OSPF Flooding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Topology Change Detected] --> P2[Router Originates New LSA]
    P2 --> P3[Set Seq to 0x80000001 or increment]
    P3 --> P4[Build LSU with all changed LSAs]
    P4 --> P5[Send LSU to All Adjacencies]
    P5 --> P6{Neighbour Receives LSU}
    P6 --> P7[Validate Checksum]
    P7 --> P8{Valid}
    P8 -->|No| P9[Drop and Log Error]
    P8 -->|Yes| P10[Compare with LSDB]
    P10 --> P11{Newer}
    P11 -->|No| P12[Send LSAck and Discard]
    P11 -->|Yes| P13[Install in LSDB]
    P13 --> P14[Send LSAck]
    P13 --> P15[Re-flood LSU out other interfaces]
    P15 --> P5
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping Diagram: OSPF Packet Suite vs LSU Position

```mermaid
graph LR
    H[Hello - Type 1 - Discover Neighbours] --> R[LSR - Type 3 - Request Specific LSAs]
    R --> U[LSU - Type 4 - Deliver Requested or Changed LSAs]
    U --> K[LSAck - Type 5 - Acknowledge Receipt]
    H --> D[DBD - Type 2 - Summarise LSDB]
    D --> R
    U -.Carries.-> LSA[LSA General Header - 20 bytes]
    LSA --> L1[LS Age]
    LSA --> L2[Options]
    LSA --> L3[LS Type]
    LSA --> L4[Link State ID]
    LSA --> L5[Advertising Router]
    LSA --> L6[LS Sequence Number]
    LSA --> L7[LS Checksum]
    LSA --> L8[Length]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `L3`, `D1b`) with no reserved keywords. All labels are raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Identify the OSPF packet type number that carries the Link State Update (LSU) and state its primary function in the OSPF protocol.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The OSPF packet type number that carries the Link State Update is **Type 4**. Its primary function is to **transport and flood Link State Advertisements (LSAs)** throughout an OSPF area in response to a Link State Request (LSR) packet or whenever there is a topology change requiring LSA re-origination. The LSU ensures that all routers in an area maintain an identical and up-to-date **Link State Database (LSDB)**.

*(1 mark for type number, 2 marks for function description.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six fields of the LSA General Header along with their respective sizes in bytes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Field | Size |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | LS Age | 2 bytes |
| 2 | Options | 1 byte |
| 3 | LS Type | 1 byte |
| 4 | Link State ID | 4 bytes |
| 5 | Advertising Router | 4 bytes |
| 6 | LS Sequence Number | 4 bytes |
| 7 | LS Checksum | 2 bytes |
| 8 | Length | 2 bytes |

*(½ mark per correct field with size; 6 × ½ = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **general format of an OSPF Link State Update (LSU) packet**, clearly distinguishing between the **OSPF common header** and the **LSU-specific body**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Draw and explain each field of the **20-byte LSA General Header**, highlighting the role of **LS Sequence Number** and **LS Checksum** in detecting duplicate and outdated LSAs. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) General Format of the LSU Packet — 7 marks**

The OSPF LSU packet consists of two major sections:

1. **OSPF Common Header (24 bytes):** This header is common to all five OSPF packet types. It contains:
   - Version (1 byte) — OSPF version, typically **2** for OSPFv2.
   - Type (1 byte) — **4** for LSU.
   - Packet Length (2 bytes) — Total OSPF packet length.
   - Router ID (4 bytes) — ID of the packet originator.
   - Area ID (4 bytes) — Area to which the packet belongs.
   - Checksum (2 bytes) — Fletcher checksum.
   - AuType (2 bytes) — Authentication type.
   - Authentication (8 bytes) — Authentication data.

2. **LSU-Specific Body (variable):** Immediately after the common header, the LSU contains:
   - **Number of LSAs (4 bytes)** — A 32-bit unsigned integer indicating how many LSAs are bundled in this packet.
