- **Multicast** = You speak once at the central PA system. The sound travels along the main backbone, and **only at junctions** where a CSE hostel branches off, a junction amplifier forwards the message. The non-CSE hostels receive **nothing**.

That junction amplifier is the **multicast router**, the act of branching is **packet replication**, and the listeners are the **multicast group members**.

## 3. Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Multicast Group** | A logical set of receivers identified by a Class D IP address (e.g., 239.1.1.10). |
| **IGMP** | Internet Group Management Protocol — used between hosts and the *local* (last-hop) router. |
| **PIM** | Protocol Independent Multicast — the dominant inter-router multicast routing protocol. |
| **RP** | Rendezvous Point — the root of shared distribution trees in PIM-SM. |
| **DR** | Designated Router — elected on a shared LAN segment to forward multicast traffic. |
| **TTL Scope** | Time-To-Live threshold to limit multicast reach (e.g., `ttl=16` confines to a campus). |
| **RPF** | Reverse Path Forwarding — the loop-prevention check every multicast packet must pass. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use Unicast with multiple copies?** In a video webinar with 10,000 viewers, unicast would require 10,000 streams from the server — collapsing the bandwidth. Multicast sends **one stream** and lets the network replicate at branching points, making it scalable, bandwidth-efficient, and ideal for IPTV, stock-ticker feeds, software distribution, and collaborative conferencing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Operational Building Blocks of Multicast Routing

Multicast routing is decomposed into **three orthogonal planes** that a KTU answer must mention:

### A. Group Management Plane (Host ↔ Local Router)
- **IGMPv1 (RFC 1112)** — Hosts send `Membership Report`; routers send `Membership Query` every 125 s.
- **IGMPv2 (RFC 2236)** — Adds `Leave Group` message to reduce leave latency.
- **IGMPv3 (RFC 3376)** — Adds **Source-Specific Multicast (SSM)** awareness — host declares `INCLUDE` or `EXCLUDE` source list.

### B. Routing Plane (Router ↔ Router)
The two dominant protocols tested at KTU are:

| Protocol | Mode | Tree Type | Suitability |
|----------|------|-----------|-------------|
| **DVMRP** (Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol) | Dense | Source-Based Shortest-Path Tree | Dense groups, small networks (legacy MBONE) |
| **PIM-DM** (Protocol Independent Multicast – Dense Mode) | Dense | Source-Based Shortest-Path Tree | Dense groups, LANs |
| **PIM-SM** (Protocol Independent Multicast – Sparse Mode) | Sparse | Shared Tree (rooted at RP) | Sparse groups, enterprise + ISP (most deployed) |
| **CBT** (Core-Based Trees) | Sparse | Shared Tree (rooted at Core) | Rarely deployed today |
| **MOSPF** (Multicast OSPF) | Link-State | Source-Based via Dijkstra on Group-Membership LSA | OSPF networks only |

### C. Forwarding Plane (Router Internal)
- The **Multicast Forwarding Table** is conceptually: `(Source, Group) → {Outgoing Interface List}`.
- Each incoming multicast packet is subjected to the **RPF Check** before being copied onto outgoing interfaces.

## 2. The Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Check — Core Theorem

For every multicast packet arriving on interface $I_{in}$ from source $S$:

$$
\text{RPF Pass} \iff I_{in} = \text{OutgoingInterface}\bigl(\text{UnicastRoute}(S)\bigr)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the RPF check fails, the packet is **silently dropped**. This guarantees loop-free multicast forwarding because the packet always travels *away* from the source along the unicast shortest path.

## 3. The Two Distribution Tree Models

### (i) Source-Based Shortest-Path Tree (SPT) — used by DVMRP, PIM-DM, PIM-SSM

For source $S$ and group $G$, the SPT is constructed as the union of the unicast shortest paths from $S$ to every receiver of $G$. This is the **union of $k$ reverse-path trees**, where $k$ = number of receivers.

### (ii) Shared Tree (RPT) — used by PIM-SM, CBT

A single tree is shared by **all senders** in group $G$, rooted at the **Rendezvous Point (RP)**. Receivers register with the RP; senders send traffic toward the RP.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Question — Short Note**: "Compare SPT vs RPT." Always mention: SPT gives **optimal latency** per source but is expensive in router state (one tree per source); RPT gives **minimal router state** but sub-optimal latency for sources far from the RP.

## 4. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Rule | Units / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---------|------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| Class D Range | $224.0.0.0 \le IP < 240.0.0.0$ | 28 bits of group ID | Multicast address space |
| TTL Scope | $TTL \in \{1, 15, 63, 127, 191, 255\}$ | Hops | Administrative scoping |
| IGMP Query Timer | $T_q \approx 125\,\text{s}$ | Seconds | Group liveness |
| Multicast Bandwidth Saved | $B_{saved} = (R-1)\cdot B_{stream}$ | Bytes/s | Network design |
| RPF Check | $I_{in} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{UnicastNextHop}(S)$ | Boolean | Loop prevention |
| SPT Hop Count (dense) | $H_{SPT} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} d(S,R_i)$ | Hops | Optimal delay tree |
| RPT Hop Count | $H_{RPT} = d(S, RP) + d(RP, R_i)$ | Hops | Shared tree cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule (Anti-Trap)**: When writing RPF or SPT formulas in your answer script, NEVER write $\vert x \vert$ using the ASCII pipe inside a markdown table — the renderer will break the column. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX and `$|x|$` only in plain-text paragraphs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Construction of the Reverse Path Forwarding Table

We start from a unicast routing table that already gives the **shortest-path next-hop** to any source $S$. Let $u_i$ denote router $i$, and let $NH_{i}(S)$ be the next-hop interface towards $S$.

**Step 1 — Unicast Prerequisite.** We assume the unicast forwarding information base (FIB) contains the entry:
$$
NH_i(S) = \arg\min_{j \in N(i)} \bigl[\text{Cost}(u_i, S) \text{ along } u_j\bigr]
$$
where $N(i)$ is the set of neighbor routers of $u_i$.

**Step 2 — RPF Interface Definition.** The RPF interface for $(S, G)$ at router $u_i$ is defined as:
$$
I_{RPF}(S) \equiv NH_i(S)
$$

**Step 3 — Outgoing Interface List (OIL).** When a packet $(S,G)$ arrives on $I_{RPF}(S)$, the router copies it onto every interface $j$ in the OIL, EXCEPT $I_{RPF}(S)$:
$$
OIL(S,G) = \bigl\{ j \,\bigm|\, j \neq I_{RPF}(S) \;\wedge\; \text{downstream receiver on } j \text{ for } G \bigr\}
$$

**Step 4 — Loop-Proof Argument.** Suppose a loop exists: packet returns to a previously visited router via interface $p$ but $p \neq I_{RPF}(S)$. Then by definition of RPF check:
$$
\text{Packet}(S,G) \text{ on interface } p \implies \text{DROP}
$$
Contradiction. Hence the multicast forwarding graph is loop-free. $\blacksquare$

## Derivation 2 — DVMRP Truncated Reverse Path Broadcasting (TRPB) — Branch Pruning

In DVMRP (RFC 1075), every router initially forwards the multicast packet out **all** interfaces except the RPF interface — this is *flood-and-prune* (Dense Mode).

**Step 1 — Broadcast phase.** Router $u_i$ forwards $(S,G)$ onto all $j \neq I_{RPF}(S)$, regardless of interest.

**Step 2 — Pruning.** A leaf router with **no local members** of $G$ and **no downstream routers** sending $(S,G)$ traffic sends a **Prune message** back toward $S$ on $I_{RPF}(S)$:
$$
\text{Prune}_{S,G}: \quad \text{Parent}(u_i) \rightarrow \text{do NOT forward } (S,G) \text{ downstream of } u_i
$$

**Step 3 — State timeout.** Prunes expire after $T_{prune} \approx 12\,\text{min}$ (default DVMRP lifetime), after which the flow is re-flooded and re-pruned.

> [!NOTE]
> The bandwidth saved by pruning a sub-tree of size $k$ receivers is:
$$
B_{saved} = (k-1) \cdot B_{(S,G)}
$$

## Derivation 3 — PIM-SM Shared Tree Switchover Trigger

PIM-SM initially uses the shared tree rooted at the RP. When traffic from source $S$ exceeds threshold $B_{th}$ (typically the **configured threshold**, default often 0 kbps, i.e., immediate), the last-hop router (LHR) may switch to the SPT.

**Step 1 — Initial RPT Path.**
$$
\text{Path}_{RPT}(S \to R) = S \xrightarrow{\text{unicast to RP}} RP \xrightarrow{\text{shared tree}} R
$$
Hop count:
$$
H_{RPT} = d(S, RP) + d(RP, R)
$$

**Step 2 — SPT Path.**
$$
H_{SPT} = d(S, R)
$$

**Step 3 — Switchover Decision.**
$$
\text{Switch to SPT} \iff H_{SPT} < H_{RPT} \;\wedge\; B(S,G) \geq B_{th}
$$

$$
\text{Equivalent latency gain: } \Delta H = d(S, RP) + d(RP, R) - d(S, R) = d(S,RP) + d(RP,R) - d(S,R)
$$

By the **triangle inequality** $d(S,R) \le d(S,RP) + d(RP,R)$, therefore $\Delta H \ge 0$, with equality iff $R$, $RP$, and $S$ are collinear on the shortest path.

## Analytical Matrix — Trade-offs (Mandatory for 7-mark questions)

| Property | SPT (Source-Based) | RPT (Shared Tree) |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Optimal delay per source | **Yes** | No (extra hop via RP) |
| Router state | $O(S \cdot G)$ trees | $O(G)$ trees |
| Single-point-of-failure | None | **RP is SPOF** |
| Scalability to many senders | Poor | **Good** |
| Used by | DVMRP, PIM-DM, SSM | PIM-SM, CBT |
| Bandwidth efficiency at low traffic | High | **Very high** |
| Convergence after topology change | Slow (re-flood) | Slow (RP discovery) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — End-to-End Multicast Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

<!--
Mermaid Safeguards applied:
- All node IDs are alphanumeric + prefixed (routerA, hostA, etc.)
- No reserved keywords (end, graph, style) used as node IDs
- Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text, NO bold or italics inside quotes
-->

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[SOURCE SERVER S] --> igmpClient1[ROUTER R1 FIRST HOP DR]
    igmpClient1 --> igmpPkt1{MEMBER OF GROUP G YES}
    igmpPkt1 -- YES --> rpNode[RENDEZVOUS POINT RP]
    igmpPkt1 -- NO --> dropPkt1[DROP PACKET]
    rpNode --> coreTree[SHARED TREE ROOTED AT RP]
    coreTree --> branchA[ROUTER R3 BRANCH]
    coreTree --> branchB[ROUTER R4 BRANCH]
    branchA --> rpfCheckA{RPF CHECK PASS}
    branchA --> dropA[DROP]
    rpfCheckA -- YES --> hostA[HOST H1 RECEIVER]
    rpfCheckA -- YES --> hostB[HOST H2 RECEIVER]
    branchB --> rpfCheckB{RPF CHECK PASS}
    rpfCheckB -- YES --> hostC[HOST H3 RECEIVER]
    rpfCheckB -- NO --> dropB[DROP]
```

## Diagram 2 — SPT vs RPT Tree Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPT_BLOCK[SPT PER SOURCE]
        sptSrc1[S1] --> sptN1[N1]
        sptN1 --> sptL1[L1 RECEIVER]
        sptN1 --> sptN2[N2]
        sptN2 --> sptL2[L2 RECEIVER]
        sptSrc2[S2] --> sptN3[N3]
        sptN3 --> sptL3[L3 RECEIVER]
    end

    subgraph RPT_BLOCK[RPT SHARED TREE]
        rptRP[RP CORE] --> rptA[A]
        rptA --> rptR1[R1 RECEIVER]
        rptA --> rptB[B]
        rptB --> rptR2[R2 RECEIVER]
        rptB --> rptR3[R3 RECEIVER]
        rptS1[S1 SENDER] -. unicast to RP .-> rptRP
        rptS2[S2 SENDER] -. unicast to RP .-> rptRP
    end
```

## Diagram 3 — IGMP Membership Lifecycle (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NO_MEMBER
    NO_MEMBER --> JOINING : HOST SENDS IGMP REPORT
    JOINING --> ACTIVE : ROUTER ACKNOWLEDGES
    ACTIVE --> LEAVING : HOST SENDS IGMP LEAVE V2
    LEAVING --> ACTIVE : OTHER HOST RESPONDS WITH REPORT
    LEAVING --> NO_MEMBER : TIMEOUT NO OTHER MEMBER
    ACTIVE --> NO_MEMBER : QUERY TIMEOUT 125 SEC
```

## Diagram 4 — Dense-Mode Flood-and-Prune Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as SOURCE S
    participant R1 as ROUTER R1
    participant R2 as ROUTER R2
    participant R3 as LEAF R3 NO MEMBERS
    participant R4 as LEAF R4 HAS MEMBERS
    SRC->>R1: DATA PACKET S G
    R1->>R2: FLOOD DATA
    R1->>R3: FLOOD DATA
    R2->>R4: FLOOD DATA
    R3-->>R2: PRUNE S G
    R2->>R4: CONTINUE FORWARDING
    Note over R1,R3: AFTER 12 MIN PRUNE EXPIRES REFLOOD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between unicast, broadcast, and multicast communication with a real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Unicast**: One-to-one. Example: A web browser fetching a single HTML page from a server. The sender transmits one packet stream and exactly one receiver processes it.
- **Broadcast**: One-to-all on the local subnet. Example: An ARP request from a host asking "Who has IP 192.168.1.5?" — every device on the LAN receives it, but only the owner replies.
- **Multicast**: One-to-many, selective group. Example: A live stock-ticker feed (e.g., Bloomberg) streamed to 50,000 subscribed traders via a single stream replicated at network branches. Routers forward only along paths leading to subscribed receivers.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of IGMP in multicast routing? Mention any two IGMP message types.**

**Model Answer:**
**IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)** operates between a host and its directly attached (last-hop) multicast router. Its purpose is to inform the local router that the host wishes to **join** or **leave** a particular multicast group, and to allow the router to **query** for live group membership on its attached LAN.

Two message types (IGMPv2):
1. **Membership Query** — sent by the router (destination 224.0.0.1, TTL=1) every 125 s to ask "Anyone still interested in group G?"
2. **Membership Report (Join)** — sent by a host to announce membership in group G.
3. *(Additional credit)* **Leave Group** — sent by a host in IGMPv2/v3 to expedite pruning.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### `Question A (14 Marks)`

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` Explain the operation of **DVMRP** with the concept of Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) and prune messages. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Overview (1 mark)** — DVMRP is a *dense-mode* multicast protocol using a *distance-vector* approach combined with the **RPF check**. It is the protocol that originally powered the MBONE.
2. **Unicast Underlay (1 mark)** — DVMRP requires a unicast routing protocol (typically RIP-derived) to provide the next-hop towards every source $S$.
3. **RPF Check (2 marks)** — When a multicast packet $(S,G)$ arrives on interface $I_{in}$, the router verifies that $I_{in}$ is the same interface the unicast table would use to send TO $S$. If yes, the packet is accepted and flooded onto all other interfaces (broadcast behavior); if no, the packet is dropped. This guarantees **no forwarding loops**.
4. **Truncated Reverse Path Broadcasting (1 mark)** — Routers do not forward the packet back out the interface on which it arrived; the RPF interface is implicitly excluded.
5. **Pruning (2 marks)** — If a downstream router has no local members of $G$ and no downstream routers wishing to receive $(S,G)$, it sends a **Prune** message back along the RPF interface to its upstream neighbor. The upstream router then stops forwarding $(S,G)$ onto that branch and records a *prune state* with a soft-state timeout of ~12 minutes.
6. **Conclusion** — DVMRP scales poorly to sparse groups because bandwidth is wasted in the initial flood, but it is simple and optimal for dense deployments.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` With a neat diagram, describe the **PIM-Sparse Mode (PIM-SM)** operation. Explain the role of the **RP** and the **SPT switchover**. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Overview (1 mark)** — PIM-SM is *protocol-independent* (assumes any unicast routing protocol supplies the RPF info) and is designed for **sparse** groups where few receivers are scattered widely. The dominant production protocol.
2. **RP Function (1 mark)** — The **Rendezvous Point** is the root of the shared distribution tree. Both senders and receivers register with the RP.
3. **Receiver Joins (1 mark)** — Last-hop router (LHR) sends `(, G) Join` toward the RP hop-by-hop, building the RPT branch. Intermediate routers create `(*,G)` state.
4. **Sender Registration (1 mark)** — First-hop router (FHR) receives source traffic, encapsulates it in **PIM Register** unicast messages toward the RP. The RP de-encapsulates and forwards down the shared tree.
5. **SPT Switchover (2 marks)** — When the LHR sees traffic rate from source $S$ exceeding the SPT threshold, it sends an `(S,G) Join` directly toward $S$. Once the SPT branch is established, the LHR sends `(S,G) RP-bit prune` to switch off the RPT branch for that source.
6. **Diagram (1 mark)** — Draw RP at center, RPT spokes to receivers R1, R2, R3, and sender S unicast-tunneling to RP via Register message; SPT branch directly from LHR to S.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — PIM-SM**
> 1. Many students **forget that PIM-SM uses TWO tree types**: it begins on the **Shared Tree (RPT)** and may later switch to **SPT**. Writing only one tree loses 2 marks.
> 2. Students confuse **DR (Designated Router)** with **RP**. The DR is elected per LAN segment (highest IP or priority) to forward IGMP joins toward the RP; the RP is the central tree root for the entire group.
> 3. Writing "PIM-SM uses flood-and-prune" is **wrong** and indicates a Dense/Sparse confusion costing 2 marks.
> 4. The PIM **Register message is unicast-encapsulated**, NOT native multicast — students often miss this nuance.

### `Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice`

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` Compare **PIM-DM**, **PIM-SM**, and **DVMRP** in terms of mode, tree type, scalability, and suitable deployment scenario. `[KTU University Exam — May 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | **DVMRP** | **PIM-DM** | **PIM-SM** |
|-----------|-----------|------------|------------|
| Mode | Dense | Dense | Sparse |
| Tree Type | Source-based SPT | Source-based SPT | Shared RPT, may switch to SPT |
| Push/Pull | Push (flood) | Push (flood) | Pull (explicit join) |
| Pruning | Yes | Yes | N/A (joins are explicit) |
| RP Required | No | No | **Yes** |
| Scalability | Poor (flood) | Poor (flood) | **Excellent** |
| Unicast Dependency | RIP-like DV | **Protocol-Independent** (uses RPF info from any unicast table) | Protocol-Independent |
| Typical Use | Legacy MBONE | LAN, dense campus | ISP, enterprise, global IPTV |

PIM-DM is essentially DVMRP without DVMRP's built-in unicast routing engine — hence "protocol-independent." PIM-SM is the industry standard for modern multicast.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` Explain the **Class D address range**, **TTL scoping**, and **administratively scoped multicast (RFC 2365)**. Why are these mechanisms needed? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Class D Range (1 mark)** — The IP address block $224.0.0.0$ to $239.255.255.255$ is reserved for multicast. The first 4 bits are fixed as `1110`, leaving 28 bits for the group ID.
2. **Reserved Sub-Ranges (2 marks)**:
   - $224.0.0.0/24$ — **Local Network Control**: packets with TTL=1, never forwarded by routers (e.g., 224.0.0.1 = All Hosts, 224.0.0.5 = OSPF, 224.0.0.9 = RIPv2).
   - $232.0.0.0/8$ — **Source-Specific Multicast (SSM)**.
   - $233.0.0.0/8$ — **GLOP** (assigned statically using AS number).
   - $239.0.0.0/8$ — **Administratively Scoped (RFC 2365)**: private multicast, analogous to RFC 1918 private unicast.
3. **TTL Scoping (1 mark)** — The TTL field limits how many router hops the packet can traverse. Common thresholds: TTL=1 (link-local), 15 (site), 63 (region), 127 (continent), 191 (global), 255 (unrestricted). This is a coarse-grained administrative boundary.
4. **Administrative Scoping (1 mark)** — RFC 2365 designates $239.0.0.0/8$ for organization-private multicast. Boundary routers apply **scoped multicast zone** filters, dropping packets destined to addresses like 239.x.y.z at the administrative boundary, similar to a firewall.
5. **Why Needed? (1 mark)** — Without scoping, multicast traffic from one organization could leak into another organization's network (just as broadcast storms do), wasting bandwidth and violating isolation. TTL + administrative scope together form a two-tier containment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Address Ranges**
> - Writing "$224.x.x.x$ to $240.x.x.x$" loses 1 mark; the upper bound is exclusive (239.255.255.255 is the last).
> - Saying "$239.0.0.0$ is for SSM" is **wrong** — SSM is $232.0.0.0/8$. Mixing these up is a 2-mark penalty.
> - Forgetting that $224.0.0.0/24$ has TTL=1 by convention (routers MUST NOT forward) costs 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: Multicast = one-to-many selective group delivery, IP range **224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255** (Class D).
- **Host-to-Router protocol**: **IGMP** (v1/v2/v3). Router-to-Router protocols: **DVMRP, PIM-DM, PIM-SM, MOSPF, CBT**.
- **RPF Check** = the core loop-prevention mechanism; packet dropped if incoming interface ≠ unicast next-hop toward source.
- **Two tree types**: **SPT (Source-Based)** — optimal latency, expensive router state; **RPT (Shared)** — single root (RP/Core), minimal state, suboptimal latency.
- **Dense Mode (DVMRP, PIM-DM)**: Flood-and-prune, suitable for dense groups; bandwidth wasted during flood.
- **Sparse Mode (PIM-SM)**: Explicit join to RP; dominant production protocol; SPT switchover is optional optimization.
- **PIM-SM key entities**: **RP** (Rendezvous Point), **DR** (Designated Router), **FHR** (First-Hop Router), **LHR** (Last-Hop Router), **Register message** (unicast-encapsulated).
- **Reserved Multicast Sub-blocks to memorize**:
  - $224.0.0.0/24$ — Local Network Control (TTL=1)
  - $232.0.0.0/8$ — SSM
  - $233.0.0.0/8$ — GLOP
  - $239.0.0.0/8$ — Administratively Scoped (RFC 2365)
- **TTL Scoping**: $\{1, 15, 63, 127, 191, 255\}$ — link-local → unrestricted.
- **Bandwidth Savings**: Multicast saves $(R-1)\cdot B_{stream}$ where $R$ = receivers, $B_{stream}$ = per-receiver bandwidth.
- **Common exam traps**:
  - Conflating RP with DR.
  - Stating PIM-SM uses flood-and-prune.
  - Mixing up SSM and administratively scoped ranges.
  - Forgetting that Class D extends only up to $239.255.255.255$, not $240.x.x.x$.
- **Diagram must accompany** any 7-mark explanation (RPT+SPT topology or flood-and-prune sequence) — diagrams alone contribute ~1 mark in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multicast Routing Protocols - MOSPF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multicast Routing Protocols - MOSPF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 - Multicast Routing Protocols: MOSPF

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (RFC 1584):** **Multicast OSPF (MOSPF)** is an extension to the **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** unicast link-state routing protocol that adds native support for **IP multicast** routing. It leverages OSPF's existing link-state database (LSDB) and Dijkstra shortest-path algorithm to dynamically compute an optimal, loop-free **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** for every active `(Source, Group)` pair.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, MOSPF belongs to the family of **intra-domain multicast routing protocols** (alongside **DVMRP** studied in the same module). While DVMRP uses a *distance-vector* approach, MOSPF uses a *link-state* approach.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy - The Smart City Dispatcher:**
> Imagine a chain of cities connected by highways, and a **local fire** (multicast source) needs to send a fire-alert SMS to all subscribers (group members) across the country. With **DVMRP**, every city only knows "who is my next-door neighbour in the direction of the source", so messages get cut-and-pruned back. With **MOSPF**, however, every city already has the **complete, identical road-map** of the entire country (the OSPF LSDB) and a list of "who subscribed to the fire-alert service" (Group Membership LSA). Therefore, the moment a fire-alert arrives, the dispatcher instantly computes the **shortest path to every fire-station that opted-in** and forwards along the tree.

### Key Standardized Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

* **Protocol Number / Standard:** Defined in **RFC 1584** (deprecated by RFC 4750 for IGMP-style operations, but historically canonical).
* **LSA Type for Group Membership:** **Type 6 MOSPF LSA** (also called the **Group-Membership LSA**).
* **Routing Computation:** Per **`(S, G)` pair** (Source IP + Multicast Group IP).
* **Algorithm Used:** **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF)** algorithm.
* **Path Cost Metric:** OSPF's standard **link cost** (typically inversely proportional to bandwidth, e.g., $\text{Cost} = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$, where Reference Bandwidth is **100 Mbps** by default).
* **Forwarding Check:** **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Check** - the packet must arrive on the interface that is the shortest path back to the source.

### Classification Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **MOSPF at a Glance:**
> * **Type:** Intra-domain, Link-state multicast protocol
> * **Transport Dependency:** Uses **OSPF** (Protocol 89) infrastructure
> * **Scope:** Works only within a single **Autonomous System (AS)**
> * **Tree Type:** **Source-Specific Shortest Path Tree (SPT)**
> * **State Storage:** Heavyweight (stores topology + per-group state)
> * **Current Status:** Rarely deployed in production; superseded by **PIM-SSM** / **PIM-SM**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Foundation

MOSPF is *not* a standalone protocol. It is a **set of extensions** to the existing OSPFv2 protocol. The extensions comprise:

1. **A new LSA Type** - **Type 6 (Group-Membership LSA)** flooded alongside the standard OSPF LSAs.
2. **A Local Group Database** maintained at every MOSPF-enabled router.
3. **A Multicast Routing Table** keyed by `(S, G)` pairs.
4. **A new Dijkstra run** triggered by data arrival, computing the SPT for `(S, G)`.

## 2.2 Operational Workflow (The "Why" and "How")

### Step 1 - Group Discovery via IGMP
* Each MOSPF router runs **IGMP** (Internet Group Management Protocol) on its directly connected subnets.
* Hosts wishing to join a multicast group `G` send IGMP Membership Reports.
* The router records this in its **Local Group Database**: `Group G → Interface I`.

### Step 2 - Flooding the Group-Membership LSA (Type 6)
* Every MOSPF router originates a **Type 6 LSA** into its area.
* The LSA's body lists all multicast groups for which that router has **directly connected members**.
* LSAs are flooded reliably (using OSPF's standard flooding mechanism) so every router in the area builds an identical picture.

### Step 3 - SPT Computation on Data Arrival
MOSPF is a **"data-driven" or "demand-driven"** protocol. The router runs Dijkstra's algorithm **only when the first packet of a new `(S, G)` flow arrives** - this conserves CPU.
* It uses the **OSPF LSDB** for topology and link costs.
* It uses the **Group-Membership LSAs** as "destinations" on that topology.
* Output: an **SPT rooted at the source S**, with leaves only at routers serving group `G`.

### Step 4 - Packet Forwarding
For every subsequent multicast packet:
1. **RPF Check** - Verify the packet arrived on the interface that is the shortest path *to* `S`. If not, drop it (loop prevention).
2. **Look up SPT** for `(S, G)`.
3. **Forward** to all child interfaces in the SPT, **except** the one on which the packet arrived.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All essential formulas and constants for MOSPF numerical/analytical questions:

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| OSPF Link Cost | $\text{Cost} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}} \right\rceil$ | dimensionless | Default $\text{Reference BW} = 10^{8} \text{ bps} = 100 \text{ Mbps}$ |
| Cumulative Path Cost | $\text{Cost}(\text{path}) = \sum_{i \in \text{edges}} \text{Cost}(i)$ | dimensionless | Used in Dijkstra relaxation |
| Number of SPTs | $N_{\text{SPT}} = N_{\text{sources}} \times N_{\text{groups}}$ | integer | Scalability bottleneck |
| RPF Check | $\text{Forward iff } \text{incoming\_iface} == \text{SPF}(S)$ | boolean | Prevents forwarding loops |
| Truncated Forwarding | $\text{Forward to } \{\text{children of SPT}\} \setminus \{\text{arrival iface}\}$ | set of interfaces | Implements prune implicitly |

*(Note: The table deliberately uses $\left\lceil \cdot \right\rceil$ instead of the vertical-pipe absolute value to maintain clean markdown rendering.)*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although MOSPF itself is rarely deployed today, the **concepts it pioneered** are fundamental to modern networking:

* **Modern multicast protocols** (PIM-SM, PIM-SSM) still rely on **RPF checks** - a direct descendant of MOSPF's check.
* **Segment Routing (SR)** and **Traffic Engineering** in service-provider networks use **per-source trees** conceptually identical to MOSPF SPTs.
* **Data Center fabrics** (e.g., BGP-based underlays) borrow the **link-state + per-flow path computation** philosophy.
* The **scalability lesson** (computing $N_{\text{sources}} \times N_{\text{groups}}$ SPTs does not scale) directly motivated the design of **PIM-SM**, which uses a single shared tree to amortize state.

## 2.5 Comparison: MOSPF vs DVMRP (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | DVMRP | MOSPF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Base Protocol | Distance-Vector (RIP-like) | Link-State (OSPF) |
| Algorithm | RPF + Prune/Graft | Dijkstra SPF per `(S,G)` |
| Tree Type | Source-based (with prune) | Source-based SPT |
| Scalability | Moderate (periodic floods) | Poor (S, G explosion) |
| Inter-domain | Yes (uses Tunneling) | No (intra-AS only) |
| Loop Prevention | RPF check | RPF check |
| Convergence | Slow (triggered updates) | Fast (LSDB sync) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Dijkstra SPT Computation for MOSPF (Worked Numerical Example)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 14-mark questions. We will trace MOSPF's exact procedure on a small network.

### 3.1.1 Network Topology

Consider the following graph (links annotated with OSPF cost):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nodes: } & R1, R2, R3, R4, R5 \\
\text{Edges (undirected):} & \\
& R1 \xrightarrow{2} R2 \\
& R1 \xrightarrow{5} R3 \\
& R2 \xrightarrow{1} R3 \\
& R2 \xrightarrow{3} R4 \\
& R3 \xrightarrow{2} R4 \\
& R4 \xrightarrow{1} R5
\end{aligned}
$$

* Assume **R1** is the source of multicast group **G = 224.1.1.1**.
* Assume **R3, R4, and R5** have directly connected members of `G` (i.e., they originated Type 6 LSAs).

### 3.1.2 Building the LSDB Cost Matrix

The OSPF LSDB (after full synchronization) gives the cost matrix. By symmetry of OSPF costs, the adjacency cost between any two nodes is identical in both directions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}(R1, R2) = 2, \quad & \text{Cost}(R1, R3) = 5, \quad \text{Cost}(R2, R3) = 1 \\
\text{Cost}(R2, R4) = 3, \quad & \text{Cost}(R3, R4) = 2, \quad \text{Cost}(R4, R5) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Applying Dijkstra's Algorithm from Source R1

We maintain a table of **tentative** distances and a **visited** set.

**Iteration 0 (Initialize):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{dist}(R1) = 0 \;\; (\text{visited}) \\
& \text{dist}(R2) = \infty, \quad \text{dist}(R3) = \infty, \quad \text{dist}(R4) = \infty, \quad \text{dist}(R5) = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1 - Visit R1 (dist = 0):**
Relax all neighbours of R1. Direct links are: $R1 \to R2$ (cost 2) and $R1 \to R3$ (cost 5).
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{dist}(R2) = \min(\infty, 0 + 2) = 2 \\
& \text{dist}(R3) = \min(\infty, 0 + 5) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2 - Visit R2 (smallest tentative, dist = 2):**
Neighbours of R2: $R1$ (visited), $R3$, $R4$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{dist}(R3) = \min(5, 2 + 1) = 3 \quad (\text{updated via } R1 \to R2 \to R3) \\
& \text{dist}(R4) = \min(\infty, 2 + 3) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3 - Visit R3 (smallest tentative, dist = 3):**
Neighbours of R3: $R1$ (visited), $R2$ (visited), $R4$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{dist}(R4) = \min(5, 3 + 2) = 5 \quad (\text{tie - keep existing or update; equal cost}) \\
& \text{dist}(R5) = \infty \;\; (R3 \text{ not directly connected to } R5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4 - Visit R4 (smallest tentative, dist = 5):**
*(R1 and R3 are both at dist 5; R4 chosen next by tie-breaking.)*
Neighbours of R4: $R2$ (visited), $R3$ (visited), $R5$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{dist}(R5) = \min(\infty, 5 + 1) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 5 - Visit R5 (dist = 6):**
No unvisited neighbours. Algorithm terminates.

### 3.1.4 Final SPT Distances and Parent Map

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{dist}(R1) = 0, \quad \text{parent} = \text{none} \\
& \text{dist}(R2) = 2, \quad \text{parent} = R1 \\
& \text{dist}(R3) = 3, \quad \text{parent} = R2 \\
& \text{dist}(R4) = 5, \quad \text{parent} = R2 \;\; (\text{or } R3, \text{ tie}) \\
& \text{dist}(R5) = 6, \quad \text{parent} = R4
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Truncating to MOSPF Group Members

Only **R3, R4, R5** have group `G` members. The other routers (`R1` is the source, `R2` is a transit) are stripped from the tree as leaves. The **MOSPF forwarding tree** for `(R1, 224.1.1.1)` is therefore:

$$
R1 \to R2 \to \begin{cases} R3 \\ R4 \to R5 \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **R1 → R2 → R4 (cost 5)** is preferred over **R1 → R3 → R4 (cost 7)**. MOSPF always picks the **lowest cumulative cost**, not the lowest hop-count. This is why it is called *Shortest Path Tree* and not *Minimum Hop Tree*.

## 3.2 MOSPF Forwarding Trace (Analytical Matrix)

Now consider a multicast packet arriving at R4 (let's say forwarded by R2). The router performs:

| Step | Action | Equation / Condition | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Identify (S, G)** | $S = R1, G = 224.1.1.1$ | Look up in SPT cache |
| 2 | **RPF Check** | $\text{Arrival iface} = \text{parent\_iface from SPT?}$ | $R2 \to R4$ link is the parent - PASS |
| 3 | **Determine Children** | $\text{Children}(R4) = \{R5\}$ (R3 is sibling via R2) | $\{R5\}$ |
| 4 | **Truncate to Group Members** | $\text{Children} \cap \text{Group\_Members}(G) = \{R5\}$ | $\{R5\}$ |
| 5 | **Forward** | Send to $\{R5\}$ | Packet replicated to R5 |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why MOSPF Scales Poorly

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{SPT}} & = N_{\text{sources}} \times N_{\text{active\_groups}} \\
\text{Memory}_{\text{per\_router}} & = O(N_{\text{nodes}} \times N_{\text{SPT}}) \\
\text{CPU}_{\text{per\_new\_flow}} & = O(E + N \log N) \;\; \text{(one Dijkstra run)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical ISP with $N_{\text{sources}} = 1000$ senders and $N_{\text{groups}} = 500$, this implies $N_{\text{SPT}} = 5 \times 10^{5}$ SPTs - computationally infeasible. This is the **central reason** for MOSPF's failure in production.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MOSPF Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OSPF Link-State Database LSDB] --> C[MOSPF Control Plane]
    B[IGMP Local Group Database] --> C
    D[Incoming Multicast Packet] --> E{RPF Check}
    C --> E
    E -- RPF Pass --> F[SPT Lookup for S,G]
    E -- RPF Fail --> G[Drop Packet Silently]
    F --> H{Truncated SPT Exists?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Forward to Children Interfaces]
    H -- No --> J[Trigger New Dijkstra Run]
    J --> K[Compute SPT Using LSDB + Group LSAs]
    K --> I
    I --> L[Replicate Packet to Outgoing Interfaces]
```

## 4.2 MOSPF Data Flow: From Source to Receivers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src[Source Host on R1]
    R1[R1 Source DR]
    R2[R2 Transit Router]
    R3[R3 Has Group Members]
    R4[R4 Has Group Members]
    R5[R5 Has Group Members]
    R3H[Receiver on R3]
    R4H[Receiver on R4]
    R5H[Receiver on R5]

    src -->|Unicast to R1| R1
    R1 -->|Multicast packet, SPT hop| R2
    R2 -->|Branch 1| R3
    R2 -->|Branch 2| R4
    R4 -->|Branch| R5
    R3 --> R3H
    R4 --> R4H
    R5 --> R5H
```

## 4.3 Group Membership LSA (Type 6) Flooding Process

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant H as Host
    participant R3 as R3 with member
    participant LSDB as Area LSDB
    participant R1 as R1 Source
    participant R5 as R5 with member

    H->>R3: IGMP Membership Report for Group G
    R3->>LSDB: Originate Type 6 Group Membership LSA
    Note over LSDB: All MOSPF routers receive LSA via OSPF flooding
    R1->>LSDB: Query LSDB for Group G on packet arrival
    LSDB->>R1: Return list of group-member routers R3, R5
    R1->>R1: Run Dijkstra from source to R3, R5
    R1->>R3: Forward first packet along SPT
    R1->>R5: Forward first packet along SPT
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a MOSPF Router

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputPlane[Input Plane]
        IGMP[IGMP Snooping Module]
        OSPF[OSPF Neighbor Module]
        PHY[Physical Interfaces]
    end

    subgraph ControlPlane[Control Plane]
        LGDB[Local Group Database]
        LSDB2[OSPF Link State Database]
        SPFEngine[Dijkstra SPF Engine]
        SPTCache[SPT Cache for S,G pairs]
        MRTable[Multicast Routing Table]
    end

    subgraph ForwardingPlane[Forwarding Plane]
        RPFCheck[RPF Check Logic]
        Replicator[Packet Replicator]
        OIFList[Outgoing Interface List OIF]
    end

    IGMP --> LGDB
    OSPF --> LSDB2
    LGDB --> SPFEngine
    LSDB2 --> SPFEngine
    SPFEngine --> SPTCache
    SPTCache --> MRTable
    PHY --> RPFCheck
    MRTable --> RPFCheck
    RPFCheck --> Replicator
    MRTable --> OIFList
    OIFList --> Replicator
    Replicator --> PHY
```

## 4.5 Comparison Workflow: DVMRP vs MOSPF Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need Multicast Routing?] --> Q1{Intra-domain or Inter-domain?}
    Q1 -- Intra --> Q2{Network Size and Group Count?}
    Q1 -- Inter --> DVMRP[Use DVMRP with Tunnels]
    Q2 -- Small to Medium --> MOSPF[Use MOSPF]
    Q2 -- Large or Many Groups --> PIMSM[Use PIM-SM instead]
    MOSPF --> Adv1[Reuses OSPF topology]
    MOSPF --> Dis1[Scalability bottleneck]
    DVMRP --> Adv2[Simple, works across AS]
    DVMRP --> Dis2[Slow convergence, periodic floods]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** 2-3 sentences, direct, definition-based. Marks awarded for keyword hits.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define MOSPF. What type of LSA does it introduce and what is its purpose?**

**Model Answer:**
* **MOSPF (Multicast Open Shortest Path First)** is an extension to OSPF that provides intra-domain multicast routing.
* It introduces a new **Type 6 LSA**, called the **Group-Membership LSA**, which is flooded within an OSPF area to advertise the multicast groups for which a router has directly attached members.
* Using this LSA and the standard OSPF link-state database, each MOSPF router can compute a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** for every active `(Source, Group)` pair.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three differences between DVMRP and MOSPF.**

**Model Answer:**
* **DVMRP** is a **distance-vector** protocol using RPF and prune/graft messages; **MOSPF** is a **link-state** protocol using the OSPF LSDB and Dijkstra's algorithm.
* **DVMRP** periodically floods the network and uses data-driven pruning; **MOSPF** is **data-driven** and only computes a new SPT on the arrival of the first packet for a new `(S, G)`.
* **DVMRP** can span multiple Autonomous Systems (using tunnels), whereas **MOSPF** is strictly **intra-domain** (limited to a single OSPF area/AS).

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Internal choice is **must**.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of MOSPF. How does it reuse the OSPF infrastructure to provide multicast routing? (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the MOSPF Shortest Path Tree (SPT) for the following topology. Source = **A**, Multicast group members are at routers **D, E, and F**. Edge costs: A-B = 4, A-C = 2, B-D = 3, C-D = 1, C-E = 5, D-F = 2, E-F = 1. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**
1. MOSPF is an **extension module** to OSPFv2; it does not replace OSPF.
2. The existing **OSPF link-state database (LSDB)** is reused as the network topology map.
3. A **new Type 6 LSA (Group-Membership LSA)** is added. Every MOSPF router injects one into its area, listing the groups for which it has directly connected hosts (learned via IGMP).
4. When a multicast data packet for `(S, G)` arrives at a router:
   * The router **performs an RPF check** on the packet's source `S`.
   * It then **triggers a Dijkstra computation** (or uses a cached SPT) using the OSPF LSDB with the group-member routers as the destinations.
   * The resulting tree is **truncated** - only routers serving group `G` are kept as leaves.
   * The packet is **replicated** to all child interfaces except the arrival one.
5. The **forwarding plane** uses an OIF list derived from this SPT; no separate multicast routing protocol daemon is needed.

**Model Solution to (b) - Dijkstra Trace:**

Nodes: A, B, C, D, E, F. Source: A. Group members: D, E, F.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 0:} \quad & \text{dist}(A) = 0 \;\; (\text{visited}) \\
& \text{All others} = \infty \\
\text{Iteration 1 (visit A):} \quad & \text{dist}(B) = 4,\; \text{dist}(C) = 2 \\
\text{Iteration 2 (visit C, dist=2):} \quad & \text{dist}(D) = 2 + 1 = 3,\; \text{dist}(E) = 2 + 5 = 7 \\
\text{Iteration 3 (visit D, dist=3):} \quad & \text{dist}(F) = 3 + 2 = 5 \\
\text{Iteration 4 (visit B, dist=4):} \quad & \text{dist}(D) = \min(3, 4 + 3) = 3 \;\; \text{(no change)} \\
\text{Iteration 5 (visit F, dist=5):} \quad & \text{dist}(E) = \min(7, 5 + 1) = 6 \;\; \text{(updated)} \\
\text{Iteration 6 (visit E, dist=6):} \quad & \text{Terminate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final SPT (parent map) - truncated to group members {D, E, F}:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{dist}(D) = 3, \quad \text{parent} = C \\
& \text{dist}(F) = 5, \quad \text{parent} = D \\
& \text{dist}(E) = 6, \quad \text{parent} = F
\end{aligned}
$$

The **MOSPF forwarding tree for (A, G)** is therefore:

$$
A \to C \to D \to F \to E
$$

Total cost to farthest receiver (E) = **6**. R4's link A→B (cost 4) is unused for this tree because C provides a shorter path to D.

### Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of the **Group-Membership LSA (Type 6)** in MOSPF. Why is this LSA needed when OSPF already exists? (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare MOSPF with DVMRP under the following heads: (i) Algorithm, (ii) Tree Type, (iii) Scalability, (iv) Inter-domain Support, (v) Loop Prevention, (vi) Convergence, (vii) Deployment Status. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **Group-Membership LSA (Type 6)** is a new Link-State Advertisement introduced by MOSPF and flooded only within an OSPF area.

* **Why it is needed:** OSPF's existing LSAs (Router LSA, Network LSA, Summary LSA) only describe **topology and reachability for unicast prefixes**. They carry no information about *which router serves which multicast group*. Without this information, no router could decide *where the leaves of the multicast tree should be*. The Type 6 LSA fills this gap by advertising, for each MOSPF router, the set of multicast groups for which the router has directly attached, interested hosts.
* **Structure:** Contains the **router's OSPF Router ID**, a list of `(Group Address, TTL)` entries indicating the multicast groups the router has local members for.
* **Lifecycle:** Originated by a router when it learns of a new local group member (via IGMP). It is **reflooded** when the membership set changes. It is **withdrawn** when the group has no more local members.
* **Scope:** **Area-local** - it does not cross OSPF area boundaries (matches the scope of the topology it augments).

**Model Solution to (b) - Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | DVMRP | MOSPF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Algorithm | Distance-Vector (Bellman-Ford style) + RPF | Link-State (Dijkstra SPF) |
| (ii) Tree Type | Source-based, prune/graft | Source-based SPT |
| (iii) Scalability | Moderate (flood every minute) | Poor (S, G explosion) |
| (iv) Inter-domain | Yes, via IP tunnels | No, intra-AS only |
| (v) Loop Prevention | RPF check + prune | RPF check |
| (vi) Convergence | Slow (triggered/periodic) | Fast (LSDB sync, on-demand) |
| (vii) Deployment Status | Historical (mrouted) | Largely experimental, replaced by PIM |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> * **Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **DVMRP and MOSPF**, claiming MOSPF uses "distance vector". It uses **link-state + Dijkstra** - this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss if wrong.
> * **Mistake 2:** Forgetting that MOSPF's SPT is **per `(S, G)`** and not per group. With $N$ sources and $M$ groups, you have $N \times M$ trees. The scalability question is the most common 14-mark "explain why MOSPF failed" question.
> * **Mistake 3:** Skipping the **RPF check** step in forwarding. Every multicast protocol in this module (DVMRP, MOSPF, PIM) requires an RPF check - mentioning it is a free 1-2 marks.
> * **Mistake 4:** Writing `|` (vertical bar) inside LaTeX expressions in answer sheets. Use $\lvert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value to match how examiners expect mathematical clarity.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist - MOSPF (Module 3):**
> * **Full Name:** Multicast Open Shortest Path First - **RFC 1584**.
> * **Base Protocol:** OSPFv2 (Link-State, Protocol 89).
> * **New LSA Type:** **Type 6** - Group-Membership LSA.
> * **Tree Type:** **Source-Specific SPT** rooted at the source, truncated to group members.
> * **Algorithm:** **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF)** run per `(S, G)` flow on **first packet arrival** (data-driven).
> * **RPF Check:** Mandatory before any forwarding - prevents loops.
> * **IGMP Role:** Used on every router's directly connected subnets to discover local group members.
> * **Scope:** **Intra-domain** (single OSPF area/AS). Not used for inter-domain multicast.
> * **Default OSPF Reference Bandwidth:** **100 Mbps** (for cost calculation).
> * **Path Cost Formula:** $\text{Cost}_{\text{path}} = \sum \text{Cost}_{\text{edges}}$ along the path.
> * **LSDB Reuse:** MOSPF's greatest strength - no separate topology protocol needed.
> * **Scalability Bottleneck:** $N_{\text{sources}} \times N_{\text{groups}}$ SPTs; $O(E + N \log N)$ Dijkstra per new flow.
> * **Forwarding Equation:** $\text{OIF}(S, G) = \{\text{children of SPT}\} \setminus \{\text{arrival iface}\} \cap \{\text{group-member interfaces}\}$.
> * **Comparison Anchors:** DVMRP = distance vector + RPF; MOSPF = link state + Dijkstra + SPT.
> * **Deployment Status:** **Largely obsolete**; superseded by **PIM-SM / PIM-SSM** in production networks, but conceptually crucial for understanding modern multicast.
> * **Key Differentiator from DVMRP:** MOSPF builds a *true* SPT (minimum cost), while DVMRP's prune leaves remnants of the broadcast tree.
> * **Examiner Keywords to Use:** "Type 6 LSA", "Dijkstra", "RPF check", "truncated tree", "data-driven computation", "intra-domain", "group-membership".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DVMRP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DVMRP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol (DVMRP)** is an interior gateway protocol (IGP) specifically engineered to facilitate the efficient delivery of IP multicast datagrams across an autonomous system. It is essentially the **multicast counterpart of the unicast Routing Information Protocol (RIP)**, because it borrows directly from RIP's distance-vector algorithmic foundation. DVMRP is defined in the experimental IETF document **RFC 1075** and historically powers the **MBONE (Multicast Backbone)** — the early experimental multicast overlay network layered across the public Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** DVMRP is studied in Module 3 alongside other multicast routing protocols. Students must clearly distinguish *flood-and-prune* protocols (like DVMRP) from *link-state* multicast protocols (like MOSPF) and *shared-tree* protocols (like PIM Sparse Mode and CBT).

At the heart of DVMRP lie **three classical mechanisms** that together produce an efficient multicast distribution tree:

1. **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** — prevents forwarding loops.
2. **Reverse Path Broadcasting (RPB)** — guarantees exactly one copy per link.
3. **Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM)** — dynamically prunes branches that have no listeners (flood-and-prune behaviour).

DVMRP routers also rely on the **Internet Group Management Protocol (IGMP)** to discover which physical hosts on their directly-attached LAN segments actually want to receive which multicast groups. Finally, **DVMRP tunnels** are used to transparently transport multicast packets across legacy unicast-only transit networks.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a public-address system in a large apartment complex where a single announcer broadcasts a live cricket match commentary.

- **Initial Phase (Flooding):** The announcer shouts the commentary so loud that *every* apartment can hear it — even flats where nobody is interested. This is wasteful, but it guarantees that the *interested* flats *do* receive the audio. This is **RPF/RPB flooding**.
- **Discovery Phase:** As each apartment turns on the TV and starts watching, the *uninterested* households call the building manager and say, *"Please stop sending us this commentary — we don't watch cricket."* This is the **PRUNE message** in DVMRP.
- **Re-engagement:** If a previously disinterested flat suddenly has guests who want to watch the match, the occupant calls the manager and says, *"Please resume sending us the commentary."* This is the **GRAFT message**.
- **Tunnels:** If the complex is split into two buildings connected only by a narrow mail-shaft corridor that normally only carries letters (unicast traffic), a special "letterbox tube" is used to physically carry the audio stream across. This is a **DVMRP tunnel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Snapshot:** DVMRP = "Broadcast everything first, then silence the rooms that don't care." It trades bandwidth efficiency for *simplicity* and *guaranteed delivery during initial transmission*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Distance Vector** | Each router advertises only its *next-hop* and *hop-count metric* to every destination — no full topology map is exchanged. |
| **Multicast Group** | A Class-D IP address (224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255) identifying a logical set of receivers. |
| **IGMP** | Layer-3 signalling protocol between host and local multicast router. |
| **TTL (Time-To-Live)** | Used as a scope-limiting mechanism to prevent multicast packets from leaking beyond administrative boundaries. |
| **MBONE** | The historical virtual multicast network composed of DVMRP tunnels. |
| **MBB (Multicast Border Router)** | Border router that connects one DVMRP "cloud" to another. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Position of DVMRP in the Protocol Stack

DVMRP operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** of the OSI model. It interacts with:

- **IGMP (above the data-link layer)** — for local group membership polling.
- **IP** — for carrying its own control messages using **IP Protocol Number 3**.
- **The underlying unicast routing table** — because RPF checks *depend* on a pre-existing unicast forwarding table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pre-requisite:** Before a DVMRP router can do RPF, it *must* already have a unicast routing table — typically populated by **RIP**, **OSPF**, or a static route. DVMRP is therefore **not a standalone routing protocol**; it is a *multicast extension* over a unicast substrate.

## 2.2 Operational Pipeline of DVMRP

The end-to-end operational flow of DVMRP can be broken down into the following **seven logical phases**:

### Phase 1 — IGMP Local Membership Discovery
- A host sends an **IGMP Membership Report** (IGMPv2) when it joins a group `G`.
- The directly-attached DVMRP router records the group's presence on that interface in its *local-membership table*.
- If no host responds to periodic **IGMP Membership Queries** for a group, the router times out the entry and concludes the branch is a *leaf* with no listeners.

### Phase 2 — Distance-Vector Table Exchange
- DVMRP routers periodically (default **every 10 seconds**) broadcast a **DVMRP Route Report** to all DVMRP neighbours.
- Each report contains *destination network prefix* → *hop-count* → *next-hop router*.
- Hop-count is the metric; values greater than **32** are considered *infinity* (route is poisoned).

### Phase 3 — Source-Based Tree Construction (Truncated Broadcast Tree)
- When a source `S` sends the first packet to group `G`, DVMRP *floods* it down the **shortest-path tree (SPT)** rooted at `S`.
- The tree is built *implicitly* using the **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** check at every router.

### Phase 4 — Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Check
- Upon receiving a packet with source `S` on interface `I`, the router consults its unicast routing table to find the *expected* interface for reaching `S`.
- If `I == expected_interface`, the packet is **accepted** and forwarded to *all other interfaces* (except the one it arrived on).
- If `I ≠ expected_interface`, the packet is **silently discarded** (RPF failure → potential loop).

### Phase 5 — Reverse Path Broadcasting (RPB)
- Among the *downstream* interfaces that passed RPF, RPB guarantees that **exactly one copy of the packet is forwarded per link**, eliminating duplicate deliveries to a multi-access LAN.

### Phase 6 — Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM) — Pruning
- A leaf router with no interested hosts sends a **PRUNE message** upstream toward the source `S` for the `(S, G)` pair.
- The upstream router caches this prune and stops forwarding the `(S, G)` stream on the pruned interface.
- Prunes have a lifetime — by default **2 hours** — after which flooding resumes unless the prune is re-issued.

### Phase 7 — GRAFTing (Re-joining)
- A previously pruned leaf router with a newly arrived host sends a **GRAFT message** to the upstream router.
- The upstream router immediately resumes forwarding and acknowledges with a **GRAFT-ACK**.

## 2.3 DVMRP Message Format (Conceptual Header)

DVMRP uses IP protocol number 3. The IP header carries:

- **Version (1 byte):** 1, 2, or 3.
- **Type (1 byte):** 1 = Probe, 2 = Route Report, 3 = Prune, 4 = Graft, 5 = Graft-Ack.
- **Checksum (2 bytes):** Standard Internet checksum.
- **Minor Version (1 byte):** 0 = DVMRPv1, 1 = DVMRPv2, 3 = DVMRPv3.

> [!NOTE]
> **DVMRP Neighbour Discovery** is performed via a **DVMRP Probe** sent every **10 seconds** on every DVMRP-enabled interface. A router learns its neighbours from probe responses. The default **neighbour timeout** is **35 seconds** (≈ 3.5 × probe interval).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all numeric constants, metric rules, and timer settings that the KTU examiner expects a student to recall verbatim.

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Rule | Unit |
|-----------|--------|--------------|------|
| Hop-count metric | $m$ | $0 \le m \le 32$ (32 = infinity) | hops |
| Hop increment on forwarding | $\Delta m$ | $+1$ per router hop | hops |
| DVMRP Protocol Number in IP | $P_{DVMRP}$ | $3$ | — |
| IGMP Protocol Number in IP | $P_{IGMP}$ | $2$ | — |
| Default Route-Report interval | $T_{rep}$ | $10$ | seconds |
| Default Probe interval | $T_{pr}$ | $10$ | seconds |
| Default Prune lifetime | $T_{prune}$ | $2$ hours $= 7200$ | seconds |
| Default Neighbour timeout | $T_{nbr}$ | $35$ | seconds |
| Multicast Group address range | $G$ | $\mathbf{224.0.0.0}$ – $\mathbf{239.255.255.255}$ | IP class D |
| TTL scope boundaries | $T_{TTL}$ | $0$ (host), $1$ (LAN), $32$ (regional), $64$ (wide), $255$ (global) | — |

### 2.4.1 Key Decision Equations

**(a) RPF Acceptance Condition:**

$$ \text{Packet}_{S \rightarrow G} \text{ accepted at router } R \iff \text{RecvIf}(R) = \text{ForwardingIf}_{R}(S) $$

**(b) Hop-Count Update (Bellman-Ford Style):**

$$ D_{R}(S) = \min_{k \in \text{Neighbours}} \bigl( D_{k}(S) + 1 \bigr) $$

**(c) Infinity (Route Poisoning):**

$$ \text{Metric}_{\infty} = 32 \quad ; \quad \text{If } D_{R}(S) \ge 32 \Rightarrow S \text{ unreachable} $$

**(d) Prune State Machine — TTL Arithmetic:**

$$ T_{\text{remaining}} = T_{prune} - (T_{\text{now}} - T_{\text{prune\_received}}) $$

If $T_{\text{remaining}} \le 0$, the prune expires → flooding re-starts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **IPTV / Live Sports Streaming** | DVMRP was historically used by ISPs to push live feeds to regional headends. |
| **Stock Ticker Distribution** | Financial data exchanges used DVMRP to multicast tick-by-tick quotes to thousands of brokers. |
| **Software Distribution** | Enterprise networks used DVMRP to push OS images to many clients simultaneously, conserving bandwidth vs. unicast repetition. |
| **Teleconferencing & Distance Learning** | Multiparty video/audio sessions over MAN/WAN backbones. |
| **MBONE Seminars** | IETF meetings, NASA broadcasts, and academic seminars were distributed globally over the MBONE using DVMRP tunnels. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — How DVMRP Builds the Distribution Tree

### 3.1.1 The Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Decision

We start with a generic topology of $N$ routers $\{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_N\}$ and a multicast source $S$ attached to $R_1$.

**Given:**
- Unicast routing table at each router $R_i$ giving the next-hop interface for $S$, denoted $F_i(S)$.
- A packet arrives at $R_i$ on interface $I_{\text{in}}$ with source IP = $S$.

**Find:** Whether the packet is forwarded, and on which interfaces.

**Step 1 — RPF Check**

We consult the unicast forwarding table:

$$ \text{If } F_i(S) = I_{\text{in}} \Rightarrow \text{RPF PASS} $$

$$ \text{If } F_i(S) \neq I_{\text{in}} \Rightarrow \text{RPF FAIL} \Rightarrow \text{DROP} $$

**Step 2 — Reverse Path Broadcasting (RPB)**

Assuming RPF passed, the set of candidate output interfaces is:

$$ \mathcal{O}_i = \{ \text{All interfaces of } R_i \} \setminus \{ I_{\text{in}} \} $$

For each interface $j \in \mathcal{O}_i$, we consult whether the *neighbour downstream* of $j$ also uses $j$ as its **shortest path to $S$**. If multiple downstream routers share a common multi-access link $j$, only **one copy** is forwarded on that link (this is the *designated forwarder* for the link).

$$ \text{Forwarder on link } j = \arg\min_{k \in \text{Neighbours on } j} \bigl[ D_k(S) \bigr] $$

If the local router $R_i$ is the designated forwarder for link $j$, then $j$ remains in the output set; otherwise it is removed.

**Step 3 — Reverse Path Multicasting (RPM) — Pruning**

For every leaf interface $j$ that has *no IGMP-reporting hosts* for group $G$:

$$ \text{If } \text{IGMP\_Members}(j, G) = \emptyset \Rightarrow R_i \text{ sends PRUNE}(S, G) \text{ upstream} $$

The upstream router $R_u$ (the one on $F_i(S)$) caches the prune:

$$ \text{PruneCache}_{R_u} \leftarrow (S, G, I_{\text{out}}) $$

The interface $I_{\text{out}}$ is removed from $\mathcal{O}_u$ for the duration of $T_{prune}$.

**Step 4 — Re-join via GRAFT**

If a host later joins $G$ on interface $j$:

$$ \text{If } \text{IGMP\_Members}(j, G) \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow R_i \text{ sends GRAFT}(S, G) \text{ upstream} $$

The upstream router immediately reinstates the interface, and the multicast stream resumes within milliseconds.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A source $S$ is on network `10.0.0.0/24` attached to router $R_1$. Group $G = 224.1.1.1$. Five routers form a chain: $R_1 – R_2 – R_3 – R_4 – R_5$. $R_2$ has 3 leaf networks (LA, LB, LC) where *only* LA has a listener for $G$. $R_4$ has 2 leaf networks (LD, LE) where *only* LE has a listener. Compute the active forwarding interfaces at every hop, and the prune state.

**Step A — Build the unicast routing table (RIP-like, hop-count):**

| Router | Distance to $S$ | Next-Hop |
|--------|----------------|----------|
| $R_1$ | $0$ | local |
| $R_2$ | $1$ | $R_1$ |
| $R_3$ | $2$ | $R_2$ |
| $R_4$ | $3$ | $R_3$ |
| $R_5$ | $4$ | $R_4$ |

**Step B — Identify listener interfaces**

- $R_2$: LA = LISTENER, LB = NO, LC = NO
- $R_4$: LE = LISTENER, LD = NO

**Step C — At $R_1$ (source side):** Floods to all interfaces except the one leading back to $S$.

- Active outputs at $R_1$: {link to $R_2$}

**Step D — At $R_2$ (RPF check using $R_1$ as expected):** $F_{R_2}(S) = \text{link to } R_1$. Packet arrives from $R_1$ → RPF PASS.

- Candidate outputs: {LA, LB, LC, link to $R_3$}
- Apply RPM pruning: LB and LC are *pruned*. Only LA forwards; $R_3$ link is forwarded.
- Active outputs at $R_2$: {LA, link to $R_3$}
- $R_2$ sends PRUNE(S,G) to $R_1$ on interfaces LB and LC.

**Step E — At $R_3$:** RPF PASS via $R_2$.

- Candidate outputs: {link to $R_4$}
- Active outputs at $R_3$: {link to $R_4$}

**Step F — At $R_4$:** RPF PASS via $R_3$.

- Candidate outputs: {LD, LE, link to $R_5$}
- Pruning: LD is *pruned*; LE forwards; $R_5$ link forwards.
- Active outputs at $R_4$: {LE, link to $R_5$}

**Step G — At $R_5$:** Leaf router.

- Candidate outputs: {local users}
- Assuming no listeners, $R_5$ issues PRUNE(S,G) to $R_4$ → $R_4$ stops forwarding toward $R_5$ and the prune propagates upward.

**Final Active Distribution Set:**

$$ \mathcal{T}_{active} = \{ S \to R_1 \to R_2 \to \text{LA}\} \cup \{R_2 \to R_3 \to R_4 \to \text{LE}\} $$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DVMRP vs Other Multicast Protocols

> [!NOTE]
> This table is one of the most frequently asked artefacts in KTU module-end exams. Memorise the contrasts across *tree type*, *state*, *join direction*, and *scalability*.

| Property | DVMRP | MOSPF | PIM-DM | PIM-SM | CBT |
|----------|-------|-------|--------|--------|-----|
| **Algorithm Class** | Distance-Vector | Link-State | Dense-Mode | Sparse-Mode | Sparse-Mode (Shared Tree) |
| **Tree Type** | Source Tree (SPT) | Source Tree (SPT) | Source Tree (SPT) | Initially Shared, then SPT | Shared Tree (RP/Core) |
| **Initial Strategy** | Flood + Prune | Pre-computed Dijkstra | Flood + Prune | Explicit Join | Explicit Join |
| **Router State per (S,G)** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (only *( *,G)* state) |
| **Signalling Protocol** | IGMP + DVMRP | IGMP + OSPF | IGMP + PIM | IGMP + PIM | IGMP + CBT |
| **Scalability** | Poor (floods) | Medium | Poor in sparse nets | Excellent | Good |
| **Suitable For** | Dense membership | Single OSPF domain | Dense membership | Sparse, global scale | Sparse, stable groups |
| **IP Protocol Number** | $3$ | $89$ (OSPF) | $103$ | $103$ | $7$ |
| **Underlying Unicast Protocol** | Independent (uses unicast table) | OSPF (mandatory) | Independent | Independent | Independent |
| **Tunneling Support** | Yes (built-in) | No | No | No | No |
| **MBONE Heritage** | Yes (core protocol) | No | No | No | No |

## 3.3 Merits and Demerits — Engineering Trade-off Table

| ✅ Merits | ❌ Demerits |
|----------|------------|
| Conceptually simple; "flood first, prune later." | Periodic flooding wastes bandwidth. |
| Inherits RIP's straightforward configuration. | Scales poorly to large or sparse networks. |
| Works over *any* unicast routing protocol (RIP, OSPF, static). | Periodic full-flood every 10 s — control overhead. |
| Tunneling mechanism built-in (MBONE-friendly). | Prunes expire after 2 h → re-flood storms. |
| Fast initial delivery — no signalling before first packet. | Source-tree state in every router: $O(S \cdot G)$ memory. |
| RPF/RPB eliminates forwarding loops & duplicates. | Slow convergence due to distance-vector periodic updates. |
| | Counting-to-infinity pathologies inherited from RIP. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DVMRP Operational Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Host S sends packet to Group G] --> B[Packet arrives at first-hop Router R1]
    B --> C{RPF Check: Is receive interface == unicast route to S?}
    C -- No --> D[Drop Packet Silently]
    C -- Yes --> E[Reverse Path Broadcasting: Build truncated broadcast tree]
    E --> F[Forward one copy per link to downstream neighbours]
    F --> G{Leaf Router: Any IGMP Members for G on this branch?}
    G -- No --> H[Send PRUNE message upstream toward S]
    H --> I[Prune cached for T_prune = 2 hours]
    I --> J{Prune lifetime expired?}
    J -- No --> K[Continue to suppress forwarding]
    J -- Yes --> L[Re-flood after expiry unless re-pruned]
    G -- Yes --> M[Forward packet to local hosts on that branch]
    M --> N{Host later leaves group G?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Send IGMP Leave upstream]
    N -- No --> P[Continue receiving stream]
    L --> E
    O --> G
```

## 4.2 DVMRP Message Exchange Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Host as Interested Host
    participant LeafR as Leaf DVMRP Router
    participant MidR as Intermediate Router
    participant Source as Multicast Source

    Host->>LeafR: IGMP Membership Report (Group G)
    LeafR->>MidR: DVMRP Route Report (periodic)
    MidR->>LeafR: DVMRP Route Report (periodic)
    Source->>MidR: First Multicast Packet (S, G)
    MidR->>LeafR: Flood (S, G) downstream
    LeafR-->>Host: Deliver packet to local members
    Note over LeafR,MidR: Another branch has NO listeners
    LeafR-->>MidR: PRUNE (S, G) for non-listener branch
    Note over MidR: Prune cached for 2 hours
    Note over Host,LeafR: New host joins G on previously pruned branch
    Host->>LeafR: IGMP Membership Report (Group G)
    LeafR-->>MidR: GRAFT (S, G)
    MidR-->>LeafR: GRAFT-ACK
    MidR->>LeafR: Resume forwarding (S, G)
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DVMRP Router

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["INPUT PIPELINE"]
        A1[Network Interface Card]
        A2[IP Layer Demultiplexer]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL PLANE"]
        B1[IGMP Module - Local Membership]
        B2[Unicast Routing Table - From RIP/OSPF]
        B3[DVMRP Neighbour Discovery via Probes]
        B4[Distance-Vector Computation Bellman-Ford]
    end

    subgraph DATA["DATA PLANE - MULTICAST ENGINE"]
        C1[RPF Check Unit]
        C2[RPB Designated Forwarder Selector]
        C3[RPM Prune/Graft State Machine]
        C4[(S,G) Forwarding Cache]
    end

    subgraph OUTP["OUTPUT PIPELINE"]
        D1[IP Encapsulation]
        D2[Interface Dispatcher]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B1 --> C3
    B3 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> A1
```

## 4.4 DVMRP Tunneling Across Non-Multicast Cloud

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Multicast Network A"]
        Src[Multicast Source]
        Ra[DVMRP Router A]
    end

    subgraph C["Non-Multicast Transit Cloud (e.g. legacy Internet)"]
        X1[IP Router X]
        X2[IP Router Y]
    end

    subgraph B["Multicast Network B"]
        Rb[DVMRP Router B]
        Rcv[Multicast Receiver]
    end

    Src --> Ra
    Ra -- IP-in-IP Encapsulated DVMRP Tunnel --> X1
    X1 --> X2
    X2 -- Decapsulate --> Rb
    Rb --> Rcv
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Tunnel Mechanics:** Router $R_a$ encapsulates the original multicast packet (destined to `224.1.1.1`) inside a *unicast* IP packet whose destination is the tunnel-endpoint address of $R_b$. The transit routers $X_1, X_2$ see only the unicast outer header. At $R_b$, the packet is decapsulated and processed as a normal multicast datagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]`
**Define DVMRP. Mention any two key features that distinguish it from unicast RIP.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol (DVMRP) is an *interior multicast routing protocol* defined in **RFC 1075** that provides best-effort delivery of IP multicast datagrams across an autonomous system. It is the multicast analogue of the unicast Routing Information Protocol (RIP).
- **Distinguishing features (1.5 marks — any two):**
  1. Uses **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** to avoid forwarding loops.
  2. Employs **flood-and-prune** (RPM) instead of always-on broadcasting.
  3. Exchanges **DVMRP-specific Prune, Graft, and Probe messages** that RIP does not have.
  4. Supports **IP tunneling** for multicast transport across non-multicast clouds (e.g., the MBONE).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S2 Model QP]`
**What is a DVMRP tunnel? Why is it required in the MBONE context?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A DVMRP tunnel is a *virtual point-to-point link* created by encapsulating multicast IP packets inside the payload of a unicast IP packet. The source and destination of the outer IP header are the two tunnel-endpoint DVMRP routers.
- **Purpose in MBONE (2 marks):**
  1. The MBONE was constructed over the *existing unicast Internet* whose core routers were *not multicast-aware*. Tunnels allowed DVMRP islands to *transparently* extend the multicast cloud through legacy unicast routers.
  2. They preserved the *Group and Source IP addresses* of the original multicast packet during transit, so the decapsulating end-router could resume normal multicast processing without modification to intermediate routers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, S5 B.Tech CSE – Module 3]`

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **DVMRP** protocol. Describe **RPF, RPB, and RPM** mechanisms in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare DVMRP with **PIM Dense Mode (PIM-DM)**. Mention the **merits and demerits** of DVMRP. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working of DVMRP with RPF, RPB, RPM (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-requisite — Unicast Routing Table (0.5 mark):** Before any multicast forwarding, the router loads a unicast forwarding table (e.g., from RIP/OSPF). This is essential for the RPF check.

2. **IGMP Local Membership Polling (0.5 mark):** The router periodically sends **IGMP Membership Queries** (default every 125 s) on every attached LAN. Hosts respond with **IGMP Membership Reports** for each group they want.

3. **Reverse Path Forwarding — RPF (2 marks):**
   - On receiving a packet with source $S$, the router checks the unicast routing table for the *expected interface* toward $S$.
   - If the packet's incoming interface equals the expected interface, the RPF check **passes** and the packet is accepted. Otherwise the packet is **dropped** (to break potential forwarding loops).
   - *Why?* It ensures the packet has travelled along the *shortest path* from the source, so forwarding is loop-free.

4. **Reverse Path Broadcasting — RPB (1.5 marks):**
   - Once RPF passes, the packet would normally be flooded to *all* interfaces except the incoming one.
   - On a multi-access LAN, however, multiple downstream routers might be present, and each would forward the packet, producing *duplicates*. RPB elects a **designated forwarder** per link — the router with the smallest distance to $S$ — and only that router forwards one copy. This yields a **truncated broadcast tree** rooted at the source.

5. **Reverse Path Multicasting — RPM (1.5 marks):**
   - Leaves with no IGMP members for group $G$ send a **PRUNE(S, G)** message upstream.
   - The upstream router caches the prune in its *(S, G)* state table and stops forwarding the stream on the pruned interface.
   - Prune lifetime is **2 hours**; if not refreshed, forwarding resumes.
   - If a host later joins, the leaf sends a **GRAFT** → upstream re-enables forwarding within milliseconds → acknowledges with **GRAFT-ACK**.

6. **Neighbour Discovery:** Routers exchange **DVMRP Probes** every 10 s; **DVMRP Route Reports** carry distance-vector metrics; default **metric infinity = 32**.

> **Diagram requirement (1 mark):** Draw a network with source $S$, routers $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, leaf LANs, and annotate active vs. pruned branches using arrows (as in Section 4.1 above).

**(b) DVMRP vs. PIM-DM and merits/demerits of DVMRP (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Property | DVMRP | PIM-DM |
|----------|-------|--------|
| IETF Status | Experimental (RFC 1075) | Proposed Standard (RFC 3973) |
| Underlying Unicast | Independent | Independent |
| Encapsulation | Its own (IP Proto 3) | IP Protocol 103, native |
| Tunneling | Built-in | Not built-in |
| Prune State | Per (S, G) | Per (S, G) |
| Prune Lifetime | 2 hours | 3 minutes |
| Graft Support | Yes (full Graft + Graft-Ack) | Yes (PIM Graft) |
| State in Core Routers | High | High |
| Implementation in Modern Routers | Rare | Common |

**Merits of DVMRP (1.5 marks):**
1. Algorithmically simple; quick to deploy.
2. Tunneling feature made it the *de facto* protocol of the MBONE.
3. Works over *any* underlying unicast protocol.
4. RPF/RPB/RPM triple guarantees *exactly-once* delivery on every link.
5. Very fast initial delivery — no explicit join signalling is needed before the first packet.

**Demerits of DVMRP (2 marks):**
1. **Bandwidth waste** due to periodic flooding of *all* branches every 10 s and after each 2-hour prune expiry.
2. **Scalability bottleneck:** state per (S, G) per router grows as $O(S \times G)$.
3. **Convergence delay:** distance-vector updates propagate slowly → slow reaction to link failures → potential count-to-infinity.
4. **Sparse-network inefficiency:** in groups with few widely distributed listeners, the flood-prune cycle generates heavy control traffic on bandwidth-expensive WAN links.
5. **Metric cap of 32** limits network diameter to 32 hops.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, S5 B.Tech CSE – Module 3]`

> **(a)** Explain the **DVMRP Route Report** and the **DVMRP Probe** mechanisms. How is the **neighbour table** built and maintained? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is the role of **IGMP** in DVMRP? Describe the operation of **IGMPv2** with message diagrams. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) DVMRP Route Report, Probe, and Neighbour Table (7 marks):**

1. **DVMRP Probe (2 marks):**
   - A DVMRP-enabled router transmits a **Probe** message out of every DVMRP-enabled interface every $T_{pr} = 10$ s.
   - The Probe carries the router's identity, capability flags, generation ID, and a list of **neighbours from which it has lost connectivity** (so that other routers can prune their tables).
   - The Probe serves three purposes: (i) discovers DVMRP neighbours on the link, (ii) detects one-way connectivity, and (iii) detects when a *previously-known* neighbour has gone down without sending a final Probe.
   - Upon receiving a Probe, a router records the sender in its **Neighbour Table** with a timestamp.

2. **DVMRP Route Report (2.5 marks):**
   - Periodic full **Route Reports** are sent every $T_{rep} = 10$ s, but are *triggered updates* sent immediately if a metric changes.
   - Each Route Report contains a list of **(destination network, hop-count)** tuples.
   - The recipient applies the **Bellman-Ford update rule**: $D_{R}(S) = \min(D_{R}(S), D_{neighbour}(S) + 1)$.
   - Any entry with hop-count $\geq 32$ is considered *infinity* and the route is poisoned.
   - A *poisoned reverse* technique may be used to defeat routing loops.

3. **Neighbour Table Maintenance (2.5 marks):**
   - **Creation:** An entry is created when the first Probe is received from a router on a DVMRP interface.
   - **Refresh:** The entry's timestamp is updated on every received Probe.
   - **Expiry:** If no Probe is received within $T_{nbr} = 35$ s (3.5 × probe interval), the neighbour is declared *down* and removed.
   - **Capabilities negotiated:** Through Probes, neighbours agree on the **DVMRP version** (1, 2, or 3) and exchange tunnelling capability flags.

**(b) Role of IGMP and Operation of IGMPv2 (7 marks):**

1. **Role of IGMP in DVMRP (2 marks):**
   - IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol) is the **signalling protocol between an end-host and its directly-attached (first-hop) multicast router**.
   - It enables a host to *join* or *leave* a multicast group and to *respond* to membership queries.
   - **Without IGMP, the router has no knowledge of which receivers exist on its LAN** and would unnecessarily flood multicast packets everywhere. IGMP, in effect, *feeds the leaf-pruning decision* in RPM.

2. **IGMPv2 Message Types (2.5 marks):**
   - **Membership Query (Type 0x11):** Sent by the router to discover which groups have listeners. Two sub-types: *General Query* (all groups) and *Group-Specific Query* (one group).
   - **Version 2 Membership Report (Type 0x16):** Sent by a host to announce its interest in a group.
   - **Leave Group (Type 0x17):** Sent by a host when it leaves a group, allowing the router to quickly issue a Group-Specific Query instead of waiting for the report timer.

3. **IGMPv2 Operation (2.5 marks):**
   - **Joining:** Host sends an unsolicited Membership Report for the desired group.
   - **Maintaining:** Router periodically (every 125 s) sends a General Query on each LAN. Hosts with active memberships respond with Membership Reports; a *report-suppression* mechanism prevents multiple hosts on the same LAN from sending simultaneous reports.
   - **Leaving:** Host sends a Leave Group message to **224.0.0.2 (All-Routers)**. The router immediately issues a Group-Specific Query. If no Membership Report arrives within the **Max Response Time** (default 10 s — IGMPv2's key enhancement over IGMPv1), the group is timed out and the router sends a Prune upstream.

> **Diagram requirement (1 mark — included in marks above):** Show the three IGMPv2 message types on a sequence diagram (host ↔ router) and annotate the Max-Response-Time timer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Zones**
> 1. **Confusing DVMRP and PIM-DM.** They both flood-and-prune, but DVMRP has *its own* route-reports and tunnels; PIM-DM piggybacks on the unicast table *and* has shorter prune lifetimes (3 min vs. 2 h). Examiners award marks only for explicit mention of these.
> 2. **Forgetting that DVMRP needs an underlying unicast protocol.** Many students describe RPF without stating the unicast-table dependence, losing 0.5–1 mark.
