> 3. **Writing "RIP" when you mean "DVMRP".** The KTU evaluator reads distance-vector multicast questions strictly; using the wrong protocol name is treated as a conceptual error.
> 4. **Omitting the IGMP–RPM linkage.** You *must* say "RPM uses IGMP local membership information to decide *when* to send a Prune." This sentence alone is worth 1 mark on most papers.
> 5. **In numerical problems, omitting the RPF check at every router.** A common mistake is to draw a tree and assume forwarding without verifying RPF. Always include the RPF condition in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and read it the night before the exam. It is a high-density summary of every high-yield fact.**

- ✅ **DVMRP = Multicast RIP.** It is the distance-vector multicast protocol defined in **RFC 1075**.
- ✅ **IP Protocol Number = 3** for DVMRP control packets.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** RPF → RPB → RPM (flood-and-prune).
- ✅ **RPF rule:** Forward only if incoming interface = unicast next-hop toward source.
- ✅ **RPB rule:** On a multi-access link, the router closest to the source is the *designated forwarder*; others suppress.
- ✅ **RPM rule:** Leaf with no IGMP members → PRUNE(S,G) upstream; cache prune for **2 hours**.
- ✅ **GRAFT/GRAFT-ACK** provide *fast re-join* without waiting for prune expiry.
- ✅ **Default timers:** Probe = **10 s**, Route-Report = **10 s**, Neighbour-timeout = **35 s**, Prune-lifetime = **2 hours = 7200 s**.
- ✅ **Metric:** Hop-count, **infinity = 32** (poisoned routes are at 32).
- ✅ **DVMRP depends on a pre-existing unicast table** — it is *not* a standalone protocol.
- ✅ **Tunnels:** IP-in-IP encapsulation between two DVMRP routers; transparently extend multicast across unicast-only clouds (MBONE).
- ✅ **Group address range:** Class D = **224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255**.
- ✅ **MBONE:** The historical multicast overlay built primarily with DVMRP tunnels.
- ✅ **DVMRP vs. PIM-DM:** Both flood-and-prune, but DVMRP has built-in tunnelling, longer prune-lifetime (2 h vs. 3 min), and uses its own IP protocol number (3 vs. 103).
- ✅ **DVMRP vs. PIM-SM/CBT:** DVMRP is *dense-mode* (flood first). PIM-SM and CBT are *sparse-mode* (explicit-join, no initial flood).
- ✅ **Merits:** Simple, fast first-packet delivery, works over any unicast protocol, RPF/RPB guarantee loop-freedom.
- ✅ **Demerits:** Bandwidth-wasting periodic floods, $O(S \times G)$ state, slow distance-vector convergence, count-to-infinity risk, max diameter of 32 hops.
- ✅ **KTU buzzwords to use:** *flood-and-prune*, *truncated broadcast tree*, *shortest-path tree*, *designated forwarder*, *reverse-path check*, *MBONE*, *graft*, *poisoned reverse*, *IGMP-driven leaf pruning*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TCP - TCP Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** TCP - TCP Services ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** is a connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream transport layer protocol defined in **RFC 793** (later refined in **RFC 9293**). The *services* provided by TCP form a contract between the transport layer and the upper-layer applications, ensuring that data is exchanged between two end systems in a **process-to-process**, **sequenced**, **error-controlled**, and **flow-controlled** manner over an inherently unreliable IP network. In the context of the **TCP/IP reference model**, TCP services sit directly above the **Internet Protocol (IP)** and below the **application layer (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, SSH, DNS-over-TCP, etc.)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "TCP Services" focuses on *what* TCP guarantees to an application — the *features* — rather than *how* it implements them (which is covered in subsequent modules on flow control, error control, and congestion control). The **eight canonical services** offered by TCP are: 1. **Process-to-Process Communication** 2. **Stream Delivery Service** 3. **Sending and Receiving Buffers** 4. **Segments and Full-Duplex Communication** 5. **Connection-Oriented Service** 6. **Reliable Service** 7. **Byte-Numbering Service** 8. **Encapsulation and Decapsulation** --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Registered Post with Tracking" Model Imagine IP (the network layer) as the **ordinary postal system**. You drop a letter into a mailbox; the letter travels through sorting offices, planes, and delivery vans. There is **no guarantee** of delivery, no guarantee of **order**, no guarantee of **no duplication**, and the letter might even be **lost in transit**. Now imagine **TCP** as a **premium registered-post service** with the following added guarantees: | Real-World Postal Service | Equivalent TCP Service | |---|---| | Every letter gets a unique tracking ID | **Byte Numbering / Sequence Numbers** | | Sender receives a signed delivery receipt | **Acknowledgments (ACKs)** | | Letters are delivered in the exact order sent | **Sequenced, In-Order Delivery** | | Lost letters are re-dispatched automatically | **Retransmission & Error Recovery** | | Two-way conversation (you can reply in the same letter thread) | **Full-Duplex Communication** | | Caller must first "dial" and confirm before speaking | **3-Way Handshake (Connection-Oriented)** | | Each letter is addressed to a *specific person* in a building | **Port Numbers (Process-to-Process)** | | Letters are bundled into envelopes before dispatch | **Segmentation & Encapsulation** | > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary for First-Time Learners:** TCP turns a chaotic, best-effort network (the Internet) into a **reliable, ordered, two-way pipe** between two specific application programs. Whenever you load a webpage, send an email, or transfer a file, TCP is the "quality control" layer that promises the bytes arrive **once, in order, and intact**. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Examiner Favourites) - **Port Number Range:** **0 to 65,535** (16 bits, $2^{16}$ values). - **Sequence Number Range:** **0 to $2^{32} - 1$** (**4,294,967,295**). - **Default Maximum Segment Size (MSS):** **536 bytes** (RFC 879), commonly negotiated up to **1,460 bytes** on Ethernet. - **TCP Header Length:** **20 bytes minimum**, up to **60 bytes** (with options). - **Standard MTU (Ethernet):** **1,500 bytes**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Eight TCP Services — Structured Breakdown #### 2.1.1 Process-to-Process Communication - TCP uses **port numbers** (16-bit identifiers) to multiplex multiple applications on the same host. - A connection is uniquely identified by the **4-tuple**: *(Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port)*. - **Why?** The IP layer only delivers host-to-host. TCP must refine this to **process-to-process**. #### 2.1.2 Stream Delivery Service - TCP presents data to the application as a **continuous byte stream**, not as discrete packets. - The sender's application writes bytes into a send buffer; TCP chops them into segments. - **Why?** Applications (e.g., file transfers) think in terms of a stream of bytes, not packets. #### 2.1.3 Sending and Receiving Buffers - TCP maintains **two buffers** per connection: - **Send Buffer** (at sender) — bytes written by the application, waiting to be sent. - **Receive Buffer** (at receiver) — bytes received, waiting to be read by the application. - These buffers decouple the application speed from the network speed. - Buffer size is governed by the **receiver's advertised window** (covered in Module 5). #### 2.1.4 Segments - TCP breaks the byte stream into **segments** (the PDU of TCP). - Each segment is encapsulated inside an **IP datagram** at the network layer. - **Why?** IP packets have a Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU); TCP segments must fit inside it. #### 2.1.5 Full-Duplex Communication - Data can flow **simultaneously in both directions** over a single TCP connection. - Each direction has its **own sequence number space** and **independent flow control**. - **Why?** Most applications (web browsing, SSH, email) are inherently two-way. #### 2.1.6 Connection-Oriented Service - Before any data is exchanged, TCP performs a **3-way handshake**: **SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK** - This establishes state (sequence numbers, window sizes, MSS) at both ends. - At the end, a **4-step termination** (FIN/ACK exchange) cleanly closes the connection. - **Why?** Reserves resources, synchronizes initial sequence numbers, and prevents old duplicates from a previous connection. #### 2.1.7 Reliable Service TCP guarantees reliability via: - **Sequence numbers** for ordering and loss detection. - **Cumulative + Selective Acknowledgments** to confirm receipt. - **Checksum** for corruption detection. - **Retransmission Timeout (RTO)** for lost segments. - **Duplicate detection** and **reordering** at the receiver. #### 2.1.8 Encapsulation and Decapsulation - **Encapsulation (Sender side):** App data → TCP segment (with TCP header) → IP datagram → Ethernet frame. - **Decapsulation (Receiver side):** Reverse path; TCP header is stripped, payload delivered to socket. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **high-yield formula & boundary sheet** — memorize these values for KTU Part A derivations and Part B 7-mark sub-questions. | Parameter / Quantity | Formula or Value | Boundary Condition | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Total number of port numbers | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | From 0 to $2^{16}-1$ | dimensionless | | Well-known port range | $0 \le p \le 1023$ | Privileged (require root) | dimensionless | | Registered port range | $1024 \le p \le 49151$ | IANA-assigned to apps | dimensionless | | Dynamic / Private port range | $49152 \le p \le 65535$ | Ephemeral / client-side | dimensionless | | Sequence number space | $2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296$ | Wraps modulo $2^{32}$ | bytes | | Acknowledgment number (next expected byte) | $ACK = \text{last\_received\_seq} + 1$ | Cumulative ACK semantics | bytes | | TCP header minimum size | 20 bytes | Without options | bytes | | TCP header maximum size | 60 bytes | With 40 bytes of options | bytes | | Data offset field | $4 \text{ bits}$, value $\times 4$ | Range 5 to 15 | 32-bit words | | Window size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ (classical), up to $2^{30}$ with Window Scaling | RFC 1323 | bytes | | Default MSS | 536 | RFC 879 | bytes | | Typical Ethernet MSS | $MTU - 40 = 1500 - 40 = 1460$ | IPv4 (TCP + IP headers) | bytes | > **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are not academic trivia — they govern **socket binding** in OS kernels, **firewall rule design**, **load-balancer port mapping**, and **cloud container networking** (e.g., Kubernetes NodePort ranges 30,000–32,767). --- ### 2.3 The TCP Segment Format — Field-by-Field Logical Justification | Field | Size (bits) | Purpose | Why It Exists | |---|---|---|---| | Source Port | 16 | Identifies sender's process | Demultiplexing return traffic | | Destination Port | 16 | Identifies receiver's process | Demultiplexing inbound traffic | | Sequence Number | 32 | Byte-stream position of first byte in segment | Ordering & loss detection | | Acknowledgment Number | 32 | Next byte expected from other side | Reliability | | Data Offset | 4 | TCP header length in 32-bit words | Locate payload start | | Reserved | 6 | Set to zero | Future use | | URG / ACK / PSH / RST / SYN / FIN | 6 (1 each) | Connection & data control | State machine | | Window Size | 16 | Receiver's buffer availability | Flow control | | Checksum | 16 | End-to-end integrity (mandatory) | Corruption detection | | Urgent Pointer | 16 | Offset of urgent data | Out-of-band signaling | | Options + Padding | 0–320 | MSS, Window Scale, Timestamps, SACK | Extensibility | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Draw and explain the TCP segment format with the function of each field." This is a **7-mark standard** in the ESE Part B section. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Why 65,536 Port Numbers? A port number must uniquely identify a process on a single host at any given instant. TCP stores the port number in a **16-bit field** in the segment header. The number of distinct values representable in an $n$-bit field is: $$ N = 2^{n} $$ Substituting $n = 16$ (the width of the port-number field): $$ N = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 $$ Therefore the **port-number space** spans the integer interval: $$ p \in \left[0,\ 2^{16} - 1\right] = \left[0,\ 65{,}535\right] $$ This is split into three IANA-defined ranges: $$ \text{Well-Known} : 0 \le p \le 1023 \quad (\text{count} = 1024) $$ $$ \text{Registered} : 1024 \le p \le 49151 \quad (\text{count} = 48{,}128) $$ $$ \text{Dynamic / Private} : 49152 \le p \le 65535 \quad (\text{count} = 16{,}384) $$ **Verification:** $$ 1024 + 48{,}128 + 16{,}384 = 65{,}536 \;\; \checkmark $$ > **Engineering Insight:** The Linux kernel parameter `ip_local_port_range` defaults to `32768–60999` (a subset of the dynamic range), giving about **28,232 ephemeral ports** per host for outbound client connections. --- ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis — How the Cumulative Acknowledgment Works Let the sender transmit segments with sequence numbers $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ (each carrying, say, **100 bytes** of data, so byte ranges $[S_i, S_i + 99]$). **Step 1 — Initial State:** Sender's variables: $\text{SND.NXT} = S_1$ (next byte to send). Receiver's variables: $\text{RCV.NXT} = S_1$ (next byte expected). **Step 2 — First segment arrives:** Receiver receives bytes $[S_1, S_1+99]$. - All bytes contiguous from $RCV.NXT$, so receiver advances: $$ RCV.NXT \leftarrow RCV.NXT + 100 = S_1 + 100 $$ - Receiver sends ACK with **acknowledgment number**: $$ ACK = S_1 + 100 $$ **Step 3 — Second segment arrives normally:** Same logic yields $ACK = S_2 + 100$, with $RCV.NXT$ advancing by 100. **Step 4 — Third segment is lost:** Receiver never gets $[S_3, S_3+99]$. It still expects $S_3$, so it sends a **duplicate ACK** carrying the same $ACK = S_3$ (the next expected byte). **Step 5 — Fourth segment arrives out-of-order:** Bytes $[S_4, S_4+99]$ arrive but are *not* contiguous to $RCV.NXT = S_3$. The receiver **buffers** them but still sends a duplicate $ACK = S_3$ (this duplicate ACK acts as a **fast-retransmit trigger** at the sender). **Step 6 — Sender reacts to 3 duplicate ACKs:** $$ \text{If duplicate\_ACK\_count} = 3 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{retransmit segment starting at } S_3 $$ **Step 7 — Retransmission arrives:** Bytes $[S_3, S_3+99]$ arrive; the receiver now has a contiguous run $[S_3, S_3+199]$ and advances: $$ RCV.NXT \leftarrow S_3 + 200 $$ It sends a **single cumulative ACK**: $$ ACK = S_3 + 200 $$ This is the **Selective Acknowledgment / Cumulative ACK** principle that makes TCP reliable. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TCP Service vs OSI Requirement | OSI Transport-Layer Requirement | TCP Service That Satisfies It | Failure Without It | |---|---|---| | End-to-end addressing | Process-to-Process (port numbers) | App processes indistinguishable | | Data transparency | Stream Delivery Service | Apps forced to think in packets | | Speed decoupling | Send/Receive Buffers | App must match network speed | | Network MTU adaptation | Segmentation | Cannot traverse links | | Bidirectional traffic | Full-Duplex | Apps forced to half-duplex | | Resource reservation | Connection-Oriented (3-way handshake) | Wasted bandwidth, ghost data | | Loss recovery | Reliable Service (ACKs + Retransmit) | Corrupted files, broken pages | | Order preservation | Byte-Numbering / Sequence Numbers | Reassembled document gibberish | | Protocol demultiplexing | Encapsulation/Decapsulation | Impossible to layer protocols | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Why does TCP need a connection-oriented service?", a model answer should mention: (1) **synchronization of sequence numbers**, (2) **negotiation of MSS and window scale**, and (3) **prevention of old duplicate segments** from confusing a new incarnation of the same connection. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of TCP Services ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application Process
write bytes] --> B[TCP Send Buffer] B --> C[Segment Construction
add TCP header] C --> D[IP Encapsulation
add IP header] D --> E[Network Interface
add Ethernet frame] E --> F[Physical Medium
bits on the wire] F --> G[Receiver Ethernet] G --> H[IP Decapsulation] H --> I[TCP Segment Arrives] I --> J{Checksum OK?} J -- No --> K[Discard Segment] J -- Yes --> L[TCP Receive Buffer] L --> M[Reorder via Seq Numbers] M --> N[Send ACK to Sender] L --> O[Application Process
read bytes] N --> P[Sender retransmit timer
if ACK missing] P --> C style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style O fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 style J fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e style C fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a style I fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a style L fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — TCP Segment Header (Block Layout) ```mermaid block-beta columns 8 block:row1 srcport["Source Port 16"] space space space dstport1["Dest Port 16"] space space space end block:row2 seqnum1["Sequence Number 32"] space space space seqnum2 space space space end block:row3 acknum1["Acknowledgment Number 32"] space space space acknum2 space space space end block:row4 offset["Data Offset 4"] reserved1["Reserved 6"] flags["URG ACK PSH RST SYN FIN 6"] window1["Window 16"] space end block:row5 checksum1["Checksum 16"] urgent1["Urgent Pointer 16"] options["Options + Padding variable"] space end block:row6 data1["DATA Payload variable length"] space space space end style row1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style row2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style row3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style row4 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46 style row5 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style row6 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Connection Lifecycle (3-Way Handshake + Data + Teardown) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant S as Server C->>S: SYN seq=x S-->>C: SYN seq=y, ACK x+1 C->>S: ACK y+1 (Connection Established) C->>S: DATA seq=x+1, ACK y+1 S-->>C: ACK x+101 C->>S: FIN seq=x+101 S-->>C: ACK x+102 S-->>C: FIN seq=y+1 C->>S: ACK y+2 (Connection Closed) ``` > [!NOTE] > These diagrams are KTU 2024-compliant: only alphanumeric node IDs (no `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` reserved words), no markdown bold/italics inside the node labels, and full uppercase descriptive text for clarity. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. List any three services provided by TCP and state their purpose.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three services are: 1. **Process-to-Process Communication** – TCP uses 16-bit port numbers to deliver data to the correct application process on a host. *(1 mark)* 2. **Stream Delivery Service** – TCP presents data to the application as a continuous byte stream, hiding the segmentation from the upper layer. *(1 mark)* 3. **Reliable Service** – TCP guarantees delivery via sequence numbers, acknowledgments, checksums, and retransmission of lost segments. *(1 mark)* > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2. Differentiate between TCP and UDP with respect to the services they provide.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | TCP | UDP | |---|---|---| | Connection type | Connection-oriented (handshake) | Connectionless | | Reliability | Reliable (ACK + retransmit) | Unreliable, best-effort | | Service | Stream-oriented | Message-oriented (datagram) | | Overhead | High (20-byte header + state) | Low (8-byte header, stateless) | | Use case | HTTP, FTP, SSH, SMTP | DNS, VoIP, video streaming | *(1 mark per row × 3 most distinguishing features)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain in detail the **process-to-process communication** and **stream delivery service** offered by TCP, with suitable diagrams. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TCP segment format** and the function of each header field. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Question A** **(a) Process-to-Process & Stream Delivery (7 marks)** 1. **Process-to-Process (3.5 marks):** - IP delivers host-to-host; TCP refines this to **process-to-process** using **16-bit port numbers** (0 to 65,535). - A connection is uniquely identified by the **4-tuple** *(Src IP, Src Port, Dst IP, Dst Port)*. - IANA assigns **well-known ports** (0–1023) to servers (e.g., HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, SSH=22, FTP=21, SMTP=25). - The client uses an **ephemeral port** from the dynamic range (49,152–65,535). - At the receiver, the OS demultiplexes the incoming segment to the correct socket using the destination port number. 2. **Stream Delivery (3.5 marks):** - TCP provides a **byte-stream abstraction** — the application sees an ordered, contiguous sequence of bytes. - Internally, TCP **chops the byte stream into segments** fitting the network MTU (e.g., 1460 bytes on Ethernet). - The **send buffer** stores bytes from the application; the **receive buffer** stores incoming bytes until the application reads them. - This decouples application speed from network speed (producer-consumer model with bounded buffer). --- **(b) TCP Segment Format (7 marks)** The TCP segment consists of a **20-byte minimum header** plus optional fields and the **payload data**. **Header fields (1 mark for diagram + 0.5 mark each for 12 fields ≈ 6 marks):** | Field | Size | Function | |---|---|---| | Source Port | 16 bits | Sender's process port | | Destination Port | 16 bits | Receiver's process port | | Sequence Number | 32 bits | Byte offset of first data byte in this segment | | Acknowledgment Number | 32 bits | Next byte expected from the other side (cumulative ACK) | | Data Offset | 4 bits | TCP header length in 32-bit words (5–15) | | Reserved | 6 bits | Must be zero | | Flags (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN) | 6 bits | Connection & data control | | Window Size | 16 bits | Receiver's buffer space (flow control) | | Checksum | 16 bits | End-to-end integrity check (mandatory) | | Urgent Pointer | 16 bits | Offset of last urgent data byte (if URG=1) | | Options | 0–320 bits | MSS, Window Scale, Timestamps, SACK Permitted | | Padding | Variable | Aligns header to 32-bit boundary | > *Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 for the visual layout.* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **connection-oriented service** of TCP, including the **3-way handshake** and **connection termination** with a sequence diagram. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain how TCP achieves **reliable service** using **sequence numbers, acknowledgments, checksums, and retransmission**, with an example scenario. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution — Question B** **(a) Connection-Oriented Service & 3-Way Handshake (7 marks)** **Why connection-oriented? (2 marks)** - Synchronizes **Initial Sequence Numbers (ISNs)** at both ends. - Negotiates **MSS**, **window scale**, and **SACK permission**. - Prevents **old duplicate segments** from corrupting a new connection (TIME_WAIT state). **3-Way Handshake (3 marks):** 1. **Client → Server:** `SYN, seq=x` 2. **Server → Client:** `SYN, seq=y, ACK=x+1` 3. **Client → Server:** `ACK=y+1` → Connection **ESTABLISHED** at both ends. **Connection Termination (2 marks):** - Each side sends **FIN** (no more data) and **ACKs** the other's FIN. - 4 segments total: `FIN → ACK ← FIN → ACK`. - The side that initiated the close enters **TIME_WAIT (2 × MSL ≈ 4 minutes)** to absorb stray segments. *(Refer to the sequence diagram in Section 4.3.)* --- **(b) Reliable Service Mechanism (7 marks)** | Mechanism | Marks | Explanation | |---|---|---| | **Sequence Numbers** | 1.5 | Each byte has a unique 32-bit number; receiver detects gaps, duplicates, and out-of-order delivery. | | **Acknowledgments** | 1.5 | Receiver sends `ACK = next_expected_byte` (cumulative). Duplicate ACKs (3 of them) trigger **fast retransmit**. | | **Checksum** | 1 | 16-bit one's-complement sum of header + data + pseudo-header; detects bit errors. | | **Retransmission Timeout (RTO)** | 1.5 | Sender retransmits unacknowledged segments after RTO (computed via RTT estimation: `RTO = SRTT + 4 × RTTVAR`). | | **Reordering / Duplicate suppression** | 1.5 | Receiver discards duplicate data; reordered segments are buffered until the gap is filled. | **Example Scenario (1 mark included above):** Sender transmits 5 segments of 100 bytes each (seq 100, 200, 300, 400, 500). Segment 300 is lost. Receiver ACKs 200, then sends **duplicate ACKs (200)** for segments 400, 500, and any further segments. On the **3rd duplicate ACK**, sender **fast-retransmits** seq 300 without waiting for timeout. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse TCP services with TCP mechanisms.** "Reliable service" is the *guarantee*; "sequence numbers, ACKs, retransmission" are the *mechanisms*. Examiners deduct marks if you list mechanisms under "services" without explaining the *guarantee*. > 2. **In the segment diagram, never forget the Data Offset field.** It is the most-skipped field in KTU answer sheets, yet it is critical to locating the payload. > 3. **Port-number ranges must be exact.** Writing "0 to 65535" is correct, but writing "well-known ports 0–1024" or "registered ports 1024–65535" is **wrong** and will cost 0.5 marks. > 4. **Do not write "TCP header = 32 bytes"** — it is **20 bytes minimum, 60 bytes maximum**, governed by the 4-bit Data Offset field. > 5. **The 3-way handshake is NOT just "SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK"** — you must also mention the **sequence numbers exchanged** in the first two segments. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **TCP = Connection-Oriented, Reliable, Byte-Stream, Full-Duplex** transport protocol. - **Eight TCP Services:** Process-to-Process, Stream Delivery, Buffers, Segments, Full-Duplex, Connection-Oriented, Reliable, Encapsulation/Decapsulation. - **Port numbers are 16 bits (0–65,535)**, split into Well-Known (0–1023), Registered (1024–49151), Dynamic (49152–65535). - **TCP header is 20 bytes minimum, 60 bytes maximum**, with **12 distinct fields**. - **Sequence numbers are 32 bits**, wrap modulo $2^{32}$. - **Cumulative ACK = next expected byte number**. - **3-Way Handshake = SYN, SYN+ACK, ACK** with sequence-number exchange. - **4-Step Termination = FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK** with TIME_WAIT state. - **Checksum is mandatory** in TCP (unlike IPv4, where it is optional). - **MSS = MTU − 40** (TCP 20 + IP 20) for typical Ethernet = **1460 bytes**. - **Connection identified by 4-tuple:** (Src IP, Src Port, Dst IP, Dst Port). - **Buffers (send & receive) decouple app speed from network speed**. - **Reliable service is achieved by 4 mechanisms:** sequence numbers, ACKs, checksums, retransmission. - **Full-duplex = two independent byte streams** (each direction has its own seq space). - **KTU-favourite buzzwords for full marks:** "process-to-process", "byte-stream", "cumulative acknowledgment", "fast retransmit", "flow control", "graceful close". > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Strategy (KTU 2024):** Memorize the **TCP segment diagram** and the **3-way handshake sequence diagram** verbatim. These two diagrams alone account for **7–10 marks** in any Module-4 Part-B question. Pair them with the **8-service list** and the **port-number range table** for a guaranteed 14/14 answer.

topics.title: TCP Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TCP Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)**, defined originally in RFC 793 and refined in RFC 9293, is a **connection-oriented, reliable, full-duplex, byte-stream transport layer protocol** of the TCP/IP suite. It provides a robust end-to-end communication channel between two communicating hosts, guaranteeing in-order, error-free delivery of a continuous stream of octets (bytes) over an inherently unreliable network (typically IP).

The **"Features" of TCP** refer to the set of design mechanisms, control algorithms, and header fields that collectively make TCP a trustworthy transport service. These features are *not* just peripheral add-ons — they form the *core engineering contract* between the sender and the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The official syllabus explicitly requires the student to identify and explain:
> 1. Connection-oriented service & logical connections
> 2. Stream-based data transfer (byte stream abstraction)
> 3. Full-duplex operation
> 4. Reliable delivery via acknowledgments, sequencing, and retransmission
> 5. Flow control (sliding window)
> 6. Error control (checksum + ARQ)
> 7. Multiplexing using port numbers
> 8. Connection establishment (3-way handshake) and termination (4-way handshake)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Registered Postal Courier" Model

Imagine you are sending a thick manuscript (say, **500 pages**) to a friend across the country through the **Indian Registered Post** system.

- You do **not** just hand it over in one giant bundle. The post office **segments** it into smaller registered envelopes.
- Each envelope is given a **serial number** (1, 2, 3, ... 50).
- The receiver **acknowledges** each envelope on arrival: *"Got envelope #1, send #2."*
- If envelope #7 is lost, the receiver complains and you **resend** it.
- The post office has a **tracking window** — it will not accept your 11th envelope until your previous 10 have been acknowledged (this is flow control).
- A **formal receipt (registered post slip)** is exchanged at both ends before mailing begins — this is the **3-way handshake**.
- At the end, both parties sign a **delivery confirmation** — this is connection **termination**.

> TCP works *exactly* this way. The "post office" is the Internet, the "envelopes" are TCP **segments**, the "serial numbers" are **sequence numbers**, the "receipts" are **ACKs**, and the "tracking window" is the **sliding window**.

## 1.3 The Seven Defining Features at a Glance

| # | Feature | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Connection-Oriented Service** | Establishes a logical session (virtual circuit) before data exchange. |
| 2 | **Full-Duplex Communication** | Both ends can transmit simultaneously over the same connection. |
| 3 | **Byte-Stream Abstraction** | The application sees a continuous byte pipe, not discrete messages. |
| 4 | **Reliable Delivery** | ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest) using ACKs, sequence numbers, and retransmission timers. |
| 5 | **Flow Control** | Sliding-window protocol prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. |
| 6 | **Error Control** | 16-bit checksum + ACK-based retransmission. |
| 7 | **Multiplexing / Demultiplexing** | Uses **16-bit port numbers** (Source + Destination) to support multiple concurrent applications. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Point-to-emphasize in ESE Answers:** TCP is *not* a "message protocol." A `write()` of 500 bytes at the sender may be delivered as **one** 500-byte segment, **two** segments, or merged with the **next** `write()` call. The protocol is bound to **bytes**, not to application messages.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Feature-by-Feature Engineering Analysis

### 2.1.1 Connection-Oriented Service (Virtual Circuit)

Before any application data is exchanged, the two TCP entities (the *client* and the *server*) perform a synchronization handshake. This creates a **stateful session** known in the kernel as a **Transmission Control Block (TCB)**. The TCB stores:
- Local and remote socket pair `<local_IP, local_port, remote_IP, remote_port>`
- Send and receive sequence variables (`SND.UNA`, `SND.NXT`, `RCV.NXT`, `RCV.WND`)
- Round-trip time estimators
- Congestion window state

> **The "Why":** IP itself is *stateless* and *best-effort* — it forgets a packet the moment it is forwarded. TCP overlays a **stateful** layer on top to give applications the illusion of a *reliable pipe*.

### 2.1.2 Byte-Stream Service

The sender maintains a **send buffer** and the receiver maintains a **receive buffer**. The protocol is fundamentally concerned with **sequence numbers** that count **bytes**, not segments. The first byte of the stream carries sequence number **ISN** (Initial Sequence Number), and every subsequent byte is numbered sequentially.

$$
\text{SeqNo of byte } k \;=\; \text{ISN} + k
$$

### 2.1.3 Full-Duplex Operation

A single TCP connection carries two independent byte streams: one from A→B and another from B→A. Acknowledgments for A→B can be "piggy-backed" on data segments flowing from B→A, halving the number of pure ACK segments on the wire.

### 2.1.4 Reliability — The Triad

TCP's reliability rests on three pillars:

1. **Sequencing** — every byte has a unique 32-bit sequence number.
2. **Cumulative Acknowledgment** — the ACK field advertises the *next* expected byte (`ACK = x` means "I have received all bytes up to $x-1$").
3. **Retransmission Timeout (RTO)** — if an ACK does not arrive within RTO, the segment is resent.

### 2.1.5 Flow Control — Sliding Window

The receiver advertises a **receive window** `rwnd` in every ACK. The sender must never have more than `rwnd` bytes of unacknowledged data outstanding. This protects a slow receiver from a fast sender.

$$
\text{MaxUnackedBytes} \;=\; \min(\text{rwnd},\, \text{cwnd})
$$

where `cwnd` is the **congestion window** enforced by the sender.

### 2.1.6 Error Control — The TCP Checksum

A **16-bit one's complement checksum** is computed over a **pseudo-header + TCP header + payload**. Any segment failing the checksum at the receiver is silently discarded, and the lack of an ACK triggers retransmission.

### 2.1.7 Multiplexing via Port Numbers

TCP identifies an application endpoint by a **16-bit port number** (range 0–65535). Well-known ports 0–1023 are reserved (e.g., HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=21, SSH=22, Telnet=23, SMTP=25).

> **The socket pair** `<SourceIP, SourcePort, DestIP, DestPort>` uniquely identifies *every* TCP connection on the entire Internet.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below collects **every equation** you are likely to need in a KTU ESE numerical or derivation question. **Memorize the symbols, not the syntax.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | Byte sequence number | $Seq_{byte} = ISN + k$ | dimensionless | $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | ACK semantics | $ACK = x \Rightarrow$ next expected byte is $x$ | dimensionless | **Cumulative** ACK |
| 3 | Window limit | $\text{Unacked} \le \min(\text{rwnd},\,\text{cwnd})$ | bytes | Sender constraint |
| 4 | SRTT (smoothed RTT) | $SRTT_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)\,SRTT_n + \alpha\,R_n$ | milliseconds | RFC 6298: $\alpha = \frac{1}{8}$ |
| 5 | RTTVAR | $RTTVAR_{n+1} = (1-\beta)\,RTTVAR_n + \beta\,\vert R_n - SRTT_n \vert$ | milliseconds | $\beta = \frac{1}{4}$ |
| 6 | Retransmission Timeout | $RTO = SRTT + \max(G,\, 4\cdot RTTVAR)$ | milliseconds | $G = \text{timer clock granularity}$ |
| 7 | First RTO | $RTO_0 = SRTT + 4\cdot RTTVAR$ | milliseconds | Set before first measurement |
| 8 | Karn backoff | $RTO_{k+1} = 2 \cdot RTO_k$ (on retransmit) | milliseconds | Exponential |
| 9 | Slow-start threshold (Tahoe) | $ssthresh = \frac{cwnd}{2}$ on loss event | bytes (segments) | Multiplicative decrease |
| 10 | Slow-start growth | $cwnd \leftarrow cwnd + 1$ MSS per ACK | bytes (segments) | **Exponential** growth |
| 11 | Congestion-avoidance growth | $cwnd \leftarrow cwnd + \frac{MSS^2}{cwnd}$ per ACK | bytes (segments) | **Linear** growth |
| 12 | Fast retransmit trigger | 3 duplicate ACKs | count | RFC 5681 |
| 13 | Fast recovery | $cwnd = ssthresh + 3\cdot MSS$ | bytes | Then linear increase |
| 14 | TCP checksum | $\text{Checksum} = \overline{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i}$ (16-bit 1's complement sum) | 16 bits | $W_i$ = 16-bit words |
| 15 | SeqNo wraparound time | $T_{wrap} = \frac{2^{32} \text{ bytes}}{B}$ | seconds | $B$ = bottleneck bandwidth |
| 16 | Max data per connection | $2^{32} = 4\,294\,967\,296$ bytes | bytes | Without window scaling |
| 17 | Window scale factor | $\text{RealRcvWindow} = \text{AdvertisedRcvWindow} \ll \text{Shift}$ | bytes | RFC 7323 |
| 18 | MSS default | $MSS = MTU - 40$ | bytes | IPv4, no options |
| 19 | MTU default (Ethernet) | $MTU = 1500$ | bytes | RFC 894 |
| 20 | Port range | $0 \le \text{port} \le 65535$ | integer | $2^{16}$ values |

> **Pitfall note (commonly tested):** Sequence numbers are counted in **bytes**, but the **ACK field** also counts **bytes**. Congestion window growth is counted in **MSS-sized segments** in textbook notation, but the underlying equation is in **bytes**. Always state the unit.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

TCP's features underpin every mission-critical Internet service: **HTTP/1.1, HTTPS, SSH, FTP, SMTP, IMAP, POP3, MQTT-over-TLS, gRPC, database replication, banking transactions, video streaming (with QUIC)**, and modern microservice communication. Without TCP's reliability, the modern Web simply could not exist. The trade-off — TCP's overhead compared to UDP — is *the* classic engineering compromise in the transport layer, and it is routinely tested in interviews, GATE, and KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents **three exhaustive derivations** that are most frequently asked in KTU ESE Module-4 questions: (A) the TCP Checksum, (B) the RTT/RTO estimator, and (C) the slow-start/congestion-avoidance throughput.

## 3.1 Derivation A — TCP Checksum Computation

### 3.1.1 The Pseudo-Header (RFC 9293)

The checksum is computed over a **96-bit pseudo-header** concatenated with the TCP header and data:

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| Source IP Address | 32 | From IP layer |
| Destination IP Address | 32 | From IP layer |
| Zero | 8 | Reserved |
| Protocol | 8 | `6` for TCP |
| TCP Length | 16 | Header + data, in bytes |

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1 — Construct the 16-bit words.**
Take the pseudo-header, the TCP header (with checksum field = 0), and the data. If the data has an odd number of bytes, pad with a trailing zero byte.

**Step 2 — Form 16-bit words.** Concatenate the entire stream into $N$ words:
$$
W_1, W_2, W_3, \ldots, W_N
$$
where each $W_i$ is a 16-bit integer.

**Step 3 — Add all words using 1's-complement arithmetic.**
1's-complement addition is *ordinary binary addition with end-around carry*: if the sum exceeds $2^{16}-1$, the carry-out is wrapped back into the low-order bit.

Let the running sum be $S$. Then:

$$
S_0 = 0
$$
$$
S_{i+1} = (S_i + W_{i+1}) \bmod (2^{16}) \;\;\text{with end-around carry}
$$

**Step 4 — One's complement the final sum.**
The checksum field stored in the TCP header is:

$$
\text{Checksum} = \overline{S_N} \quad (\text{bitwise NOT of } S_N)
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (at the receiver).**
The receiver repeats Steps 1–3 **including the checksum field**. If the final result equals $0xFFFF$ (all 1's in 1's-complement), the segment is considered error-free.

### 3.1.3 Worked Example

Suppose the pseudo-header + TCP header + data yields 4 words:

$$
W_1 = 0x4500,\;\; W_2 = 0x003C,\;\; W_3 = 0x1C46,\;\; W_4 = 0x5000
$$

**Compute the sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= W_1 + W_2 \\
    &= 0x4500 + 0x003C \\
    &= 0x453C
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_2 &= S_1 + W_3 \\
    &= 0x453C + 0x1C46 \\
    &= 0x6182
\end{aligned}
$$

No carry-out (since $0x6182 < 0x10000$), so end-around carry is **not** needed here.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_3 &= S_2 + W_4 \\
    &= 0x6182 + 0x5000 \\
    &= 0xB182
\end{aligned}
$$

Still under $0x10000$, no end-around carry.

**Compute the checksum:**

$$
\text{Checksum} = \overline{0xB182} = 0x4E7D
$$

**Receiver verification:** Receiver sums all four words *including* $0x4E7D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{final} &= 0xB182 + 0x4E7D \\
           &= 0x10000 - 1 \\
           &= 0xFFFF \;\;(\text{with end-around carry: } 0x0000 + 1 = 0x0001 \text{ ?})
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let me re-do this carefully because $0xB182 + 0x4E7D = 0x10000 - 1 = 0xFFFF$. With end-around carry: $0xFFFF + 1 = 0x10000 \to 0x0000$ (carry out is wrapped). So the final receiver result is $0x0000$? That is the *other* canonical representation. Both $0x0000$ and $0xFFFF$ are considered "no error" in 1's-complement (since the design accepts either form as valid).

$$
\boxed{\text{Checksum} = \overline{S_N}, \quad \text{Receiver result} \in \{0x0000, 0xFFFF\} \Rightarrow \text{Valid}}
$$

> **Why does this work?** In 1's-complement arithmetic, every number has *two* representations differing by $2^n$ (e.g., 0 and $2^n-1$). The sum of a number and its bitwise complement is always $2^n - 1$, which is the "all-ones" pattern. This is mathematically equivalent to "sum = 0 mod $2^n-1$".

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — RTT and RTO Estimation (RFC 6298)

### 3.2.1 Why a Simple Average Fails

A naïve estimator is the simple arithmetic mean:
$$
\overline{R} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i
$$
This estimator has **no memory of recent samples** and is **dominated by old outliers**. A more useful estimator should:
- Give **exponentially decaying** weight to old samples.
- Track **both** the mean and the **variance** (because RTT distributions are usually right-skewed).

### 3.2.2 The Exponential Weighted Moving Average (EWMA) for SRTT

Define $\alpha \in (0,1)$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_{n+1} &= (1-\alpha)\,SRTT_n + \alpha\,R_n \\
           &= SRTT_n + \alpha\,(R_n - SRTT_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the classic **EWMA** update. RFC 6298 mandates $\alpha = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$.

**Why $1/8$?** It is a power of 2 (so the implementation is a single right-shift by 3 bits) and it gives roughly **94%** of total weight to the most recent $\frac{1}{\alpha} = 8$ samples — a reasonable smoothing horizon.

### 3.2.3 The Mean Deviation (RTTVAR)

Track the **absolute deviation** $\vert R_n - SRTT_n \vert$ using a second EWMA with weight $\beta = \frac{1}{4}$:

$$
RTTVAR_{n+1} = (1-\beta)\,RTTVAR_n + \beta\,\vert R_n - SRTT_n \vert
$$

RFC 6298 mandates $\beta = \frac{1}{4}$.

### 3.2.4 The RTO Formula

The retransmission timeout is the **mean plus four times the deviation** (4σ rule of thumb for roughly-Gaussian distributions):

$$
\boxed{\; RTO = SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR \;}
$$

**First-RTT special case:** Before the first measurement, $SRTT$ and $RTTVAR$ are uninitialized. The RFC specifies:
$$
RTO_0 = 1 \text{ second} \quad (\text{initial value})
$$

**Lower bound:** $RTO \ge 1$ second (RFC 6298).
**Upper bound:** $RTO \le 60$ seconds (practical limit, but technically unbounded).

### 3.2.5 Worked Example (typical KTU question)

Suppose the first RTT measurement $R_1 = 100$ ms, with initial $SRTT_0 = 0$ and $RTTVAR_0 = 0$ (for the sake of the first sample). Using the **first sample** as a bootstrap:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_1 &= R_1 = 100 \text{ ms} \\
RTTVAR_1 &= \frac{R_1}{2} = 50 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{per RFC 6298}) \\
RTO_1 &= SRTT_1 + 4 \cdot RTTVAR_1 = 100 + 4(50) = 300 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now suppose $R_2 = 120$ ms arrives. Update with $\alpha = 1/8$, $\beta = 1/4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRTT_2 &= (1 - 0.125) \cdot 100 + 0.125 \cdot 120 \\
       &= 0.875 \cdot 100 + 0.125 \cdot 120 \\
       &= 87.5 + 15 = 102.5 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
RTTVAR_2 &= (1 - 0.25) \cdot 50 + 0.25 \cdot \vert 120 - 100 \vert \\
         &= 0.75 \cdot 50 + 0.25 \cdot 20 \\
         &= 37.5 + 5 = 42.5 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
RTO_2 &= 102.5 + 4 \cdot 42.5 = 102.5 + 170 = 272.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Observation:** Even though $R_2 > R_1$, the RTO *decreased* slightly. This is because RTTVAR shrank — the system recognized the previous RTTVAR was an over-estimate.

### 3.2.6 Karn's Algorithm (Re-transmission Ambiguity)

When a segment is retransmitted, the ACK that comes back cannot be unambiguously associated with the **first** transmission or the **retransmission**. Therefore, RTT samples are **not taken** for retransmitted segments (Karn's Algorithm, RFC 6298). On a timeout event, the RTO is **exponentially backed off**:

$$
RTO_{k+1} = 2 \cdot RTO_k
$$

This continues until a non-retransmitted segment is acknowledged, at which point the RTT samples are used to recompute SRTT, RTTVAR, and a fresh RTO.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Slow Start vs Congestion Avoidance Throughput

### 3.3.1 Definitions

- **cwnd** = congestion window (sender side, in MSS units)
- **ssthresh** = slow-start threshold
- **MSS** = maximum segment size
- **RTT** = round-trip time

### 3.3.2 Phase 1 — Slow Start (Exponential Growth)

While $cwnd < ssthresh$, on **every ACK** the sender increases cwnd by 1 MSS:

$$
cwnd \leftarrow cwnd + MSS \quad (\text{per ACK received})
$$

Since each ACK acknowledges 1 MSS, cwnd effectively *doubles* every RTT:

$$
cwnd(t + 1\,\text{RTT}) = 2 \cdot cwnd(t)
$$

**Throughput growth** is therefore **exponential**:

$$
T(t) = cwnd_0 \cdot 2^{t / RTT}
$$

### 3.3.3 Phase 2 — Congestion Avoidance (Linear Growth)

Once $cwnd \ge ssthresh$, growth switches to **1 MSS per RTT** (not per ACK):

$$
cwnd \leftarrow cwnd + \frac{MSS^2}{cwnd} \quad (\text{per ACK})
$$

The reason for the $\frac{MSS^2}{cwnd}$ form: in one RTT, the sender receives $cwnd / MSS$ ACKs (each for 1 MSS). Adding $\frac{MSS^2}{cwnd}$ per ACK gives a per-RTT increment of:

$$
\frac{cwnd}{MSS} \cdot \frac{MSS^2}{cwnd} = MSS
$$

Hence **linear** growth of 1 MSS per RTT.

### 3.3.4 Loss Reaction

On a loss event (3 duplicate ACKs → *fast retransmit/recovery*, OR timeout → *slow restart*):

- **Tahoe TCP:** $ssthresh = cwnd/2$, then $cwnd = 1$ MSS (slow start from 1).
- **Reno TCP:** $ssthresh = cwnd/2$, then $cwnd = ssthresh + 3$ MSS (fast recovery).

### 3.3.5 Steady-State Throughput Approximation

For a TCP Reno connection in congestion avoidance, the **long-run average throughput** can be approximated using the *square-root formula* (from Mathis, Semke, Mahdavi, Ott, 1997):

$$
B \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}}
$$

where $p$ is the packet loss probability. This shows that **TCP throughput is inversely proportional to the square root of the loss rate**, a result universally taught in networking courses.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Feature Exists

| Engineering Problem | TCP Feature That Solves It | Mechanism |
|---------------------|---------------------------|-----------|
| Packets may be lost in the network | **Retransmission** | Timeout-based ARQ |
| Packets may arrive out of order | **Sequence numbers** | Byte-stream numbering + in-order reassembly |
| Sender may overload receiver's buffer | **Flow control (rwnd)** | Receiver-advertised window |
| Network may be congested (router queues overflow) | **Congestion control (cwnd)** | Slow start + congestion avoidance |
| Bit errors in transit | **Checksum + ARQ** | 16-bit 1's-complement sum + retransmit on bad checksum |
| Multiple apps per host | **Port numbers** | 16-bit source/destination ports |
| Connection not yet established | **3-way handshake** | SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK |
| Need clean shutdown | **4-way handshake** | FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK |
| Long fat networks (high B×D) | **Window scaling + timestamps + large cwnd** | RFC 7323 |
| Spurious timeouts after packet reordering | **Fast retransmit** | 3 duplicate ACKs |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** syntax. Node IDs follow the alpha-prefix rule (no reserved keywords), and labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

## 4.1 TCP Feature Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    appA[APPLICATION LAYER HTTP FTP SSH SMTP]
    sockA[SOCKET API SOCKET PAIR SRC IP SRC PORT DST IP DST PORT]
    f1[F1 CONNECTION ORIENTED 3 WAY HANDSHAKE]
    f2[F2 FULL DUPLEX BIDIRECTIONAL STREAM]
    f3[F3 BYTE STREAM SERVICE SEND AND RECEIVE BUFFER]
    f4[F4 RELIABLE DELIVERY ARQ ACK SEQ NO RETRANSMIT]
    f5[F5 FLOW CONTROL SLIDING WINDOW RWND]
    f6[F6 ERROR CONTROL 16 BIT CHECKSUM]
    f7[F7 MULTIPLEXING PORT NUMBERS 0 TO 65535]
    cc[CONGESTION CONTROL SLOW START CONGESTION AVOIDANCE FAST RETRANSMIT]
    tcpHdr[TCP HEADER 20 BYTES MINIMUM SOURCE PORT DST PORT SEQ NO ACK NO FLAGS WINDOW CHECKSUM]
    ipL[IP LAYER BEST EFFORT UNRELIABLE]

    appA --> sockA
    sockA --> f1
    f1 --> f2
    f2 --> f3
    f3 --> f4
    f4 --> f5
    f5 --> f6
    f6 --> f7
    f7 --> cc
    cc --> tcpHdr
    tcpHdr --> ipL
```

## 4.2 TCP Connection State Machine (3-Way Handshake + 4-Way Termination)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT: client sends SYN seq x
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED: client receives SYN ACK seq y ack x plus 1 sends ACK seq x plus 1 ack y plus 1
    CLOSED --> LISTEN: server opens passive socket
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD: server receives SYN sends SYN ACK seq y ack x plus 1
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED: server receives ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1: client sends FIN seq u
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2: client receives ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT: server receives FIN sends ACK
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK: server sends FIN
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT: client receives FIN sends ACK
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED: server receives ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED: after 2 MSL timer expires
```

## 4.3 Sender-Side Sliding Window Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    base[SND UNA LEFT EDGE OF SEND WINDOW]
    nxt[SND NXT NEXT BYTE TO SEND]
    last[SND UNA PLUS SND WND RIGHT EDGE OF SEND WINDOW]
    used[SENT AND ACKED REGION]
    avail[AVAILABLE WINDOW USABLE FOR NEW DATA]
    blocked[BLOCKED REGION WAIT FOR ACKS]

    base --> used
    used --> nxt
    nxt --> avail
    avail --> last
    last --> blocked
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The byte stream is conceptually divided into four regions, all lying on a 1-D number line of sequence numbers.
- **SND.UNA** is the left edge — the oldest unacknowledged byte. Everything to its left has been *cumulatively* ACKed.
- **SND.NXT** is the next byte the sender is about to send.
- **SND.UNA + SND.WND** is the right edge — the largest sequence number the sender may inject into the network.
- The **Available Window** = `SND.WND - (SND.NXT - SND.UNA)` is the room left for fresh data.
- When the **Available Window** shrinks to 0, the sender is **blocked**.

## 4.4 TCP Header Format Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    hdr[TCP HEADER 20 BYTES MINIMUM]
    sp[SOURCE PORT 16 BITS]
    dp[DESTINATION PORT 16 BITS]
    seq[SEQUENCE NUMBER 32 BITS]
    ack[ACKNOWLEDGMENT NUMBER 32 BITS]
    offset[DATA OFFSET 4 BITS]
    res[RESERVED 3 BITS]
    ns[NS FLAG 1 BIT]
    cwr[CWR FLAG 1 BIT]
    ece[ECE FLAG 1 BIT]
    urg[URG FLAG 1 BIT]
    ackf[ACK FLAG 1 BIT]
    psh[PSH FLAG 1 BIT]
    rst[RST FLAG 1 BIT]
    syn[SYN FLAG 1 BIT]
    fin[FIN FLAG 1 BIT]
    wnd[WINDOW SIZE 16 BITS]
    csum[CHECKSUM 16 BITS]
    uptr[URGENT POINTER 16 BITS]
    opts[OPTIONS AND PADDING VARIABLE]
    data[DATA VARIABLE]

    hdr --> sp
    sp --> dp
    dp --> seq
    seq --> ack
    ack --> offset
    offset --> res
    res --> ns
    ns --> cwr
    cwr --> ece
    ece --> urg
    urg --> ackf
    ackf --> psh
    psh --> rst
    rst --> syn
    syn --> fin
    fin --> wnd
    wnd --> csum
    csum --> uptr
    uptr --> opts
    opts --> data
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. List and briefly explain any three features of TCP that make it a reliable transport protocol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Connection-Oriented Service:** TCP establishes a logical connection between sender and receiver using a 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) before any data is exchanged. This guarantees that both ends are ready and have agreed on initial sequence numbers. *(1 mark)*

2. **Sequencing and Cumulative Acknowledgments:** Every byte transmitted carries a 32-bit sequence number, and the receiver acknowledges the next expected byte. This allows the receiver to reassemble data in correct order even if segments arrive out of order. *(1 mark)*

3. **Retransmission on Timeout (ARQ):** The sender maintains a Retransmission Timeout (RTO) for every unacknowledged segment. If no ACK arrives before RTO expires, the segment is resent, ensuring no data is permanently lost. *(1 mark)*

> **Marking insight:** Examiners look for the *feature name* + *one-line purpose*. Do **not** write paragraphs. A bulleted list of 3 items × 1 mark each is the safe format.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between TCP and UDP based on (i) reliability, (ii) connection, (iii) header size.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | TCP | UDP |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| (i) Reliability | Connection-oriented, reliable, uses ACK and retransmission | Connectionless, unreliable, no ACK, no retransmission |
| (ii) Connection | Establishes logical connection via 3-way handshake | No connection setup; data sent as independent datagrams |
| (iii) Header Size | 20 bytes (minimum), 60 bytes (maximum with options) | 8 bytes (fixed) |

*(1 mark per correct row of the comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Full Module Coverage]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the TCP header format with a neat diagram. List the function of each field.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The TCP header has a minimum size of 20 bytes (without options) and a maximum of 60 bytes. The layout, in order of transmission, is:

| Bits | Field | Function |
|------|-------|----------|
| 0–15 | **Source Port** | 16-bit port number of the sending application. |
| 16–31 | **Destination Port** | 16-bit port number of the receiving application. |
| 32–63 | **Sequence Number** | 32-bit number identifying the first byte of data in this segment. |
| 64–95 | **Acknowledgment Number** | Next byte expected from the other side (cumulative ACK). Valid only when ACK flag = 1. |
| 96–99 | **Data Offset** | Length of TCP header in 32-bit words. Minimum 5 (20 bytes), maximum 15 (60 bytes). |
| 100–102 | **Reserved** | Must be zero. |
| 103 | **NS** | ECN-nonce concealment flag (RFC 3540). |
| 104 | **CWR** | Congestion Window Reduced flag, set by sender to indicate ECE response. |
| 105 | **ECE** | ECN-Echo flag; indicates congestion experienced. |
| 106 | **URG** | Urgent pointer field is valid. |
| 107 | **ACK** | Acknowledgment number field is valid. |
| 108 | **PSH** | Push function — deliver data to application immediately. |
| 109 | **RST** | Reset the connection. |
| 110 | **SYN** | Synchronize sequence numbers (used in handshake). |
| 111 | **FIN** | No more data from sender (used in termination). |
| 112–127 | **Window Size** | Receive window in bytes (scaled by window-scale option). |
| 128–143 | **Checksum** | 16-bit 1's-complement checksum over pseudo-header + header + data. |
| 144–159 | **Urgent Pointer** | Offset from sequence number indicating end of urgent data (valid if URG=1). |
| 160+ | **Options + Padding** | Variable length; common options are MSS, Window Scale, Timestamps, SACK. |

*(Diagram carries 2 marks; field explanation carries 5 marks; allocate marks proportionally.)*

---

**(b) With neat timing diagrams, explain how TCP establishes and terminates a connection.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Connection Establishment — 3-Way Handshake (4 marks):**

1. **Step 1 (SYN):** The client sends a segment with the **SYN** flag = 1 and an initial sequence number `x` (chosen randomly for security). The client enters the `SYN_SENT` state.
2. **Step 2 (SYN + ACK):** The server, which is in `LISTEN` state, replies with both **SYN** and **ACK** flags = 1, its own ISN `y`, and acknowledgment number `x + 1`. The server enters the `SYN_RCVD` state.
3. **Step 3 (ACK):** The client sends a segment with **ACK** = 1 and acknowledgment number `y + 1`. Both sides enter the `ESTABLISHED` state. Data transfer can now begin.

**Timing Diagram (textual):**

```
Client                                Server
  | ---- SYN seq=x ---------------->  |
  |                                    |
  | <--- SYN seq=y ACK=x+1 ----------- |
  |                                    |
  | ---- ACK=y+1 ------------------->  |
  |                                    |
  | <===== DATA TRANSFER (ESTABLISHED) =====> |
```

**Connection Termination — 4-Way Handshake (3 marks):**

1. **Step 1 (FIN):** The side that wants to close (say, the client) sends a segment with **FIN** = 1 and enters `FIN_WAIT_1`.
2. **Step 2 (ACK):** The server ACKs the FIN and enters `CLOSE_WAIT`.
3. **Step 3 (FIN):** The server, when ready, sends its own **FIN** and enters `LAST_ACK`.
4. **Step 4 (ACK):** The client sends the final **ACK** and enters `TIME_WAIT` (lasting **2 × MSL**, typically 2 minutes) before fully closing. The server closes immediately on receiving this ACK.

```
Client                                Server
  | ---- FIN seq=u ---------------->  |
  |                                    |
  | <--- ACK=u+1 --------------------- |
  |                                    |
  | <--- FIN seq=v ------------------- |
  |                                    |
  | ---- ACK=v+1 ------------------->  |
  |                                    |
  v                                    v
TIME_WAIT (2*MSL)                   CLOSED
```

**Why 3-way and not 2-way?** A 2-way handshake cannot prevent **old duplicate SYNs** from a previous connection from being mistaken for a new connection. The 3-way handshake synchronizes both sides' ISNs and clears any stale state.

**Why TIME_WAIT?** To absorb any late, in-flight segments from the old connection so they do not corrupt the next connection on the same socket pair.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Explain TCP's flow control mechanism using the sliding window protocol.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Problem addressed (1 mark):** A fast sender (e.g., a 10 Gbps server) must not overwhelm a slow receiver (e.g., a mobile phone on 4G). Flow control is the **receiver-side** mechanism that protects the receiver's buffer.

**Receiver's Window — `rwnd` (2 marks):**
- The receiver maintains a **receive buffer** of size `RcvBuffer`.
- At any moment, the *occupied* part is `RcvBuffer - (RCV.NXT - RCV.WND)`.
- The **free space** is the **receive window** `rwnd` advertised to the sender:

$$
\text{rwnd} = \text{RcvBuffer} - (\text{LastByteReceived} - \text{LastByteRead})
$$

- `rwnd` is sent to the sender in the **Window Size** field of every ACK.

**Sender's Constraint (2 marks):**
- The sender ensures that the number of unacknowledged bytes never exceeds the *minimum* of the receiver window and the congestion window:

$$
\text{LastByteSent} - \text{LastByteAcked} \le \min(\text{rwnd},\, \text{cwnd})
$$

- If `rwnd = 0`, the sender stops and periodically sends a 1-byte "**window probe**" segment to elicit a new ACK and re-discover a non-zero window.

**Sliding Window Movement (2 marks):**
- The window has a **left edge** (oldest unacked byte) and a **right edge** (left + advertised window).
- As ACKs arrive, the **left edge advances rightward** (window slides forward).
- As data is acknowledged, the **right edge may also advance** if the receiver's buffer has more free space.
- The window is therefore *dynamic*, expanding, contracting, and sliding with each ACK.

---

**(b) Explain the congestion control mechanism in TCP (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit, and fast recovery).** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Why congestion control is needed (1 mark):** The Internet is a **shared resource**. If every sender pushed data as fast as the receiver allows, intermediate router queues would overflow, leading to **congestion collapse** (observed in the early Internet, October 1986).

**Two Windows: `cwnd` and `ssthresh` (1 mark):**
- `cwnd` = congestion window (sender-side, in bytes).
- `ssthresh` = slow-start threshold (in bytes).
- Effective sending limit: $\min(\text{cwnd},\, \text{rwnd})$.

**Slow Start (2 marks):**
- On connection start, set `cwnd = 1 MSS` and `ssthresh = 65535` bytes (typical initial).
- For each ACK received, `cwnd = cwnd + 1 MSS`.
- This yields **exponential growth** — `cwnd` doubles each RTT.
- Continues *until* `cwnd >= ssthresh` *or* a loss is detected.

**Congestion Avoidance (1 mark):**
- Once `cwnd >= ssthresh`, growth switches to **linear**: add `MSS` per RTT (equivalently, $MSS^2 / cwnd$ per ACK).

**Fast Retransmit (1 mark):**
- When the sender receives **3 duplicate ACKs** for the same ACK number, it concludes that one segment was lost.
- It **immediately retransmits** the missing segment **without waiting for the RTO** to expire.

**Fast Recovery (1 mark):**
- On the 3rd duplicate ACK, set `ssthresh = cwnd / 2`.
- Set `cwnd = ssthresh + 3 MSS` (inflate by 3 to account for the 3 segments that have left the network).
- Enter **congestion avoidance** (linear growth) from this new `cwnd`.
- This avoids a "slow start from 1" penalty for transient losses.

> **Comparison table (often asked):**

| Event | ssthresh | cwnd | Phase |
|-------|----------|------|-------|
| Connection start | 65535 B (or higher) | 1 MSS | Slow Start |
| `cwnd` reaches `ssthresh` | unchanged | unchanged at boundary | Switch to CA |
| 3 duplicate ACKs (Reno) | `cwnd/2` | `ssthresh + 3 MSS` | Fast Recovery + CA |
| Timeout (Tahoe/Reno) | `cwnd/2` | 1 MSS | Slow Start from 1 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TCP Features Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the ACK field with the sequence number.** The ACK field advertises the *next* expected byte, *not* the last received byte. Students routinely write "ACK = last byte received" and lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Saying "TCP establishes a connection" without naming the 3-way handshake.** Vague answers get *partial* credit at best. Always use the exact term "**three-way handshake**" and list the three flags: **SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the TIME_WAIT state in connection termination.** Many answers describe a 3-way termination. The correct answer is *4-way* (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK) with the active closer spending **2 × MSL** in TIME_WAIT.
>
> 4. **Mixing up cwnd growth in slow start vs congestion avoidance.** Slow start is **exponential** (cwnd doubles per RTT). Congestion avoidance is **linear** (cwnd grows by 1 MSS per RTT). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Skipping the checksum in the header diagram.** The 16-bit checksum is a *mandatory* field; many textbook headers list it as "optional." It is **NOT** optional in TCP.
>
> 6. **Using "TCP guarantees delivery" without qualifying it.** TCP guarantees in-order, error-free delivery *under non-pathological conditions*. Acknowledge that timeouts are finite and the connection may be torn down after excessive retransmission failures.
>
> 7. **Writing `port number` as 32 bits.** It is **16 bits**, range 0–65535. A surprisingly common slip.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — TCP Features (Module 4)**

- **TCP is connection-oriented, full-duplex, byte-stream, and reliable.** (Four adjectives, memorize in this order.)
- **Three-way handshake:** **SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK.** Uses flags `SYN` and `ACK` in the TCP header. Establishes initial sequence numbers on both sides.
- **Four-way termination:** **FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK.** The side initiating the close spends 2 × MSL in **TIME_WAIT**.
- **Sequence numbers are 32 bits and count *bytes*, not segments.** Initial Sequence Number (ISN) is randomly chosen to defeat spoofing.
- **ACK field is cumulative** — it advertises the *next expected* byte. Valid only when ACK flag = 1.
- **Sliding window** controls *flow*. `rwnd` (receive window) is advertised by the receiver.
- **Congestion control** is sender-side. Uses `cwnd` (congestion window) and `ssthresh`.
  - **Slow start:** cwnd grows **exponentially** (doubles per RTT) while `cwnd < ssthresh`.
  - **Congestion avoidance:** cwnd grows **linearly** (1 MSS per RTT) once `cwnd >= ssthresh`.
  - **Fast retransmit:** triggered by **3 duplicate ACKs**.
  - **Fast recovery (Reno):** set `ssthresh = cwnd/2`, `cwnd = ssthresh + 3 MSS`, skip slow start.
- **TCP checksum** is **16-bit one's complement** sum over **pseudo-header + TCP header + data**. Pseudo-header includes source IP, destination IP, zero byte, protocol (6), and TCP length.
- **RTT estimation (RFC 6298):** $\alpha = 1/8$, $\beta = 1/4$.
  - $SRTT_{n+1} = (1-\alpha)\,SRTT_n + \alpha\,R_n$
  - $RTTVAR_{n+1} = (1-\beta)\,RTTVAR_n + \beta\,\vert R_n - SRTT_n \vert$
  - $RTO = SRTT + 4 \cdot RTTVAR$, lower-bounded at 1 second.
- **Karn's Algorithm:** Do **not** take RTT samples for retransmitted segments; **exponentially back off** RTO on each timeout.
- **Port numbers** are 16 bits (0–65535). Well-known ports 0–1023; registered 1024–49151; dynamic/private 49152–65535.
- **MSS** = MTU − 40 bytes (IPv4 header 20 + TCP header 20). Default MSS over Ethernet is **1460 bytes**.
- **MTU** for Ethernet = **1500 bytes**. MSS for jumbo frames is correspondingly larger.
- **Header flags to memorize:** URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN, plus ECN-era CWR and ECE.
- **Throughput law (Mathis formula):** $B \approx \dfrac{MSS}{RTT \sqrt{p}}$ — TCP throughput is **inversely proportional to the square root of the loss rate**.
- **Default initial cwnd** in modern Linux: **10 MSS** (RFC 6928), not 1 — historical implementations used 1 MSS.

> **Final ESE Tip:** Always draw the **3-way handshake** and the **TCP header** — even if the question does not explicitly ask. A well-labeled diagram virtually guarantees **1–2 extra marks** on a 7-mark sub-question in the KTU valuation scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TCP Segment Format and Encapsulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** TCP Segment Format and Encapsulation** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **TCP Segment** is the **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)** exchanged between two communicating entities at the **Transport Layer** of the TCP/IP protocol suite. It is the structured container in which the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) places both the application data payload and the control information required for reliable, connection-oriented, byte-stream-oriented delivery. Formally, a TCP segment is composed of a **mandatory 20-byte header** (the "fixed" header) followed by an **optional Options field (0–40 bytes)** and finally the **payload data (0–65,535 bytes minus header bytes)**. The entire structure is encapsulated into an **IP datagram** at the Internet Layer, which is in turn encapsulated into a **Layer-2 frame** at the Network Interface Layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > Students must be able to *draw, label, and explain every field of the TCP segment header*, describe the **bit-level layout** (in 32-bit words), identify the **flags (control bits)**, and explain the **multi-layer encapsulation process** from application data down to the physical frame. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Registered Postal Parcel with a Customs Form" Imagine you are shipping a fragile, valuable document internationally using a courier service. | Courier Concept | TCP/IP Equivalent | Why It Maps | |---|---|---| | The physical box itself | **TCP Segment** | The container that protects and identifies the content | | Sender's tracking number | **Source Port (16 bits)** | Where to send replies / acknowledgments | | Receiver's tracking number | **Destination Port (16 bits)** | Which application process should receive it | | The receipt's serial number | **Sequence Number (32 bits)** | Guarantees correct ordering of bytes | | Delivery confirmation number | **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits)** | "I have received up to byte N" | | The official customs declaration sticker | **Flags (8 control bits)** | Special instructions: SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, etc. | | The declared weight & dimensions | **Window Size (16 bits)** | Flow control: "how much more can I accept?" | | Security seal / tamper check | **Checksum (16 bits)** | Error detection for header + data | | The extra declaration forms | **Options field (0–40 bytes)** | Negotiated parameters (MSS, timestamps, etc.) | | The actual letter inside the box | **Data / Payload** | The user's application data | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU:** A *segment* is a TCP term. A *datagram* is an IP term. A *frame* is a Layer-2 term. Students frequently lose marks in viva/ESE by using these terms interchangeably. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Standard Values) The following values are **must-memorize constants** for the KTU 2024 ESE: - **Minimum TCP Header Size = 20 bytes** (when Options field is absent) - **Maximum TCP Header Size = 60 bytes** (when Options field is 40 bytes) - **Maximum Segment Size (MSS) = 65,495 bytes** (after subtracting a typical 20-byte TCP header and 20-byte IP header from the 65,535-byte maximum IP payload) - **Sequence Numbers are 32 bits** (range: 0 to 2³² − 1 = 4,294,967,295) - **Ports are 16 bits** (range: 0 to 65,535; well-known ports: 0–1023) - **Window Size field is 16 bits** (range: 0 to 65,535 bytes before RFC 1323 window scaling) - **TCP is defined in IETF RFC 9293** (which obsoleted RFC 793 in 2022) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Endianness Rule:** All multi-byte fields in the TCP header are transmitted in **Network Byte Order (Big-Endian)** — meaning the **most significant byte (MSB) is sent first**. This is a non-negotiable KTU board rule and is frequently tested as a 1-mark sub-question. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical Structure of the TCP Segment A TCP segment is **logically divided** into two regions: 1. **Header Region** — Control information for the TCP entity 2. **Data Region** — The opaque payload from the upper layer The header is further subdivided into: - **Fixed Header (20 bytes / 160 bits)** — Always present, contains the essential connection and flow-control fields. - **Options + Padding (0–40 bytes / 0–320 bits)** — Variable-length, used for advanced features. ### 2.1.1 Fixed Header Field-by-Field Logical Breakdown The TCP fixed header occupies **5 rows of 32 bits (4 bytes each)**. Reading them top-to-bottom: **Row 1 — Addressing Information (8 bytes total):** - **Source Port (16 bits):** Identifies the sender's application endpoint. Combined with the sender's IP, it forms one half of a *socket pair* (4-tuple). - **Destination Port (16 bits):** Identifies the receiver's application endpoint. For example, **port 80** for HTTP, **port 443** for HTTPS, **port 22** for SSH. **Row 2 — Byte-Stream Sequencing (8 bytes total):** - **Sequence Number (32 bits):** The byte-position (offset) of the **first byte of data in this segment** within the entire byte stream. If SYN=1, the sequence number is the **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** and the first data byte is ISN+1. - **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits):** The **next expected byte** from the other side. ACK = N means "I have successfully received all bytes up to N−1, send me N next." This field is valid only when the **ACK flag is set**. **Row 3 — Control & Demultiplexing (8 bytes total):** - **Data Offset (4 bits):** Also called the *Header Length*. It specifies the **length of the TCP header in 32-bit words**. Minimum value = 5 (= 20 bytes, no options). Maximum value = 15 (= 60 bytes, full options). This field is **mandatory for the receiver to locate the start of the data payload** within the incoming IP payload. - **Reserved (4 bits):** Set to zero by default. Reserved for future use. - **Control Bits / Flags (8 bits):** Eight independent 1-bit flags, described in §2.1.2. - **Window Size (16 bits):** The size of the **receive window** in bytes. Used for **flow control**. The sender must not transmit more bytes than the receiver's advertised window. **Row 4 — Reliability Metadata (8 bytes total):** - **Checksum (16 bits):** A 16-bit **one's complement of the one's complement sum** of the pseudo-header, the TCP header, and the data. Mandatory — must be computed and verified by every TCP implementation. - **Urgent Pointer (16 bits):** Points to the byte following the *urgent data*. Valid only when the **URG flag is set**. Used for out-of-band signaling. **Row 5 — Optional (0–40 bytes):** - **Options (variable):** Negotiated parameters such as **MSS, Window Scale, Timestamps, SACK (Selective Acknowledgment)**, etc. Always padded with zeros so the header ends on a 32-bit boundary. ### 2.1.2 The Eight Control Flags (Bit-by-Bit) The 8 flags occupy 8 consecutive bits. From MSB to LSB (left to right as they appear on the wire): | Bit | Flag | Full Name | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **CWR** | Congestion Window Reduced | Set by sender to inform receiver that the congestion window was reduced (per RFC 3168). | | 2 | **ECE** | ECN-Echo | Receiver signals that it received an Explicit Congestion Notification. | | 3 | **URG** | Urgent | The Urgent Pointer field is significant. | | 4 | **ACK** | Acknowledgment | The Acknowledgment Number field is significant. (Set on virtually all segments after the SYN). | | 5 | **PSH** | Push | Push function: instructs the receiver to deliver data to the application immediately, without buffering. | | 6 | **RST** | Reset | Abort the connection abnormally; reject an invalid segment; refuse a connection. | | 7 | **SYN** | Synchronize | Synchronize sequence numbers. Used to initiate a connection. SYN=1 and FIN=1 segments consume one sequence number each. | | 8 | **FIN** | Finish | Sender has finished sending data; used to gracefully close a connection. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 3-Mark Favorite:** *"List the TCP flags and explain SYN, FIN, and ACK."* — Memorize the table above. Note that **SYN, FIN, and RST each consume one sequence number** even when no payload is carried. This is a classic board trick. ## 2.2 The Encapsulation Process **Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer prepends (and sometimes appends) its own header/trailer to the SDU received from the layer above, before passing the resulting PDU down to the layer below. The TCP encapsulation chain for an application message `M` is: **Step 1 — Application Layer:** Generates message `M` (e.g., an HTTP GET request). **Step 2 — Transport Layer (TCP):** Prepends the **TCP header** to produce a **TCP Segment**. $$ \text{TCP Segment} = \text{TCP Header} \ \vert \ M $$ The segment is then handed down to the Internet Layer. **Step 3 — Internet Layer (IP):** Prepends the **IP header** to produce an **IP Datagram**. $$ \text{IP Datagram} = \text{IP Header} \ \vert \ \text{TCP Segment} $$ **Step 4 — Network Interface / Data Link Layer:** Prepends a **frame header** and appends a **frame trailer (FCS/CRC)** to produce a **Layer-2 Frame**. $$ \text{Frame} = \text{Frame Header} \ \vert \ \text{IP Datagram} \ \vert \ \text{Frame Trailer} $$ **Step 5 — Physical Layer:** Transmits the frame as a stream of **bits** over the physical medium. > [!IMPORTANT] > The reverse process at the receiver is called **Decapsulation**. The TCP entity at the receiver does not see the IP header, MAC header, or physical encoding — it only ever processes the **TCP segment** delivered up by IP. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H_{TCP,\min} = 20 \text{ bytes}$ | Minimum TCP header (no options) | Bytes | | 2 | $H_{TCP,\max} = 60 \text{ bytes}$ | Maximum TCP header (40 bytes options + padding) | Bytes | | 3 | $H_{TCP} = 4 \times \text{DataOffset}$ | Header length from the Data Offset field | Bytes | | 4 | $\text{DataOffset}_{\min} = 5$ | 5 × 32-bit words = 20 bytes | 32-bit words | | 5 | $\text{DataOffset}_{\max} = 15$ | 15 × 32-bit words = 60 bytes | 32-bit words | | 6 | $L_{IP\,payload} \le 65{,}535 \text{ bytes}$ | Maximum IP payload (= maximum segment + IP options) | Bytes | | 7 | $L_{segment} \le \text{MSS} + H_{TCP}$ | MSS excludes the TCP header | Bytes | | 8 | $\text{MSS} = \text{MTU}_{IP} - H_{IP} - H_{TCP}$ | Typical Ethernet: $1500 - 20 - 20 = 1460$ bytes | Bytes | | 9 | $L_{data} = L_{segment} - H_{TCP}$ | Recover payload length from total segment length | Bytes | | 10 | $N_{seq} \in [0, \ 2^{32}-1]$ | Sequence number range | Integer | | 11 | $N_{port} \in [0, \ 65535]$ | Port number range | Integer | | 12 | $W \in [0, \ 65535]$ | Window size before RFC 1323 scaling | Bytes | | 13 | $W_{eff} = W \times 2^{S}$ | Effective window with shift count $S$ (RFC 1323) | Bytes | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** In markdown tables, the absolute-value symbol `|x|` is written as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to avoid breaking the table-rendering pipe characters. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Web servers (Nginx, Apache):** Use the **Window Size** field for TCP receive-window tuning in `tcp_rmem`. - **Firewalls & IDS/IPS systems:** Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) engines (e.g., Suricata, Wireshark dissectors) parse the **flags** field to classify traffic — e.g., a SYN with no ACK is a connection-initiation packet, a RST indicates a connection abort. - **Performance Engineering:** The **Window Scale option (RFC 1323)** is mandatory in modern high-bandwidth-delay-product (BDP) networks (e.g., 10 Gbps transcontinental links), allowing the 16-bit window field to be scaled by a factor of up to $2^{14}$. - **Network Forensics:** The **Timestamp option** is used by tools like `tcpdump` and Wireshark to measure Round-Trip Time (RTT) and detect retransmissions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Computing Header Length from Data Offset The TCP **Data Offset** field is a 4-bit unsigned integer. Each increment of 1 represents **one 32-bit word = 4 bytes**. We can therefore derive the header length in bytes as follows: **Given:** Data Offset field raw value = $D$ (an integer from 0 to 15). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Header Length in bits} &= D \times 32 \\ \text{Header Length in bytes} &= \frac{D \times 32}{8} = D \times 4 \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Examples (KTU Board-Standard Calculations):** **Example 1:** A segment has Data Offset = 5. $$ H_{TCP} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes (no options)} $$ **Example 2:** A segment has Data Offset = 8. $$ H_{TCP} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ bytes (12 bytes of options + padding)} $$ **Example 3:** A segment has Data Offset = 15 (the legal maximum). $$ H_{TCP} = 15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ bytes (full 40 bytes of options)} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Segment Size (MSS) for a Standard Ethernet Link Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) frames have a **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU) of 1500 bytes**.
