This means the IP datagram (and therefore the TCP segment it carries) cannot exceed 1500 bytes. **Step 1 — Subtract the IP header:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{IP\,payload} &= \text{MTU} - H_{IP} \\ &= 1500 - 20 \\ &= 1480 \text{ bytes (assuming no IP options)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Subtract the TCP header:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MSS} &= L_{IP\,payload} - H_{TCP} \\ &= 1480 - 20 \\ &= 1460 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** On a standard Ethernet link, a typical TCP segment carries up to **1460 bytes of application data per segment**. > [!NOTE] > **Why MSS Matters:** If the sender transmits more than MSS bytes in a single segment, IP must fragment the datagram. Fragmentation is **undesirable** because losing any single fragment forces retransmission of the entire datagram, and modern networks often drop fragmented packets outright (RFC 8200 prohibits fragmentation in IPv6). ## 3.3 Derivation: Recovering Data Length from Encapsulated Frame Sizes A frequently-asked KTU question gives you the total frame size and asks you to compute the application data length. **Problem Statement:** An Ethernet frame carrying a TCP segment is received. The Ethernet frame has: - Ethernet header = 14 bytes - Ethernet trailer (FCS) = 4 bytes - IP header = 20 bytes - TCP header = 20 bytes - Total frame size on the wire = 1514 bytes **Find the length of the application data.** **Step 1 — Compute the size of the IP datagram (Layer-3 PDU):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{IP\,datagram} &= L_{frame} - H_{eth} - T_{eth} \\ &= 1514 - 14 - 4 \\ &= 1496 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the size of the TCP segment (Layer-4 PDU):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{TCP\,segment} &= L_{IP\,datagram} - H_{IP} \\ &= 1496 - 20 \\ &= 1476 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Subtract the TCP header to obtain the data length:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{data} &= L_{TCP\,segment} - H_{TCP} \\ &= 1476 - 20 \\ &= 1456 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer: 1456 bytes of application data were carried in this frame.** This is slightly less than the typical MSS of 1460 because the IP datagram in this example is 1496 bytes (i.e., it is using 4 extra bytes — likely for IP options or a VPN encapsulation such as a 4-byte GRE or IPsec tunnel header). ## 3.4 Derivation: Pseudo-Header Used in TCP Checksum The TCP checksum is **not** computed only over the segment — it also includes a **pseudo-header** constructed from the IP layer. This protects against **misrouted segments**. **Pseudo-Header Structure (12 bytes):** | Field | Size | |---|---| | Source IP Address | 4 bytes | | Destination IP Address | 4 bytes | | Zero (reserved) | 1 byte | | Protocol Number (6 for TCP) | 1 byte | | TCP Segment Length (header + data) | 2 bytes | **Checksum Computation Algorithm (Step-by-Step):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1:} \ \text{Form the pseudo-header from the IP layer's source/destination and protocol fields.} \\ &\text{Step 2:} \ \text{Append the full TCP header (with checksum field set to zero).} \\ &\text{Step 3:} \ \text{Append the TCP data. If data has odd length, pad with one zero byte at the end.} \\ &\text{Step 4:} \ \text{Add all 16-bit words using 1's-complement arithmetic.} \\ &\text{Step 5:} \ \text{Take the 1's-complement of the final sum to obtain the checksum.} \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Example:** Suppose the sum of all 16-bit words (pseudo-header + segment) is `0x4AC2`. The transmitted checksum is: $$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{0x4AC2} = 0xB53D $$ At the receiver, the sum of all 16-bit words **including the checksum** must equal `0xFFFF` if the segment is uncorrupted. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When Each Flag Is Typically Set This matrix is a KTU 2024 high-yield topic. Memorize the typical flag combinations: | Connection Phase | SYN | ACK | FIN | RST | PSH | URG | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Connection Initiation** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | First packet of the 3-way handshake | | **SYN-ACK** | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Second packet of the 3-way handshake | | **Data Transfer** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0/1 | 0/1 | Carrying payload; PSH set on the last segment of a burst | | **Graceful Close (active)** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | First FIN of the 4-way termination handshake | | **Connection Abort** | 0 | 0/1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | Used to reject a SYN or signal an error | | **Scan Probe** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Classic Nmap SYN scan (stealth) | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Error:** A SYN segment carries **no data** but **does consume one sequence number**. The receiver's acknowledgment for a SYN with ISN = X is `ACK = X + 1`. Confusing "no data" with "no sequence-number consumption" is one of the top mark-deductions in KTU 2024 ESE answer sheets. ## 3.6 Derivation: Window Scaling (RFC 1323) For a standard TCP header, the Window Size field is **16 bits**, giving a maximum advertised window of **65,535 bytes**. On high-BDP links, this caps throughput at: $$ \text{Throughput} \le \frac{W_{max}}{RTT} $$ **Step 1 — Compute the throughput ceiling for a transcontinental link with RTT = 100 ms:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Throughput}_{std} &= \frac{65{,}535 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{0.1 \text{ s}} \\ &= 5{,}242{,}800 \text{ bps} \approx 5.24 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — This is far too low for a 1 Gbps link.** RFC 1323 introduces a **Window Scale option** negotiated during the 3-way handshake. The sender and receiver agree on a **shift count $S$** (0–14). **Step 3 — The effective window becomes:** $$ W_{eff} = W \times 2^{S} $$ **Example:** With $S = 7$ and the raw 16-bit window = 65,535: $$ W_{eff} = 65{,}535 \times 2^{7} = 65{,}535 \times 128 = 8{,}388{,}480 \text{ bytes} \approx 8 \text{ MB} $$ **Step 4 — Resulting maximum throughput at RTT = 100 ms:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Throughput}_{scaled} &= \frac{8{,}388{,}480 \times 8}{0.1} \\ &= 671{,}088{,}640 \text{ bps} \approx 671 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Window scaling is **mandatory** on modern high-speed TCP paths (e.g., 10 Gbps transatlantic links require $S \ge 8$). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: TCP Segment Header — Bit-Level Layout The following Mermaid block diagram renders the **exact bit-level organization** of the 160-bit (20-byte) TCP fixed header. Each block is annotated with its size in bits and its function. The optional fields row is also illustrated. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ROW1["Row 1 (32 bits) - Addressing"] A1["Source Port
16 bits"] A2["Destination Port
16 bits"] end subgraph ROW2["Row 2 (32 bits) - Sequencing"] B1["Sequence Number
32 bits"] end subgraph ROW3["Row 3 (32 bits) - Control"] B2["Acknowledgment Number
32 bits"] end subgraph ROW4["Row 4 (32 bits) - Flow & Reliability"] C1["Data Offset
4 bits"] C2["Reserved
4 bits"] C3["Flags CWR ECE URG ACK PSH RST SYN FIN
8 bits"] C4["Window Size
16 bits"] end subgraph ROW5["Row 5 (32 bits) - Integrity"] D1["Checksum
16 bits"] D2["Urgent Pointer
16 bits"] end subgraph ROW6["Rows 6 to 15 (Optional) - 0 to 40 bytes"] E1["Options + Padding
0 to 320 bits"] end subgraph ROW7["Payload Region"] F1["Data
0 to 65,495 bytes"] end ROW1 --> ROW2 --> ROW3 --> ROW4 --> ROW5 --> ROW6 --> ROW7 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** The **vertical order** in this schematic is the **order in which bits appear on the wire** (MSB-first, big-endian). A common KTU board question asks you to "redraw the TCP segment format" — reproduce the structure above with all field labels. ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Encapsulation Chain (Top-Down Flow) This block diagram illustrates the **multi-layer encapsulation** of an application message as it travels down the TCP/IP stack. Each layer adds its own header (and the Data-Link layer also adds a trailer). ```mermaid flowchart TD APP["Application Layer
User Message: GET /index.html HTTP/1.1"] H_TCP["TCP Header
20 bytes minimum
Source Port | Dest Port | Seq | Ack | Offset | Flags | Window | Checksum | Urgent | Options"] SEG["TCP Segment = TCP Header + Data
Layer-4 PDU"] H_IP["IP Header
20 bytes minimum
Version | IHL | TTL | Protocol=6 | Src IP | Dst IP | etc."] DAT["IP Datagram = IP Header + TCP Segment
Layer-3 PDU"] H_ETH["Ethernet Header
14 bytes
Dest MAC | Src MAC | EtherType 0x0800"] T_ETH["Ethernet Trailer
4 bytes
FCS / CRC-32"] FRM["Ethernet Frame
Layer-2 PDU
= Eth Header + IP Datagram + Eth Trailer"] BITS["Physical Layer
Bits transmitted on copper / fiber / RF medium"] APP --> SEG H_TCP --> SEG SEG --> DAT H_IP --> DAT DAT --> FRM H_ETH --> FRM T_ETH --> FRM FRM --> BITS ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Decapsulation Insight:** At the receiver, the physical layer reconstructs the frame → the Data-Link layer strips the Ethernet header/trailer and hands the IP datagram to IP → IP strips its header and hands the TCP segment up to TCP → TCP strips its header and delivers the data to the application. **Each layer is "blind" to the layers below it.** ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flags & Their Effects This diagram maps each TCP flag to the **functional effect** it triggers inside a TCP state machine. It is a frequent KTU viva question. ```mermaid flowchart LR FLAG_SYN["SYN flag = 1
Initiate connection
Consume 1 seq number"] FLAG_ACK["ACK flag = 1
Ack field is valid
Confirm receipt of bytes"] FLAG_FIN["FIN flag = 1
Graceful close
Consume 1 seq number"] FLAG_RST["RST flag = 1
Abort connection
Reject invalid segment"] FLAG_PSH["PSH flag = 1
Push data to app
Do not buffer"] FLAG_URG["URG flag = 1
Urgent Pointer valid
Out-of-band data"] FLAG_CWR["CWR flag = 1
Notify ECE received
Congestion window reduced"] FLAG_ECE["ECE flag = 1
ECN-Echo to peer
Network congestion"] FLAG_SYN --> STATE_SYN_SENT["State: SYN-SENT"] FLAG_ACK --> STATE_ESTABLISHED["State: ESTABLISHED
(virtually always set)"] FLAG_FIN --> STATE_FIN_WAIT["State: FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2"] FLAG_RST --> STATE_CLOSED["State: CLOSED (abrupt)"] FLAG_PSH --> APP_DELIVERY["Application delivery
immediate"] FLAG_URG --> URG_HANDLER["Urgent data handler"] FLAG_CWR --> CW_REDUCED["Congestion control
reduce cwnd"] FLAG_ECE --> CW_REDUCED ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block Architecture — Where Each TCP Field Is Used in the Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CONTROL["Connection Control (SYN/FIN/RST)"] F1["Flags + Seq + Ack + Options"] end subgraph FLOW["Flow Control"] F2["Window Size field"] end subgraph RELIABILITY["Reliability & Ordering"] F3["Sequence Number
Acknowledgment Number"] end subgraph INTEGRITY["Integrity"] F4["Checksum (with pseudo-header)"] end subgraph ADDRESS["Endpoint Addressing"] F5["Source Port + Destination Port
(socket pair)"] end subgraph SPECIAL["Out-of-Band Signaling"] F6["URG flag + Urgent Pointer"] end F5 --> SOCKET_PAIR["Forms 4-tuple socket:
SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort"] F3 --> RETRANS["Retransmission Timer
Fast Retransmit / Recovery"] F2 --> RWIN["Receive Window rwnd
Sliding window protocol"] F1 --> FSM["TCP Finite State Machine
11 states (LISTEN, SYN-SENT,
ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, etc.)"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the functions of the following TCP header fields: Sequence Number, Acknowledgment Number, and Window Size.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Sequence Number (32 bits):** It indicates the byte-position of the first data byte in this segment within the entire byte stream. TCP numbers every byte in the stream. The sequence number of the first byte in a connection is the Initial Sequence Number (ISN) + 1. Sequence numbers enable the receiver to **reorder segments** that may arrive out of order and to **detect missing segments**. *(1 mark)* 2. **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits):** This field carries the **next expected byte** from the sender. The value $N$ means "I have successfully received all bytes up to $N-1$; please send $N$ next." It is valid only when the **ACK flag is set** (which is true for virtually all segments after the SYN). It implements the **cumulative acknowledgment** mechanism of TCP. *(1 mark)* 3. **Window Size (16 bits):** This field specifies the size of the **receive window** (in bytes) of the sender of this segment. It is the principal **flow-control** mechanism: the receiver advertises how much data it can currently buffer, and the sender must not exceed that amount. With RFC 1323 window scaling, the effective window is $W \times 2^S$. *(1 mark)* --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between a TCP segment, an IP datagram, and an Ethernet frame. State the typical header sizes of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | PDU Name | Layer | Header Size | What It Contains | |---|---|---|---| | **TCP Segment** | Transport (L4) | **20–60 bytes** | Source/Dest Port, Seq, Ack, Flags, Window, Checksum, Options | | **IP Datagram** | Internet (L3) | **20–60 bytes** | Version, IHL, TTL, Protocol (=6 for TCP), Src/Dst IP, Fragment info | | **Ethernet Frame** | Data Link (L2) | **14 bytes header + 4 bytes trailer** | Source/Dest MAC, EtherType, FCS/CRC | *(2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for explicitly stating the size of each header.)* > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** A segment is encapsulated inside the IP datagram's *payload*, and the datagram is encapsulated inside the Ethernet frame's *payload*. Hence the nesting is **Frame → Datagram → Segment → Data**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT** draw the TCP header diagram without labeling **every field** and its **bit-size**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing bit-sizes. > - **Do NOT** confuse *Data Offset* with *Total Length*. Data Offset = header length in 32-bit words; IP's Total Length = entire datagram length in bytes. > - **Do NOT** forget to state that all multi-byte fields are in **Network Byte Order (Big-Endian)**. > - When asked about the **MSS**, state the formula and substitute the standard Ethernet values. > - For checksum questions, mention the **pseudo-header** explicitly — most students forget it and lose 2–3 marks. --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a) Draw and explain the TCP segment format with a neat diagram. Label every field and specify its size in bits. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The TCP segment format is structured as a fixed 20-byte (160-bit) header followed by an optional 0–40-byte options field and then the data payload. The header is drawn as 5 rows of 32 bits each: **Row 1 (32 bits):** - **Source Port (16 bits):** Sender's application port. - **Destination Port (16 bits):** Receiver's application port. **Row 2 (32 bits):** - **Sequence Number (32 bits):** Byte-offset of the first data byte in the stream. **Row 3 (32 bits):** - **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits):** Next expected byte from the peer. **Row 4 (32 bits):** - **Data Offset (4 bits):** Header length in 32-bit words (min 5, max 15). - **Reserved (4 bits):** Zero. - **Control Flags (8 bits):** CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN. - **Window Size (16 bits):** Receive window for flow control. **Row 5 (32 bits):** - **Checksum (16 bits):** 1's-complement sum over pseudo-header + segment. - **Urgent Pointer (16 bits):** Valid only if URG=1. **Optional Rows 6–15:** Options + Padding (0–40 bytes / 0–320 bits). **Payload:** Data (0–65,495 bytes). *(Award full marks: 1 mark for the diagram; 4 marks for labeling every field with its size; 2 marks for explaining the function of each major field.)* --- **(b) Explain the TCP encapsulation process. Describe each layer's contribution to the encapsulated PDU as data moves from the application layer down to the physical layer. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Application Layer:** The user application (e.g., web browser) generates a message $M$ such as `GET / HTTP/1.1`. This message is passed to the Transport Layer via a socket API (e.g., `send()`). *(1 mark)* **Step 2 — Transport Layer (TCP):** TCP prepends its **20-byte header** (or more with options) to $M$, producing a **TCP Segment**: $$ \text{Segment} = \text{TCP Header} \ \vert \ M $$ The segment is then handed to the Internet Layer. *(2 marks)* **Step 3 — Internet Layer (IP):** IP prepends its **20-byte header** (Protocol field = 6 to identify TCP) to the segment, producing an **IP Datagram**: $$ \text{Datagram} = \text{IP Header} \ \vert \ \text{Segment} $$ The datagram is handed to the Network Interface Layer. *(2 marks)* **Step 4 — Data Link Layer (Ethernet):** A **14-byte Ethernet header** (Dst MAC, Src MAC, EtherType 0x0800) is prepended and a **4-byte FCS trailer** (CRC-32) is appended, producing a **frame**: $$ \text{Frame} = \text{Eth Header} \ \vert \ \text{Datagram} \ \vert \ \text{Eth Trailer} $$ *(1 mark)* **Step 5 — Physical Layer:** The frame is converted into a bitstream and transmitted as electrical, optical, or RF signals over the medium. *(1 mark)* --- ### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a) Explain the purpose of each of the eight TCP control flags. State which flags consume a sequence number. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The eight TCP control flags occupy 8 consecutive bits in the third row of the header: 1. **CWR (Congestion Window Reduced):** Set by the sender to inform the receiver that the congestion window has been reduced in response to an ECN signal. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **ECE (ECN-Echo):** Set by the receiver to signal that it has received an Explicit Congestion Notification from the network, prompting the sender to reduce its rate. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **URG (Urgent):** Indicates that the **Urgent Pointer** field is significant and that out-of-band data is present. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **ACK (Acknowledgment):** Indicates that the **Acknowledgment Number** field is valid. Set on virtually all segments after the SYN. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **PSH (Push):** Instructs the receiver to push the data to the application immediately, without buffering. Often set on the last segment of a burst. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **RST (Reset):** Aborts the connection abruptly. Used to reject a connection attempt or to signal a fatal error. *(0.5 mark)* 7. **SYN (Synchronize):** Initiates a connection and synchronizes sequence numbers. Set in the first two packets of the 3-way handshake. *(0.5 mark)* 8. **FIN (Finish):** Gracefully closes the connection (no more data from the sender). Used in the 4-way termination handshake. *(0.5 mark)* **Flags that consume one sequence number:** SYN, FIN, and RST each consume one sequence number even when no payload is carried. *(3 marks for correctly identifying these three and explaining the rule.)* --- **(b) An Ethernet frame carrying a TCP segment has a total length of 1514 bytes on the wire. The Ethernet header is 14 bytes, the Ethernet trailer is 4 bytes, the IP header is 20 bytes, and the TCP header is 20 bytes. Compute the application data length and the typical MSS for a standard Ethernet link. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Compute the IP Datagram length:** *(2 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} L_{IP\,datagram} &= L_{frame} - H_{eth} - T_{eth} \\ &= 1514 - 14 - 4 \\ &= 1496 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the TCP Segment length:** *(2 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} L_{TCP\,segment} &= L_{IP\,datagram} - H_{IP} \\ &= 1496 - 20 \\ &= 1476 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the application data length:** *(1 mark)* $$ \begin{aligned} L_{data} &= L_{TCP\,segment} - H_{TCP} \\ &= 1476 - 20 \\ &= 1456 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the typical MSS for standard Ethernet:** *(2 marks)* $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MTU}_{Ethernet} &= 1500 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{MSS} &= \text{MTU} - H_{IP} - H_{TCP} \\ &= 1500 - 20 - 20 \\ &= 1460 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The received frame carries **1456 bytes** of application data. This is **4 bytes less than the standard MSS of 1460 bytes**, indicating that the IP layer has added 4 extra bytes (likely for an option, a tunnel header, or fragmentation metadata). *(Full marks for showing all four steps with correct units.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE** - **TCP Segment = TCP Header (20–60 bytes) + Data (0–65,495 bytes).** Minimum header = 20 bytes, maximum = 60 bytes. *(High-weightage.)* - **Header Layout:** 5 rows × 32 bits = 160 bits = 20 bytes fixed. Data Offset is in 32-bit words; the actual header size in bytes = $4 \times \text{DataOffset}$. - **Mandatory fields in the fixed header:** Source Port, Destination Port, Sequence Number, Acknowledgment Number, Data Offset, Flags, Window Size, Checksum, Urgent Pointer. - **The 8 Flags (MSB to LSB):** CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN. - **Sequence-number-consuming flags:** SYN, FIN, RST — each consumes exactly 1 sequence number, even with zero payload. - **Acknowledgment field is valid only when ACK = 1.** - **Window field = 16 bits = max 65,535 bytes; can be scaled by $2^S$ via the Window Scale option (RFC 1323).** - **Checksum uses a pseudo-header** containing the source IP, destination IP, zero byte, protocol number (6 for TCP), and TCP length. *(Frequently tested.)* - **All multi-byte fields are in Network Byte Order = Big-Endian.** Most-significant byte transmitted first. - **MSS for Ethernet = 1460 bytes** (i.e., $1500 - 20 - 20$). If the IP header is 20 bytes and TCP header is 20 bytes with no options. - **Encapsulation Chain:** Application Data → TCP Segment → IP Datagram → Ethernet Frame → Physical Bits. Each layer adds its own header; the Data-Link layer also adds a trailer. - **Reverse process is Decapsulation** at the receiver. Each layer strips its own header and passes the inner PDU up. - **TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream-oriented, and full-duplex.** It is defined in **RFC 9293** (which obsoletes RFC 793). - **Common Viva Questions:** "What is the difference between a segment, a datagram, and a frame?" "Why is the TCP checksum computed over a pseudo-header?" "What is the role of the PSH flag?" "What is the role of the URG flag?" > **End of Module 4 Topic — TCP Segment Format and Encapsulation**

topics.title: State Transition Diagram (TCP Connection Management)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** State Transition Diagram (TCP Connection Management)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# State Transition Diagram (TCP Connection Management)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **TCP State Transition Diagram** is a finite state machine (FSM) model that precisely defines every possible state a TCP connection can occupy throughout its lifecycle, along with the events (incoming/outgoing segments) that trigger transitions between states. It forms the formal specification governing **connection establishment**, **data transfer**, and **connection termination** as standardized in **RFC 793**.

The TCP state transition diagram is the **engineer's blueprint** of how a Transmission Control Protocol session evolves. Unlike a connectionless protocol (such as UDP) that simply fires packets into the network, TCP is a **connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream protocol** that must carefully negotiate and tear down a virtual circuit between a **client** and a **server** before and after useful data flows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Formal Telephone Call":**
> Imagine you need to make an important business call. Before discussing business (data transfer), you must:
> 1. **Pick up the phone and dial** (SYN — synchronize sequence numbers).
> 2. The **receiver picks up and says "Hello, this is Bob"** (SYN + ACK — agreement on initial sequence numbers).
> 3. You reply **"Hi Bob, this is Alice, glad we connected"** (ACK — connection confirmed).
>
> This **3-way handshake** mirrors TCP's connection setup. When the call ends, both parties say **"Goodbye"** (FIN) and acknowledge it, but the caller waits **2 × MSL** (Maximum Segment Lifetime) to ensure the other side received the final goodbye — exactly like TCP's **TIME-WAIT** state. The state diagram is simply the rulebook for this polite conversation.

### The 11 Standard TCP States (KTU Mandatory List)

| # | State | Type of Endpoint | Purpose |
|---|-------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | **CLOSED** | Both | No active connection exists. |
| 2 | **LISTEN** | Server | Server is waiting for incoming connection requests. |
| 3 | **SYN-SENT** | Client | Client has sent a SYN and is awaiting matching SYN+ACK. |
| 4 | **SYN-RCVD** | Server | Server has received a SYN and sent SYN+ACK; awaiting ACK. |
| 5 | **ESTABLISHED** | Both | Connection is open; data transfer in progress. |
| 6 | **FIN-WAIT-1** | Active Closer | First FIN sent; awaiting ACK or FIN from remote. |
| 7 | **FIN-WAIT-2** | Active Closer | ACK to our FIN received; awaiting remote's FIN. |
| 8 | **CLOSE-WAIT** | Passive Closer | Remote sent FIN; we have acknowledged it. |
| 9 | **LAST-ACK** | Passive Closer | We sent our own FIN; awaiting final ACK. |
| 10 | **CLOSING** | Simultaneous | Both sides sent FINs simultaneously; awaiting ACK. |
| 11 | **TIME-WAIT** | Active Closer | Final ACK sent; waiting 2×MSL to ensure delivery. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expectation):** The default value of **MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime)** is **2 minutes**, hence the mandatory waiting period in TIME-WAIT is **2 × MSL = 4 minutes (240 seconds)**. This guarantees that all old segments expire and do not interfere with a new connection reusing the same 4-tuple (source IP, source port, dest IP, dest port).

### Why 11 States and Not Just 2 ("Connected / Not Connected")?

TCP must robustly handle **simultaneous opens**, **simultaneous closes**, **half-closed connections**, **aborts (RST)**, and **timeouts**. A flat binary state model would collapse under these edge cases, which is why the formal FSM has 11 distinct states. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the *active close*, *passive close*, and *simultaneous close* paths.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Phases of a TCP Connection

The state transition diagram is naturally partitioned into three major operational phases, each with its own characteristic states and transitions.

### Phase A — Connection Establishment (The 3-Way Handshake)
**Path taken:** `CLOSED → LISTEN / SYN-SENT → SYN-RCVD → ESTABLISHED`

### Phase B — Data Transfer
**State held:** `ESTABLISHED` (the "steady state" of TCP).

### Phase C — Connection Termination (The 4-Way Handshake)
**Path taken:** `ESTABLISHED → FIN-WAIT-1 → FIN-WAIT-2 → TIME-WAIT → CLOSED`
**Passive variant:** `ESTABLISHED → CLOSE-WAIT → LAST-ACK → CLOSED`
**Simultaneous variant:** `ESTABLISHED → FIN-WAIT-1 → CLOSING → TIME-WAIT → CLOSED`

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is connection setup 3-way but termination 4-way?**
> During setup, **SYN** and **ACK** are combined into a single segment (`SYN+ACK`) by the server, saving one round-trip. During termination, **FIN** and **ACK** are conceptually independent — the side that receives a FIN must first ACK it, and only later send its own FIN. Hence 4 segments are exchanged. This asymmetry is a **classic KTU 2-mark question**.

## 2.2 Trigger Events (The Arrows of the State Machine)

Each transition in the diagram is triggered by one of the following events:

| Event | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| **passive open** | Application calls `listen()` — server begins waiting. |
| **active open / connect** | Application calls `connect()` — client initiates. |
| **send SYN** | Outgoing segment with SYN flag set. |
| **receive SYN** | Incoming segment with SYN flag set. |
| **send ACK** | Outgoing segment with ACK flag set. |
| **receive ACK** | Incoming segment with ACK flag set. |
| **send FIN** | Outgoing segment with FIN flag set (`close()`). |
| **receive FIN** | Incoming segment with FIN flag set. |
| **close** | Application closes the socket. |
| **receive RST** | Incoming Reset — abrupt teardown. |
| **timeout (2×MSL)** | TIME-WAIT timer expires. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Engineering Use |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| TIME-WAIT duration | $T_{TW} = 2 \times \text{MSL}$ | Ensures last ACK reaches peer before socket reuse. |
| Default MSL (RFC 793) | $\text{MSL} = 120 \text{ seconds}$ | Standard internet lifetime of an IP datagram. |
| Default TIME-WAIT | $T_{TW} = 240 \text{ s} = 4 \text{ min}$ | Used unless socket option `SO_REUSEADDR` is set. |
| Sequence Number Initial | $\text{ISN}_{new} = \text{ISN}_{old} + 1 \text{ every } 4\ \mu s$ (originally) | Prevents stale segments from new connections. |
| Socket Identity (4-tuple) | $\{\text{SrcIP}, \text{SrcPort}, \text{DstIP}, \text{DstPort}\}$ | Uniquely identifies a TCP connection. |
| Control Flags used | SYN, ACK, FIN, RST | Drive state transitions. |
| States in FSM | $\text{N}_{states} = 11$ | Canonical TCP state count. |
| Maximum Half-Open Backlog | Implementation-defined | Protects SYN-RCVD queue from SYN flood. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes `|` are used in formulas above** — absolute-value and conditional notations are spelled out to comply with KTU markdown table-rendering rules. When writing inline math, prefer `\vert` or `\mid` if a delimiter is needed, e.g., $x \mid y$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TCP state machine is the **operational heart of every web service**, **database connection pool**, and **API endpoint** on the internet. Understanding it enables engineers to:

- **Diagnose connection pool exhaustion** in microservices (often caused by leaking sockets stuck in `CLOSE-WAIT` or `FIN-WAIT-2`).
- **Tune servers under DDoS/SYN flood attacks** (servers accumulate sockets in `SYN-RCVD`).
- **Configure load balancers** that multiplex thousands of clients onto a single backend socket table.
- **Implement reliable messaging protocols** (e.g., MQTT-over-TCP, SMTP, HTTP/1.1 keep-alive).
- **Reason about TIME-WAIT assassinations** and the `SO_REUSEADDR` socket option for high-throughput servers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern KTU Context (2024 Scheme):** Even though **QUIC** (used in HTTP/3) replaces TCP for transport, the **TCP state machine remains a mandatory exam topic** because the KTU 2024 syllabus specifies it under *Module 4 — TCP Connection Management* and it forms the foundation for understanding QUIC's connection-ID-based stream multiplexing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the TIME-WAIT Duration

The **2 × MSL** wait is not arbitrary — it is derived from two independent failure scenarios that TCP must defend against. Let us derive it formally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Premise:** TCP guarantees reliability using **positive acknowledgments with retransmission**. However, an ACK or FIN segment itself can be lost. The state machine must survive such losses without leaving dangling duplicates on the wire.

### Step 1 — Define the Maximum Segment Lifetime

The Maximum Segment Lifetime (MSL) is the maximum time any IP datagram is allowed to survive in the network before being discarded. RFC 793 originally set this as:

$$
\text{MSL} = 120 \text{ seconds} = 2 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Two Failure Scenarios

**Scenario A — The final ACK is lost.**
The active closer sends the last ACK in response to the passive closer's FIN. If this ACK is lost, the passive closer (in `LAST-ACK`) will retransmit its FIN after its own retransmission timeout. The active closer must still be in a state capable of receiving and re-ACKing this retransmitted FIN.

**Scenario B — Old duplicate segments from the same 4-tuple.**
If the active closer immediately reused the 4-tuple for a new connection, old segments from the previous incarnation might still be in flight (delayed by routers). They could be mis-delivered to the new connection, corrupting its byte stream.

### Step 3 — Bound Scenario A

For the active closer to receive a retransmitted FIN, it must remain in the network-reachable state for at least one full MSL after sending the final ACK. This gives the lower bound:

$$
T_{\text{wait}} \geq \text{MSL}
$$

### Step 4 — Bound Scenario B

Old segments from the previous connection may live in the network for up to MSL seconds. To ensure none survive when the new connection is opened, the active closer must wait an additional MSL after the connection is logically closed:

$$
T_{\text{wait}} \geq \text{MSL (for old segs)} + \text{MSL (for re-ACK)} = 2 \times \text{MSL}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine and Conclude

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{TIME-WAIT}} = 2 \times \text{MSL} = 2 \times 120 \text{ s} = 240 \text{ seconds} = 4 \text{ minutes}}
$$

This is precisely the value implemented in **Linux**, **Windows**, and **BSD** sockets (though it is often configurable via the `tcp_fin_timeout` sysctl and the `TIME_WAIT` sockets linger parameter in Windows).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — The Three Termination Scenarios

The KTU 2024 scheme examiners expect students to **compare** the three termination paths. The matrix below is a high-yield tool for board answers.

| Parameter | **Normal Close (Active/Passive Split)** | **Simultaneous Close** | **Abort (RST)** |
|-----------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| FIN exchange | 2 separate FINs (4 segments) | Both FINs sent at once (crossed) | No FIN — RST only |
| States visited (active) | FIN-WAIT-1 → FIN-WAIT-2 → TIME-WAIT | FIN-WAIT-1 → **CLOSING** → TIME-WAIT | ESTABLISHED → CLOSED |
| States visited (passive) | CLOSE-WAIT → LAST-ACK → CLOSED | FIN-WAIT-1 → **CLOSING** → TIME-WAIT | ESTABLISHED → CLOSED |
| TIME-WAIT entered? | **Yes (active side)** | **Yes (both sides)** | **No** |
| Graceful delivery? | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No — data may be lost** |
| Total segments | 4 (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK) | 4 (FIN, FIN, ACK, ACK) | 1 (RST) |
| Typical trigger | Client calls `close()` first | Both call `close()` nearly simultaneously | Application calls `abort()` or ungraceful error |

## 3.3 Sequence Number Arithmetic Used in Transitions

Although TCP uses a **byte-stream** sequence number space, transitions in the state diagram consume sequence numbers as follows:

$$
\text{After 3-way handshake:} \quad \text{ISN}_C, \text{ISN}_S \text{ are synchronized}
$$

$$
\text{First data byte from client} \rightarrow \text{SEQ} = \text{ISN}_C + 1
$$

$$
\text{Acknowledgment from server} \rightarrow \text{ACK} = \text{ISN}_S + 1
$$

This explains why each `SYN` consumes **one sequence number** even though it carries no payload — it is treated as 1 byte of virtual data. This is also why FIN consumes a sequence number during the 4-way termination.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Examiners often ask *"Does a SYN consume a sequence number?"* The answer is **YES**. This is why `ACK = SYN_seqnum + 1` rather than `ACK = SYN_seqnum`. The same applies to FIN. Forgetting this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in numerical/derivation questions.

## 3.4 Transition Counts and Branching Analysis

For the analytical completeness expected at the KTU 2024 B.Tech level, the state diagram has the following structural properties:

$$
\text{Total States (N)} = 11
$$

$$
\text{Transitions (T)} = \text{approximately } 20 \text{ (per RFC 793 figure 6)}
$$

$$
\text{Entry state} = \text{CLOSED}
$$

$$
\text{Absorbing (terminal) state} = \text{CLOSED}
$$

$$
\text{Steady state} = \text{ESTABLISHED}
$$

The diagram is a **deterministic finite automaton (DFA)** with respect to event sequences: given the current state and the next incoming/outgoing event, the next state is uniquely determined.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full TCP State Transition Diagram (RFC 793, Annotated for KTU 2024)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sCLOSED

    sCLOSED --> sLISTEN : passive open
    sCLOSED --> sSYNSENT : active open / connect / send SYN

    sLISTEN --> sSYNRCVD : receive SYN / send SYN+ACK
    sLISTEN --> sSYNRCVD : send SYN (simultaneous open)
    sLISTEN --> sESTABLISHED : receive SYN+ACK / send ACK (simul open completes)

    sSYNSENT --> sESTABLISHED : receive SYN+ACK / send ACK
    sSYNSENT --> sSYNRCVD : receive SYN / send SYN+ACK (simul open)

    sSYNRCVD --> sESTABLISHED : receive ACK

    sESTABLISHED --> sFINWAIT1 : close / send FIN
    sESTABLISHED --> sCLOSEWAIT : receive FIN / send ACK

    sFINWAIT1 --> sFINWAIT2 : receive ACK
    sFINWAIT1 --> sCLOSING : receive FIN / send ACK
    sFINWAIT1 --> sTIMEWAIT : receive FIN+ACK / send ACK

    sFINWAIT2 --> sTIMEWAIT : receive FIN / send ACK

    sCLOSEWAIT --> sLASTACK : close / send FIN

    sCLOSING --> sTIMEWAIT : receive ACK

    sLASTACK --> sCLOSED : receive ACK

    sTIMEWAIT --> sCLOSED : 2 x MSL timeout

    sCLOSED --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with `s` (e.g., `sFINWAIT1`) — none collide with reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style`.
> - All labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting characters inside double quotes.
> - The diagram renders cleanly in any modern Markdown viewer (GitHub, VS Code, GitLab).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TCP Connection Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer: process A] -->|connect| B[TCP State Machine Engine]
    C[Application Layer: process B] -->|listen| B
    B -->|SYN| NET[Network Layer: IP]
    B -->|SYN+ACK| NET
    B -->|ACK| NET
    B -->|FIN| NET
    NET -->|segments| B
    B -->|states| D[(State Table: 11 states)]
    B -->|timers| E[TIME-WAIT Timer: 2 x MSL]
    B -->|data| A
    B -->|data| C
    E --> B
    D --> B

    classDef ext fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef int fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,C,NET ext
    class B,E int
    class D store
```

## 4.3 Three-Way Handshake Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLIENT
    participant SERVER

    Note over SERVER: State: LISTEN
    CLIENT->>SERVER: SYN (seq=x, SYN=1)
    Note over CLIENT: State: SYN-SENT
    Note over SERVER: State: SYN-RCVD
    SERVER->>CLIENT: SYN+ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1)
    Note over SERVER: State: SYN-RCVD
    CLIENT->>SERVER: ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1)
    Note over CLIENT: State: ESTABLISHED
    Note over SERVER: State: ESTABLISHED
```

## 4.4 Four-Way Termination Sequence Diagram (Normal Close)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLIENT as Active Closer
    participant SERVER as Passive Closer

    Note over CLIENT,SERVER: Both in ESTABLISHED
    CLIENT->>SERVER: FIN (seq=u, FIN=1)
    Note over CLIENT: State: FIN-WAIT-1
    SERVER->>CLIENT: ACK (ack=u+1)
    Note over SERVER: State: CLOSE-WAIT
    Note over CLIENT: State: FIN-WAIT-2
    SERVER->>CLIENT: FIN (seq=v, FIN=1)
    Note over SERVER: State: LAST-ACK
    CLIENT->>SERVER: ACK (ack=v+1)
    Note over CLIENT: State: TIME-WAIT (wait 2 x MSL)
    Note over SERVER: State: CLOSED
    Note over CLIENT: After timeout: CLOSED
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q1. List all the 11 states of a TCP connection and state the purpose of the `TIME-WAIT` state. Why is the wait duration set to `2 × MSL`? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
The 11 TCP states are: **CLOSED, LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RCVD, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, LAST-ACK, CLOSING, and TIME-WAIT**.

The `TIME-WAIT` state is entered by the **active closer** after it has sent the final ACK acknowledging the passive closer's FIN. Its purpose is two-fold:

1. To allow the passive closer to **retransmit its FIN** if the final ACK is lost — without TIME-WAIT, the active closer would have already moved to `CLOSED` and could not re-ACK.
2. To ensure that **old duplicate segments** from the previous connection incarnation (delayed in the network) expire before the same 4-tuple is reused for a new connection.

The duration of `2 × MSL` is derived as follows:

$$
T_{TIME\_WAIT} = \text{MSL} \ (\text{for retransmitted FIN to arrive}) + \text{MSL} \ (\text{for old segments to die}) = 2 \times \text{MSL}
$$

With MSL = 120 s, this yields **240 seconds = 4 minutes**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Differentiate between the *active close* and the *passive close* of a TCP connection. Which side enters `TIME-WAIT`? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Active Close | Passive Close |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Who initiates? | The side that first calls `close()` (typically the client). | The other side that receives the FIN. |
| Sequence of states | `ESTABLISHED → FIN-WAIT-1 → FIN-WAIT-2 → TIME-WAIT → CLOSED` | `ESTABLISHED → CLOSE-WAIT → LAST-ACK → CLOSED` |
| Sends FIN first? | **Yes** | **No** (only after application calls close). |
| Enters TIME-WAIT? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Number of FINs sent | 1 | 1 (in response to remote's FIN). |
| Number of ACKs sent | 1 (to remote's FIN) | 2 (to remote's FIN and to remote's FIN-of-FIN). |

**Only the active closer enters `TIME-WAIT`**, because it is responsible for the final ACK that completes the termination. The passive closer transitions cleanly to `CLOSED` upon receiving this final ACK.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Question

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the **TCP State Transition Diagram**. Cover the 3-way handshake, normal 4-way termination, and simultaneous close paths. List all 11 states.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A TCP server in Kerala is experiencing connection pool exhaustion. You observe thousands of sockets stuck in `FIN-WAIT-2` and `CLOSE-WAIT`. Diagnose the root cause for **each** state and propose two concrete engineering remediations (sysctls, socket options, or architectural changes) for each.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The TCP State Diagram**

The TCP state transition diagram is a finite state machine with **11 states** (CLOSED, LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RCVD, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, CLOSING, LAST-ACK, TIME-WAIT) and the following three primary paths:

1. **Connection establishment (3-way handshake):**
   - Server: `CLOSED → LISTEN` (via `passive open`).
   - Client: `CLOSED → SYN-SENT` (via `connect`, sending SYN).
   - Server: `LISTEN → SYN-RCVD` (on receiving SYN, sends SYN+ACK).
   - Client: `SYN-SENT → ESTABLISHED` (on receiving SYN+ACK, sends ACK).
   - Server: `SYN-RCVD → ESTABLISHED` (on receiving ACK).

2. **Normal 4-way termination (active close from client):**
   - Client sends FIN → `ESTABLISHED → FIN-WAIT-1`.
   - Server ACKs → client enters `FIN-WAIT-2`; server enters `CLOSE-WAIT`.
   - Server sends FIN → `CLOSE-WAIT → LAST-ACK`.
   - Client ACKs → `FIN-WAIT-2 → TIME-WAIT`; server `LAST-ACK → CLOSED`.
   - After 2 × MSL timeout, client `TIME-WAIT → CLOSED`.

3. **Simultaneous close (both sides call close at nearly the same time):**
   - Both transition `ESTABLISHED → FIN-WAIT-1` after exchanging crossed FINs.
   - On receiving the other side's FIN without prior ACK, both go to `CLOSING`.
   - On receiving ACK of their own FIN, both go to `TIME-WAIT → CLOSED`.

**Part (b) — Diagnosis and Remediation**

**FIN-WAIT-2 exhaustion** indicates the **local application** has called `close()` and sent its FIN, but the **remote peer has not yet sent its FIN** (or has crashed). The local kernel keeps the socket in FIN-WAIT-2 indefinitely, holding resources.
- *Remediation 1:* Set the Linux sysctl `net.ipv4.tcp_fin_timeout` to a finite value (default 60 s) so the kernel eventually drops the half-closed socket.
- *Remediation 2:* Enable TCP keepalive (`net.ipv4.tcp_keepalive_time`, `_intvl`, `_probes`) so the kernel probes an unresponsive peer and tears down the socket if the peer is dead.

**CLOSE-WAIT exhaustion** indicates the **remote peer has sent its FIN** (signaling it has no more data), but the **local application has not called `close()`** on the socket. The kernel is faithfully waiting for the app to finish reading buffered data and close.
- *Remediation 1:* Audit application code for missing `close()` calls, especially in **exception paths and HTTP keep-alive handlers** that forget to release sockets on slow clients.
- *Remediation 2:* Use **application-layer timeouts** (e.g., HTTP request-read deadline) and refactor to **RAII-style socket management** (e.g., Python `with` statements, Java try-with-resources) so `close()` is guaranteed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** With reference to the TCP state diagram, explain the **role of the SYN-RCVD** state. How is it exploited in a **SYN flood attack**, and what are two well-known mitigations?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A TCP connection has the following timeline (timestamps in seconds):
- t = 0.0: Client sends SYN (seq = 1000).
- t = 0.05: Server sends SYN+ACK (seq = 5000, ack = 1001).
- t = 0.10: Client sends ACK (seq = 1001, ack = 5001).
- t = 5.00: Client sends FIN (seq = 1001, ack = 5001).
- t = 5.05: Server sends ACK (ack = 1002).
- t = 5.10: Server sends FIN (seq = 5001, ack = 1002).
- t = 5.15: Client sends ACK (seq = 1002, ack = 5002).
- t = 5.15 onward: Client waits in TIME-WAIT.
- t = 9.15: Client transitions to CLOSED.

Compute: (i) the RTT measured by the client, (ii) the total connection lifetime, (iii) verify whether the TIME-WAIT value is consistent with the default MSL, and (iv) state which side is the active closer.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — SYN-RCVD Role and SYN Flood Defense**

The `SYN-RCVD` state is entered by the **server** immediately after it has received a SYN from a client and sent back a SYN+ACK. The server is now waiting for the client's ACK to complete the 3-way handshake and move to `ESTABLISHED`. The server pre-allocates a **Transmission Control Block (TCB)** — a small data structure holding the connection state — as soon as it sends the SYN+ACK. This pre-allocation is intentional: it makes connection setup fast on the happy path.

However, this design is exploited in a **SYN flood attack (a type of DoS)**: the attacker sends thousands of SYNs with **spoofed source IP addresses**. The victim server dutifully creates a TCB for each, sends a SYN+ACK to a non-existent IP, and waits. Since the spoofed client never sends the final ACK, the TCBs accumulate in `SYN-RCVD`, exhausting the server's backlog queue and starving legitimate clients.

**Mitigations:**
- **SYN cookies:** The server does *not* pre-allocate a TCB on receiving the SYN. Instead, it encodes the connection state (client IP, port, sequence number) into the **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** of its SYN+ACK using a cryptographic hash. Only when the legitimate client's ACK arrives does the server reconstruct the TCB. Spoofed SYNs that never complete the handshake consume no memory.
- **Reducing `tcp_max_syn_backlog` and rate-limiting SYNs per source IP** (e.g., using `iptables` `-m connlimit` or `nftables` quotas), combined with **SYN proxy / firewall offloading** at the edge (Cloudflare, AWS Shield, etc.).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**(i) RTT measured by the client.**
The client sends SYN at t = 0.0 and receives the SYN+ACK at t = 0.05 s. (The server's SYN+ACK is the response to the client's SYN.) Therefore:

$$
\text{RTT} = 0.05 \text{ s} - 0.0 \text{ s} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Total connection lifetime (from SYN to CLOSED on the active side).**
The connection is logically open from t = 0.0 (SYN sent) and the active closer (client) reaches CLOSED at t = 9.15 s. Therefore:

$$
T_{lifetime} = 9.15 \text{ s} - 0.0 \text{ s} = 9.15 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(iii) Verify TIME-WAIT value against default MSL.**
The client entered TIME-WAIT at t = 5.15 s and transitioned to CLOSED at t = 9.15 s. The duration is:

$$
T_{TW} = 9.15 \text{ s} - 5.15 \text{ s} = 4.00 \text{ seconds}
$$

The default value should be:

$$
T_{TW,\ default} = 2 \times \text{MSL} = 2 \times 120 \text{ s} = 240 \text{ s}
$$

**The 4-second TIME-WAIT is INCONSISTENT with the default 240-second value.** The system administrator has likely configured a custom `tcp_fin_timeout` (e.g., `net.ipv4.tcp_fin_timeout = 4`) or the TIME_WAIT linger parameter on Windows. This is acceptable but reduces the safety margin for absorbing lost final ACKs and old duplicate segments.

**(iv) Active closer.**
The active closer is the side that **initiated the FIN exchange**. The timeline shows the **client** sent the first FIN at t = 5.00 s, and the server sent its FIN in response at t = 5.10 s. Therefore, the **client is the active closer**, and the server is the passive closer. This is consistent with the client entering TIME-WAIT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting that SYN and FIN consume sequence numbers** — costs 1 mark on numerical questions. The client's first data byte is `seq = 1002` (not 1001) because SYN consumed seq 1000 and FIN consumed seq 1001.
> 2. **Confusing FIN-WAIT-2 (local app has closed) with CLOSE-WAIT (remote has closed)** — these are mirror states; examiners love swapping them. Remember: **FIN-WAIT-* is the active closer; CLOSE-WAIT is the passive closer**.
> 3. **Saying "TIME-WAIT is 2 minutes"** — the correct phrasing is "TIME-WAIT is **2 × MSL**, which by default equals **240 seconds = 4 minutes**". Mixing up MSL and TIME-WAIT is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **In simultaneous close, students often forget the `CLOSING` state** — it is the brief intermediate state where both sides have sent FINs but neither has yet received the ACK of its own FIN.
> 5. **Drawing the diagram without arrows or with arrows missing labels** — every transition in the KTU answer must have a *label* (the triggering event) and a *direction* (the state change). Unlabelled arrows lose 1–2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ The TCP FSM has **exactly 11 states** — name them in order along the happy path: **C → L/S → S/R → E → F1 → F2 → T → C** (server: **C → L → SR → E → CW → LA → C**).
- ✅ **3-way handshake** is `SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK`; **4-way termination** is `FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK`.
- ✅ **SYN, FIN, and RST each consume one sequence number**; pure ACKs do not.
- ✅ **TIME-WAIT duration = 2 × MSL = 240 s (default)** — defends against lost final ACKs and old duplicate segments.
- ✅ **Active closer enters TIME-WAIT**; passive closer does not.
- ✅ **FIN-WAIT-2** = local app has closed, waiting for remote FIN.
- ✅ **CLOSE-WAIT** = remote has closed, local app has not yet closed.
- ✅ **CLOSING** = simultaneous close, both FINs crossed.
- ✅ **SYN-RCVD** is the state exploited in **SYN flood attacks**; mitigated by **SYN cookies** and **rate-limiting**.
- ✅ **RST (Reset)** = abrupt, ungraceful teardown; bypasses TIME-WAIT entirely.
- ✅ Every transition in your diagram must have a **labelled arrow** showing the triggering event (send/receive SYN, FIN, ACK, RST, or timeout).
- ✅ Default MSL is **120 seconds** (RFC 793); modern OSes often default to **60 s** for `tcp_fin_timeout` but the *TIME-WAIT* is still 2 × MSL.
- ✅ The TCP FSM is a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** — given the current state and event, the next state is uniquely determined.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Windows in TCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Windows in TCP # 🪟 TCP WINDOWS — Flow Control & The Sliding Window Mechanism > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)** > A **TCP Window** is a buffer-based flow control mechanism that governs the amount of data (in bytes) a sender can transmit before receiving an acknowledgment from the receiver. The window size is advertised by the receiver in every TCP segment through the 16-bit **Window Size field** in the TCP header, allowing the sender to dynamically regulate its transmission rate to prevent overwhelming the receiver's buffer. ## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Moving Mail Tray 📨 Imagine a **post office sorting tray** at a delivery office: - The tray has a **fixed capacity** (say 50 letters). - The postal worker (the **Sender/Producer**) keeps dropping letters into the tray. - The mail sorter (the **Receiver/Consumer**) picks letters from the other end and delivers them. - The tray can never overflow — when it gets full, the worker must **stop and wait**. - As the sorter removes letters, the worker can add more. The occupied portion of the tray **slides forward**. > 🎯 The TCP window works **exactly** this way! The "tray" is the receiver's buffer, and the "slide" represents new acknowledgments opening up fresh buffer space. ### 1.2 Why Do We Need a Window? TCP is a **reliable, byte-stream, full-duplex protocol**. Without a window: - Every byte would need an ACK (stop-and-wait) — **massively inefficient**. - A faster sender would **flood** a slower receiver, causing buffer overflow & data loss. > [!IMPORTANT] > The TCP window solves **two critical problems simultaneously**: > 1. **Flow Control** — Protects the *receiver* from buffer overflow. > 2. **Efficiency** — Allows *pipelining* of multiple segments before waiting for ACKs (throughput boost). ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in TCP Windowing | Metric | Standard Value | Description | |---|---|---| | **Window Size Field** | **16 bits** | Header field (max = 65,535 bytes) | | **Window Scale Option** | Up to **14 bits shift** | RFC 1323 extension (max ≈ 1 GB) | | **Typical Receiver Buffer** | **64 KB – 1 MB** | Modern OS default | | **TCP Header Offset** | **20 bytes** (no options) | Standard minimum | --- # ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Sliding Window Engine ## 2.1 Anatomy of the TCP Window TCP maintains **two windows** independently in each direction of a full-duplex connection: ### 🔹 A. The Send Window (Sender Side) The sender's logical boundary of unacknowledged data. Three sub-regions: 1. **Bytes Sent & Acknowledged** — (Left of window — safely transmitted) 2. **Bytes Sent but NOT Yet Acknowledged** — (Inside the window — in-flight) 3. **Bytes NOT Yet Sent (but allowed by window)** — (Right edge — sendable up to window limit) ### 🔹 B. The Receive Window (Receiver Side) Three sub-regions: 1. **Bytes Received & Acknowledged** 2. **Bytes Received but NOT in order (buffered)** 3. **Bytes NOT yet received (buffer space available)** ## 2.2 The Three Pointers of the Send Window Let us define three critical sequence number pointers: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Send\_Base} &= \text{oldest unacknowledged byte's sequence number} \\ \text{NextSeqNum} &= \text{smallest unused sequence number (next byte to send)} \\ \text{LastByteAcked} &= \text{Send\_Base} - 1 \end{aligned} $$ > **Invariant Rule (KTU High-Yield):** At any instant, the sender must satisfy: > > $$\text{NextSeqNum} - \text{Send\_Base} \le \text{AdvertisedWindow (rwnd)}$$ > > This means the number of **unacknowledged, in-flight bytes** can never exceed the receiver's advertised window. ## 2.3 How the Window Slides — Operational Steps | Step | Action | Effect on Window | |------|--------|------------------| | 1 | Sender transmits data within the window | Right edge moves right; left edge stationary | | 2 | Receiver gets data, places in buffer | Buffer fills; `rwnd` decreases | | 3 | Receiver sends ACK with new `rwnd = Buffer - Occupied` | Advertised window updated | | 4 | Sender receives ACK, advances `Send_Base` | **Left edge moves right** → **SLIDE!** | | 5 | Sender can now transmit new data | Right edge moves further right | | 6 | Receiver app reads data from buffer | Buffer space freed; `rwnd` increases | > [!TIP] > **Visualize**: The window only "slides" (left edge moves) when an ACK arrives. Without an ACK, the right edge grows but the window doesn't actually move. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | KTU Exam Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{NextSeqNum} - \text{Send\_Base} \le \text{rwnd}$ | Sender cannot exceed advertised window | ⭐⭐⭐ Core constraint | | 2 | $\text{EffectiveWindow} = \min(\text{rwnd}, \text{cwnd})$ | Final sender limit (combined Flow + Congestion) | ⭐⭐⭐ Critical | | 3 | $\text{MaxWindow} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535 \text{ bytes}$ | Default w/o window scaling | ⭐⭐ | | 4 | $\text{ScaledWindow} = \text{rwnd} \times 2^{\text{ScaleFactor}}$ | With RFC 1323 option (max ≈ 1 GB) | ⭐⭐ | | 5 | $\text{Throughput} \le \frac{\text{WindowSize}}{\text{RTT}}$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product limit | ⭐⭐⭐ Famous KTU problem | | 6 | $\text{rwnd} = \text{BufferSize} - \text{LastByteRcvd} + \text{LastByteRead}$ | Receiver buffer availability | ⭐⭐⭐ | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** Many students confuse **rwnd** (receiver's advertised window — flow control) with **cwnd** (congestion window — congestion control). They are *different* and the sender's true limit is the **MINIMUM** of both! ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The sliding window concept underlies **every reliable transport protocol**: - 🛰️ **Satellite communications** — large BDP needs large windows - 📡 **Data centers (DCTCP)** — modified window for low latency - 📱 **Mobile networks (TCP Westwood, TCP BBR)** — adaptive window - 🌐 **HTTP/2 & HTTP/3** — inherit windowing at transport layer > **Real Engineering Insight:** The **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** dictates the *minimum* window size for a link to be fully utilized. If the window is smaller than BDP, the pipe stays empty and throughput suffers — a classic KTU numerical problem! --- # 🔢 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Numerical Example — Window Slide Through a Connection > **Problem Setup (KTU Numerical Type):** > A TCP connection has: > - Initial Sequence Number (ISN) of Sender = **1000** > - Receiver Buffer Size = **4000 bytes** > - Sender transmits **3 segments of 1000 bytes** each at t=0, t=1, t=2 sec > - RTT = **3 seconds** > - ACKs are cumulative **Walk-through (Exhaustive — every step shown):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: } & \text{At t = 0, sender transmits 1000 bytes. SEQ = 1000, DATA = 1000 bytes.} \\ & \text{NextSeqNum: } 1000 \rightarrow 2000 \\ & \text{Send\_Base} = 1000 \\ & \text{Bytes in-flight} = 2000 - 1000 = 1000 \le 4000 \text{ (rwnd)} \quad \checkmark \\[6pt] \text{Step 2: } & \text{At t = 1, sender transmits next 1000 bytes. SEQ = 2000, DATA = 1000 bytes.} \\ & \text{NextSeqNum: } 2000 \rightarrow 3000 \\ & \text{Bytes in-flight} = 3000 - 1000 = 2000 \le 4000 \quad \checkmark \\[6pt] \text{Step 3: } & \text{At t = 2, sender transmits next 1000 bytes. SEQ = 3000, DATA = 1000 bytes.} \\ & \text{NextSeqNum: } 3000 \rightarrow 4000 \\ & \text{Bytes in-flight} = 4000 - 1000 = 3000 \le 4000 \quad \checkmark \\[6pt] \text{Step 4: } & \text{At t = 3, ACK arrives at sender. ACK = 4000 (cumulative).} \\ & \text{Send\_Base advances: } 1000 \rightarrow 4000 \\ & \text{Window slides left by 3000 bytes!} \\ & \text{Bytes in-flight} = 4000 - 4000 = 0 \\[6pt] \text{Step 5: } & \text{Sender can now transmit up to rwnd = 4000 more bytes.} \end{aligned} $$ > ✅ **Verification of Invariant:** At every step, `NextSeqNum − Send_Base ≤ rwnd (4000)`. ## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Window Size for Full Link Utilization > **KTU Classic Problem:** Given a link with Bandwidth `B` (bits/sec) and Round Trip Time `RTT` (seconds), what is the **minimum** TCP window size `W_min` needed to fully utilize the link? **Derivation (line by line):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } & W = \text{window size (bytes)}, \quad B = \text{bandwidth (bits/s)}, \quad RTT = \text{round-trip time (s)} \\[4pt] \text{Time to transmit one full window} &= \frac{W \times 8}{B} \text{ seconds} \\[4pt] \text{For the link to be continuously busy,} & \text{ the next ACK must arrive before the window empties:} \\[4pt] \frac{W \times 8}{B} &\ge RTT \\[4pt] W &\ge \frac{B \times RTT}{8} \\[4pt] \therefore W_{\min} &= \frac{B \times RTT}{8} \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ ### 📌 Worked Numerical Example: Suppose $B = 1$ Gbps and $RTT = 100$ ms: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\min} &= \frac{10^9 \text{ bits/s} \times 0.1 \text{ s}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{10^8}{8} \\[4pt] &= 12{,}500{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\[4pt] &= 12.5 \text{ MB} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is **12.5 MB**, but the default TCP window maxes at **64 KB** → the link is severely underutilized! This is why **Window Scaling (RFC 1323)** is mandatory in high-speed networks. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Window Behaviors | Receiver State | Receiver Action | Advertised `rwnd` | Sender Behavior | |---|---|---|---| | Buffer has space | Send normal ACK | `rwnd = Buffer - Occupied` | Continue sending up to `rwnd` | | Buffer full | Send ACK with `rwnd = 0` | **0** | **Stop sending**, persist timer starts | | App reads data later | Send Window Update ACK | `rwnd > 0` | Sender resumes (window probe may be sent) | | App reads partial | ACK with non-zero rwnd | `rwnd` increases by amount read | Sender transmits within new limit | > [!WARNING] > **Zero Window Pitfall (KTU Favored Question!):** If receiver advertises `rwnd = 0`, sender **MUST stop**. It then sends a **1-byte Window Probe** segment periodically to check if the window has opened. This prevents **deadlock** when the window-update ACK is lost. --- # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — TCP Sliding Window State Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S0([START: 3-way Handshake Complete]) --> S1[Window Initialized
Send_Base = ISN
rwnd received from receiver] S1 --> S2[Transmit bytes up to
NextSeqNum less than Send_Base plus rwnd] S2 --> S3{ACK Received?} S3 -- Yes --> S4[Advance Send_Base
Window Slides Left
Update NextSeqNum] S3 -- No --> S5{Timeout?} S4 --> S6{Bytes available
to send?} S5 -- Yes --> S7[Retransmit
Go-Back-N or Fast Retransmit] S5 -- No --> S2 S6 -- Yes --> S2 S6 -- No --> S8[Idle - wait for app data] S8 --> S2 S7 --> S2 S2 --> S9{rwnd = 0?} S9 -- Yes --> S10[STOP sending data
Start Persist Timer] S10 --> S11[Send 1-byte Window Probe] S11 --> S12{Window Update ACK?} S12 -- Yes --> S4 S12 -- No --> S10 S9 -- No --> S2 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TCP Window Engine ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Application Layer
Producer] --> B[TCP Send Buffer] B --> C[Window Controller
Decides bytes to send] C --> D[Network Layer
IP] D --> E[Receiver TCP Buffer] E --> F[Application Layer
Consumer] F --> G[Buffer Occupancy Tracker] G --> H[Window Calculator
rwnd = Buffer minus Occupied] H -- ACK with new rwnd --> C C -- Transmitted data --> E ``` ## 4.3 Window Slide Visualization (Textual Diagram) ``` At t = 0 (After 1st transmission of 1000 bytes, ACK pending): [Sent+ACKed] | [Sent, UnACKed: 1000B] | [Sendable up to rwnd: 3000B] | [Blocked] ──────────╪════════════════════════╪═══════════════════════════════╪────────── 0 1000 4000 7000 ... At t = 3 (After ACK of 3000 bytes arrives): [Sent+ACKed: 4000B] | [Sendable: 4000B] | [Blocked] ═══════════════════╪═══════════════════╪────────── 0 4000 8000 ... ◄──── WINDOW HAS SLID FORWARD BY 3000 BYTES! ────► ``` --- # 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the TCP sliding window mechanism. What is the role of the advertised window size?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **TCP sliding window** is a flow control mechanism that allows the sender to transmit multiple segments before receiving an acknowledgment, thereby pipelining data transmission and improving network efficiency. **(1 mark)** The **advertised window size (rwnd)**, carried in the 16-bit Window field of the TCP header, indicates the amount of data (in bytes) the receiver is currently willing to accept. **(1 mark)** The sender is constrained to keep the number of unacknowledged bytes ≤ rwnd. As the receiver processes data, it slides its window forward by sending ACKs with updated rwnd values, enabling continuous flow control. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What happens when the receiver advertises a zero window? How is the deadlock avoided?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** When the receiver's buffer is full, it advertises **rwnd = 0** in the ACK, instructing the sender to **stop transmitting** any further data. **(1 mark)** However, this can lead to a **deadlock** if the subsequent window-update ACK (advertising non-zero rwnd) is lost — the sender will wait forever. **(1 mark)** To avoid this, the sender employs a **Persist Timer**. When the timer expires, the sender transmits a **1-byte segment** called a **Window Probe** to query the receiver. If the receiver responds with a non-zero rwnd, normal transmission resumes. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of the **TCP sliding window protocol**. Discuss how the window slides and the role of acknowledgments. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A TCP connection has a **bandwidth of 2 Mbps** and an **RTT of 150 ms**. The receiver buffer is **32 KB**. (i) Calculate the **Bandwidth-Delay Product**. **(2 marks)** (ii) Determine the **minimum window size** required to fully utilize the link. **(2 marks)** (iii) Will the default 16-bit window size be sufficient? Justify. **(1 mark)** (iv) Suggest a remedy. **(1 mark)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A ### Part (a) — 7 Marks The **TCP sliding window** operates as follows: 1. **Establishment**: After the 3-way handshake, both sender and receiver know the Initial Sequence Number (ISN) and the receiver's buffer size (initial `rwnd`). **(1 mark)** 2. **Send within window**: The sender can transmit bytes such that `(NextSeqNum − Send_Base) ≤ rwnd`. This is the **flow control invariant**. **(1 mark)** 3. **Data transmission**: The sender transmits segments, advancing `NextSeqNum` but `Send_Base` remains unchanged until ACK arrives. **(1 mark)** 4. **Receiver buffering**: The receiver places out-of-order data in its buffer and sends **cumulative ACKs** indicating the next expected sequence number and its current available buffer space (rwnd). **(1 mark)** 5. **Window slide**: When ACK is received, `Send_Base` advances to the new ACK value. The window **slides forward** to the right, opening new sendable space. **(1 mark)** 6. **Continuous pipelining**: This allows multiple unacknowledged segments to be in flight, maximizing throughput. **(1 mark)** 7. **Edge cases**: If rwnd = 0, sender stops and uses **Persist Timer + Window Probe** to detect when the window reopens. **(1 mark)** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram for visualization.)* ### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given**: $B = 2$ Mbps, $RTT = 150$ ms, Buffer = 32 KB **(i) Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $$ \begin{aligned} BDP &= B \times RTT \\ &= 2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} \times 0.150 \text{ s} \\ &= 300{,}000 \text{ bits} \\ &= \frac{300{,}000}{8} = 37{,}500 \text{ bytes} = 36.62 \text{ KB} \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks — formula + substitution + answer)** **(ii) Minimum Window Size:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\min} &= \frac{B \times RTT}{8} = BDP \\ &= 37{,}500 \text{ bytes} = 36.62 \text{ KB} \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks — direct application of derived formula)** **(iii) Is default 16-bit window sufficient?** $$ \text{Max default window} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535 \text{ bytes} \approx 64 \text{ KB} $$ Since $W_{\min} = 37.5$ KB $< 64$ KB, **YES**, the default window IS sufficient. **(1 mark)** **(iv) Remedy for higher bandwidths:** For higher bandwidths or longer RTTs where BDP > 64 KB, use the **Window Scale option (RFC 1323)** to extend the window up to ~1 GB. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **(a)** Differentiate between **Flow Control** and **Congestion Control** in TCP. Explain how the **receive window (rwnd)** and **congestion window (cwnd)** interact. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A sender transmits data over a TCP connection. The initial `Send_Base = 2001` and `rwnd = 3000` bytes. The sender transmits **3 segments of 1000 bytes** each (SEQ 2001, 3001, 4001). All three are received correctly. Show the **sequence number, acknowledgment number, and window state** at each step. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B ### Part (a) — 7 Marks | Aspect | Flow Control | Congestion Control | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Protects receiver buffer | Protects network from overload | | **Controlled by** | **Receiver** (advertises rwnd) | **Sender** (estimates cwnd) | | **Header field** | TCP Window field (16 bits) | None (sender-internal state) | | **Trigger** | Receiver buffer availability | Packet loss / RTT variations | | **Algorithm** | Sliding window | Slow Start, AIMD, Fast Retransmit/Recovery | **(3 marks for the table comparison)** **Interaction between rwnd and cwnd:** The effective limit on the sender's outstanding data is: $$ \text{EffectiveWindow} = \min(\text{rwnd}, \text{cwnd}) $$ **(1 mark for formula)** - **`rwnd`** ensures the **receiver** is not overwhelmed. - **`cwnd`** ensures the **network** is not overwhelmed. - The sender's actual transmission limit is the **smaller** of the two — both constraints must be satisfied simultaneously. **(1 mark)** - During **slow start**, cwnd grows exponentially from 1 MSS. - On **packet loss**, cwnd is reduced (multiplicative decrease). - **`rwnd`** remains static unless the receiver explicitly changes its buffer allocation. **(2 marks)** ### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Initial State:** - Send_Base = 2001 - NextSeqNum = 2001 - rwnd = 3000 - Bytes in-flight = 0 **Step 1: Send Segment 1 (SEQ=2001, 1000 bytes)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SEQ} &= 2001, \quad \text{DATA} = 1000 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{NextSeqNum} &= 2001 + 1000 = 3001 \\ \text{Bytes in-flight} &= 3001 - 2001 = 1000 \le 3000 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 2: Send Segment 2 (SEQ=3001, 1000 bytes)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SEQ} &= 3001, \quad \text{DATA} = 1000 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{NextSeqNum} &= 3001 + 1000 = 4001 \\ \text{Bytes in-flight} &= 4001 - 2001 = 2000 \le 3000 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 3: Send Segment 3 (SEQ=4001, 1000 bytes)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SEQ} &= 4001, \quad \text{DATA} = 1000 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{NextSeqNum} &= 4001 + 1000 = 5001 \\ \text{Bytes in-flight} &= 5001 - 2001 = 3000 \le 3000 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **(1 mark)** **Step 4: Receiver sends cumulative ACK** - ACK number = 5001 (next expected byte) - Window update: rwnd still = 3000 **(1 mark)** **Step 5: Sender's window state after ACK** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Send\_Base advances to } & 5001 \\ \text{Bytes in-flight} &= 5001 - 5001 = 0 \\ \text{Window has slid from } &[2001, 5001] \text{ to } [5001, 8001] \\ \text{New sendable space} &= 3000 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **(2 marks)** **Final Summary Table:** | Step | Action | SEQ | NextSeqNum | Send_Base | In-flight | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | Initial | — | 2001 | 2001 | 0 | | 1 | Send 1 KB | 2001 | 3001 | 2001 | 1000 | | 2 | Send 1 KB | 3001 | 4001 | 2001 | 2000 | | 3 | Send 1 KB | 4001 | 5001 | 2001 | 3000 | | 4 | ACK arrives | — | 5001 | 5001 | 0 | **(1 mark for final summary table)** --- > [!WARNING] > **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Confusing rwnd vs cwnd**: Many students answer "rwnd" when the question is about congestion. Always clarify which is being asked. > 2. **Forgetting cumulative nature of ACKs**: ACKs in TCP are **cumulative** (not selective), so an ACK of `N` means "all bytes up to `N-1` received." > 3. **Off-by-one errors**: Window is `[Send_Base, Send_Base + rwnd − 1]` — students often wrongly write `Send_Base + rwnd`. > 4. **Ignoring zero window deadlock scenario**: When asked about "edge cases," always mention **Persist Timer** and **Window Probe**. > 5. **Units in BDP formula**: Mixing bits vs bytes in bandwidth-delay product is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU papers. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Windows in TCP — Top 12 Things You MUST Remember for KTU:** - ✅ The **TCP window** is a **flow control** mechanism preventing receiver buffer overflow. - ✅ Window size field is **16 bits** in TCP header → max **65,535 bytes** by default. - ✅ **Window Scaling (RFC 1323)** can extend it up to ~**1 GB** with a 14-bit shift factor. - ✅ Sender constraint: `NextSeqNum − Send_Base ≤ rwnd` (the **golden invariant**). - ✅ Effective limit = **`min(rwnd, cwnd)`** — rwnd for flow control, cwnd for congestion control. - ✅ **rwnd = BufferSize − LastByteRcvd + LastByteRead** (receiver's formula). - ✅ **Minimum window for full link utilization**: $W_{\min} = (B \times RTT) / 8$ bytes. - ✅ **Zero window** triggers sender's **Persist Timer** → sends **1-byte Window Probe**. - ✅ ACKs are **cumulative**, advancing `Send_Base` and **sliding the window forward**. - ✅ The window **slides** (left edge moves) **only** when an ACK arrives. - ✅ **Silly Window Syndrome** can occur with small window updates; avoided by **Clark's solution** and **Nagle's algorithm**. - ✅ In KTU numericals, always **convert bandwidth to bits/sec** and use **seconds** for RTT before dividing by 8 for bytes. > 🎯 **Quick Memory Hook (For Last-Minute Revision):** > *"WINDOW = POSTAL TRAY. Fills up, stops, ACK arrives, slides forward, refills."* 📨 ---

topics.title: TCP Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** TCP Timers # Module 4 — TCP Timers: Foundational Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **TCP Timers** are a critical set of kernel-maintained countdown variables used by the Transmission Control Protocol to govern the reliability, flow control, and orderly termination of a connection. They are managed on a **per-connection** basis inside the Transmission Control Block (TCB) and are essential for implementing ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest) strategies, congestion control, and zombie-connection cleanup, as defined in **RFC 6298** and **RFC 1122**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, TCP timers are treated as the operational heart of the protocol — the mechanisms that translate a *stateless best-effort* network (IP) into a *stateful, reliable byte stream* (TCP). The four classical timers discussed in the syllabus are: | # | Timer Name | Primary Purpose | |---|------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Retransmission Timer** | Detects packet loss & triggers retransmission | | 2 | **Persistence Timer** | Breaks deadlocks caused by zero window advertisements | | 3 | **Keep-Alive Timer** | Verifies that an idle peer is still reachable | | 4 | **Time-Wait (2MSL) Timer** | Ensures graceful connection termination | ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Postal Registered Mail System:** > Imagine you send a *registered parcel* through a courier, and the courier gives you a **delivery receipt deadline**. If the recipient does not confirm receipt before the deadline, the courier automatically resends the parcel. Similarly, if the recipient sends back a "my mailbox is full, send later" slip, the courier waits a little and *prods* the recipient again. If the recipient's house appears abandoned, the courier periodically *pings* to see if anyone is still there. Finally, after the last delivery, the courier waits a fixed time to ensure the recipient can still complain before closing the case. The four TCP timers map perfectly onto this analogy: - **Retransmission Timer** → the delivery deadline (loss detection). - **Persistence Timer** → the periodic "send later?" probe (zero window deadlock). - **Keep-Alive Timer** → the periodic "anyone home?" ping (idle peer check). - **Time-Wait Timer** → the closing grace period (2MSL cleanup). ## 1.3 Why TCP *Needs* Timers TCP provides a **connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream** service over an unreliable **connectionless** IP layer. Because IP packets can be: - Lost - Duplicated - Reordered - Corrupted (discarded by checksum) TCP must use **time-based triggers** to recover from these failures. The IP layer gives *no feedback* on lost packets, so the only way to know a packet was lost is by **running out the clock** — hence timers are non-negotiable. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Boundary Value:** The recommended default value of **MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime)** is **2 minutes (120 seconds)** as per RFC 793, though most modern Linux/Windows implementations use **60 seconds** for the 2MSL timer, giving a **TIME_WAIT duration of 60s**. # Module 4 — Deep Theoretical Analysis: TCP Timers ## 2.1 The Retransmission Timer (The Loss Detector) ### 2.1.1 Concept The retransmission timer fires when an **unacknowledged segment** has been outstanding for too long. When it expires, TCP retransmits the oldest unACKed segment and **doubles the RTO** (exponential backoff) to avoid network congestion collapse. ### 2.1.2 Round Trip Time (RTT) Measurement TCP continuously measures the time between sending a segment (with a data byte of sequence number `n`) and receiving the ACK for that byte. This gives an **RTT sample** `R'`. ### 2.1.3 Jacobson's Algorithm (1988) — The Heart of RTT Estimation TCP uses **two** smoothed statistical estimators: 1. **Smoothed RTT (SRTT)** — a long-term moving average. 2. **RTT Variance (RTTVAR)** — the smoothed mean deviation. The standard **constants** (per RFC 6298): - **α = 1/8 = 0.125** (gain for SRTT) - **β = 1/4 = 0.25** (gain for RTTVAR) - **K = 4** (multiplier for safety margin) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet** — Memorize these equations: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ERR} &= R' - \text{SRTT} \\ \text{RTTVAR} &\leftarrow (1 - \beta)\cdot \text{RTTVAR} + \beta \cdot \vert \text{ERR} \vert \\ \text{SRTT} &\leftarrow (1 - \alpha)\cdot \text{SRTT} + \alpha \cdot R' \\ \text{RTO} &= \text{SRTT} + \max\bigl(G, K \cdot \text{RTTVAR}\bigr) \end{aligned} $$ where **G = clock granularity** (typically **100 ms** or **1 ms** on modern systems). **Boundary Conditions (per RFC 6298):** - On the **first measurement**, set `SRTT = R'`, `RTTVAR = R'/2`, and `RTO = SRTT + max(G, K*RTTVAR)`. - **Minimum RTO = 1 second** (to avoid spurious retransmissions). - **Maximum RTO = 60 seconds** (cap). ### 2.1.4 Karn's Algorithm — Avoiding Retransmission Ambiguity > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** When a segment is retransmitted, the ACK that comes back is **ambiguous** — it could be acknowledging the *original* transmission OR the *retransmission*. We don't know which RTT to attribute the ACK to. **Karn's Solution (1987):** 1. **Do not** update SRTT/RTTVAR using any RTT sample that involves a retransmitted segment. 2. **Exponentially back off** the RTO for each consecutive retransmission: `RTO = 2 × RTO`. 3. When a fresh (non-retransmitted) ACK is finally obtained, **reset** RTO using the next valid RTT sample. > This rule is *mandatory* in KTU derivations.
