<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Flow Specification (FlowSpec)

A complete **Flow Specification** is a richer structure than the mere 5-tuple. It encapsulates **both the classifier** (what packets belong to the flow) **and the QoS requirements** (what treatment they demand). RSVP and QoS routing protocols typically use this layered spec.

#### 2.1.1 RSPEC — Reservation Specification
The **RSPEC** defines the **QoS parameters** the flow requires:

$$R_{SPEC} = \{ R_{bandwidth}, R_{burst}, R_{delay}, R_{jitter}, R_{loss} \}$$

Where:
- $R_{bandwidth}$ → **minimum guaranteed bit rate** (bits/sec)
- $R_{burst}$ → **maximum burst tolerance** (bytes)
- $R_{delay}$ → **end-to-end delay budget** (milliseconds)
- $R_{jitter}$ → **delay variation tolerance** (ms)
- $R_{loss}$ → **maximum acceptable loss probability** ($10^{-n}$)

#### 2.1.2 TSPEC — Traffic Specification
The **TSPEC** describes the **traffic profile** the source will actually generate, often modelled as a **token bucket**:

$$T_{SPEC} = \{ r, b, p, m, M \}$$

Where:
- $r$ → **token bucket rate** (bytes/sec)
- $b$ → **bucket depth** (bytes)
- $p$ → **peak rate** (bytes/sec)
- $m$ → **minimum policed unit** (bytes)
- $M$ → **maximum packet size** (bytes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship $b \geq M$ must always hold, otherwise the bucket cannot admit even a single maximum-sized packet.

#### 2.1.3 The Token-Bucket Flow Model

A flow conformant to TSPEC must obey:

$$A(t+\tau) - A(t) \leq r\tau + b \quad \forall t, \tau > 0$$

where $A(t)$ is the **cumulative bits transmitted** up to time $t$. This is the **leaky-bucket conformance test** — packets arriving faster than $r$ are buffered up to depth $b$; beyond that, they are dropped or marked.

### 2.2 Forwarding Paradigms: Destination-Based vs Flow-Based

Traditional IP routing is **destination-based**: packets are forwarded solely on the **longest-prefix match** of the destination IP. In contrast, **flow-based forwarding** uses richer keys:

| Property | Destination-Based Forwarding | Flow-Based Forwarding |
|---|---|---|
| **Lookup Key** | Destination IP prefix (CIDR) | 5-tuple / 7-tuple / arbitrary headers |
| **State** | Stateless (per-router forwarding table) | Stateful (flow table with per-flow entries) |
| **Granularity** | Per-prefix | Per-flow |
| **QoS awareness** | None natively | Native via flow spec |
| **Scalability** | Excellent ($10^5$ prefixes) | Limited by flow table size |
| **Used by** | Traditional IP routers | OpenFlow switches, MPLS LSRs, SDN dataplanes |

### 2.3 IPv6 Flow Label (RFC 3697)

IPv6 introduced an explicit **20-bit Flow Label** field in its header. The rule:

> All packets belonging to the same flow **MUST** carry the same Flow Label value chosen by the source.

$$0 \leq FlowLabel \leq 2^{20} - 1 = 1{,}048{,}575$$

Routers can use the Flow Label (combined with Source Address) as a fast-path lookup key, **bypassing the longest-prefix match** for packets of the same flow. The random value rule:

$$P(Collision) \leq \frac{N(N-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{20}}$$

For $N$ concurrent flows — collision probability stays below $10^{-3}$ until $N \approx 1450$.

### 2.4 MPLS Flow — Label Switched Path (LSP)

In **MPLS**, a flow is mapped onto a **Label Switched Path (LSP)**. A packet entering an MPLS domain is classified and assigned a **label**; subsequent routers (**LSRs**) forward based purely on the **label** (not the IP header).

$$Forwarding = f(Label_{in}, Port_{in}) \rightarrow (Label_{out}, Port_{out}, NextHop)$$

The pair **(input label, input port)** uniquely identifies the flow at each LSR.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **5-Tuple Flow Key** | $\{SrcIP, DstIP, SrcPort, DstPort, Proto\}$ | L3+L4 header fields |
| **Token Bucket Admit Test** | $A(t+\tau) - A(t) \leq r\tau + b$ | Bits; $r$=rate, $b$=bucket depth |
| **Token Bucket Peak Rate** | $A(t+\tau) - A(t) \leq p\tau$ when bucket full | $p \geq r$ always |
| **Burst Tolerance** | $B_{tol} = b$ (when idle) | Bytes |
| **End-to-End Delay** | $D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^{prop} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i^{queue}$ | ms |
| **Jitter** | $J = \max(\vert d_i - d_j \vert)$ for $i,j$ in flow | ms |
| **IPv6 Flow Label Range** | $[0, \; 2^{20}-1]$ | 20 bits, $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ values |
| **Collision Probability** | $P_c \approx \dfrac{N^2}{2^{21}}$ | For $N$ flows picking random labels |
| **LSP Throughput** | $\Theta_{LSP} = \min(R_{link_i})$ over $i$ in LSP path | bits/sec, bottleneck |
| **Flow Setup Latency** | $T_{setup} = 2 \cdot RTT + \sum d_{signalling}$ | ms (one-way) |

> [!NOTE]
> All vertical bars in expressions like $\vert d_i - d_j \vert$ are written using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The flow abstraction is the **backbone of modern carrier networks**:

- **ISPs (Airtel, Jio, BSNL)** use NetFlow records to bill enterprise customers per-flow and detect DDoS.
- **5G mobile core** uses **QoS Flows** (5QI - 5G QoS Identifier) defined in 3GPP TS 23.501.
- **Data centers** (Google B4, Microsoft Azure WAN) use **SDN flow rules** to steer elephant flows dynamically.
- **VoIP / Video Conferencing (Zoom, Teams)** depend on **RSVP / DiffServ flows** for low-jitter experience.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Token-Bucket Policing Decision

The token-bucket is the canonical **flow conformance / policing** algorithm. Let us derive the per-arrival decision rule.

**Step 1 — State Variable Definition**
Let $B(t)$ be the **token count** in the bucket at time $t$, in bytes. The bucket fills at rate $r$ bytes/sec and caps at $b$ bytes.

$$\frac{dB(t)}{dt} = \begin{cases} r & \text{if } B(t) < b \\ 0 & \text{if } B(t) = b \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Arrival of a Packet**
Suppose a packet of size $L$ bytes arrives at time $t_n$. Update the bucket to the arrival time first (add tokens), then decide:

$$B'(t_n) = \min\{ B(t_{n-1}) + r(t_n - t_{n-1}), \; b \}$$

**Step 3 — Conformance Test**
The packet is admitted (and possibly marked conforming) **iff** $B'(t_n) \geq L$:

$$Decision = \begin{cases} Conforming \; (Green) & \text{if } B'(t_n) \geq L \\ Non-Conforming \; (Red) & \text{if } B'(t_n) < L \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Bucket Update on Conformity**
If conforming, deduct the consumed tokens:

$$B(t_n) = B'(t_n) - L$$

**Step 5 — Aggregate Conformance over Window**
Summing over an interval $[t, t+\tau]$, the **max transmitted bytes** is the long-run identity derived by integrating the bucket:

$$A(t+\tau) - A(t) \leq \int_{t}^{t+\tau} \min\{ r, \; \text{instantaneous rate} \} dt + b$$

Simplifying for a source that has been idle before $t$ (full bucket):

$$\max\{A(t+\tau) - A(t)\} = r\tau + b$$

This is precisely the **leaky-bucket / token-bucket envelope**, and it confirms the **TSpec conformance test** stated in Section 2.1.3.

### 3.2 Derivation: IPv6 Flow Label Hash Collision Probability

**Given:** $N$ concurrent IPv6 flows on a router, each picking a **uniformly random** 20-bit label.

**Step 1 — Total Label Space**
$$S = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ distinct labels}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of Single Label Avoiding Collision**
The probability that the $k$-th flow's random label differs from all $(k-1)$ previous labels:

$$P(\text{no collision at flow } k) = \prod_{i=0}^{k-2} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{S - i} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Approximation for Small $k \ll S$**
Using the first-order Taylor expansion $(1-x) \approx e^{-x}$:

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx \prod_{i=0}^{k-2} e^{-1/(S-i)} \approx e^{-(k-1)/S}$$

**Step 4 — Probability of At Least One Collision**
By the complement rule:

$$P_c(N) = 1 - e^{-N(N-1)/(2S)}$$

For $N(N-1) \ll 2S$, this simplifies to the **birthday-paradox approximation**:

$$P_c \approx \frac{N(N-1)}{2S} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2 \cdot 2^{20}} \approx \frac{N^2}{2^{21}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Threshold**
Setting $P_c \leq 10^{-3}$:

$$\frac{N^2}{2^{21}} \leq 10^{-3} \implies N^2 \leq 1048.576 \implies N \leq 32$$

So a router seeing **more than ~32 concurrent flows** needs to be cautious of label reuse — the **collision probability** rises quickly, which is why modern IPv6 implementations hash (SrcAddr + FlowLabel) rather than FlowLabel alone.

### 3.3 Derivation: End-to-End Delay Bound Along an LSP

For a flow traversing an MPLS LSP with $N$ hops, each hop introduces **four delay components**:

$$d_i^{total} = d_i^{proc} + d_i^{queue} + d_i^{trans} + d_i^{prop}$$

**Step 1 — Processing Delay** (fixed per router)
$$d_i^{proc} = \text{constant} \approx 1-100 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Transmission Delay** (depends on link rate $R_i$ and packet length $L$)
$$d_i^{trans} = \frac{L}{R_i}$$

**Step 3 — Propagation Delay** (depends on physical medium length $D_i$ and signal speed $s$)
$$d_i^{prop} = \frac{D_i}{s} \quad ; \quad s \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s in fiber}$$

**Step 4 — Queueing Delay** (M/D/1 approximation for Poisson arrival, deterministic service)
$$d_i^{queue} = \frac{\rho_i}{2\mu_i (1-\rho_i)}$$

where $\rho_i = \lambda / \mu_i$ is the **utilization** of link $i$, $\lambda$ is the arrival rate of the flow, $\mu_i = R_i / L$ is the service rate in pkts/sec.

**Step 5 — Total End-to-End Delay**
$$D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( d_i^{proc} + \frac{L}{R_i} + \frac{D_i}{s} + \frac{\rho_i}{2\mu_i (1-\rho_i)} \right)$$

This is the **delay bound** that an RSVP/TE flow spec must satisfy.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Flow Mechanism

| Requirement | Recommended Flow Mechanism | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hard bandwidth guarantee per call | **RSVP / IntServ** | Per-flow reservation with explicit rate |
| Class-based QoS without per-flow state | **DiffServ (PHB)** | Scalable aggregate flow treatment |
| Traffic-engineered path across MPLS cloud | **RSVP-TE / MPLS-TE** | Constraint-based LSP routing |
| Programmable per-flow forwarding | **OpenFlow / SDN** | Match-action on arbitrary headers |
| Real-time flow telemetry | **IPFIX / NetFlow v9** | Standardized flow export |
| Flow-aware forwarding in pure IPv6 | **IPv6 Flow Label** | Native header field, no new protocol |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow Lifecycle — From Source to Sink

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[App at Host A] --> B[Transport Layer adds SrcPort DstPort]
    B --> C[Network Layer adds SrcIP DstIP FlowLabel]
    C --> D{Flow Exists in Flow Table?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Apply Cached Action: Forward Queue Mark]
    D -- No --> F[First Packet of Flow: Run Classifier]
    F --> G[Install Flow Entry in Flow Table]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Router 1 L3 hop]
    H --> I[Router 2 L3 hop]
    I --> J[Router N final hop]
    J --> K[App at Host B]
    E --> L[QoS Scheduler WFQ Priority Queue]
    L --> M[Output Interface]
    M --> I
```

### 4.2 Flow Specification Components (TSPEC + RSPEC Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Class[Flow Classifier 5 Tuple]
        A1[Src IP] --> X[Flow Key]
        A2[Dst IP] --> X
        A3[Src Port] --> X
        A4[Dst Port] --> X
        A5[Protocol] --> X
    end
    X --> Y[Match Action Engine]
    subgraph Spec[Flow Specification]
        T1[TSpec Token Bucket r b p m M]
        R1[RSpec Bandwidth Burst Delay Jitter Loss]
    end
    Spec --> Y
    Y --> Z[Per Flow Forwarding and Policing]
```

### 4.3 MPLS LSP as an Engineered Flow Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PE1[Ingress LSR Provider Edge]
        N1[Incoming IP Packet]
        N2[Classifier Push Label 27]
    end
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3[Transit LSR Swap Label 27 to 55]
    N3 --> N4[Transit LSR Swap Label 55 to 91]
    N4 --> N5[Egress LSR Pop Label Forward IP]
    N5 --> N6[Destination Host B]
    N2 --- N3
    N3 --- N4
    N4 --- N5
    style PE1 fill:#E8F4FD
```

### 4.4 RSVP Reservation Process for a QoS Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Sender Host
    participant R1 as Router 1
    participant R2 as Router 2
    participant R3 as Router 3
    participant Dst as Receiver Host
    Src->>R1: PATH message TSpec RSpec desired QoS
    R1->>R2: PATH forward along routing path
    R2->>R3: PATH forward
    R3->>Dst: PATH delivers to receiver
    Dst->>R3: RESV message reverse path reservation
    R3->>R2: RESV allocate bandwidth queue resources
    R2->>R1: RESV reserve resources per flow
    R1->>Src: RESV confirms reservation established
    Note over Src,Dst: Flow now has end to end QoS guarantee
```

### 4.5 OpenFlow / SDN Flow Table Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Incoming Packet] --> B[Match against Flow Table 0]
    B --> C{Match Found?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute Action: Forward Drop Modify]
    C -- No --> E[Send Packet In to SDN Controller]
    E --> F[Controller computes new flow rule]
    F --> G[Install Flow Mod to switch]
    G --> B
    D --> H[Output Port or Queue]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "flow" in internetworking. List and explain the five fields that constitute the standard IP flow key.**

**Model Answer:**
A *flow* is a sequence of packets from a source to a destination that the network treats as a single logical entity for forwarding, QoS, or accounting. The standard 5-tuple flow key consists of:

1. **Source IP Address** – identifies the originator host.
2. **Destination IP Address** – identifies the receiver host.
3. **Source Port Number** – identifies the sending application (e.g., ephemeral port 50000).
4. **Destination Port Number** – identifies the receiving service (e.g., 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS).
5. **Protocol Number** – L4 protocol (6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP).

All packets sharing these five field values are classified into the same flow. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the IPv6 Flow Label field? Why is it 20 bits wide?**

**Model Answer:**
The IPv6 **Flow Label** is a 20-bit field in the IPv6 header used to label packets belonging to the same flow. All packets of a flow carry the same Flow Label value chosen by the source. Routers may use the (Source Address, Flow Label) pair as a fast-path lookup key to apply a cached forwarding action without performing a longest-prefix match, thus accelerating forwarding for delay-sensitive traffic (e.g., real-time video).

The **20-bit** width allows $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ distinct label values, sufficient for unique identification per source–destination pair while keeping the header compact. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Bank]`

**(a)** Define a *flow specification*. Explain in detail the **TSPEC** and **RSPEC** components of an RSVP flow spec, listing all parameters with their units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of the **token-bucket policing algorithm** used for flow conformance. Show the admission test inequality and describe how burst tolerance is enforced. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

A **flow specification (FlowSpec)** is a structured descriptor that defines both *which packets* belong to a flow and *what treatment* the flow demands from the network. It is used in **RSVP** (Resource Reservation Protocol) to set up end-to-end QoS guarantees.

The FlowSpec has **two coupled sub-specifications**:

**1. TSPEC — Traffic Specification (what the source generates)**

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Token bucket rate | $r$ | bytes/sec | Long-term average send rate |
| Token bucket depth | $b$ | bytes | Burst tolerance |
| Peak rate | $p$ | bytes/sec | Maximum instantaneous rate |
| Minimum policed unit | $m$ | bytes | Smallest packet considered |
| Maximum packet size | $M$ | bytes | Largest packet allowed |

**2. RSPEC — Reservation Specification (what the network reserves)**

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reserved rate | $R$ | bytes/sec | Guaranteed bandwidth |
| Slack term | $S$ | bytes | Permitted delay slack |
| (Optional) Loss prob. | $L$ | $10^{-n}$ | Max acceptable loss |

The **QoS requested = RSPEC**, the **traffic expected = TSPEC**, and **admission control** allows the reservation only if `R ≤ r` and the link can spare `R` bandwidth. *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

The **token bucket** is a virtual bucket holding $b$ tokens, filled at rate $r$ tokens/sec, used to police whether a flow's instantaneous transmission rate conforms to its TSPEC.

**State update on packet arrival at time $t_n$ of size $L$ bytes:**

$$B'(t_n) = \min\{B(t_{n-1}) + r(t_n - t_{n-1}), \, b\}$$

**Admission test (conformance decision):**

$$\text{Conforming} \iff B'(t_n) \geq L$$

If conforming, $B(t_n) = B'(t_n) - L$; else the packet is **dropped or marked Non-Conforming (Red)**.

**Burst tolerance**: After an idle period of duration $T_{idle}$, the bucket refills to:

$$B(t_n) = b \quad \text{when} \quad T_{idle} \geq b / r$$

Hence the flow can burst up to $b$ bytes instantaneously, then must sustain $r$ bytes/sec. The envelope over window $\tau$ is:

$$A(t+\tau) - A(t) \leq r\tau + b$$

This is the **TSPEC conformance inequality** — anything beyond is policed out. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Label Switched Path (LSP)** in MPLS. How does it relate to the notion of a network flow? Describe the role of the **ingress**, **transit**, and **egress** LSRs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **IPv6 Flow Label** as defined in RFC 3697. Derive the probability of hash collision for $N$ concurrent flows sharing a router, and show that for $N \leq 32$ the collision probability stays below $10^{-3}$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

A **Label Switched Path (LSP)** is a pre-established unidirectional tunnel through an MPLS network that carries a particular traffic flow (or aggregate of flows). An LSP is the MPLS instantiation of a *flow* — once established, all packets of the flow traverse the same set of LSRs with the same forwarding treatment.

**Three LSR roles:**

1. **Ingress LSR (LER)** — receives raw IP packets, classifies them into Forwarding Equivalence Classes (FECs), and **pushes** an MPLS label onto the packet. The Ingress performs the IP→label mapping.
2. **Transit LSR** — performs **label swap**: replaces the incoming label with an outgoing label and forwards to the next hop. The IP header is *not* examined.
3. **Egress LSR** — **pops** the label, restoring the original IP packet, and performs conventional IP forwarding to the destination.

The LSP therefore **abstracts the flow** into a label-switched path, enabling **traffic engineering, fast reroute, and QoS guarantees** without per-router per-flow IP-state.

A **flow ↔ LSP** mapping is bijective when the LSP is dedicated to one flow; multiple flows can be aggregated into one LSP based on FEC (e.g., all VoIP traffic → LSP-V). *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

The IPv6 Flow Label (RFC 3697) is a **20-bit field** in the IPv6 header. The rules:

1. A source selects a non-zero Flow Label for a flow.
2. All packets of the same flow carry the same (SrcAddr, FlowLabel) pair.
3. The label is randomly chosen to avoid collisions.

**Derivation of collision probability:**

For $N$ concurrent flows, each picking uniformly from $S = 2^{20}$ labels, the **birthday-paradox collision probability** is:

$$P_c(N) \approx \frac{N(N-1)}{2S}$$

For $N \ll S$, we approximate:

$$P_c \approx \frac{N^2}{2 \cdot 2^{20}} = \frac{N^2}{2^{21}}$$

**Numerical evaluation for $N = 32$:**

$$P_c = \frac{32^2}{2^{21}} = \frac{1024}{2{,}097{,}152} \approx 4.88 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0488\%$$

$$P_c \approx 0.000488 \ll 10^{-3} \quad \checkmark$$

For $N = 33$:

$$P_c = \frac{33^2}{2^{21}} = \frac{1089}{2{,}097{,}152} \approx 5.19 \times 10^{-4} < 10^{-3} \quad \checkmark$$

For $N = 46$:

$$P_c = \frac{46^2}{2^{21}} = \frac{2116}{2{,}097{,}152} \approx 1.009 \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(threshold crossed)}$$

Hence, **for $N \leq 32-45$ flows, the random Flow Label gives acceptable collision probability below $10^{-3}$**, justifying the use of 20-bit width. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "flow" with "connection"** — A flow is *logical equivalence class*, not stateful TCP connection. State explicitly this distinction.
> 2. **Missing units** — TSPEC uses **bytes/sec**, RSPEC bandwidth is **bits/sec**. Mixing them loses marks.
> 3. **Wrong inequality direction** — Token-bucket admit test is $A(t+\tau) - A(t) \leq r\tau + b$, **not** $\geq$.
> 4. **Forgetting ingress vs egress role** in MPLS — Examiners strictly grade push/swap/pop sequence.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps** — Numerical questions (e.g., flow label collision) require showing the **birthday paradox formula**, not just the final number.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **flow** is a logical equivalence class of packets sharing common header field values, treated as a unit by the network.
- ✅ The **5-tuple** {SrcIP, DstIP, SrcPort, DstPort, Protocol} is the canonical flow key; **7-tuple** adds ToS and input interface.
- ✅ **Flow Specification** = TSPEC (what source sends) + RSPEC (what network reserves) + classifier (which packets).
- ✅ **Token bucket** rate $r$ and depth $b$ enforce the conformance envelope $A(t+\tau) - A(t) \leq r\tau + b$.
- ✅ **IPv6 Flow Label** is a 20-bit field ($2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ values) used as a fast-path lookup key with the Source Address.
- ✅ Collision probability for $N$ flows picking random Flow Labels follows the **birthday paradox**: $P_c \approx N^2 / 2^{21}$, staying below $10^{-3}$ for $N \lesssim 45$.
- ✅ **MPLS LSPs** are engineered flow paths using label push (ingress), swap (transit), and pop (egress) operations.
- ✅ **RSVP** performs *out-of-band* signalling of FlowSpec via PATH and RESV messages along the data path.
- ✅ **Flow-based forwarding** (SDN/OpenFlow) is *stateful* and per-flow, in contrast to *stateless* destination-based IP forwarding.
- ✅ Modern 5G, VoIP, and ISP billing systems are all built on the **flow abstraction** — mastering it is essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MPLS - Switching Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** MPLS - Switching Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Multiprotocol Label Switching (MPLS)** is a packet-forwarding technology that operates at Layer 2.5 of the OSI model. It directs data from one network node to the next based on short, fixed-length **labels** (20 bits) rather than long, variable-length IP addresses. MPLS is "multiprotocol" because it can encapsulate packets of various network layer protocols (IPv4, IPv6, IPX, AppleTalk, etc.) and is independent of Layer 2 technologies (Ethernet, ATM, Frame Relay, SONET, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT522 - Module 4):**
> MPLS lies at the heart of modern service-provider networks. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests the operational mechanics: label structure, label-switched path (LSP) construction, Label Distribution Protocol (LDP), the role of Label Switch Routers (LSR) vs. Label Edge Routers (LER), and the three fundamental label operations — **Push, Swap, Pop**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Track System

Imagine a massive international airport with thousands of passengers (packets) arriving daily. Every passenger has a complicated, long international address (IPv4/IPv6 destination). If the airport read the full address of every passenger at every junction, the system would be **slow and inefficient**.

Now, imagine the airport instead issues a **color-coded boarding pass** to each passenger at the entrance (Ingress LER). Red passengers board **Track A**, Blue passengers board **Track B**, and Green passengers board **Track C**. At every internal junction (LSR), the conductor only checks the **color of the pass**, not the final destination, and shoves the passenger onto the correct next track. Only at the very last junction (Egress LER) does the system read the full final address and guide the passenger to the correct gate.

> - The **colored pass** = MPLS Label (20-bit identifier).
> - The **entrance gate** = Ingress LER (Push operation).
> - The **internal junctions** = LSR (Swap operation).
> - The **last junction** = Egress LER (Pop operation).
> - The **track** = Label Switched Path (LSP).

This is precisely how MPLS achieves line-rate forwarding: it **decouples routing intelligence (the edges) from forwarding speed (the core)**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard KTU-mandated terms must be memorized:
> - **FEC (Forwarding Equivalence Class):** A group of packets forwarded in the same manner, over the same path, with the same treatment.
> - **LER (Label Edge Router):** MPLS-aware router at the boundary of an MPLS domain. Performs Push (ingress) or Pop (egress).
> - **LSR (Label Switch Router):** MPLS-aware router in the core. Performs Swap (label switching).
> - **LSP (Label Switched Path):** The unidirectional path through the MPLS network for a given FEC.
> - **LDP (Label Distribution Protocol):** A signaling protocol used by LSRs to exchange label-binding information.
> - **PHP (Penultimate Hop Popping):** Optimization where the second-to-last LSR pops the label to offload work from the egress LER.

The standard MPLS **shim header** is **32 bits** total = **20 bits (Label)** + **3 bits (EXP/TC)** + **1 bit (Bottom-of-Stack, S)** + **8 bits (TTL)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why MPLS? The "Why" Behind the Technology

Traditional IP forwarding requires a **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** lookup in the routing table at **every hop**. The lookup complexity is **O(log n)** to **O(W)** where *W* is the address width (32 for IPv4, 128 for IPv6). On a backbone router pushing 400 Gbps, even microsecond-level delays per hop are unacceptable.

MPLS reduces this to a **simple, fixed-position label lookup** — effectively **O(1)** — by using a dedicated lookup table called the **LIB (Label Information Base)** / **LFIB (Label Forwarding Information Base)**.

### 2.1.1 Operational Advantages (KTU Frequently Asked)

1. **Speed:** Label lookup is faster than LPM because the label is an exact-match, fixed-length key.
2. **Scalability:** Supports hierarchical addressing and aggregation.
3. **Traffic Engineering (MPLS-TE):** Allows non-destination-driven explicit paths.
4. **VPNs:** MPLS L3VPN and L2VPN underpin enterprise WANs.
5. **QoS Differentiation:** The 3-bit EXP field enables per-class queuing.
6. **Protocol Independence:** Works over IP, Ethernet, ATM, etc.

## 2.2 The MPLS Shim Header — Bit-Level Anatomy

The MPLS header is inserted **between** the Layer 2 header and the Layer 3 (IP) header. This is why MPLS is classified as **Layer 2.5**.

| Field | Size (bits) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Label** | **20** | Identifies the FEC / next-hop forwarding decision |
| **EXP / TC** | **3** | Experimental / Traffic Class for QoS and PHB (Per-Hop Behavior) |
| **S (Bottom-of-Stack)** | **1** | 1 = last label, 0 = more labels follow (supports label stacking) |
| **TTL** | **8** | Decremented at every hop; prevents indefinite loops |

> [!NOTE]
> **Label Stack:** MPLS supports a **stack** of labels (nested LSPs). Used heavily in MPLS VPNs and Traffic Engineering. The S bit is the stack terminator. The minimum depth is **1**, and the typical practical maximum depth is **3–4** in service-provider networks.

## 2.3 The Three Fundamental Label Operations

MPLS defines exactly **three** operations. These are the heart of every KTU question on this topic.

| Operation | Who Performs It | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Push (Impose)** | Ingress LER | Adds (imposes) a new MPLS label on top of the IP packet. |
| **Swap** | Core LSR | Replaces the incoming top label with an outgoing top label based on the LFIB. |
| **Pop (Dispose)** | Egress LER (or Penultimate LSR if PHP enabled) | Removes the top label, exposing the inner IP header (or next label in stack). |

## 2.4 Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP) — The Optimization

If the egress LER had to pop the label **and** perform an IP lookup on every packet, it would become a bottleneck. To avoid this, the **second-to-last** LSR (penultimate hop) is instructed (via LDP) to use **label value 3** (the "implicit null" label) to signal that it must pop the label itself and forward the pure IP packet to the egress LER. This is **PHP**.

- The special label for this is the **Implicit NULL (value = 3)**.
- A related concept is the **Explicit NULL (value = 0)**, used to preserve the EXP bits all the way to the egress.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are high-yield, exam-ready metrics and formulas. Note: MPLS is not numerically intensive like Physics/Electrical subjects, but these constants and relations are repeatedly tested.

| Parameter | Value / Formula | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| MPLS Shim Header Size | **32 bits = 4 bytes** | Fixed |
| Label Field Width | **20 bits** | 2^20 = **1,048,576** usable labels |
| Practical Label Space (RFC 3032) | **0 – 1,048,575** | **0 = IPv4 Explicit NULL**, **1 = Router Alert**, **2 = IPv6 Explicit NULL**, **3 = Implicit NULL** |
| EXP / TC Field | **3 bits** | 8 QoS classes |
| Bottom-of-Stack (S) | **1 bit** | 1 = last label |
| TTL Field | **8 bits** | Same semantics as IP TTL |
| Maximum MPLS Label Stack Depth (RFC 3032) | **Unlimited** (theoretical) | Typical practical: **≤ 4** |
| Forwarding Lookup Complexity | **O(1)** exact match | vs. IP LPM **O(log n)** |
| LSP Direction | **Unidirectional** | Return traffic needs separate LSP |
| TTL Initial Value | **Copied from IP TTL** (at ingress Push) | Decremented at each MPLS hop |
| MPLS Operating Layer | **Layer 2.5** | Between L2 and L3 |
| Default MPLS MTU Overhead | **+4 bytes per label** | Adjust interface MTU to **1504+** on Ethernet |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> - **Service Provider Backbones:** Every Tier-1 ISP (Airtel, Jio, AT&T, Verizon) runs MPLS in the core.
> - **Enterprise WANs:** MPLS L3VPN replaces legacy Frame Relay / ATM.
> - **5G Transport:** MPLS-TP (Transport Profile) is used in fronthaul and backhaul.
> - **Data Center Interconnect (DCI):** MPLS-EVPN is the de-facto VXLAN-alternative.
> - **SDN underlay:** Segment Routing (SR-MPLS) is a modern evolution of MPLS.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 LSP Setup — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Walkthrough

We will trace a packet from Source `192.168.1.10` to Destination `10.0.0.5` across an MPLS domain with 4 routers: **Ingress LER (R1) → LSR1 (R2) → LSR2 (R3) → Egress LER (R4)**.

The routing protocol (e.g., OSPF/IS-IS) has already converged. LDP has distributed the following label bindings for the FEC `10.0.0.0/24`:

| Router | Role | Incoming Label | Outgoing Label | Outgoing Interface |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Ingress LER | (none — IP) | **25** (Push) | To R2 |
| **R2** | LSR | **25** | **47** (Swap) | To R3 |
| **R3** | Penultimate LSR (PHP) | **47** | **3 (Implicit NULL)** | To R4 |
| **R4** | Egress LER | **3** → Pop | (none — IP) | To destination LAN |

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Ingress LER (R1) Performs PUSH

1. R1 receives a pure IP packet destined for `10.0.0.5`.
2. R1 performs an FIB (Forwarding Information Base) lookup on the IP destination.
3. R1 identifies the FEC = `10.0.0.0/24`.
4. R1 consults the local **LFIB** and finds outgoing label = **25**.
5. R1 **Pushes** a new MPLS shim header: `[ Label=25 | EXP=000 | S=1 | TTL=copy_from_IP ]`.
6. The packet is now: `[ Eth | MPLS(25) | IP | TCP | Payload ]`.
7. R1 decrements the IP TTL by 1 and writes the new value into the MPLS TTL field. R1 then forwards to R2.

**Textual Logic:** "The edge router transforms an IP-only packet into an IP+Label packet. The label 25 is the 'boarding pass' for FEC 10.0.0.0/24."

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Core LSR (R2) Performs SWAP

1. R2 receives the frame. It sees the Ethertype `0x8847` (MPLS Unicast), so it parses the MPLS shim header.
2. R2 looks at the top label = **25**.
3. R2 queries the **LFIB** in O(1) time.
4. R2 finds: incoming label 25 → swap to **47**, output interface to R3.
5. R2 **Swaps** label 25 with label 47 in the shim header.
6. R2 decrements the MPLS TTL by 1 (TTL = 254 if originally 255).
7. R2 forwards the frame to R3.

**Textual Logic:** "The core router is 'destination-unaware' at the IP level. It only cares about the top label. The original IP header is untouched — only the label is rewritten. This is the speed advantage."

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Penultimate LSR (R3) Performs PHP POP

1. R3 receives the frame with top label = **47**.
2. R3's LFIB entry: label 47 → swap to **3 (Implicit NULL)** → output interface to R4.
3. When R3 sees the special value **3**, it interprets this as: *"I should pop the label entirely and forward a pure IP packet to R4."*
4. R3 removes the MPLS shim header (Pop).
5. R3 decrements the IP TTL by 1 (the IP TTL was preserved in a parallel processing path during transit, or restored at pop — RFC 3443).
6. R3 forwards the now IP-only packet to R4.

**Textual Logic:** "PHP offloads the label-popping work from the egress LER. The egress LER now receives a pure IP packet and only does one FIB lookup, which is its normal job."

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Egress LER (R4) Delivers

1. R4 receives a pure IP packet.
2. R4 performs a standard IP lookup on `10.0.0.5`.
3. R4 forwards the packet out the destination interface.

**Textual Logic:** "The MPLS domain has been completely transparent to R4. It is just a normal IP router for the final delivery."

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Label Operation Decision Flow

We can formalize the decision logic at any MPLS node as a deterministic function:

$$
f(\text{top\_label}) = \begin{cases}
(\text{PUSH}, \text{new\_label}, \text{out\_intf}) & \text{if node is Ingress LER} \\
(\text{SWAP}, \text{out\_label}, \text{out\_intf}) & \text{if node is core LSR and label} \neq 3 \\
(\text{POP}, \text{null}, \text{out\_intf}) & \text{if top\_label} = 3 \text{ (PHP)} \\
(\text{POP}, \text{null}, \text{out\_intf}) & \text{if node is Egress LER and S=1} \\
(\text{RECURSE}, \text{next\_label}) & \text{if S=0 (more labels in stack)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Explanation of cases:**

- **Case 1 (Push):** At the ingress, the IP packet enters and a label is imposed.
- **Case 2 (Swap):** Normal core operation. Label replaced, TTL decremented.
- **Case 3 (PHP):** Label 3 is a signal, not a real forwarding label. It triggers a pop.
- **Case 4 (Egress):** If PHP is not used, the egress LER pops the last label.
- **Case 5 (Recurse):** MPLS supports label stacks. The S bit tells the router whether to recurse into the next label.

## 3.3 TTL Handling in MPLS — Exhaustive Derivation

MPLS TTL processing has two modes defined in **RFC 3443**:

$$
\text{TTL}_{\text{MPLS,out}} = \begin{cases}
\text{TTL}_{\text{IP,original}} - (\text{hops in MPLS domain}) & \text{(Uniform Mode — default)} \\
255 & \text{(Pipe Mode — hides MPLS topology from traceroute)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Derivation logic for Uniform Mode (default in most implementations):**

1. At **ingress Push**, the MPLS TTL is set equal to the IP TTL.
2. At each **Swap**, the MPLS TTL is decremented by 1.
3. At **Pop (PHP)**, the IP TTL is set to: **(MPLS TTL at pop) - 1**.
4. Therefore, the total TTL decrement across the MPLS domain equals the number of MPLS hops.

**Derivation logic for Pipe Mode:**

1. At **ingress Push**, the MPLS TTL = 255 (independent of IP TTL).
2. At each **Swap**, MPLS TTL is decremented.
3. At **Pop**, the IP TTL is **not modified** by the MPLS TTL.
4. Result: The IP source and destination see a single hop for the entire MPLS cloud — useful for hiding internal topology and preventing traceroute-based network discovery.

## 3.4 Label Distribution — LDP vs. RSVP-TE

| Feature | LDP (Label Distribution Protocol) | RSVP-TE (Resource Reservation Protocol – Traffic Engineering) |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Hop-by-hop, follows IGP shortest path | Explicit (CSPF-computed) path |
| Constraint-aware | No | Yes (bandwidth, affinity, admin groups) |
| LSP Type | Best-effort | Traffic Engineered (TE) |
| KTU Frequency | **High** | **Medium** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 emphasis:** LDP is the default MPLS signaling protocol. It uses **TCP port 646** for reliable session establishment and operates in **downstream unsolicited** mode by default, where the downstream LSR independently assigns and advertises labels to its upstream neighbors.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — MTU Calculation

**Problem:** A service provider runs MPLS with **3 stacked labels** over Ethernet (default MTU = 1500). The customer wants to send a 1500-byte IP packet. Will it fragment? Calculate the required interface MTU.

**Step 1:** Each MPLS label adds **4 bytes** of overhead.

$$
\text{MPLS Overhead} = 3 \text{ labels} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 12 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Total frame size on the wire:

$$
\text{Frame Size} = \text{Eth Header (14 B)} + \text{IP Payload (1500 B)} + \text{MPLS Overhead (12 B)} + \text{FCS (4 B)} = 1530 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** Since 1530 > 1500, the frame **exceeds the default MTU** and will be **fragmented or dropped** (depending on DF bit).

**Step 4:** Required interface MTU on MPLS interfaces:

$$
\text{Required MTU} = 1500 + 12 = 1512 \text{ bytes (minimum)}
$$

**Best Practice:** Configure `mpls mtu 1512` (or higher) on all MPLS-enabled interfaces to prevent fragmentation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define MPLS. What is the size of the MPLS shim header and explain its fields briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

**MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching)** is a Layer 2.5 packet-forwarding technique that uses short, fixed-length labels (instead of IP addresses) to forward packets along a pre-established Label Switched Path (LSP). It works over multiple Layer 2 and Layer 3 protocols, hence "multiprotocol."

The **MPLS shim header is 32 bits (4 bytes)** in size and is inserted between the Layer 2 and Layer 3 headers. Its fields are:

1. **Label (20 bits):** Identifies the FEC (Forwarding Equivalence Class) and the next forwarding action.
2. **EXP / Traffic Class (3 bits):** Used for QoS marking (PHB - Per-Hop Behavior).
3. **S - Bottom of Stack (1 bit):** Set to 1 for the last (innermost) label; 0 indicates more labels exist in the stack.
4. **TTL (8 bits):** Decremented at every MPLS hop to prevent loops; mirrors IP TTL semantics.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Distinguish between LER and LSR in an MPLS network.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | LER (Label Edge Router) | LSR (Label Switch Router) |
|---|---|---|
| Position | At the **boundary** of MPLS network | **Inside the core** of MPLS network |
| Function | Connects MPLS domain to non-MPLS networks | Forwards labeled packets within MPLS domain |
| Label Operations | **Push** (ingress) and **Pop** (egress) | **Swap** (label rewriting) |
| IP Lookup | Performs full **FIB lookup** (LPM) | Performs **LFIB lookup** (exact match, O(1)) |
| Number in network | Typically **2 per LSP** (ingress + egress) | **N-2** intermediate routers |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **lose 2 marks** by drawing the LSP diagram without labeling the **incoming and outgoing labels** at every hop. Always annotate: `Incoming Label → Outgoing Label`.
> - Another common mistake: writing **"MPLS is a Layer 3 protocol"**. It is **Layer 2.5** — this is a 1-mark killer.
> - Forgetting the **S bit** in label stacking questions costs 2–3 marks.
> - In PHP questions, students often say "egress LER pops the label" — this is wrong. The **penultimate** hop pops it.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an MPLS network. List the functions of the main components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the MPLS shim header in detail. How does label stacking work? What is the role of the S bit? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**MPLS Network Architecture:**

The MPLS network consists of three logical components:

1. **Ingress LER (Label Edge Router):** Entry point of the MPLS domain. Receives pure IP packets, classifies them into FECs, performs an IP FIB lookup, and **Pushes** an MPLS label. All QoS classification and label imposition happen here.

2. **Transit LSRs (Label Switch Routers):** Core routers that **Swap** labels at line rate using the LFIB. They do not examine the IP header — they only process the top MPLS label. This is the source of MPLS's speed advantage.

3. **Egress LER:** Exit point of the MPLS domain. **Pops** the final MPLS label (or receives an IP packet from PHP) and performs a final IP lookup to deliver to the destination.

**Supporting protocols:**

- **IGP (OSPF/IS-IS):** Populates the routing table (RIB).
- **LDP / RSVP-TE:** Distributes label-to-FEC bindings to populate the LFIB.

**ASCII Architecture Diagram:**

```
[Host A] -- IP --> [Ingress LER] --Label25--> [LSR1] --Label47--> [LSR2] --IP--> [Egress LER] -- IP --> [Host B]
                (PUSH)                          (SWAP)              (SWAP)              (POP)
```

#### Model Solution (b):

**MPLS Shim Header:**

| Bits | Field | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 0–19 (20) | **Label** | FEC identifier; lookup key in LFIB |
| 20–22 (3) | **EXP / TC** | QoS class (8 possible values: 0–7) |
| 23 (1) | **S bit** | 1 = bottom of label stack; 0 = more labels |
| 24–31 (8) | **TTL** | Loop prevention; decremented per hop |

**Label Stacking:**

MPLS supports a **stack** of labels, enabling hierarchical tunneling. Common use cases:

1. **MPLS L3VPN:** Inner label = VPN route distinguisher, Outer label = transport (IGP) path.
2. **MPLS Traffic Engineering:** Outer label = TE tunnel, Inner label = IGP path.
3. **MPLS-TP:** For transport networks.

**Mechanics of Stacking:**

- When pushing a new label, the router sets the **S bit of the new label to 0** (if there is an existing label below) or **1** (if no label exists below).
- A router that swaps or pops **only processes the top label** of the stack.
- The stack is processed LIFO (Last In, First Out) — like a stack of plates.

**Example:** For 2 stacked labels `[Outer=25, S=0] [Inner=47, S=1]`, the egress LER first pops label 25, recurses, sees S=1 for label 47, pops it, and exposes the IP header.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Explain the three fundamental label operations in MPLS with a suitable example. What is PHP and why is it used? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare LDP and RSVP-TE as label distribution protocols. How is the LFIB populated? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**The Three Label Operations:**

**1. Push (Imposition)** — Performed at the **Ingress LER**.
- The LER receives an unlabeled IP packet.
- It performs an FIB lookup, identifies the FEC, and inserts (pushes) a 4-byte MPLS shim header on top of the IP header.
- Example: IP packet for `10.0.0.0/24` becomes `[MPLS(Label=25) | IP]`.

**2. Swap** — Performed at **Core LSRs**.
- The LSR reads the top label (exact match in LFIB) and replaces it with a new label corresponding to the next-hop downstream.
- The IP header is **not** examined, ensuring line-rate forwarding.
- Example: At R2, label 25 is swapped to 47. At R3, label 47 is swapped to 3 (Implicit NULL for PHP).

**3. Pop (Disposition)** — Performed at the **Egress LER** or the **Penultimate LSR** (with PHP).
- The top label is removed, revealing the IP header (or the next label in the stack).
- At the egress, a final IP FIB lookup delivers the packet.

**PHP (Penultimate Hop Popping):**

In PHP, the **second-to-last** LSR (penultimate hop) is instructed (via LDP, by advertising the special label 3 = Implicit NULL) to pop the label itself and forward a pure IP packet to the egress LER.

**Why PHP is used:**

- **Offloads the egress LER:** The egress LER does not need to perform both label-popping and IP FIB lookup on the same packet.
- **Faster edge processing:** The egress LER can use optimized IP-only fast paths.
- **Load balancing & scalability:** Reduces CPU pressure on the egress, which is often a shared resource for many LSPs terminating at the same site.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Comparison: LDP vs. RSVP-TE**

| Parameter | LDP | RSVP-TE |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | RFC 5036 | RFC 3209 |
| **Transport** | TCP (port 646) — reliable | Raw IP (protocol 46) — unreliable, with refreshes |
| **Path Calculation** | Follows IGP shortest path (hop-by-hop) | Explicit path via CSPF (Constraint Shortest Path First) |
| **Constraint-Aware** | No | Yes (bandwidth, affinity, diversity) |
| **Use Case** | Best-effort MPLS, L3VPN transport | Traffic Engineering, Fast Reroute (FRR) |
| **State Maintenance** | Soft-state with periodic hellos | Hard-state with explicit teardown |
| **LSP Type** | Best-effort (BE) | Traffic-engineered (TE) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | **High** | Medium |

**How the LFIB is Populated (LDP example):**

1. The IGP (OSPF/IS-IS) populates the **Routing Information Base (RIB)**.
2. For every next-hop in the RIB, the router allocates a **local label** in the **Label Information Base (LIB)**.
3. The router runs **LDP** with its neighbors, advertising `FEC → locally allocated label` mappings.
4. The router receives remote `FEC → remote label` mappings and stores them in the LIB.
5. The router merges the **RIB + LIB** to build the **Label Forwarding Information Base (LFIB)**: `[Incoming Label → (Outgoing Label, Outgoing Interface)]`.
6. When a labeled packet arrives, the LFIB is consulted in **O(1)** for the forwarding decision.

**In Short:**
- **RIB** = *"Where can I go?"* (built by IGP)
- **LIB** = *"What labels do I have and what do they mean?"* (built by LDP)
- **LFIB** = *"How do I forward a labeled packet right now?"* (derived from RIB + LIB)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ⭐ **MPLS = Layer 2.5**, not Layer 2 or Layer 3. (Common 1-mark trap.)
- ⭐ **Shim header = 32 bits (4 bytes)**: 20-bit Label + 3-bit EXP + 1-bit S + 8-bit TTL.
- ⭐ **Label range = 0 to 1,048,575** (2^20 – 1). Reserved: 0 (IPv4 Explicit NULL), 1 (Router Alert), 2 (IPv6 Explicit NULL), 3 (Implicit NULL for PHP).
- ⭐ **Three operations = Push, Swap, Pop.** Memorize who does what.
- ⭐ **LER = Edge** (Push at ingress, Pop at egress). **LSR = Core** (Swap).
- ⭐ **PHP** = Penultimate Hop Popping — the second-to-last LSR pops the label (advertised as label 3 / Implicit NULL) to offload the egress LER.
- ⭐ **LSP = Unidirectional** path. Return traffic uses a separate LSP.
- ⭐ **TTL copied from IP** at ingress; decremented per hop; can operate in **Uniform** or **Pipe** mode (RFC 3443).
- ⭐ **LDP** uses TCP **port 646**; works in **downstream unsolicited** mode; follows IGP path.
- ⭐ **RSVP-TE** uses IP protocol 46; supports explicit constrained paths (CSPF); used for Traffic Engineering.
- ⭐ **MTU overhead = 4 bytes per label**. For 3 stacked labels, set `mpls mtu` to **≥ 1512** on Ethernet.
- ⭐ **Label stack**: minimum depth 1, supported by S bit (Bottom-of-Stack). Each push decrements S appropriately.
- ⭐ **LFIB lookup is O(1)** exact match — the entire performance justification for MPLS over traditional IP LPM.
- ⭐ **Real-world**: Every major ISP, 5G transport, enterprise WAN, and modern SDN underlay uses MPLS or its evolution (Segment Routing).
- ⭐ **Diagram essentials for full marks**: Always show → incoming label, outgoing label, operation (PUSH/SWAP/POP), and interfaces.
- ⭐ **Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC)** = group of packets receiving identical treatment; typically a prefix, but can be more granular (QoS-based, source-based).

> **End of Notes — MPLS: Switching Technology (PEITT522 Module 4)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flows and Flow Setup
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flows and Flow Setup

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a "Flow" (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of TCP/IP internetworking, a **Flow** is formally defined as a sequence of packets (or datagrams) sent from a particular source to a particular unicast, anycast, or multicast destination that the source desires to be handled as a single logical entity by the intermediate routers. The Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) distinguishes flows based on transport-layer and network-layer identifiers.

A flow is uniquely identified using a **Flow Identifier (Flow ID)**, most commonly the **5-tuple** of parameters that routers and switches inspect in each packet header:

$$\text{Flow} = \{ \text{Source IP}, \ \text{Destination IP}, \ \text{Source Port}, \ \text{Destination Port}, \ \text{Protocol} \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A flow is **NOT** simply a connection. A connection (like a TCP socket) is an end-to-end application concept. A **flow** is a network-layer/forwarding-plane construct that allows routers along the path to treat a related set of packets consistently for routing, QoS, security, and accounting purposes.

An extended **7-tuple** is sometimes used (adding **DSCP/TOS byte** and **Input interface identifier**) to disambiguate flows arriving on different interfaces with the same 5-tuple.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Reservation System"

Imagine a massive Indian railway station during festival season (e.g., Onam or Diwali). Thousands of people want to travel at the same time. The railway ministry introduces a **"Reservation Slip"** (a *Flow Setup* ticket).

- **Train and Route** → A specific *Flow* from City A to City B.
- **Reservation Slip (PNR)** → The *Flow Identifier* (5-tuple) — it specifies *who* is traveling, *from where*, *to where*, and *by which service*.
- **Reserved Seat / Quota** → The *QoS guarantees* (bandwidth, delay bound, jitter bound) reserved along the path.
- **Ticket Inspector at every station** → An intermediate *router* that checks the PNR and treats the passenger consistently throughout the journey.
- **Walk-up ticket (no reservation)** → A *Best-Effort* packet (no flow setup) that gets whatever resource is left over.

Without flow setup, every packet is a "walk-up" — no guarantee, no special treatment. With flow setup, you "reserve resources" along the entire path **before** sending data, ensuring predictable quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** This concept is the foundation of **VoIP (e.g., WhatsApp/MSTeams calls)**, **live video streaming**, and **online gaming**, where packets must arrive in order and on time, not just eventually.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| **IPv4 Header Length (without options)** | **20 bytes (160 bits)** | Base header for flow identification |
| **IPv6 Flow Label field length** | **20 bits** | Native IPv6 flow marking |
| **DSCP field length** | **6 bits** | QoS Class Selector (RFC 2474) |
| **5-Tuple size (theoretical max)** | **104 bits (Source+Dest IP) + 32 bits (Ports) + 8 bits (Protocol) = 144 bits** | Per-packet classifier lookup key |
| **Default RSVP Refresh Period** | **30 seconds** | Soft-state PATH/RESV message interval (RFC 2205) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Flows

Flows can be categorized along three orthogonal axes relevant to KTU Module 4:

**A) By Longevity**
- **Microflow** — A single end-to-end conversation between two applications (e.g., one TCP connection).
- **Aggregate Flow (Macroflow)** — A collection of microflows grouped by a common prefix or DSCP class (e.g., all VoIP traffic from a campus).

**B) By Directionality**
- **Unidirectional Flow** — Packets travel in only one direction (e.g., pure UDP video stream).
- **Bidirectional Flow** — Packets in both directions are treated as one logical flow (typical TCP).

**C) By Granularity (where state is maintained)**
- **Edge Flow** — State held only at ingress/egress routers of an AS.
- **Core Flow** — State held at every router along the path (e.g., RSVP / MPLS LSP).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Flow Setup

A router, by default, is a **stateless** forwarding device. It looks at the destination IP, runs a longest-prefix match, and forwards. This is fast, but it cannot:

1. Guarantee **bandwidth** for a specific conversation.
2. Reserve **buffer space** to prevent loss.
3. Enforce **admission control** — if the link is congested, a new "VIP" call should not be silently degraded.
4. Provide **per-flow security** (stateful firewalling).
5. Enable **traffic engineering** along non-shortest paths.

**Flow setup** introduces **state** into the network, transforming a *stateless datagram* service into a *connection-oriented-like* service, without requiring the application to use TCP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off (KTU Board Favorite):** Adding per-flow state at every router is **expensive** (memory, lookup time → O(N) where N = active flows). The Internet has therefore evolved *two* competing philosophies: **IntServ (per-flow state everywhere)** vs. **DiffServ (aggregate QoS classes, no per-flow state in core)**.

## 2.3 Operational Steps in a Generic Flow Setup

Regardless of the specific protocol, flow setup follows this canonical sequence:

1. **Classification** — The ingress router inspects each packet and assigns it to a flow (or marks it with a DSCP/EXP/MPLS label).
2. **Admission Control** — The network decides whether the new flow's QoS request can be honored given current load. If not, the flow is **rejected** or **downgraded**.
3. **Resource Reservation** — Bandwidth, buffer space, and CPU cycles are reserved at each hop along the path (RSVP PATH/RESV messages, or MPLS Label Distribution).
4. **Policing & Shaping** — The flow's traffic is measured against its contract (Token Bucket). Excess is dropped (policing) or delayed (shaping).
5. **State Maintenance** — Periodic refresh messages (soft-state) keep the reservation alive. If they stop, the reservation times out.
6. **Teardown** — Explicit RELEASE message or natural timeout terminates the flow state.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $5\text{-tuple}$ | $\{S_{IP}, D_{IP}, S_{port}, D_{port}, P\}$ | Standard Flow ID | bits |
| Token Bucket rate | $\rho$ | Average allowed rate | bytes/sec |
| Token Bucket burst | $\beta$ | Maximum instantaneous burst | bytes |
| Bucket fill time | $T_{fill} = \beta / \rho$ | Time to regenerate full burst | sec |
| Effective bandwidth (Guerroui's) | $B_{eff} = \frac{1}{T} \left( L_{max} + \beta \right)$ | For a flow with period $T$ and max packet $L_{max}$ | bps |
| RSVP State Refresh | $R \le K \times J$ | Where $K$ is refresh multiplier (default 3) and $J$ is refresh interval (default 30s) | sec |
| Flow aggregation ratio | $\eta = N_{micro} / N_{macro}$ | Number of microflows per aggregate | unitless |
| Worst-case jitter (per hop) | $J_{max} = \frac{L_{max}}{C} + d_{prop}$ | For output link of capacity $C$ | sec |
| Available bandwidth | $AB = C - U$ | Where $U$ is current utilization on the bottleneck link | bps |
| DSCP PHB class | 6-bit value, 0–63 | Per-Hop Behavior selector | unitless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compute the time a token bucket takes to refill, always use $T_{fill} = \beta / \rho$ and ensure units are consistent. If $\rho$ is in **bytes/second** and $\beta$ in **bytes**, the result is in **seconds**. If $\rho$ is in **bits/second**, convert: $\rho_{bytes} = \rho_{bits} / 8$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flow setup is the cornerstone of:
- **Telephony over IP (VoIP)** — SIP/SDP signaling sets up an RTP flow with a DSCP of **EF (46)** for toll-quality voice.
- **MPLS Traffic Engineering** — RSVP-TE establishes Label Switched Paths (LSPs) with explicit bandwidth reservations.
- **Data Center Fabrics** — VXLAN/NVGRE flows are set up between Top-of-Rack switches for tenant isolation.
- **5G Core Networks** — PFCP (Packet Forwarding Control Protocol) sets up GTP-U flows between UPF and gNB for each PDU session.
- **SDN (Software-Defined Networking)** — OpenFlow's `OFPT_FLOW_MOD` message is the modern embodiment of programmatic flow setup.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Token Bucket — Effective Average Rate

**Problem Statement:** A flow's traffic shaper uses a token bucket of size $\beta$ bytes, filled at rate $\rho$ bytes/sec. A source transmits a burst of $L$ bytes at time $t = 0$, then remains silent until $t = T$. Derive the *long-term average rate* enforced by this shaper.

**Step 1 — State the constraint.**
The shaper allows at most $L_{send}(t) \le \rho \cdot t + \beta$ bytes to be transmitted in any time window $[0, t]$.

This is the fundamental token-bucket inequality:

$$L_{send}(t) \le \rho \cdot t + \beta$$

**Step 2 — Express the long-term average.**

The long-term average rate $\bar{R}$ is the total bytes transmitted divided by the total elapsed time:

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\text{Total Bytes Sent}}{\text{Total Elapsed Time}} = \frac{L}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constraint at $t = T$.**

At the moment the burst ends (and the source becomes silent), the shaper has already transmitted $L$ bytes. The constraint requires:

$$L \le \rho \cdot T + \beta$$

Rearranging to express the relationship between $L$, $T$, $\rho$, and $\beta$:

$$L - \beta \le \rho \cdot T$$

**Step 4 — Derive the long-term average rate bound.**

Dividing both sides by $T$:

$$\frac{L}{T} - \frac{\beta}{T} \le \rho$$

As $T \to \infty$ (long-term behavior), the second term vanishes because $\beta$ is finite:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \left( \frac{L}{T} - \frac{\beta}{T} \right) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{L}{T} - 0$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the result.**

$$\boxed{\bar{R} \le \rho \quad \text{(as } T \to \infty \text{)}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The token bucket permits short bursts up to $\beta$ bytes, but **enforces a long-term average rate equal to $\rho$**. This is the mathematical basis of all traffic-policing contracts in QoS-aware networks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Bandwidth for a Periodic Traffic Source

**Problem Statement:** A periodic source (e.g., a VoIP codec) generates one packet of length $L_{max}$ bits every $T$ seconds. It is policed by a token bucket of rate $\rho$ bps and burst $\beta$ bits. Derive the minimum $\rho$ and $\beta$ needed to admit this flow without drops.

**Step 1 — Analyze the source pattern.**

The source produces an *on-off* pattern: it transmits $L_{max}$ bits instantaneously at the start of each period, then is idle for $T - \epsilon$ seconds (where $\epsilon \to 0$).

**Step 2 — Determine the required burst tolerance.**

At each transmission instant, the source sends $L_{max}$ bits in a burst. The token bucket must have accumulated *at least* $L_{max}$ tokens to accept it. Since the source never sends more than $L_{max}$ in a single instant:

$$\beta \ge L_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the required average rate.**

Over one full period $T$, the source transmits $L_{max}$ bits. To sustain this indefinitely, the bucket's refill rate must equal the source's average rate:

$$\rho \ge \frac{L_{max}}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a single expression.**

$$\boxed{\rho \ge \frac{L_{max}}{T} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta \ge L_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Real-world numerical example (G.711 VoIP).**

G.711 voice codec: $L_{max} = 160$ bytes = $1280$ bits, $T = 20$ ms = $0.02$ s.

$$\rho \ge \frac{1280 \text{ bits}}{0.02 \text{ s}} = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

$$\beta \ge 1280 \text{ bits} = 160 \text{ bytes}$$

This matches exactly the classic PCM telephony channel rate — a verification that our derivation aligns with industry-standard traffic contracts.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RSVP State Refresh — Maximum Allowed Interval

**Problem Statement:** RSVP uses *soft state*. A PATH refresh message must arrive before the router's state times out. The RFC 2205 specification defines a relationship between the refresh timeout $R$, the refresh multiplier $K$, and the refresh interval $J$. Derive the safe bounds.

**Step 1 — State the RFC relationship.**

The local cleanup timeout at a router is set to:

$$R = K \times J$$

Where:
- $J$ = configured refresh interval (default **30 seconds**)
- $K$ = refresh multiplier (default **3**)
- $R$ = state lifetime (after which reservation is purged)

**Step 2 — Plug in the defaults.**

$$R = 3 \times 30 = 90 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the rationale (why $K = 3$?).**

A single lost refresh message must not cause a teardown. The system tolerates up to $(K - 1) = 2$ consecutive lost refresh messages:

$$\text{Tolerable losses} = K - 1 = 2$$

If the network drops 1 in 3 packets on average (BER $\approx 10^{-3}$ over a stable path), the probability of losing 3 in a row is:

$$P_{3 \text{ losses}} = (10^{-3})^3 = 10^{-9}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the engineering principle.**

$$\boxed{R = K \times J \quad \text{with} \quad K = 3, \ J = 30\text{s} \ \Rightarrow \ R = 90\text{s}}$$

This guarantees **99.9999999% reliability** against random refresh loss — a key reason RSVP is considered robust despite its soft-state design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Comparison for Flow Setup

| Feature | RSVP (IntServ) | MPLS-TE | L2TPv3 | OpenFlow | GTP-U (5G) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **State Location** | Every router | Every LSR | Ingress/Egress LCCE | Controller + switch | UPF + gNB |
| **Signaling Transport** | Raw IP (protocol 46) | RSVP-TE or LDP | UDP | TCP (control plane) | UDP (data plane) |
| **Granularity** | Per-microflow | Per-LSP | Per-PW | Per-flow table entry | Per-PDU session |
| **Scalability** | Poor (core) | Good | Good | Excellent (TCAM) | Excellent |
| **Failure Recovery** | PATH tear-down | FRR (local repair) | Keepalive | OpenFlow echo | GTP echo |
| **Hard/Soft State** | Soft (refresh) | Hard (LSP) | Hard | Hard (config) | Hard |
| **Standard RFC** | RFC 2205 | RFC 3209 | RFC 3931 | ONF TS-006 | 3GPP TS 29.244 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — RSVP Flow Setup Sequence (End-to-End)

This Mermaid sequence diagram illustrates the canonical RSVP PATH → RESV → DATA → TEARDOWN flow across three intermediate routers (R1, R2, R3).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source Host
    participant R1 as Ingress Router
    participant R2 as Core Router
    participant R3 as Egress Router
    participant DST as Destination Host

    Note over SRC,DST: Phase 1 - Path Discovery and Resource Request
    SRC->>R1: RSVP PATH (Sender Tspec, Adspec)
    R1->>R2: RSVP PATH (modified Adspec)
    R2->>R3: RSVP PATH (modified Adspec)
    R3->>DST: RSVP PATH (delivered)

    Note over DST,R1: Phase 2 - Reverse Reservation Setup
    DST->>R3: RSVP RESV (Flowspec, Filter spec)
    R3->>R2: RSVP RESV (reserve buffer + bandwidth)
    R2->>R1: RSVP RESV (reserve buffer + bandwidth)
    R1->>SRC: RSVP RESV (confirmation)

    Note over SRC,DST: Phase 3 - Data Transfer with QoS
    SRC->>R1: DATA packets (QoS-marked)
    R1->>R2: DATA packets (forwarded with reserved priority)
    R2->>R3: DATA packets (forwarded with reserved priority)
    R3->>DST: DATA packets (delivered)

    Note over SRC,R1: Phase 4 - Soft-State Refresh
    SRC->>R1: RSVP PATH refresh (every 30s)
    DST->>R3: RSVP RESV refresh (every 30s)

    Note over SRC,DST: Phase 5 - Teardown
    SRC->>R1: RSVP PATH TEAR
    R1->>R2: RSVP PATH TEAR
    R2->>R3: RSVP PATH TEAR
    R3->>DST: RSVP PATH TEAR
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Flow Classifier Architecture

This diagram shows how a single ingress router performs the **classification → policing → marking → queuing** pipeline that constitutes a "flow setup" in a DiffServ edge node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Packet on Interface GE0/0/1] --> B[Parse L2/L3/L4 Headers]
    B --> C{Extract 5-Tuple}
    C --> D[TCAM Lookup for Microflow]
    D -->|Match Found| E[Apply Existing PHB and Action]
    D -->|No Match| F{Behavior Aggregate Classifier}
    F -->|DSCP present| G[Map DSCP to PHB]
    F -->|No DSCP| H[Apply Default PHB Best Effort]
    G --> I[Token Bucket Policer]
    H --> I[Token Bucket Policer]
    E --> I
    I -->|Conforming| J[Set MPLS EXP or DSCP]
    I -->|Non-Conforming| K{Action}
    K -->|Drop| L[Discard Packet]
    K -->|Mark Down| J
    J --> M[Enqueue into QoS Class]
    M --> N[Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing]
    N --> O[Transmit on Output Interface GE0/0/2]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every inbound packet passes through a two-tier classifier. First, an exact-match TCAM lookup tries to find a per-microflow entry. On miss, a Behavior Aggregate (BA) classifier uses the DSCP bits to assign a Per-Hop Behavior (PHB). A token bucket policer then enforces the contract before the packet is queued. This is the operational definition of "flow setup enforcement" at an edge router.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — IPv6 Native Flow Label Mechanism

IPv6 carries a dedicated 20-bit **Flow Label** field in its main header (RFC 6437), making it the only mainstream L3 protocol with *native* flow support. The flow label is set pseudo-randomly by the source to identify a flow without needing deep-packet inspection at every router.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IPv6 Header 40 bytes
    H[Version 4 bits] --> I[Traffic Class 8 bits]
    I --> J[Flow Label 20 bits]
    J --> K[Payload Length 16 bits]
    K --> L[Next Header 8 bits]
    L --> M[Hop Limit 8 bits]
    M --> N[Source Address 128 bits]
    N --> O[Destination Address 128 bits]
    end
    O --> P[Router forwarding decision]
    P --> Q{Destination + Flow Label hash}
    Q -->|Hit| R[Apply pre-installed flow cache entry]
    Q -->|Miss| S[Perform full FIB lookup and install cache]
    R --> T[Fast forward to egress]
    S --> T
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Although IPv6 *defines* the Flow Label, **most real-world routers (Cisco IOS, Juniper Junos, Linux kernel) ignore it** by default. It is enabled in specialized contexts (e.g., MPLS-TE explicit paths, mobile backhaul with 6rd/6PE). Examiners love asking this trick question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a "flow" in the context of TCP/IP internetworking. Specify the components of a standard 5-tuple flow identifier.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

A *flow* is a sequence of packets sent from a particular source to a particular destination that the source (and intermediate routers) desires to be treated as a single logical entity for the purpose of forwarding, QoS, security, and accounting. *(1 mark)*

The standard 5-tuple flow identifier consists of: *(2 marks)*

1. **Source IP Address** (32 bits for IPv4, 128 bits for IPv6)
2. **Destination IP Address** (32 bits for IPv4, 128 bits for IPv6)
3. **Source Port Number** (16 bits, from TCP/UDP header)
4. **Destination Port Number** (16 bits, from TCP/UDP header)
5. **Protocol Number** (8 bits, e.g., 6 for TCP, 17 for UDP from the IP header's Protocol field)

Together, these five values uniquely identify a unidirectional microflow.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between IntServ and DiffServ approaches to flow-based QoS. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | IntServ (Integrated Services) | DiffServ (Differentiated Services) |
|---|---|---|
| State location | **Per-flow** at every router along the path | **Per-class aggregate** at edge routers only |
| Signaling protocol | RSVP (Resource Reservation Protocol) | None in data plane; DSCP markings in TOS byte |
| Scalability | **Poor** in the core (O(N) state) | **Excellent** (O(1) state per class) |
| Resource guarantee | Hard end-to-end (CBR, VBR service classes) | Soft, class-based statistical guarantees |

**Advantage of IntServ (1 mark):** Provides *explicit, end-to-end QoS guarantees* (bandwidth, delay, jitter) for individual flows — ideal for mission-critical real-time traffic.

**Advantage of DiffServ (1 mark):** Highly **scalable** because core routers process only the 6-bit DSCP field and need no per-flow state — deployable across the entire Internet.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark ESE Module Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Never confuse "flow" with "connection."** A TCP connection is an *end-to-end* application concept. A *flow* is a *forwarding-plane* abstraction at L3/L2.5. Examiners allocate **1 mark** purely for this distinction.
> 2. **Always state units** in numerical derivations. Writing "ρ = 64" without specifying kbps loses 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Do not skip the "why."** Just listing the 5-tuple without explaining *why* each field is needed is a common mistake costing up to **1 mark**.
> 4. **Draw the diagram.** RSVP/flow setup questions require a labeled sequence diagram or block diagram. Omitting it costs **2 marks** minimum.
> 5. **Avoid one-liners.** KTU model answers average 8–12 lines per 7-mark sub-part.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sequence diagram, explain the **RSVP flow setup process** in detail. Discuss PATH and RESV messages and the role of soft-state refresh. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A token bucket policer has parameters $\rho = 128$ kbps and $\beta = 16{,}000$ bits. Compute:
   - (i) The maximum instantaneous burst size in bytes.
   - (ii) The time required to fully refill an empty bucket.
   - (iii) The long-term average rate enforced. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

**Solution to (a):**

The **Resource Reservation Protocol (RSVP)** is a signaling protocol defined in **RFC 2205** that provides **integrated services (IntServ)** by reserving resources along the path of a flow *before* data transmission begins. It operates at the network layer (IP protocol number **46**) and uses *soft state* — reservations must be periodically refreshed, otherwise they are automatically torn down. *(1 mark for definition + RFC)*

**Phase 1 — PATH Message (Sender → Receiver):** *(2 marks)*

The sender transmits an RSVP **PATH** message toward the destination. This message contains:
- **Sender Tspec** — describes the traffic characteristics of the flow (peak rate, average rate, bucket size, max packet size).
- **Adspec (Advertising Spec)** — carries path-dependent information. As the message traverses each router, the router updates the Adspec to advertise available services and link characteristics along the route.
- **Previous Hop Address (PHOP)** — the IP address of the previous RSVP-capable router (used for reverse-path RESV messages).

The PATH message establishes the *reverse path* state needed for RESV messages to follow back to the sender. **Crucially, PATH does not itself reserve resources** — it only discovers the path and advertises capability.

**Phase 2 — RESV Message (Receiver → Sender):** *(2 marks)*

Upon receiving the PATH message, the receiver responds with an RSVP **RESV** message flowing *backwards* along the exact reverse path. The RESV message carries:
- **Flowspec** — the desired QoS (e.g., bandwidth = 64 kbps, delay bound = 100 ms, buffer = 16 KB).
- **Filter spec** — identifies which packets belong to the flow (the 5-tuple + possibly sender IP).

At each hop, the router performs **admission control**: if it can satisfy the requested Flowspec given current load, it reserves buffer space and schedules priority, then forwards the RESV upstream. If not, the reservation fails and an RESV ERROR is returned.

**Phase 3 — Soft-State Refresh:** *(1 mark)*

RSVP does NOT maintain hard, permanent state. Both sender and receiver re-send PATH and RESV messages every **$J = 30$ seconds** (default). Each router's state has a lifetime of **$R = K \times J = 90$ seconds** (with $K = 3$). If refreshes stop arriving, the router automatically purges the reservation. This provides **robustness** against router crashes and graceful degradation under network failures.

**Phase 4 — Teardown:**
PATH TEAR and RESV TEAR messages provide immediate explicit cleanup when the application ends.

**Diagram (mandatory for full marks):**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R1 as Router 1
    participant R2 as Router 2
    participant Rec as Receiver
    S->>R1: PATH (Tspec, Adspec)
    R1->>R2: PATH (Adspec updated)
    R2->>Rec: PATH (Adspec updated)
    Rec->>R2: RESV (Flowspec, Filterspec)
    R2->>R1: RESV (admit, reserve)
    R1->>S: RESV (admit, reserve)
    S->>R1: DATA (QoS-marked)
    R1->>R2: DATA
    R2->>Rec: DATA
    loop Every 30s
        S->>R1: PATH refresh
        Rec->>R2: RESV refresh
    end
```

*(1 mark reserved for the diagram quality and correct arrow directions.)*

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Token rate: $\rho = 128$ kbps = $128 \times 10^3$ bits/sec
- Bucket size: $\beta = 16{,}000$ bits

**Sub-part (i) — Maximum instantaneous burst in bytes:** *(2 marks)*

$$\beta_{bytes} = \frac{\beta_{bits}}{8} = \frac{16{,}000 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = 2{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Time to refill an empty bucket:** *(2 marks)*

$$T_{fill} = \frac{\beta}{\rho} = \frac{16{,}000 \text{ bits}}{128{,}000 \text{ bits/sec}} = 0.125 \text{ seconds} = 125 \text{ milliseconds}$$

**Sub-part (iii) — Long-term average rate:** *(1 mark)*

The token bucket enforces a sustained long-term rate exactly equal to the fill rate:

$$\bar{R} = \rho = 128 \text{ kbps}$$

**Why these values matter (explanation, 2 marks):** The bucket allows a 2000-byte instantaneous burst (suitable for a small file chunk or one video frame), but enforces that over any sufficiently long period, the source cannot exceed 128 kbps. This is the canonical traffic contract for **a single high-quality voice channel** or **low-bandwidth video telemetry** in QoS-aware networks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **flow aggregation** and **microflow vs. macroflow**. Discuss how aggregation is implemented using the **DSCP** field and the role of the **Behavior Aggregate (BA) classifier**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A VoIP source using the G.729 codec generates packets of **20 bytes payload + 12 bytes RTP header + 8 bytes UDP header + 20 bytes IPv4 header + 14 bytes Ethernet header** every **20 ms**.
   - (i) Compute the wire rate on the access link in **kbps**.
   - (ii) If a token bucket of $\rho = 32$ kbps and $\beta = 800$ bytes is used, will this flow be admitted? Show the calculation. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

**Solution to (a):**

**Definition of Flow Aggregation:** *(2 marks)*
Flow aggregation is the process of combining multiple individual microflows into a smaller number of *aggregate flows* (or macroflows) for the purpose of simplifying QoS handling in the network core. Instead of maintaining per-microflow state at every router, the network maintains state only for the aggregate, dramatically reducing the state burden in the core.

**Microflow vs. Macroflow:** *(2 marks)*

- A **Microflow** is a single end-to-end conversation between two applications, uniquely identified by the complete 5-tuple $\{S_{IP}, D_{IP}, S_{port}, D_{port}, P\}$. For example, a single video call between a student in Trivandrum and a professor in Kochi is one microflow. Microflows are typically classified at the **edge** of the network where intelligence is concentrated.
- A **Macroflow (Aggregate Flow)** is a collection of microflows grouped by a common criterion — for example, all microflows with the same DSCP value, or all microflows with the same destination network prefix. The network core treats an aggregate as a single forwarding entity.

**DSCP and the Behavior Aggregate Classifier:** *(2 marks)*

The **Differentiated Services Code Point (DSCP)** is a **6-bit** field in the **Type of Service (ToS)** byte of the IPv4 header (and the equivalent **Traffic Class** byte in IPv6). The first 3 bits historically formed the IP Precedence field; the full 6 bits are now the DSCP.

The **Behavior Aggregate (BA) Classifier** at a router uses *only* the DSCP field to determine the **Per-Hop Behavior (PHB)** of a packet. There are three standard PHBs:

- **Default Forwarding (DF)** — DSCP = 000000, best-effort.
- **Assured Forwarding (AF)** — four classes (AF1x, AF2x, AF3x, AF4x) each with three drop precedences (x = 1, 2, 3). Example: AF41 = DSCP 100010.
- **Expedited Forwarding (EF)** — DSCP = 101110, for premium service (VoIP).

Because the BA classifier only inspects **6 bits** (or even fewer with class-selector compatibility), the lookup is **O(1)** and hardware-friendly — a key reason DiffServ scales to the global Internet.

**Practical Example (1 mark for synthesis):** A campus edge router classifies 10,000 individual VoIP microflows and marks them all with **DSCP EF**. The ISP core router sees only one aggregate "EF" class and provides it with low-loss, low-jitter forwarding. State is reduced by a factor of 10,000.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Payload: 20 bytes
- RTP header: 12 bytes
- UDP header: 8 bytes
- IPv4 header: 20 bytes
- Ethernet header: 14 bytes
- Inter-packet period: $T = 20$ ms

**Sub-part (i) — Wire rate calculation:** *(3 marks)*

Total packet size on the wire:

$$L_{total} = 20 + 12 + 8 + 20 + 14 = 74 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to bits:

$$L_{bits} = 74 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 592 \text{ bits}$$

Number of packets per second:

$$f_{pps} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.020 \text{ s}} = 50 \text{ packets/sec}$$

Wire rate:

$$R = 592 \text{ bits} \times 50 \text{ pps} = 29{,}600 \text{ bps} \approx 29.6 \text{ kbps}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Admission test:** *(4 marks)*

For a periodic source, the admission criterion is:

$$\rho \ge \frac{L_{max}}{T} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta \ge L_{max}$$

Compute the source's required parameters:

$$\rho_{req} = \frac{592 \text{ bits}}{0.020 \text{ s}} = 29{,}600 \text{ bps} \approx 29.6 \text{ kbps}$$

$$\beta_{req} = L_{max} = 74 \text{ bytes}$$

Compare with the policer's offer:

| Parameter | Required by flow | Offered by policer | Satisfied? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | 29.6 kbps | 32 kbps | **Yes** (29.6 ≤ 32) |
| $\beta$ | 74 bytes | 800 bytes | **Yes** (74 ≤ 800) |

**Verdict:** The flow is **admitted**.
