The policer provides a 32 kbps channel, which exceeds the source's average demand of 29.6 kbps by a margin of **2.4 kbps (~7.5%)**, providing headroom for occasional jitter bursts. The burst capacity of 800 bytes is more than 10× the packet size, so no single packet will be dropped.

**Note for full marks:** The actual G.729 codec has a payload of 10 bytes (not 20). The 20-byte figure in the question is a *deliberate stress test*; the calculation method is identical.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential points to remember at a glance (high-frequency KTU recall):**

- A **flow** is a forwarding-plane construct, **NOT** a connection.
- Standard flow identifier = **5-tuple** $\{S_{IP}, D_{IP}, S_{port}, D_{port}, P\}$.
- **IntServ** = per-flow state at every router; uses **RSVP**; poor scalability.
- **DiffServ** = aggregate QoS classes via **DSCP (6 bits)**; excellent scalability.
- **RSVP** uses **soft state** with refresh $J = 30$ s, multiplier $K = 3$, lifetime $R = 90$ s.
- **Token Bucket** is the canonical traffic-policing primitive: rate $\rho$, burst $\beta$.
- Long-term average rate of a token-bucketed source **converges to $\rho$** as $T \to \infty$.
- Bucket refill time: $T_{fill} = \beta / \rho$ — always check unit consistency.
- IPv6 has a **native 20-bit Flow Label** in its header, but most routers ignore it.
- **MPLS** uses RSVP-TE to set up LSPs with bandwidth reservations (RFC 3209).
- **5G core** uses GTP-U + PFCP for per-PDU-session flow setup (3GPP TS 29.244).
- **OpenFlow** `OFPT_FLOW_MOD` = modern programmatic flow setup; controller-installable.
- VoIP traffic is typically marked with **DSCP EF (46 = 101110₂)**.
- A microflow is end-to-end; a macroflow is an aggregate classified by DSCP or prefix.
- **Admit-or-reject** logic: $\rho_{req} \le \rho_{offer}$ **AND** $\beta_{req} \le \beta_{offer}$.
- The **Behavior Aggregate (BA) classifier** operates on DSCP only — fast, scalable, hardware-friendly.
- For G.711 voice: 160 bytes every 20 ms = 64 kbps (the textbook standard).
- PATH message = forward (no reservation); RESV message = reverse (reserves resources).
- **Soft state** = robust to crashes; **Hard state** = explicit teardown required.
- **Flow setup ≠ Connection setup**: a flow can be unidirectional and may use UDP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Large Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Large Network"?

A **Large Network** in TCP/IP terminology refers to an internetwork that has outgrown the limits of a single administrative domain and a single routing protocol. When the number of routers, links, and subnets reaches a point where:

- A single routing table cannot hold all destinations efficiently,
- Routing updates consume excessive bandwidth and CPU,
- A single administrative policy cannot govern the entire topology,
- Failure in one part of the network must be isolated from the whole,

...we say the network has crossed the threshold into the **Large Network regime**. The Internet itself is the canonical example — it is composed of tens of thousands of independently administered networks stitched together through standardized inter-domain routing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A *Large Network* is an internetwork composed of multiple **Autonomous Systems (AS)** interconnected via standardized routing protocols, employing **hierarchical addressing**, **route aggregation**, and **policy-based path selection** to ensure scalability, stability, and administrative autonomy.

### 1.2 Core Problems a Large Network Must Solve

| # | Problem | Symptom in a Flat Network |
|---|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Routing Table Explosion** | Every router must know every prefix; tables grow into millions of entries |
| 2 | **Excessive Routing Traffic** | Periodic broadcasts flood the entire topology |
| 3 | **Slow Convergence** | Count-to-infinity, routing loops |
| 4 | **No Administrative Boundaries** | One company's policies affect another's network |
| 5 | **Poor Fault Isolation** | A single failure propagates globally |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Indian National Highway System**

Think of India’s highway network. It is **not one giant system** — it is layered:

- **Village Roads (Access Layer)** — connect individual houses to towns. Each village manages its own roads.
- **State Highways (Distribution Layer)** — connect towns within a state, managed by state PWD.
- **National Highways (Backbone Layer)** — connect states, managed centrally by NHAI.

You don't need every village to know every other village's road layout — only how to reach the nearest town, and how to get onto the national highway.

A **Large Network applies the exact same logic**:
- **Access Layer** = individual subnets and LANs
- **Distribution Layer** = intranets of organizations (intra-AS routing with **IGP**)
- **Backbone Layer** = inter-organization routing (inter-AS with **BGP**)

This is **hierarchical routing**, the central idea behind every large network.

### 1.4 The Three Foundational Building Blocks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Large Network Design:**
> 1. **Autonomous System (AS)** — administrative domain
> 2. **Hierarchical Routing** — divide routing into Intra-AS and Inter-AS
> 3. **Address Aggregation (CIDR/Supernetting)** — shrink routing tables

All other topics in this module (RIP, OSPF, BGP, CIDR, Supernetting) are built on these three pillars.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Autonomous Systems (AS) — The Administrative Atom

An **Autonomous System** is a collection of connected IP networks operated by a single organization (ISP, university, government, corporation) that presents a **common external routing policy** to the rest of the Internet.

Every AS is identified by a globally unique **16-bit or 32-bit AS Number (ASN)** assigned by IANA / Regional Internet Registries (RIRs).

> [!NOTE]
> **16-bit ASN range:** 1 to 65535 (1 to 64511 public, 64512 to 65535 private)
> **32-bit ASN range:** 65536 to 4294967295

**Two flavors of routing protocols operate over ASes:**
- **Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP)** — operates *inside* an AS. Examples: **RIP, OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP**.
- **Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP)** — operates *between* ASes. The only EGP in production use today is **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)**.

## 2.2 Hierarchical Routing — The Mathematical Justification

In a flat network with $N$ destinations, every router maintains $N$ entries. With hierarchical routing, the table is split:

$$T_{flat} = N \quad \rightarrow \quad T_{hier} = K + \frac{N}{K}$$

where:
- $N$ = total number of destinations
- $K$ = number of hierarchical groups (regions, subnets, AS partitions)
- $K$ entries point to the *region* and $N/K$ entries point to *hosts* within the current region.

For typical values ($N = 10^6$, $K = 10^3$):

$$T_{hier} \approx 10^3 + 10^3 = 2000 \;\;\; \text{(vs. } 10^6 \text{ flat)}$$

This is a **500-fold reduction** in routing table size.

## 2.3 RIP — Routing Information Protocol (Distance Vector)

RIP is the simplest IGP. It uses **hop count** as the sole metric.

**Operational rules:**
1. Maximum hop count = **15**; 16 = unreachable (infinity).
2. Every router sends its entire routing table to **directly connected neighbors** every **30 seconds**.
3. Uses **UDP port 520**.
4. Metric update rule (Bellman-Ford):

$$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in \text{neighbors}} \left[ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right]$$

where $c(x,v)$ is the cost of the link from router $x$ to neighbor $v$, and $D_v(y)$ is the distance from $v$ to destination $y$.

5. **Split Horizon with Poison Reverse** is used to prevent routing loops.
6. **Triggered Updates** speed up convergence.

## 2.4 OSPF — Open Shortest Path First (Link State)

OSPF uses **Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm** on a complete topology map (Link-State Database / LSDB).

**Key features:**
1. Hierarchical design with **Areas** — Area 0 (Backbone) is mandatory.
2. **Cost metric:** 
$$C_{interface} = \left\lfloor \frac{BW_{reference}}{BW_{interface}} \right\rfloor$$
Standard reference bandwidth = **10^8 bps (100 Mbps)**. Minimum cost = 1.
3. Uses **IP protocol number 89** (not TCP/UDP).
4. **Link-State Advertisements (LSAs)** are flooded reliably within an area.
5. Supports **VLSM** and **CIDR** natively.
6. Fast convergence (typically < 1 second).

**Dijkstra's update rule:**
$$D(v) = \min_{u \in \text{visited}} \left[ D(u) + w(u,v) \right]$$

## 2.5 BGP — Border Gateway Protocol (Path Vector)

BGP is the **only EGP** used on the Internet today. It is a **path-vector** protocol that carries the full **AS-PATH** in each advertisement.

**Key attributes (in priority order for path selection):**

| Priority | Attribute | Meaning |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **WEIGHT** | Cisco-local, higher is better |
| 2 | **LOCAL_PREF** | AS-wide preference, higher is better |
| 3 | **Locally originated** | Prefer routes this router originated |
| 4 | **AS_PATH** | Shorter is better |
| 5 | **ORIGIN** | IGP < EGP < INCOMPLETE |
| 6 | **MED** | Multi-Exit Discriminator, lower is better |
| 7 | **eBGP > iBGP** | External over internal |
| 8 | **IGP metric to NEXT_HOP** | Lower is better |
| 9 | **Oldest path** | Tie-breaker |
| 10 | **Lowest Router ID** | Final tie-breaker |

**Connection facts:**
- Uses **TCP port 179**.
- Two flavors: **eBGP** (between ASes) and **iBGP** (within an AS).
- Supports **policy-based routing** — administrators can filter, prefer, or reject routes based on policy.

## 2.6 CIDR & Supernetting

**Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)** replaced the rigid Class A/B/C system to allow prefix lengths of any length. It enables **supernetting** — aggregating multiple contiguous classful networks into a single shorter prefix.

**Aggregation rule:** Find the **longest common bit prefix** of all component addresses.

$$L_{aggregated} = \max_{L} \left[ \text{all addresses share first } L \text{ bits} \right]$$

For $k$ networks of size $\vert N \vert$ each, aggregation requires that all $k$ networks be **contiguous** and **power-of-two aligned**, giving:

$$L_{aggregated} = 32 - \log_2(k)$$

provided the block size $\vert N_{block} \vert = k \cdot \vert N \vert$ is a power of two.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Time Quick Reference — All key formulas for Large Networks**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary / Unit |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Flat routing table size | $T_{flat} = N$ | $N$ = destinations |
| Hierarchical table size | $T_{hier} = K + N/K$ | $K$ = groups |
| Bellman-Ford (RIP) update | $D_x(y) = \min_v \left[ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right]$ | Hop count (integer) |
| RIP max metric | 15 hops = 16 unreachable | $0 \le \text{metric} \le 15$ |
| RIP update interval | 30 s | seconds |
| RIP transport | UDP | Port **520** |
| OSPF cost | $C = \left\lfloor BW_{ref} / BW_{iface} \right\rfloor$ | Reference BW = $10^8$ bps |
| OSPF minimum cost | $\min C = 1$ | dimensionless |
| OSPF transport | Direct IP | Protocol **89** |
| BGP transport | TCP | Port **179** |
| AS_PATH length preference | Shorter wins | Hop count of ASes |
| Aggregation prefix length | $L = 32 - \log_2(k)$ | $k$ = # of networks |
| Number of aggregatable nets | $k = 2^{32-L}$ | L = aggregated prefix |
| Subnets from CIDR $/n$ | $2^{32-n}$ host addresses | addressable units |
| Dijkstra update | $D(v) = \min_u \left[ D(u) + w(u,v) \right]$ | shortest cumulative cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas govern how ISPs in India (Airtel, Jio, BSNL, Tata Communications) allocate address blocks, design their backbone OSPF areas, and run BGP sessions to peer with each other and with upstream providers. The **IN-APNIC** registry in Chennai hands out CIDR blocks using precisely these calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations and Worked Numerical Examples

This section is exam-oriented. Each worked example below has been hand-crafted to mirror the calculation style expected in KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

### 3.1.1 Worked Example 1 — OSPF Cost Calculation

**Problem:** Compute the OSPF cost of the following interface if the reference bandwidth is $10^8$ bps: a **Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps)** link.

**Step 1 — Identify the bandwidth:**

$$BW_{interface} = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the reference bandwidth:**

$$BW_{ref} = 10^8 \text{ bps} = 100 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the cost formula:**

$$C_{interface} = \left\lfloor \frac{BW_{ref}}{BW_{iface}} \right\rfloor$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad C = \left\lfloor \frac{10^8}{10^9} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the fraction:**

$$C = \left\lfloor 0.1 \right\rfloor$$

**Step 5 — Apply the minimum cost rule:**

OSPF defines the minimum cost as **1**, never 0 (a cost of 0 would mean the link is "free" and Dijkstra would behave incorrectly).

$$C_{final} = \max \left( 1, \; \left\lfloor \frac{BW_{ref}}{BW_{iface}} \right\rfloor \right) = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The OSPF cost of a 1 Gbps link = **1** (with default reference bandwidth).

**Practical implication:** Since modern links (1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 100 Gbps) all yield cost = 1 with the default reference, real-world OSPF deployments tune `auto-cost reference-bandwidth` to a higher value (e.g., 10 Gbps or 100 Gbps) so that link speeds are distinguishable.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Example 2 — Dijkstra's Algorithm on a Link-State Topology

**Problem:** Use **Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm** to compute the shortest path from source node **A** to every other node in the graph below.

**Given topology and link costs:**

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| A–B  | 4    |
| A–C  | 2    |
| B–C  | 1    |
| B–D  | 5    |
| C–D  | 8    |
| C–E  | 10   |
| D–E  | 2    |

**Initialization (Step 0):**

$$D(A) = 0, \quad D(B) = \infty, \quad D(C) = \infty, \quad D(D) = \infty, \quad D(E) = \infty$$

Visited set: $\mathcal{V} = \{ A \}$, Tentative set: $\mathcal{T} = \{ B, C, D, E \}$.

---

**Iteration 1 — Settle node A (smallest tentative distance = 0):**

Visit A. Relax its outgoing edges:
- A → B: new distance = $0 + 4 = 4 < \infty$, so $D(B) = 4$, predecessor $p(B) = A$.
- A → C: new distance = $0 + 2 = 2 < \infty$, so $D(C) = 2$, predecessor $p(C) = A$.

$$\mathcal{T} = \{ B:4, \; C:2, \; D:\infty, \; E:\infty \}$$

---

**Iteration 2 — Settle node C (smallest tentative = 2):**

Visit C. Relax outgoing edges of C:
- C → B: new distance = $2 + 1 = 3 < 4$, so $D(B) = 3$, predecessor $p(B) = C$.
- C → D: new distance = $2 + 8 = 10 < \infty$, so $D(D) = 10$, predecessor $p(D) = C$.
- C → E: new distance = $2 + 10 = 12 < \infty$, so $D(E) = 12$, predecessor $p(E) = C$.

$$\mathcal{T} = \{ B:3, \; D:10, \; E:12 \}$$

---

**Iteration 3 — Settle node B (smallest tentative = 3):**

Visit B. Relax outgoing edges of B:
- B → D: new distance = $3 + 5 = 8 < 10$, so $D(D) = 8$, predecessor $p(D) = B$.

$$\mathcal{T} = \{ D:8, \; E:12 \}$$

---

**Iteration 4 — Settle node D (smallest tentative = 8):**

Visit D. Relax outgoing edges of D:
- D → E: new distance = $8 + 2 = 10 < 12$, so $D(E) = 10$, predecessor $p(E) = D$.

$$\mathcal{T} = \{ E:10 \}$$

---

**Iteration 5 — Settle node E (smallest tentative = 10):**

Visit E. No unvisited neighbors remain. Algorithm terminates.

---

**Final Shortest-Path Tree from A:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D(A) &= 0, \quad \text{path} = \{A\} \\
D(B) &= 3, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \\
D(C) &= 2, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow C \\
D(D) &= 8, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \\
D(E) &= 10, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow C \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow E
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Example 3 — CIDR Supernetting

**Problem:** An ISP owns the following four contiguous Class C networks:
- 200.1.0.0/24
- 200.1.1.0/24
- 200.1.2.0/24
- 200.1.3.0/24

Aggregate these into a **single supernet** advertisement.

**Step 1 — Convert the third octet of each network to 8-bit binary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
200.1.0.0 &\Rightarrow \text{third octet} = 0000\,0000 \\
200.1.1.0 &\Rightarrow \text{third octet} = 0000\,0001 \\
200.1.2.0 &\Rightarrow \text{third octet} = 0000\,0010 \\
200.1.3.0 &\Rightarrow \text{third octet} = 0000\,0011
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the longest common bit prefix:**

The first **6 bits** (000000) are identical across all four octets. The remaining 2 bits vary (00, 01, 10, 11).

$$L_{common} = 8 + 8 + 6 = 22 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the aggregated prefix length:**

The original /24 prefix contributed 24 bits. We added 2 more bits to cover all 4 networks, so the new prefix length is:

$$L_{aggregated} = 24 - \log_2(4) = 24 - 2 = 22 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the aggregated network address:**

Keep the first 22 bits, set the remaining 10 bits to 0:

$$200.1.0.0/22$$

**Step 5 — Verify the block size:**

$$N_{addresses} = 2^{32-22} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ addresses}$$

This covers 200.1.0.0 through 200.1.3.255 — exactly our four /24 networks. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The aggregated supernet is **200.1.0.0/22**, advertised as a single BGP route.

---

### 3.1.4 Worked Example 4 — RIP Convergence Using Bellman-Ford

**Problem:** Three routers A, B, C in a line: A — B — C. Initial routing tables:

| Router | To Network X | Via | Metric |
|--------|--------------|-----|--------|
| A      | X            | —   | 0 (direct) |
| B      | X            | A   | 1 |
| C      | X            | B   | 2 |

Now the link **A–X** fails. Show the distance-vector convergence using Bellman-Ford until stability.

**Round 1 (after A–X failure, A sends metric = 16 to B):**

- A advertises X with metric 16 (infinity) to B.
- B recalculates: was metric 1, now B sees 16+1 = 17 (still > 16), so B sets its metric to 16.

$$D_B(X) = 16 \quad \text{(unreachable)}$$

- C receives update from B: was metric 2, now sees 16+1 = 17 → set to 16.

$$D_C(X) = 16 \quad \text{(unreachable)}$$

**Round 2 (stability):** No further updates change. All routers correctly report X as unreachable. **Convergence achieved** after 1 update cycle (under split-horizon).

> [!NOTE]
> **Without split-horizon and poison-reverse**, B might still advertise the old metric 1 to C in the next round, causing C to oscillate. This is why RIP requires these loop-prevention mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Routing — Three-Tier Architecture

This diagram illustrates the **hierarchical routing structure** that any large network uses. Compare it to the highway analogy from Section 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph coreTier["BACKBONE TIER - Inter-AS Routing via BGP"]
        coreNodeA["Core Router CR1"]
        coreNodeB["Core Router CR2"]
        coreNodeC["Core Router CR3"]
    end

    subgraph distTierA["DISTRIBUTION TIER - Intra-AS Routing via OSPF Area 0"]
        distNodeA1["Area 0 Router AR1"]
        distNodeA2["Area 0 Router AR2"]
    end

    subgraph distTierB["DISTRIBUTION TIER - Intra-AS Routing via OSPF Area 1"]
        distNodeB1["Area 1 Router BR1"]
        distNodeB2["Area 1 Router BR2"]
    end

    subgraph accTier["ACCESS TIER - LAN and Subnet Edge"]
        accNode1["Access Router SW1 - Subnet 10.1.0.0/24"]
        accNode2["Access Router SW2 - Subnet 10.1.1.0/24"]
        accNode3["Access Router SW3 - Subnet 10.2.0.0/24"]
        accNode4["Access Router SW4 - Subnet 10.3.0.0/24"]
    end

    coreNodeA --- coreNodeB
    coreNodeB --- coreNodeC
    coreNodeA --- coreNodeC

    coreNodeA --- distNodeA1
    coreNodeB --- distNodeA2
    coreNodeB --- distNodeB1
    coreNodeC --- distNodeB2

    distNodeA1 --- accNode1
    distNodeA1 --- accNode2
    distNodeB1 --- accNode3
    distNodeB2 --- accNode4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each tier uses a different protocol class. Backbone = **BGP**, Distribution = **OSPF with Areas**, Access = typically direct routing or static routes. Information summarization happens at tier boundaries.

---

## 4.2 Autonomous System Interconnection — IGP vs EGP

This diagram shows how multiple **Autonomous Systems** connect via **BGP (eBGP)** at their borders and run **OSPF** internally.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph asBlockA["AUTONOMOUS SYSTEM 65001 - Tier-1 ISP North"]
        asArouter1["Edge Router ER-A1"]
        asArouter2["Core Router CR-A1"]
        asArouter3["Core Router CR-A2"]
    end

    subgraph asBlockB["AUTONOMOUS SYSTEM 65002 - Tier-2 ISP South"]
        asBrouter1["Edge Router ER-B1"]
        asBrouter2["Core Router CR-B1"]
    end

    subgraph asBlockC["AUTONOMOUS SYSTEM 65003 - Enterprise Customer"]
        asCrouter1["Edge Router ER-C1"]
        asCrouter2["Internal Router IR-C1"]
    end

    asArouter2 --- asArouter3
    asArouter1 --- asArouter2

    asBrouter1 --- asBrouter2

    asCrouter1 --- asCrouter2

    asArouter1 ===|eBGP Session - TCP 179| asBrouter1
    asBrouter1 ===|eBGP Session| asCrouter1

    asArouter2 ---|iBGP| asArouter3
    asArouter2 ---|OSPF Area 0| asArouter1

    asBrouter2 ---|OSPF Area 5| asBrouter1

    asCrouter2 ---|OSPF Area 12| asCrouter1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observations:**
> - **eBGP sessions** (bold double lines) sit between ASes — between ER-A1 and ER-B1, between ER-B1 and ER-C1.
> - **iBGP and OSPF** operate inside each AS — they never cross the AS boundary.
> - The AS number is globally unique and is appended to BGP AS_PATH on every outbound advertisement.

---

## 4.3 CIDR Supernetting — Aggregation Flow

This diagram illustrates how four contiguous /24 networks collapse into one /22 advertisement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA["200.1.0.0 / 24"]
    nB["200.1.1.0 / 24"]
    nC["200.1.2.0 / 24"]
    nD["200.1.3.0 / 24"]

    nA --> agg["Aggregated Supernetwork 200.1.0.0 / 22 - Block of 1024 addresses"]
    nB --> agg
    nC --> agg
    nD --> agg

    agg --> bgpAdv["Single BGP Route Advertisement to Upstream Provider"]
    bgpAdv --> ispPeer["ISP Upstream Router - One Entry Replaces Four"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering value:** Four routing-table entries at the upstream ISP collapse to one. For an ISP aggregating hundreds of thousands of /24s, the table-size savings become massive.

---

## 4.4 RIP Distance-Vector Update Cycle

This diagram traces a single RIP convergence cycle after a link failure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rtA["Router A - Direct Link to Net X Fails"]
    rtA -->|"Metric 16 to X"| rtB["Router B - Receives Update"]
    rtB -->|"Recalculate: 16 + 1 = 17 > 16"| rtBupd["B Marks X as Unreachable - Metric 16"]
    rtBupd -->|"Periodic 30-second advertisement"| rtC["Router C - Receives Update"]
    rtC -->|"Recalculate: 16 + 1 = 17 > 16"| rtCupd["C Marks X as Unreachable"]
    rtCupd --> stability["Network Converged - All Routers Agree X is Down"]
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — When Mermaid Cannot Draw Physics

For complex scenarios (e.g., full Internet topology visualization), Mermaid falls back to a **functional block architecture** mapping the data flow between architectural layers of a large network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userLAN["User LAN - 10.0.0.0/24 - Ethernet Switch"] --> arpLayer["ARP Resolution - MAC to IP Binding"]
    arpLayer --> defaultGW["Default Gateway - Router R1"]
    defaultGW --> subnetRoute["Subnet Route Lookup in Routing Table"]
    subnetRoute --> igpProcess["IGP Decision - OSPF SPF Computation"]
    igpProcess --> asbr["AS Border Router - ASBR R2"]
    asbr --> bgpLookup["BGP Table Lookup - Best Path Selection"]
    bgpLookup --> policyCheck["Policy Filter - Inbound and Outbound Route Maps"]
    policyCheck --> ebgpSession["eBGP TCP Session - Port 179 to Neighbor AS"]
    ebgpSession --> remoteAS["Remote AS - Upstream Provider Network"]
    remoteAS --> destNet["Destination Network Reached"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **functional flow** mirrors what happens inside the routers of Airtel/Jio when a user in Kerala browses a website hosted in the USA — the request traverses Access → Distribution → Backbone → Remote AS, each governed by the protocol class appropriate to that tier.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Explain the concept of an Autonomous System. Differentiate between Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) and Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP).**

**Model Answer:**

An **Autonomous System (AS)** is a collection of IP networks and routers under the control of a single administrative authority (such as an ISP, an enterprise, or a university), which presents a **common external routing policy** to the rest of the Internet. Each AS is identified by a unique **AS Number (ASN)** assigned by IANA / APNIC.

| Parameter | IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol) | EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Scope | Inside a single AS | Between different ASes |
| Examples | RIP, OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP | BGP (only operational EGP) |
| Algorithm class | Distance Vector or Link State | Path Vector |
| Metric | Hop count, cost, bandwidth, delay | AS_PATH length, policy attributes |
| Trust model | All routers trust each other | Strict policy-based filtering |
| Optimizes for | Fast convergence, low overhead | Policy compliance, stability |
| Transport | UDP (RIP), IP (OSPF) | TCP (port 179) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume EIGRP is an EGP. **EIGRP is an IGP** (Cisco-proprietary). Only BGP is currently a true EGP.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**With a suitable example, explain CIDR and supernetting. Why is CIDR considered essential for the modern Internet?**

**Model Answer:**

**CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing)** is an addressing technique that replaces the rigid Class A / B / C system with variable-length prefixes. Instead of being limited to /8, /16, or /24, an address block can be any length $/$n.

**Supernetting** (or **route aggregation**) is the aggregation of multiple contiguous CIDR blocks into a single shorter prefix.

**Example:** The four contiguous networks 200.1.0.0/24, 200.1.1.0/24, 200.1.2.0/24, 200.1.3.0/24 can be aggregated into the single advertisement **200.1.0.0/22**.

**Why CIDR is essential:**
1. **Routing table reduction** — without CIDR, the global routing table would exceed millions of entries; CIDR keeps it manageable.
2. **Efficient address allocation** — APNIC hands out precisely-sized blocks (e.g., /19, /22) instead of forcing wasteful class B allocations.
3. **VLSM compatibility** — supports hierarchical subnet design within an organization.
4. **Scalability of the Internet** — current global routing table is ~900,000 prefixes vs. the 4+ billion it would be without aggregation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module-end questions in ESE carry 14 marks and offer **internal choice** between two full-length questions. You must answer **exactly one** of the two choices.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 Pattern]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Explain the **OSPF protocol** in detail. Discuss its hierarchical design with **Areas**, the concept of **Area 0 (Backbone)**, the **types of routers** (Internal, Backbone, Area Border Router, AS Boundary Router), and the **five LSA types**. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Compare and contrast **Distance Vector** and **Link State** routing protocols with suitable examples. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** is an open-standard, classless **Link-State Interior Gateway Protocol** standardized by the IETF in **RFC 2328**. It operates directly over IP (protocol number 89) and uses **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** to compute the optimal path to every destination in the topology.

**1. Hierarchical Design — Areas:**

OSPF introduces a two-level hierarchy to overcome the scalability limits of flat link-state routing.

- **Area 0 (Backbone Area):** The central transit area to which all other areas MUST connect. Acts as the "highway" between non-backbone areas.
- **Non-backbone Areas:** Each area is identified by a 32-bit Area ID. All routers inside an area maintain an identical **Link-State Database (LSDB)** for that area only.
- **Area Range summarization:** Routes can be summarized at Area Border Routers (ABRs), shrinking routing tables.

**2. Router Types:**

| Router Type | Function |
|-------------|----------|
| **Internal Router (IR)** | All interfaces in one area; holds one LSDB |
| **Backbone Router (BR)** | At least one interface in Area 0 |
| **Area Border Router (ABR)** | Interfaces in multiple areas; runs multiple SPF computations; performs summarization |
| **AS Boundary Router (ASBR)** | Connects OSPF to other routing domains (e.g., BGP); injects external routes |

**3. LSA Types (5 common types):**

| LSA Type | Name | Flooded In | Purpose |
|----------|------|------------|---------|
| 1 | Router LSA | Within area | Lists router's links and costs |
| 2 | Network LSA | Within area | Lists routers attached to a multi-access network |
| 3 | Summary LSA | Between areas | Inter-area prefix advertisement by ABR |
| 4 | ASBR Summary LSA | Between areas | Locates the ASBR |
| 5 | AS External LSA | Throughout AS | External routes injected by ASBR |

**4. OSPF Packet Types:**

1. **Hello** — discovers and maintains neighbor adjacencies
2. **DBD (Database Description)** — summarizes LSDB contents
3. **LSR (Link-State Request)** — requests specific LSAs
4. **LSU (Link-State Update)** — delivers requested LSAs
5. **LSAck (Link-State Acknowledgment)** — acknowledges LSU receipt

**5. Adjacency Formation States:**

Down → Init → 2-Way → Exstart → Exchange → Loading → Full

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget the **DR/BDR election** on multi-access networks (Ethernet). Without the Designated Router (DR) and Backup DR (BDR), OSPF would form $n(n-1)/2$ adjacencies on a $n$-router LAN — a scalability disaster. Always mention DR/BDR explicitly in multi-access topology questions.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Distance Vector | Link State |
|-----------|----------------|------------|
| **Information shared** | Entire routing table to neighbors | Link state (cost of each interface) to all routers in the area |
| **Network view** | "What my neighbor tells me" (second-hand) | "Direct knowledge of the topology" (first-hand) |
| **Algorithm** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra (SPF) |
| **Examples** | RIP, IGRP, EIGRP (hybrid) | OSPF, IS-IS |
| **Convergence speed** | Slow (minutes) | Fast (seconds) |
| **CPU & Memory** | Low | High (maintains full LSDB) |
| **Bandwidth usage** | Periodic full-table broadcasts | Event-driven incremental updates |
| **Routing loops** | Prone (count-to-infinity) | Immune (each router sees full topology) |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large networks | Good for large networks (with areas) |
| **Loop prevention** | Split horizon, poison reverse, hold-down timers | No loops by construction |

**When to use which:**
- **RIP**: small, flat, stable networks (e.g., < 15 routers).
- **OSPF**: enterprise and ISP networks with hierarchical design.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 Pattern]`

**Answer the following:**
**(a)** Explain the **Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)** in detail. Discuss the **eBGP vs iBGP** distinction, **BGP message types** (OPEN, UPDATE, NOTIFICATION, KEEPALIVE), and **important BGP attributes** (AS_PATH, NEXT_HOP, LOCAL_PREF, MED, ORIGIN). (7 Marks)
**(b)** Apply **Dijkstra's algorithm** to the network topology given below. Source node = **A**. Show all iterations and produce the **final shortest-path tree**. (7 Marks)

**Topology:**

| Edge | Cost |
|------|------|
| A–B  | 1    |
| A–D  | 4    |
| B–C  | 2    |
| B–D  | 3    |
| C–D  | 1    |
| C–E  | 5    |
| D–E  | 6    |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)** is the **path-vector Exterior Gateway Protocol** of the global Internet, standardized in **RFC 4271**. It is the only protocol that runs between Autonomous Systems and is the backbone of inter-domain routing.

**1. eBGP vs iBGP:**

| Parameter | eBGP (external BGP) | iBGP (internal BGP) |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Operates | Between two different ASes | Within a single AS |
| Peer IP | Directly connected interface typically | Logical loopback IP |
| AS_PATH | Prepended with own AS | NOT modified (loop prevention) |
| NEXT_HOP | Set to eBGP peer's IP | Preserved from eBGP |
| Local preference propagation | No | Yes (LOCAL_PREF) |
| Admin distance | 20 (Cisco) | 200 (Cisco) |

**2. BGP Message Types (over TCP port 179):**

| Message | Function |
|---------|----------|
| **OPEN** | Establishes BGP session; sends AS number, BGP RID, hold time |
| **UPDATE** | Advertises new feasible routes or withdraws unreachable routes; carries NLRI + path attributes |
| **NOTIFICATION** | Reports errors and terminates the BGP session |
| **KEEPALIVE** | Sent every 60 seconds (default) to maintain the session |
| **ROUTE-REFRESH** | (Optional) Requests a peer to resend all routes after a policy change |

**3. Important BGP Attributes:**

| Attribute | Type | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------|
| **AS_PATH** | Well-known mandatory | List of ASes traversed; used for loop detection and path selection (shorter wins) |
| **NEXT_HOP** | Well-known mandatory | IP address of the next-hop router to reach the advertised prefix |
| **ORIGIN** | Well-known mandatory | IGP (i) < EGP (e) < INCOMPLETE (?) — lower origin code preferred |
| **LOCAL_PREF** | Well-known discretionary | AS-wide preference; higher wins; used to influence outbound traffic |
| **MED (MULTI_EXIT_DISC)** | Optional non-transitive | Hints to neighbors about preferred entry point; lower wins |
| **COMMUNITY** | Optional transitive | Tag for grouping routes and applying common policy |
| **WEIGHT** | Cisco proprietary | Local-to-router preference; higher wins; not advertised to peers |

**4. BGP States:**

Idle → Connect → Active → OpenSent → OpenConfirm → Established

The **Established state** indicates a fully functional BGP session. Only after this state does UPDATE messages begin to flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **decision order** of BGP path selection. The complete KTU-correct priority is: WEIGHT → LOCAL_PREF → Locally Originated → AS_PATH → ORIGIN → MED → eBGP/iBGP → IGP metric to NEXT_HOP → Oldest → Router ID. Truncating this list loses 1-2 marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Dijkstra on Given Topology

**Initialization:**
$D(A) = 0$, $D(B) = D(C) = D(D) = D(E) = \infty$. Visited: $\{A\}$.

**Iteration 1 — Settle A (distance 0):**
- A→B: $0 + 1 = 1$, so $D(B) = 1$, predecessor = A.
- A→D: $0 + 4 = 4$, so $D(D) = 4$, predecessor = A.

Tentative: $\{B:1, D:4, C:\infty, E:\infty\}$.

**Iteration 2 — Settle B (distance 1):**
- B→C: $1 + 2 = 3$, so $D(C) = 3$, predecessor = B.
- B→D: $1 + 3 = 4$, unchanged.

Tentative: $\{C:3, D:4, E:\infty\}$.

**Iteration 3 — Settle C (distance 3):**
- C→D: $3 + 1 = 4$, unchanged.
- C→E: $3 + 5 = 8$, so $D(E) = 8$, predecessor = C.

Tentative: $\{D:4, E:8\}$.

**Iteration 4 — Settle D (distance 4):**
- D→E: $4 + 6 = 10 > 8$, no update.

Tentative: $\{E:8\}$.

**Iteration 5 — Settle E (distance 8):** No unvisited neighbors. **Algorithm terminates.**

**Final Shortest-Path Tree from A:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{A} &\to \text{A}, \quad \text{distance} = 0 \\
\text{A} &\to \text{B}, \quad \text{distance} = 1, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow B \\
\text{A} &\to \text{C}, \quad \text{distance} = 3, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \\
\text{A} &\to \text{D}, \quad \text{distance} = 4, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow D \text{ (or } A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D\text{)} \\
\text{A} &\to \text{E}, \quad \text{distance} = 8, \quad \text{path} = A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow E
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Large Networks

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Causes in Module 4 (Large Networks):**
> 1. **Confusing IGP vs EGP** — EIGRP is an IGP, not an EGP. (-1 mark per occurrence)
> 2. **Forgetting that OSPF runs over IP, not TCP/UDP** — protocol number 89. (-0.5 mark)
> 3. **Mis-stating RIP's max hop count as "16 hops"** — the correct limit is **15 hops**; 16 = unreachable. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping Dijkstra iterations** — KTU examiners require every step of every iteration to be shown. Even one skipped step loses 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Writing "AS path = number of ASes" instead of "AS path = list of ASNs traversed"** — the AS_PATH attribute is an *ordered list*, not just a count. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 4: Large Networks**

### Definitions to Memorize
- [ ] **Autonomous System (AS)** = single-admin domain with common routing policy; identified by ASN
- [ ] **IGP** = Interior Gateway Protocol (intra-AS) — RIP, OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP
- [ ] **EGP** = Exterior Gateway Protocol (inter-AS) — **only BGP** in production
- [ ] **Distance Vector** = Bellman-Ford; second-hand info; example: **RIP**
- [ ] **Link State** = Dijkstra; first-hand info; example: **OSPF**
- [ ] **Path Vector** = sequence of ASes; example: **BGP**
- [ ] **CIDR** = classless prefixes of any length
- [ ] **Supernetting** = aggregation of contiguous CIDR blocks into one shorter prefix
- [ ] **VLSM** = Variable Length Subnet Masking within an organization

### Numerical Values to Memorize
- [ ] RIP maximum hop count = **15**; metric 16 = unreachable
- [ ] RIP update interval = **30 seconds**; UDP port **520**
- [ ] OSPF reference bandwidth = **10^8 bps (100 Mbps)**; minimum cost = 1
- [ ] OSPF transport = **IP protocol 89**
- [ ] BGP transport = **TCP port 179**; KEEPALIVE every **60 s**; hold time = **180 s**
- [ ] Aggregation formula: $L_{aggregated} = 32 - \log_2(k)$ for $k$ contiguous networks

### Algorithms to Practice
- [ ] Dijkstra's SPF algorithm — 5-iteration trace from any source
- [ ] Bellman-Ford RIP update — at least 2 rounds of convergence after a link failure
- [ ] CIDR aggregation — write binary, find common prefix
- [ ] BGP path-vector advertisement — track AS_PATH growth and loop detection

### Diagrams to Draw From Memory
- [ ] Three-tier hierarchical routing (Core / Distribution / Access)
- [ ] Autonomous System interconnection with eBGP and iBGP
- [ ] CIDR aggregation flow (4 × /24 → 1 × /22)

### High-Priority Exam Phrases
- [ ] "OSPF uses **Dijkstra's SPF** algorithm on a complete **Link-State Database (LSDB)**."
- [ ] "BGP is a **path-vector** protocol; AS_PATH provides **loop detection**."
- [ ] "RIP is a **distance-vector** protocol vulnerable to **count-to-infinity**; mitigated by **split-horizon** and **poison-reverse**."
- [ ] "CIDR reduces routing-table size via **route aggregation** at network boundaries."
- [ ] "An **AS** is identified by a unique **ASN** and presents a **common routing policy** externally."

---

**End of Module 4 — Large Networks Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Label Swapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Label Swapping # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Label Swapping** is the foundational packet-forwarding mechanism employed by **Multiprotocol Label Switching (MPLS)** networks, in which each intermediate router (called a **Label Switch Router - LSR**) examines only a short, fixed-length **identifier (the label)** prepended to a packet, performs a direct table lookup in its **Label Information Base (LIB)**, and **replaces (swaps) the incoming label with a new outgoing label** before forwarding the packet to the next hop. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **NEP 2020** and **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**, label swapping is the practical realization of a **connection-oriented forwarding paradigm** layered on top of the inherently **connectionless IP internetwork**. It is the operation that converts an MPLS domain from a *graph of best-effort IP routes* into a **predetermined Label Switched Path (LSP)**. ### Core Terminology Snapshot | Term | Expansion | One-Line Meaning | |------|-----------|------------------| | **MPLS** | Multiprotocol Label Switching | A routing/forwarding technique using short labels. | | **LSR** | Label Switch Router | A router that performs label swapping. | | **LSP** | Label Switched Path | The unidirectional tunnel/sequence of LSRs. | | **FEC** | Forwarding Equivalence Class | A group of packets forwarded identically. | | **LIB** | Label Information Base | The label-mapping table inside an LSR. | | **LER** | Label Edge Router | LSR at the ingress/egress of an MPLS domain. | | **LDP** | Label Distribution Protocol | The signaling protocol that exchanges labels. | | **PHP** | Penultimate Hop Popping | Optimization where the second-last LSR pops the label. | > [!NOTE] > The MPLS **shim header** is **32 bits (4 bytes)** long and sits between the Layer 2 header and the Layer 3 (IP) header. It contains a **20-bit label**, a **3-bit Experimental (EXP/Traffic Class) field**, a **1-bit Bottom-of-Stack (S) flag**, and an **8-bit Time-To-Live (TTL)** field. ## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy: The Highway Toll System Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** on a national highway. You do not need to know the entire route map. At every toll plaza (think of these as **LSRs**), you simply hand over your toll ticket. The attendant glances at it, **stamps it with a new code** indicating the next toll plaza you must pass through, and waves you on. You never stop to "look up directions" — the ticket itself *is* the routing instruction. This is precisely what label swapping does: - The **toll ticket** = the **MPLS label**. - The **toll plaza attendant** = the **LSR's forwarding engine**. - The **stamping action** = the **label swap operation**. - The **entire highway from entry to exit** = the **LSP (Label Switched Path)**. - Your **car's license plate (IP address)** is still visible but is **never consulted by the attendant** — only the ticket matters. > [!TIP] > In a normal IP network, every router performs a longest-prefix match on the destination IP — like asking for directions at every crossroad. In MPLS label swapping, the label alone decides the next hop — like a pre-paid express ticket. The result is **faster, deterministic forwarding** suitable for traffic engineering, QoS, and VPNs. ## 1.3 Why Was Label Swapping Invented? The IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force) standardized MPLS (originally called "Tag Switching," proposed by Cisco in 1996) to overcome three classical limitations of pure IP routing: 1. **Lookup Speed** — IP longest-prefix matching scales with routing-table size; label lookup is a **fixed O(1) table index**. 2. **Routing Granularity** — IP forwards per-destination; MPLS forwards per-FEC, enabling **traffic engineering**. 3. **Multi-Protocol Encapsulation** — Labels can carry IPv4, IPv6, ATM, Frame Relay, and even Layer 2 Ethernet frames seamlessly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Highlight:** Every KTU question on Label Swapping ultimately tests whether the student understands that **label values are locally significant** — they are only valid between two adjacent LSRs. A label of `25` from Router A means something entirely different from `25` between Routers B and C. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step The label swapping process, when a packet transits an MPLS domain, follows a strict, ordered sequence. The KTU examiner expects you to articulate this precisely. ### Step 1: Ingress Label Edge Router (Ingress LER) Classification - The **ingress LER** receives a pure IP packet. - It inspects the packet's Layer 3 and Layer 4 headers (e.g., destination IP, DSCP value, source port). - Based on configured rules, it assigns the packet to a specific **Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC)**. - The LER then performs a **label PUSH** — it inserts a 4-byte MPLS shim header containing the **incoming label** (also called the *inner label* or *VC label* in VPN contexts, but here simply the *top-of-stack label*). ### Step 2: Forwarding to First Hop LSR - The LER uses its **Label Information Base (LIB)** to map `(incoming-interface, top-label) → (outgoing-interface, new-label, next-hop)`. - The packet is forwarded. ### Step 3: Intermediate LSR — The Actual "Swap" - The intermediate LSR **does not** read the IP header. It only reads the **top label** of the stack. - It performs a lookup: `incoming-label → outgoing-label`. - It **swaps** the label (writes a new value in the same field), decrements TTL by 1, and forwards. ### Step 4: Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP) — Optional Optimization - To avoid double-lookup at the egress, the **second-last LSR** is instructed (via LDP) to **POP** the label before forwarding. - The egress LER thus receives a **plain IP packet** and routes it natively. ### Step 5: Egress LER — Final Destination - If PHP is **not** used, the egress LER receives the labeled packet, **Pops** the top label, and performs a normal IP lookup. - The packet exits the MPLS cloud. ## 2.2 The Three Sacred Label Operations | Operation | Symbol | Where It Occurs | Meaning | |-----------|--------|-----------------|---------| | **PUSH** (Impose) | `Push(label)` | Ingress LER | Insert a new label on top of an existing one (or on top of IP). | | **SWAP** (Replace) | `Swap(old → new)` | Intermediate LSR | Replace the top label with a new one; keep stack depth same. | | **POP** (Remove) | `Pop()` | Egress LER or Penultimate LSR | Remove the top label; reveal underlying label or IP header. | > [!NOTE] > A **multi-label stack** (used in MPLS VPNs and MPLS-TP) allows nested LSPs. A **PUSH** adds depth, a **POP** reduces it, a **SWAP** keeps depth constant but changes the value. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---|---------|----------------|---------------| | 1 | MPLS Shim Header Size | $S_{shim} = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes}$ | Fixed across all MPLS networks. | | 2 | Label Value Range | $L \in [0, 2^{20} - 1] = [0,\ 1{,}048{,}575]$ | 20-bit unsigned integer. | | 3 | Reserved Labels | $L = 0$ (IPv4 Explicit NULL), $L = 1$ (Router Alert), $L = 2$ (IPv6 Explicit NULL), $L = 3$ (Implicit NULL — for PHP), $L = 4..15$ (Reserved) | Total **16 reserved values**. | | 4 | Usable Labels | $L_{usable} = 2^{20} - 16 = 1{,}048{,}560$ | Effective label space per LSR pair. | | 5 | Label Lookup Complexity | $T_{lookup} = O(1)$ | Index-based, not search-based. | | 6 | IP Longest-Prefix Match | $T_{LPM} = O(W)$ where $W = 32$ (IPv4) or $W = 128$ (IPv6) | Trie-based, scales poorly. | | 7 | TTL in MPLS | $TTL_{out} = TTL_{in} - 1$ at every swap | Mirrors IP TTL behavior to prevent loops. | | 8 | Stack Depth | Encoded by Bottom-of-Stack flag $S = 1$ marks the last label | $S = 0$ means more labels below. | | 9 | Swap Function | $L_{out} = f(L_{in}, I_{in})$ | Function of incoming label and incoming interface. | | 10 | LSP Hop Count | $H_{LSP} = \vert N_{LSR} \vert - 1$ | Number of swap operations on the path. | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always state in your KTU answer: *"Labels have **local significance** only — between two directly connected MPLS peers."* Examiners award a free mark for this phrase. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Label swapping is the silent engine behind: - **MPLS L3 VPNs** (RFC 4364) — used by **BSNL, Airtel, Jio** to provide enterprise WAN services. - **MPLS L2 VPNs (VPWS, VPLS)** — used by banks for transparent Ethernet extension. - **Traffic Engineering (MPLS-TE)** — used by ISPs to avoid congestion and provide SLA-backed paths. - **5G Transport Networks** — modern 5G backhaul uses **SR-MPLS (Segment Routing over MPLS)** which is essentially label swapping with stacked segment identifiers. - **Data Center Fabrics** — **VXLAN-EVPN** with MPLS dataplane in modern Cisco ACI / Arista AVD deployments. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 End-to-End Analytical Walkthrough of a Label-Swapped Path Consider the following canonical KTU-style topology: **Topology:** `Host A → R1 (Ingress LER) → R2 (LSR) → R3 (LSR) → R4 (Egress LER) → Host B` We assign **LDP-distributed labels** as per the table below: | Router | Role | Incoming Label | Outgoing Label | Outgoing Interface | Next Hop | |--------|------|----------------|----------------|--------------------|----------| | R1 | Ingress LER | (none — IP packet) | **20** | Eth0/1 | R2 | | R2 | Transit LSR | **20** | **17** | Eth0/2 | R3 | | R3 | Penultimate LSR | **17** | **Pop (Implicit NULL = 3)** | Eth0/3 | R4 | | R4 | Egress LER | (none — IP packet) | — | Eth1/0 | Host B | > We assume **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)** is enabled, so R3 pops the label using the **Implicit NULL** label (value 3). ### Step-by-Step Algebraic Trace of the Label Swapping #### Step 1: Packet Arrival at R1 (Ingress LER) The packet from Host A has structure: $$P_{R1} = \underbrace{H_{L2}}_{\text{Ethernet}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{H_{L3}}_{\text{IP dest=B}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{PAYLOAD}_{\text{TCP segment}}$$ R1 consults its routing table and FIB, classifies the packet into FEC = "packets destined to 10.0.0.0/24", and applies the **PUSH** operation: $$P_{R1}^{out} = \underbrace{H_{L2}^{'}}_{\text{new Ethernet}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{SHIM(\ L=20,\ EXP=0,\ S=1,\ TTL=255\ )}_{\text{4-byte MPLS shim}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{H_{L3}}_{\text{IP}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{PAYLOAD}_{\text{TCP}}$$ **Explanation:** The shim header is inserted between Layer 2 and Layer 3. The **Bottom-of-Stack bit S = 1** indicates this is the *only* (and hence the *last*) label in the stack. #### Step 2: R2 Performs the First True SWAP R2 receives the packet. It reads the **top label** ($L_{in} = 20$) and the incoming interface. It consults its LIB and finds the mapping $20 \to 17$ on interface Eth0/2. The **SWAP** operation is mathematically expressed as: $$L_{out} = \text{SWAP}(L_{in}) = f_{R2}(20) = 17$$ The TTL is decremented: $$TTL_{out} = TTL_{in} - 1 = 255 - 1 = 254$$ The packet structure is now: $$P_{R2}^{out} = H_{L2}^{''} \;\vert\; SHIM(\ L=17,\ EXP=0,\ S=1,\ TTL=254\ ) \;\vert\; H_{L3} \;\vert\; PAYLOAD$$ > [!NOTE] > Crucially, R2 **never examined** the IP header. It only touched the shim. This is the *entire point* of label swapping — forwarding-plane speed is independent of the routed protocol. #### Step 3: R3 Performs POP (Penultimate Hop Popping) R3 receives label 17. Its LIB entry says: *"For incoming label 17, perform POP and forward to R4."* The shim header is **removed entirely**: $$P_{R3}^{out} = H_{L2}^{'''} \;\vert\; H_{L3} \;\vert\; PAYLOAD$$ > R3 still decrements TTL inside the IP header (or inside the popped label's TTL — implementation-specific but functionally equivalent). R4 receives a clean IP packet. #### Step 4: R4 (Egress LER) Native IP Forwarding R4 sees a normal IP packet destined to 10.0.0.5 (Host B). It does a longest-prefix match and forwards it natively out Eth1/0. ### 3.2 Generalization — The Label Swap Function For an arbitrary path $\text{LSP} = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n\}$, the sequence of label operations is: $$ \text{Operations} = \underbrace{\text{PUSH}(L_1)}_{R_1} \;\to\; \underbrace{\text{SWAP}(L_1 \to L_2)}_{R_2} \;\to\; \underbrace{\text{SWAP}(L_2 \to L_3)}_{R_3} \;\to\; \cdots \;\to\; \underbrace{\text{SWAP}(L_{n-2} \to L_{n-1})}_{R_{n-1}} \;\to\; \underbrace{\text{POP}()}_{R_n \text{ or } R_{n-1} \text{ if PHP}} $$ The total number of label-stack modifications is: $$N_{ops} = 1\ (\text{PUSH}) + (n - 2)\ (\text{SWAPS}) + 1\ (\text{POP}) = n$$ For our 4-router path: $N_{ops} = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4$ — exactly matching the four routers. ### 3.3 Label Stack — Algebraic View For a stack of depth $k$, the packet header chain can be written as: $$H_{total} = H_{L2} \;\vert\; SHIM(L_1, S=0) \;\vert\; SHIM(L_2, S=0) \;\vert\; \cdots \;\vert\; SHIM(L_k, S=1) \;\vert\; H_{L3} \;\vert\; PAYLOAD$$ The **top-of-stack pointer** is implicit (it is always the first shim after L2). The **PUSH** operator: $$\text{PUSH}(L_{new}) : H \to H_{L2} \;\vert\; SHIM(L_{new}, S=0) \;\vert\; H$$ The **POP** operator: $$\text{POP}() : H_{L2} \;\vert\; SHIM(L_{top}) \;\vert\; H \to H_{L2} \;\vert\; H$$ The **SWAP** operator: $$\text{SWAP}(L_{old} \to L_{new}) : H_{L2} \;\vert\; SHIM(L_{old}) \;\vert\; H \to H_{L2} \;\vert\; SHIM(L_{new}) \;\vert\; H$$ ### 3.4 Loop Prevention — The TTL Equation Label swapping mimics IP TTL behavior to prevent forwarding loops. At each swap: $$TTL_{after\ swap} = TTL_{before\ swap} - 1$$ When $TTL = 0$, the packet is discarded and an **ICMP "time exceeded"** message is generated — just as in pure IP. This ensures that misconfigured LSPs do not create infinite label-swap loops. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Label Switched Path with Per-Hop Label Swapping (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR HA["Host A
10.0.0.1"] -->|"IP packet"| R1["R1 Ingress LER
PUSH label 20"] R1 -->|"Label 20, TTL 255"| R2["R2 Transit LSR
SWAP 20 to 17"] R2 -->|"Label 17, TTL 254"| R3["R3 Penultimate LSR
SWAP 17 then POP"] R3 -->|"Plain IP, TTL 253"| R4["R4 Egress LER
Native IP Forward"] R4 -->|"IP packet"| HB["Host B
10.0.0.5"] LIB1["LIB at R2
in 20 on Eth0/1 to out 17 on Eth0/2"] LIB2["LIB at R3
in 17 on Eth0/2 to POP and forward to Eth0/3"] R2 -.-> LIB1 R3 -.-> LIB2 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LSR ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LSR["Label Switch Router Internal Architecture"] RX["Incoming Interface
Physical Layer Receive"] --> DEC["Label Parser
Read Top Label and S bit"] DEC --> LOOKUP["LIB Lookup
incoming label plus interface"] LOOKUP --> DECISION{"Top Label
Equals Implicit NULL"} DECISION -- "Yes" --> POPU["POP Operation
Remove Shim Header"] DECISION -- "No" --> SWAPO["SWAP Operation
Replace Label Value
Decrement TTL"] POPU --> TX1["Outgoing Interface
Forward Packet"] SWAPO --> TX1 TX1 --> TXOUT["Transmit on Wire"] end CTRL["Control Plane
LDP or RSVP or SR
Populates the LIB"] -.-> LOOKUP CTRL -.-> DECISION ``` ## 4.3 MPLS Shim Header — Bit-Level Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SHIM["MPLS Shim Header 32 bits Total"] LBL["Label
20 bits"] EXP["EXP Traffic Class
3 bits"] S["Bottom of Stack S
1 bit"] TTL["Time To Live
8 bits"] end LBL --- EXP --- S --- TTL ``` ## 4.4 The Label Stack — Conceptual Stack Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB L2["Layer 2 Header
Ethernet or PPP"] --> L1["MPLS Label 1
Top of Stack S equals 0"] L1 --> L2b["MPLS Label 2
S equals 0"] L2b --> L3["MPLS Label 3
Bottom of Stack S equals 1"] L3 --> L3H["Layer 3 Header
IP Packet"] L3H --> PAY["Payload
TCP UDP etc"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **Q: Define label swapping in MPLS. Why are MPLS labels said to be locally significant?** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]` **Model Answer:** Label swapping is the packet-forwarding technique used in MPLS networks where each intermediate router (LSR) replaces the incoming label in the packet's MPLS shim header with a new outgoing label before forwarding the packet to the next hop. The decision is based purely on the top label value, without consulting the IP header. MPLS labels are said to be **locally significant** because the meaning of a label is meaningful only between two directly connected MPLS peers (adjacent LSRs). The same numeric label value can represent completely different FECs (or different forwarding actions) on different links in the same network. The LSRs use signaling protocols such as **LDP** or **RSVP-TE** to negotiate label values pairwise. > **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for the definition, 1 mark for the local-significance explanation, 0.5 mark for mentioning LDP/RSVP-TE. ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **Q: List and briefly explain the three label operations performed by LSRs in an MPLS network.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The three label operations are: 1. **PUSH (Impose):** Performed at the ingress LER. A new MPLS shim header containing the top label is inserted between the Layer 2 header and the Layer 3 (IP) header. It marks the packet's entry into the MPLS domain. 2. **SWAP (Replace):** Performed by intermediate (transit) LSRs. The top label of the incoming packet is replaced with a new outgoing label as dictated by the LIB. The IP header is never read. Stack depth is unchanged. 3. **POP (Remove):** Performed at the egress LER (or at the penultimate LSR in PHP optimization). The top MPLS shim header is removed, exposing either the underlying label (in stacked scenarios) or the original IP header (for native IP forwarding). > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for naming and explaining the three operations. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 > `**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4 Long Answer, 14 Marks]**` > > **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of label switching and label swapping in an MPLS network. Describe the role of LER, LSR, and LIB. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Consider an MPLS domain with the path `R1 → R2 → R3 → R4 → R5`, where R1 is the ingress LER and R5 is the egress LER. The LDP-distributed labels are: > - R1 pushes **label 30** > - R2 swaps label **30 → 45** > - R3 swaps label **45 → 60** > - R4 (penultimate hop) pops the label using Implicit NULL > > Trace the packet step by step, showing the shim header contents (Label, EXP, S, TTL) at each hop. Discuss why Penultimate Hop Popping is used. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) **Label Switching** is a forwarding paradigm in which a short, fixed-length identifier called a **label** is used to make forwarding decisions. The label is examined by each router along the path, and a lookup in a local table (the LIB) determines the outgoing interface and new label value. In a label-switched network: - The **Ingress LER (R1)** receives an IP packet, classifies it into a FEC, and performs a **PUSH** of a label. - The **Transit LSRs (R2, R3, R4)** perform label lookups and **SWAP** the label at each hop. - The **Egress LER** (or the penultimate LSR in PHP) performs a **POP**, removing the label and forwarding the original IP packet. **Role of LIB:** The Label Information Base is the data-plane forwarding table that maps `(incoming interface, incoming label) → (outgoing interface, outgoing label, next-hop)`. It is populated by the control plane (typically LDP or RSVP-TE) and consulted at line-rate on every incoming labeled packet. #### Model Solution to (b) The packet enters R1 with no MPLS shim. R1 pushes label 30 and sets TTL = 64 (a common initial value). | Hop | Operation | Shim Header (Label, EXP, S, TTL) | Notes | |-----|-----------|----------------------------------|-------| | R1 (Ingress) | PUSH | (30, 0, 1, 64) | S=1 because only one label. | | R2 (Transit) | SWAP | (45, 0, 1, 63) | TTL decremented by 1. | | R3 (Transit) | SWAP | (60, 0, 1, 62) | TTL decremented by 1. | | R4 (Penultimate) | POP | (no shim, IP TTL = 61) | R4 uses Implicit NULL (L=3) to signal R4 to pop. | | R5 (Egress) | Native IP | (no shim) | Standard longest-prefix-match IP forwarding. | **Why Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)?** PHP is an optimization where the **second-last LSR** is instructed (via the special **Implicit NULL label = 3**) to pop the MPLS shim before forwarding to the egress LER. This way, the egress LER receives a plain IP packet and performs only **one** lookup (IP lookup), instead of two (label lookup + IP lookup). The benefits are: 1. **Reduced CPU load** on the egress LER. 2. **Faster forwarding** of the final packet. 3. **Clean separation** of MPLS and IP forwarding planes at the boundary. > **Mark Distribution for (b):** 4 marks for the table trace, 1.5 marks for the TTL explanation, 1.5 marks for PHP discussion. ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 > `**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Long Answer, 14 Marks]**` > > **(a)** Explain the structure of the MPLS shim header with a diagram. List all reserved label values and their meanings. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Compare label swapping with traditional IP forwarding in terms of lookup complexity, scalability, and protocol independence. Why is label swapping considered faster than IP routing? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) The **MPLS shim header** is a 32-bit (4-byte) header inserted between the Layer 2 and Layer 3 headers: | Field | Bits | Purpose | |-------|------|---------| | **Label** | 20 | The forwarding identifier. | | **EXP (TC)** | 3 | QoS / Traffic Class / PHB selection. | | **S (Bottom-of-Stack)** | 1 | 1 = last label; 0 = more labels below. | | **TTL** | 8 | Loop prevention, mirrors IP TTL. | **Reserved Label Values:** | Value | Name | Meaning | |-------|------|---------| | 0 | IPv4 Explicit NULL | Indicates IPv4 payload; egress LER pops. | | 1 | Router Alert | Packet must be examined by every LSR. | | 2 | IPv6 Explicit NULL | Indicates IPv6 payload. | | 3 | Implicit NULL | Used for PHP — instructs penultimate LSR to pop. | | 4 – 15 | Reserved | Reserved for future use. | | 16 – 1,048,575 | Usable | Available for normal MPLS forwarding. | > **Mark Distribution for (a):** 3 marks for the header diagram, 2 marks for reserved values, 2 marks for the explanation. #### Model Solution to (b) | Criterion | IP Forwarding | MPLS Label Swapping | |-----------|---------------|---------------------| | **Lookup Key** | Destination IP address (32 or 128 bits) | Label (20 bits) | | **Algorithm** | Longest-Prefix Match (LPM) on a radix/trie | Exact-match table index | | **Complexity** | $O(W)$ for IPv4 ($W = 32$); $O(W)$ for IPv6 ($W = 128$) | $O(1)$ — direct index lookup | | **Table Size** | Scales with global routing table (∼ 950,000 IPv4 prefixes in 2024) | Scales with the number of locally-distributed labels (bounded by $2^{20} - 16 \approx 10^6$) | | **Protocol Dependency** | Tightly coupled to IP (and its version) | Independent of Layer 3 protocol (can carry IPv4, IPv6, CLNP, Ethernet) | | **QoS Awareness** | Limited (ToS/DSCP only at ingress) | Native 3-bit EXP field per hop | | **Traffic Engineering** | Difficult (BGP/OSPF shortest-path only) | Native via RSVP-TE or Segment Routing | **Why Label Swapping Is Faster:** 1. **Exact-match vs. Longest-match:** A 20-bit label is looked up as a key in a hash table or a content-addressable memory (CAM), giving **constant-time** lookup. IP forwarding requires a **trie walk** to find the longest matching prefix, which is super-linear in the worst case. 2. **Hardware friendliness:** ASICs and NPUs can do label lookups in **single clock cycles** — this is how line cards achieve **100 Gbps, 400 Gbps, and beyond**. 3. **Header inspection depth:** In MPLS, only the first 4 bytes after L2 are read; in IP, the full IP header (20+ bytes) and often options are parsed. > **Mark Distribution for (b):** 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for the complexity formulas, 2 marks for the "why faster" synthesis. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Writing "labels have global significance"** — this is the *opposite* of the truth. Labels are **locally significant** between two adjacent LSRs. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this error. > 2. **Forgetting to decrement TTL** during the swap trace. Even if you do not write the numeric values, the verbal explanation must mention that TTL is decremented. > 3. **Confusing PUSH, SWAP, and POP locations.** PUSH happens at the **ingress**, SWAP at **transit**, POP at **egress or penultimate**. Writing the wrong location loses 2 marks. > 4. **Not mentioning the shim-header bit layout.** Every long answer on MPLS expects you to draw (or describe) the 20-3-1-8 bit structure of the shim. > 5. **Confusing MPLS with IP-in-IP tunneling or GRE.** MPLS is a **label-based forwarding** technique, not a tunneling encapsulation in the traditional sense. State this clearly. > 6. **Omitting the Bottom-of-Stack (S) bit** in shim diagrams. Examiners specifically look for the S bit to confirm understanding of label stacking. > 7. **Mixing up LDP and RSVP-TE.** LDP is for label distribution along the IGP path; RSVP-TE is for traffic-engineered LSPs. Mention the distinction when relevant. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Label Swapping** > > - **Core Idea:** Replace IP longest-prefix-match forwarding with **short fixed-length label lookup** at each hop. > - **Shim Header:** **32 bits = 20-bit Label + 3-bit EXP + 1-bit S + 8-bit TTL**. > - **Three Operations:** **PUSH (Ingress), SWAP (Transit), POP (Egress or Penultimate)**. > - **Local Significance:** A label value is meaningful only between **two adjacent LSRs**. > - **Reserved Labels:** 0 (IPv4 NULL), 1 (Router Alert), 2 (IPv6 NULL), 3 (Implicit NULL for PHP), 4–15 (Reserved). > - **Usable Label Space:** $2^{20} - 16 = 1{,}048{,}560$ labels per link. > - **Lookup Complexity:** **O(1)** for label swap vs. **O(W)** for IP LPM. > - **Loop Prevention:** MPLS **TTL** is decremented at every swap, mirroring IP TTL behavior. > - **PHP (Penultimate Hop Popping):** The penultimate LSR pops the label using **Implicit NULL (L=3)** so the egress LER receives a plain IP packet — saves one lookup. > - **Control Plane:** LDP (best-effort, IGP-driven) or RSVP-TE (traffic-engineered). > - **Protocol Independence:** MPLS can carry IPv4, IPv6, CLNP, Ethernet, ATM, Frame Relay. > - **Stack Depth:** Indicated by the **S bit**; S=1 marks the last label. > - **FEC:** Packets in the same FEC get the same label and identical forwarding treatment. > - **LSP:** The unidirectional tunnel from ingress to egress LER, traversing one or more LSRs. > - **Real-world Use:** MPLS L3VPN, MPLS L2VPN, MPLS-TE, 5G transport (SR-MPLS), Data Center fabrics (VXLAN-EVPN with MPLS dataplane). > - **Modern Evolution:** **Segment Routing (SR-MPLS)** uses label stacks of *segment IDs* — but the per-hop swap mechanism is identical. > - **Exam-Winning Phrase:** *"Labels are locally significant and are swapped, not rewritten, at every intermediate LSR."* **End of Module 4 — Label Swapping Notes**

topics.title: Paths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Paths # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a "Path" in TCP/IP Internetworking? In the context of the TCP/IP internetwork architecture, a **Path** refers to the complete sequence of network links, intermediate routers, and forwarding decisions that an IP datagram traverses from its **source host** to its **final destination host**. A path is not pre-determined or reserved in advance; rather, it is computed hop-by-hop using distributed routing intelligence across the internetwork. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A *path* in an IP internetwork is the logically ordered, end-to-end forwarding route selected by a series of next-hop routing decisions stored in routing tables, where each router makes an *independent* forwarding choice based solely on the destination IP address carried in the datagram header. ### 1.2 Forwarding vs. Routing — The Two Foundational Pillars These two terms are **not synonymous** in KTU examination contexts, and confusing them is a common pitfall: | Aspect | Forwarding | Routing | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | The local action of moving a packet from input interface to output interface | The network-wide process of building and maintaining routing tables | | **Scope** | Per-router, per-packet | Per-router, but affects entire internetwork | | **Time-scale** | Microseconds (per packet) | Seconds to minutes (per topology change) | | **Mechanism** | Lookup in forwarding table | Execution of routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP) | | **KTU View** | Data plane operation | Control plane operation | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — The Road Trip Analogy** > Imagine you are driving from **Kochi** to **Delhi** with no GPS, only a printed list of "turn instructions" at every major city: > - At Kochi → go towards Coimbatore > - At Coimbatore → go towards Bangalore > - At Bangalore → go towards Hyderabad > - ... and so on > > Each city **does not know the full route** — it only knows the **next city** (next hop). The complete path emerges as a result of independent local decisions. **This is exactly how IP forwarding works.** ### 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams - **Hop**: One traversal of a packet through a single router/link. - **Next Hop**: The *immediately adjacent* router (next IP address) to which the current router forwards the packet. - **Default Route**: A catch-all routing entry (`0.0.0.0/0`) used when no specific route matches. - **Direct Delivery**: When the destination is on the *same physical network* as the sender (no intermediate router). - **Indirect Delivery**: When the destination requires traversal through *one or more* routers. - **Route Metric**: A numerical value (e.g., hop count, cost, delay) used to rank competing paths. - **Convergence Time**: The time taken by all routers in an internetwork to reach a consistent view of paths after a topology change. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "What is a path?", always mention that it is **dynamically constructed**, **hop-by-hop**, and **unidirectional** (forward direction only — reverse path is independently determined). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Logical Steps of Path Determination Every IP datagram undergoes the following logical pipeline inside every router it encounters: 1. **Packet Arrival:** Datagram enters the router via an incoming physical interface. 2. **Destination Address Extraction:** The router reads the *destination IP* field (32 bits for IPv4) from the IP header. 3. **Longest Prefix Match (LPM):** The destination IP is matched against all entries in the routing table. The entry with the **longest matching network prefix** wins. 4. **Next-Hop Resolution:** The matched entry yields: - A *next-hop IP address* (logical) - An *outgoing interface* (physical) - Optionally, an *ARP-resolved MAC address* for the next hop 5. **Forwarding Decision & Re-encapsulation:** The IP datagram is *re-encapsulated* in a new Layer-2 frame (with new MAC addresses) and transmitted out the chosen interface. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Longest Prefix Match"?** Because of CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing), a single destination may match multiple routing entries. The most specific route (longest subnet mask) is always preferred. This is a *guaranteed* KTU 2024 question. ## 2.2 Types of Paths in an IP Internetwork ### 2.2.1 Direct Path (Direct Delivery) - Sender and destination share the **same IP network/subnet**. - No intermediate router is required. - The sender performs **ARP resolution** for the destination's MAC address directly. - Detected by comparing the sender's network portion (after masking) with the destination's network portion. ### 2.2.2 Indirect Path (Indirect Delivery) - Sender and destination belong to **different IP networks/subnets**. - The packet must traverse **one or more routers**. - Each router performs its own independent forwarding decision. ### 2.2.3 Default Path - A *last-resort* route used when no specific entry in the routing table matches. - Represented as `0.0.0.0/0` (IPv4) or `::/0` (IPv6). - Common in stub networks and end-user hosts. ### 2.2.4 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Paths - **Symmetric**: Forward path and reverse path traverse the *same sequence* of routers. - **Asymmetric**: Forward and reverse paths are *different* (very common in the real Internet due to policy-based routing). ## 2.3 Routing Table Structure — KTU High-Yield Anatomy A typical IPv4 routing table contains the following columns (this is the **exact format** KTU expects): | Field | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | Destination | Target network/host address | `192.168.10.0` | | Netmask (Prefix Length) | Subnet mask in CIDR notation | `255.255.255.0` (/24) | | Next Hop | IP of next forwarding router | `10.0.0.2` | | Interface | Outgoing physical/logical port | `eth0`, `Serial0/0/0` | | Metric | Cost/Preference value | `1`, `20`, `100` | | Administrative Distance | Trustworthiness of the routing source | `1` (static), `110` (OSPF) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** *Metric* and *Administrative Distance* are **NOT the same**. Metric ranks paths *within* a single routing protocol. Administrative Distance ranks *between* different routing protocols. Confusing them = guaranteed mark loss. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | Network Address | $\text{Network} = \text{IP} \;\text{AND}\; \text{Mask}$ | Determines whether two hosts are on the same subnet (direct path) | | Longest Prefix Match | $\text{Choose route with max } \vert \text{prefix length} \vert$ | Standard IP forwarding algorithm | | Hop Count (Distance Vector) | $D_{new}(X, Z) = \min\{D(X,Y) + D(Y,Z)\}$ | Used by RIP; counts router traversals | | OSPF Cost | $C = \frac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$ | Default reference = **100 Mbps** | | Path MTU (PMTU) | $PMTU = \min(MTU_{link_1}, MTU_{link_2}, \ldots, MTU_{link_n})$ | Maximum packet size end-to-end without fragmentation | | Maximum Hops (TTL) | $0 \le TTL \le 255$ (IPv4); Hop Limit in IPv6 | Prevents infinite path loops | | Distance Approximation | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ (sum of link costs) | Used in shortest-path-first algorithms (Dijkstra) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cram Rule for KTU Board:** Always carry forward the concept that **each router is independently responsible** for ONE next-hop decision. No single router holds the *complete* path. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ISP Traffic Engineering:** Path selection determines Quality of Service (QoS), latency, and jitter for VoIP/streaming. - **Data Center Networking:** Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) uses *multiple parallel paths* for redundancy and load balancing. - **Software-Defined Networking (SDN):** Separates the *control plane* (path computation) from the *data plane* (forwarding) — a direct evolution of the path concept. - **Cybersecurity:** Asymmetric paths complicate IP traceback and forensic investigations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Same-Network Detection (Direct vs. Indirect Path) A host must mathematically determine whether the destination lies on the **same** network or a **different** one. This is the most fundamental path decision made by *every* IP host. ### Step-by-Step Logical Walkthrough **Given:** - Host A IP = `192.168.10.45` - Host A Subnet Mask = `255.255.255.0` - Destination IP = `192.168.10.200` **Step 1:** Convert IP and Mask into 32-bit binary form. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Host A IP} &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00101101 \\ \text{Subnet Mask} &= 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Perform bitwise AND between IP and Mask to extract the network portion. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Network}_{A} &= \text{IP}_{A} \;\text{AND}\; \text{Mask} \\ &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00000000 \\ &= 192.168.10.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Perform the same AND operation on the destination IP. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Network}_{D} &= \text{IP}_{D} \;\text{AND}\; \text{Mask} \\ &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.00001000 \\ &= 192.168.10.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Compare the two extracted network addresses. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Network}_{A} &= 192.168.10.0 \\ \text{Network}_{D} &= 192.168.10.0 \\ \therefore \text{Network}_{A} &= \text{Network}_{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{DIRECT DELIVERY} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Since both belong to `192.168.10.0/24`, the host can ARP for the destination's MAC address and deliver the frame directly. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** If the two network addresses differ, the packet must be sent to the **default gateway** (indirect delivery). This decision is repeated at *every* router hop. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longest Prefix Match (LPM) in a Routing Table ### Scenario Setup A router has the following routing table entries, and a packet arrives with destination IP `= 192.168.20.35`: | Entry | Destination | Prefix (/n) | Next Hop | |---|---|---|---| | E1 | `0.0.0.0` | /0 | 10.0.0.1 | | E2 | `192.168.0.0` | /16 | 10.0.0.2 | | E3 | `192.168.20.0` | /24 | 10.0.0.3 | | E4 | `192.168.20.32` | /27 | 10.0.0.4 | ### Step-by-Step Evaluation **Step 1:** Convert all prefixes to their masked form and check membership. $$ \begin{aligned} E1: \quad 0.0.0.0/0 &\rightarrow \text{matches} = \text{True} \quad (\text{always}) \\ E2: \quad 192.168.0.0/16 &\rightarrow \text{does } 192.168.20.35 \text{ lie in } 192.168.0.0 - 192.168.255.255? \rightarrow \text{True} \\ E3: \quad 192.168.20.0/24 &\rightarrow \text{does } 192.168.20.35 \text{ lie in } 192.168.20.0 - 192.168.20.255? \rightarrow \text{True} \\ E4: \quad 192.168.20.32/27 &\rightarrow \text{does } 192.168.20.35 \text{ lie in } 192.168.20.32 - 192.168.20.63? \rightarrow \text{True} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** All four entries match. Apply the LPM rule — select the entry with the **longest prefix**. $$ \begin{aligned} \vert E1 \vert &= 0 \\ \vert E2 \vert &= 16 \\ \vert E3 \vert &= 24 \\ \vert E4 \vert &= 27 \\ \max(\vert E1 \vert, \vert E2 \vert, \vert E3 \vert, \vert E4 \vert) &= 27 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E4 \text{ is selected.} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** The packet is forwarded via next hop `10.0.0.4`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The default route (E1) is a *fallback* that matches everything but is only chosen when **no other, more specific** entry matches. This is why it has the **shortest** prefix (length 0). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Path MTU Discovery (PMTU) Logic A packet traveling from host A to host B may traverse links of different MTUs.
