<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MPLS Encapsulation** is the standardized mechanism by which a short, fixed-length identifier called an **MPLS shim header (label)** is inserted between the **Layer 2 (Data Link)** header and the **Layer 3 (Network)** header of a packet. This pre-pended label enables Label Switched Routers (LSRs) within an MPLS domain to perform high-speed, table-driven forwarding based purely on the label value, rather than performing a complex, longest-match lookup on the IP destination prefix.

The encapsulation process conforms to **IETF RFC 3032** (MPLS Label Stack Encoding) and is a **link-layer agnostic** operation, meaning it can ride over Ethernet, PPP, ATM, Frame Relay, or even SONET/SDH without altering the underlying Layer 2 framing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MPLS Encapsulation is the **foundational pillar** of Module 4. Without mastering the 32-bit shim header layout and label operations (Push, Swap, Pop), students cannot answer questions on MPLS forwarding, LSPs, or L3VPNs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Sorting System"

Imagine an international courier hub:
- A package arrives at the **Ingress Hub** (LER — Label Edge Router). A clerk looks at the full destination address (IP lookup), then **sticks a colored sticker** on the package (Push operation).
- Inside the hub, sorting machines only read the **color of the sticker** (label value) and forward the package along a conveyor belt (LSP — Label Switched Path). They do **NOT** re-read the printed address.
- At each intermediate sorting station (**LSR — Label Switched Router**), the sticker color is changed based on the next conveyor belt (**Swap operation**).
- At the **Egress Hub** (another LER), the sticker is removed (**Pop operation**) and the package exits with its original address visible for the outside world.

The "colored sticker" is the **MPLS label**. The act of sticking it on, changing it, and removing it is **MPLS Encapsulation**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Header Fields

| Parameter | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Shim Header Size** | **4 bytes (32 bits)** | Fixed-length for hardware-speed lookup |
| Label Field | **20 bits** | Identifies the Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC) |
| EXP (Experimental) | **3 bits** | QoS/CoS marking (often called TC — Traffic Class) |
| S (Bottom of Stack) | **1 bit** | Indicates last label in stack (S=1) |
| TTL (Time to Live) | **8 bits** | Loop prevention (copied from IP TTL on ingress) |

> [!NOTE]
> The MPLS TTL field uses the **same default initial value of 255** as IP, but follows MPLS-specific TTL handling rules (Uniform vs. Pipe mode) as defined in RFC 3443.

## 1.4 Position of the MPLS Label in the Frame

The MPLS shim header is inserted **between** the Layer 2 header and the Layer 3 (IP) payload. For an Ethernet frame, the stack appears as:

`[ Ethernet Header | MPLS Label(s) | IP Header | IP Payload ]`

This unique placement is what makes MPLS "**Layer 2.5**" — it operates above the link layer but below the network layer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the MPLS Shim Header (RFC 3032)

The 32-bit shim header is divided into four sub-fields with explicit bit positions:

$$\text{MPLS Shim Header} = \{\text{Label}_{[0:19]} \mid \text{EXP}_{[20:22]} \mid \text{S}_{[23]} \mid \text{TTL}_{[24:31]}\}$$

- **Label (20 bits):** Numerical identifier in the range **0 to 2²⁰−1 (1,048,575)**. Labels 0–15 are **reserved** for special use (e.g., Label 0 = IPv4 Explicit Null, Label 1 = Router Alert, Label 2 = IPv6 Explicit Null, Label 3 = Implicit Null).
- **EXP / Traffic Class (3 bits):** Used for QoS marking. Maps to IP DSCP precedence bits.
- **Bottom of Stack (S) bit (1 bit):** Set to **1** when this label is the last in the stack; **0** otherwise. Enables MPLS tunneling and hierarchical LSPs.
- **TTL (8 bits):** Decremented at every hop. If TTL reaches 0, the packet is dropped and an ICMP "time exceeded" message is generated.

## 2.2 Three Core Label Operations

The entire behavior of an MPLS network is governed by **three primitive operations**, executed at every LSR based on its forwarding table:

### 2.2.1 Push (Imposition)
- Performed at the **Ingress LER**.
- A **new label is added** on top of the existing label stack (or above the IP header if no labels exist).
- Multiple labels can be pushed to enable LSP tunneling, MPLS VPNs, or TE tunnels.

### 2.2.2 Swap (Label Switching)
- Performed at **transit LSRs**.
- The incoming label is **replaced** with a new outgoing label.
- The incoming and outgoing interfaces may differ; only the label value is swapped.

### 2.2.3 Pop (Disposition)
- Performed at the **Egress LER** (or at the penultimate hop for "PHP — Penultimate Hop Popping").
- The **top label is removed**.
- If S=1 after pop, the IP header is exposed and forwarded natively.

## 2.3 The Label Stack Concept

A **label stack** is an ordered sequence of MPLS labels. The top label is the one currently used for forwarding decisions. The stack enables:

- **Hierarchical LSPs** (carrier's carrier)
- **MPLS Layer 3 VPNs** (provider + customer labels)
- **MPLS Traffic Engineering** tunnels

The **maximum label stack depth** is theoretically unlimited but in practice is bounded by the MTU; implementations typically support **up to 8–10 labels**.

## 2.4 Encapsulation over Different Link Types

MPLS Encapsulation must adapt to the underlying link technology. RFC 3032 specifies rules for each:

| Link Type | Encapsulation Behavior | Label Placement |
|---|---|---|
| **Ethernet** | Uses Ethertype **0x8847** (unicast) and **0x8848** (multicast) | Between Ethernet header and IP payload |
| **PPP** | Uses Protocol ID **0x0281** | Between PPP header and IP payload |
| **ATM** | Uses VPI/VCI as the label (cells carry no shim header) | VPI/VCI cell fields act as label |
| **Frame Relay** | Uses DLCI as the label | DLCI field acts as label |
| **Generic Routing Encapsulation (GRE)** | MPLS label inserted inside GRE payload | Used for MPLS over IP networks |

> [!NOTE]
> For ATM and Frame Relay, the link-layer fields (VPI/VCI, DLCI) **are** the labels — there is no separate shim header. This is a high-yield KTU fact.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Shim Header Length | $L_{\text{shim}} = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes}$ | Fixed |
| Label Range | $0 \le \text{Label} \le 2^{20}-1$ | Decimal |
| Reserved Labels | $0 \text{ to } 15$ | Special purpose |
| S-bit (Last Label) | $S = 1$ | Boolean flag |
| TTL Decrement | $\text{TTL}_{\text{out}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{in}} - 1$ | Per hop |
| Max Stack Depth | Implementation-dependent, typically $\le 8$ | Layers |
| Ethernet Ethertype | $\text{0x8847}$ (unicast), $\text{0x8848}$ (multicast) | Hexadecimal |
| PPP Protocol ID | $\text{0x0281}$ | Hexadecimal |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

MPLS Encapsulation is the foundational mechanism behind:
- **Service Provider Backbones** (AT&T, Verizon, Reliance Jio use MPLS for backbone forwarding)
- **MPLS L3VPNs** (RFC 4364) for enterprise connectivity
- **MPLS L2VPNs** (Pseudowires, RFC 4448) for legacy service emulation
- **Traffic Engineering** with RSVP-TE tunnels
- **Segment Routing (SR-MPLS)** — modern evolution using label stacks
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — End-to-End Encapsulation Walkthrough

Consider a packet flowing from Host A (10.1.1.1) to Host B (10.2.1.1) through an MPLS network with three LSRs (R1, R2, R3).

### Step 1: Ingress at R1 (LER — Label Edge Router)
- R1 performs **traditional IP lookup** on destination 10.2.1.1.
- R1 determines the packet belongs to FEC `F` and the egress is via R3.
- R1 **Pushes** label 25 onto the packet (this is its outgoing label for FEC F).

### Step 2: Transit at R2 (LSR — Label Switched Router)
- R2 receives packet with top label = 25.
- R2 performs **table lookup** on incoming label 25.
- R2 determines: outgoing label = 47, outgoing interface = port 2.
- R2 performs **Swap**: replaces 25 with 47, decrements TTL.

### Step 3: Penultimate Hop Popping at R3
- R3 (acting as penultimate LSR) is told (via LDP) to **PHP** — pop label before forwarding.
- R3 receives packet with top label = 47.
- R3 swaps 47 with special **Implicit Null label (Label 3)**, decrements TTL.
- When R3 forwards to the egress LER, it performs **Pop** and removes the label entirely, exposing the original IP packet.
- This optimizes the egress LER from having to do a label lookup.

### Step 4: Egress LER
- The egress LER sees a plain IP packet.
- Forwards via standard IP routing to Host B.

## 3.2 Formal Derivation — TTL Behavior in MPLS

MPLS TTL handling has two modes defined in **RFC 3443**:

### 3.2.1 Uniform Mode (Default, Pipe-like at ingress)

At the **Ingress LER**, the IP TTL is copied into the MPLS TTL:

$$\text{TTL}_{\text{MPLS,in}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{IP,in}}$$

At each **transit LSR**, the MPLS TTL is decremented:

$$\text{TTL}_{\text{MPLS,out}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{MPLS,in}} - 1$$

At the **Egress LER**, the final MPLS TTL is copied back into the IP TTL:

$$\text{TTL}_{\text{IP,out}} = \text{TTL}_{\text{MPLS,final}}$$

This provides **end-to-end TTL consistency** so that traceroute shows the entire LSP path.

### 3.2.2 Pipe Mode (Tunneling Mode)

At the **Ingress LER**, the MPLS TTL is set to a fixed value (typically **255**), **independent** of the IP TTL:

$$\text{TTL}_{\text{MPLS,in}} = 255$$

The IP TTL is **not modified**. This hides the MPLS core topology from traceroute output.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Shim Header Construction

Let us construct an MPLS shim header for label = 200, EXP = 5, S = 0, TTL = 64.

**Step 1:** Convert label 200 to 20-bit binary.

$$200_{10} = 0000\,0000\,1100\,1000_2$$

**Step 2:** Convert EXP = 5 to 3-bit binary.

$$5_{10} = 101_2$$

**Step 3:** S-bit = 0 (more labels below).

**Step 4:** Convert TTL = 64 to 8-bit binary.

$$64_{10} = 0100\,0000_2$$

**Step 5:** Concatenate all four fields in order:

$$\text{Shim} = \underbrace{0000000011001000}_{\text{Label (20b)}} \;\underbrace{101}_{\text{EXP (3b)}} \;\underbrace{0}_{\text{S (1b)}} \;\underbrace{01000000}_{\text{TTL (8b)}}$$

**Step 6:** Convert concatenated 32-bit value to hexadecimal for transmission:

$$\text{Binary} = 0000\,0000\,1100\,1000\,1010\,0100\,0000$$
$$\text{Hex} = \text{0x00C8A40}$$

This 32-bit value is the actual MPLS shim header that the router transmits on the wire.

## 3.4 Stack Depth Mathematical Constraint

The label stack adds to the packet overhead. The total MPLS overhead in bytes is:

$$\text{Overhead}_{\text{total}} = 4 \times N_{\text{labels}} \text{ bytes}$$

where $N_{\text{labels}}$ is the number of labels in the stack. For an Ethernet network with default MTU = 1500 bytes, the effective IP MTU becomes:

$$\text{IP MTU}_{\text{effective}} = 1500 - 4 \times N_{\text{labels}} \text{ bytes}$$

This is why **MPLS MTU** must be configured on interfaces to prevent fragmentation, especially for L3VPN (2 labels) or MPLS+TE (2 labels) scenarios.

## 3.5 Reserved Label Derivation — Why 0 to 15?

The 20-bit label space theoretically allows $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ unique labels. However, **IANA reserved labels 0 through 15** for protocol-specific use. The key reserved labels are:

| Label | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | IPv4 Explicit Null | Used for PHP signaling in IPv4 networks |
| **1** | Router Alert | Router must examine packet payload |
| **2** | IPv6 Explicit Null | Used for PHP signaling in IPv6 networks |
| **3** | Implicit Null | Signaled via LDP; never appears on wire |
| **4–6** | Unassigned | Reserved for future use |
| **7** | Entropy Label Indicator (ELI) | Used with ECMP load balancing |
| **8–15** | Reserved | Reserved for future use |

Therefore, the **usable label space** for ordinary LSPs is:

$$\text{Usable Labels} = 16 \text{ to } 2^{20}-1 = 1{,}048{,}560 \text{ labels}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MPLS Forwarding Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN["Incoming Packet with Top Label"] --> LFIB["LFIB Lookup by Incoming Label"]
    LFIB --> DEC["Decrement TTL"]
    DEC --> DECCHECK{"TTL equals 0?"}
    DECCHECK -->|Yes| DROP["Drop packet and send ICMP time exceeded"]
    DECCHECK -->|No| OPCheck{"Operation Type"}
    OPCheck -->|Push| PUSHOP["Push new label on top"]
    OPCheck -->|Swap| SWAPOP["Replace top label with new value"]
    OPCheck -->|Pop| POPOP["Remove top label"]
    OPCheck -->|Aggregate| AGGOP["Aggregate label operation"]
    PUSHOP --> FWD
    SWAPOP --> FWD
    POPOP --> FWD
    AGGOP --> FWD
    FWD["Forward via Outgoing Interface"] --> OUT["Outgoing Frame"]
    style IN fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style LFIB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style DEC fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style DROP fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style PUSHOP fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style SWAPOP fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style POPOP fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

    style FWD fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define MPLS Encapsulation. List the four fields of the MPLS shim header with their respective bit sizes.**

**Model Answer:**

MPLS Encapsulation is the process of inserting a 32-bit MPLS shim header between the Layer 2 and Layer 3 headers of a packet to enable label-based forwarding.

The four fields of the MPLS shim header are:
1. **Label** — **20 bits**, identifies the Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC).
2. **EXP / Traffic Class** — **3 bits**, used for QoS marking.
3. **Bottom of Stack (S) bit** — **1 bit**, indicates the last label in the stack.
4. **TTL** — **8 bits**, used for loop prevention.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the three label operations Push, Swap, and Pop with reference to MPLS Encapsulation.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Push:** Performed at the **Ingress LER**, a new label is added on top of the label stack (or above the IP header).
- **Swap:** Performed at **transit LSRs**, the incoming top label is replaced with a new outgoing label.
- **Pop:** Performed at the **Egress LER** or via **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)**, the top label is removed, exposing either another label or the IP header.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the MPLS Shim Header. Discuss how MPLS Encapsulation is performed over Ethernet and PPP link layers.**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: MPLS Shim Header Diagram and Explanation**

**Model Solution:**

The MPLS shim header is a **4-byte (32-bit)** structure inserted between the Layer 2 and Layer 3 headers:

| Field | Bits | Size | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Label | 0–19 | 20 bits | Identifies the FEC; range 0 to 2²⁰−1 |
| EXP / TC | 20–22 | 3 bits | QoS/CoS marking |
| S (Bottom of Stack) | 23 | 1 bit | S=1 indicates last label |
| TTL | 24–31 | 8 bits | Loop prevention |

**Key Properties:**
- Labels 0–15 are **reserved** (Label 3 = Implicit Null, Label 0 = IPv4 Explicit Null).
- The S bit enables **hierarchical label stacks** for VPNs and TE tunnels.
- TTL is initialized from the IP TTL on ingress and decremented at every hop.

**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Encapsulation over Ethernet and PPP**

**Model Solution:**

**Ethernet:**
- MPLS-labeled frames use dedicated **Ethertypes**:
  - **0x8847** — MPLS Unicast
  - **0x8848** — MPLS Multicast
- Frame layout: `[ Ethernet Header (14 B) | MPLS Shim (4 B) | IP Payload ]`
- The MPLS label is inserted **between the Ethernet header and the IP header**.

**PPP:**
- MPLS uses **PPP Protocol ID 0x0281** to indicate an MPLS payload.
- Frame layout: `[ PPP Header | Protocol 0x0281 | MPLS Shim (4 B) | IP Payload ]`
- LCP and NCP negotiations occur normally; MPLS rides as a PPP network-layer protocol.

For ATM and Frame Relay, **no shim header is used**; the link-layer fields (VPI/VCI, DLCI) themselves act as the label.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the concept of Label Stack in MPLS. With an example, describe how Push, Swap, and Pop operations are performed along an LSP. Also explain Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP).**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Label Stack Concept and Operations**

**Model Solution:**

A **label stack** is an ordered sequence of one or more MPLS labels. The **top label** is used for forwarding; the **Bottom of Stack (S) bit** marks the last label.

**Example LSP Walkthrough:**

Consider three routers — R1 (Ingress LER), R2 (Transit LSR), R3 (Penultimate LSR), R4 (Egress LER):

| Hop | Router | Operation | Incoming Label | Outgoing Label |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | R1 | **Push** | None (IP) | 25 |
| 2 | R2 | **Swap** | 25 | 47 |
| 3 | R3 | **Swap** | 47 | 3 (Implicit Null) |
| 4 | R4 | **Pop** | (already popped by R3) | IP exposed |

- At R1, label **25 is Pushed** on the IP packet.
- At R2, label **25 is Swapped** to **47**.
- At R3 (penultimate hop), label **47 is Swapped** to **Implicit Null (3)** — this signals PHP.
- R4 (egress LER) receives a pure IP packet with no label lookup required.

**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)**

**Model Solution:**

**Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)** is an MPLS optimization in which the **penultimate LSR (second-to-last router)** removes the top label before forwarding the packet to the egress LER.

**Mechanism:**
- The egress LER advertises a special label called the **Implicit Null (Label 3)** to its neighbors via **LDP (Label Distribution Protocol)**.
- When the penultimate LSR sees this label in its LFIB, it knows that the next hop is the egress LER and that **no label forwarding is required** at the egress.
- The penultimate LSR therefore **pops the label locally** and forwards the packet as a plain IP packet.

**Advantages of PHP:**
1. **Egress LER is relieved** of label lookup overhead — saves CPU cycles on the most critical edge device.
2. **Faster forwarding** at the egress boundary.
3. **Simplifies QoS handling** since the egress LER sees the original IP DSCP marking without MPLS EXP in the way.

**Disadvantage:**
- Traceroute from outside the LSP shows the egress LER as one hop away from the penultimate hop, hiding the true LSP length (mitigated by using **Explicit Null labels** if needed).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the "S bit" with the "EXP bit"** — many students swap their bit positions in the diagram and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Reserved label range (0–15)** is a favorite KTU question — memorize at least labels 0, 1, 2, and 3.
> 3. **PHP involves the penultimate (second-to-last) LSR popping the label**, NOT the egress LER. Conflating these loses 3 marks instantly.
> 4. **For ATM/Frame Relay**, the shim header is **NOT used** — VPI/VCI and DLCI fields themselves act as labels. Writing "MPLS shim is used over ATM" is incorrect.
> 5. **TTL behavior** (Uniform vs Pipe mode) is rarely asked but is a 14-mark question favorite — always mention both modes.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MPLS Encapsulation** = insertion of a **32-bit shim header** between Layer 2 and Layer 3.
- The shim header has **four fields**: Label (20), EXP (3), S (1), TTL (8) — total **4 bytes**.
- **Three core operations**: **Push** (Ingress), **Swap** (Transit), **Pop** (Egress / PHP).
- Labels are **link-local** and have meaning **only between adjacent LSRs**.
- Reserved label range is **0–15**; usable range is **16 to 2²⁰−1**.
- **S bit = 1** marks the bottom of the label stack (last label).
- **TTL** decrements per hop (Uniform mode) or is fixed at 255 (Pipe mode).
- **PHP** uses **Implicit Null (Label 3)** to optimize egress forwarding.
- **Ethernet** uses Ethertypes **0x8847** (unicast) and **0x8848** (multicast).
- **PPP** uses Protocol ID **0x0281** for MPLS.
- **ATM/Frame Relay** do NOT use a shim header — link-layer fields serve as labels.
- MPLS is often called **"Layer 2.5"** because it sits between Layer 2 and Layer 3.
- MPLS Encapsulation standards: **RFC 3032** (Shim Header), **RFC 3443** (TTL), **RFC 4364** (L3VPN).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Label Switching Router
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT522 - INTERNETWORKING USING TCP/IP > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Label Switching Router # 📘 MODULE 4 — LABEL SWITCHING ROUTER (LSR) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > A **Label Switching Router (LSR)** is a high-performance packet-forwarding device that operates at **Layer 2.5** of the OSI model. It makes forwarding decisions based on a short, fixed-length identifier called a **label** (or *shim header*), rather than performing the slow, variable-length IP header lookups performed by traditional Layer-3 routers. An LSR is the fundamental building block of an **MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching)** network. ### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Toll Booth" Model Imagine driving on a long highway that passes through multiple states. At every state border, a toll booth operator **does not** ask for your complete address, vehicle registration, license number, and identity proof. Instead, the operator simply scans a **sticker** (a *label*) on your windshield that says: *"Route 66 — Express Lane — Keep Moving."* - The **sticker** is the **MPLS label**. - Each **toll booth** is an **LSR**. - The operator's action of reading the sticker and directing you to the next booth without thinking is a **label swap** operation. - The moment you exit the highway, the sticker is **peeled off** — this is the **label pop (PHP — Penultimate Hop Popping)** at the egress LSR. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In traditional IP routing, every router along the path must perform a longest-prefix match in the routing table (slow). In MPLS, an LSR performs an **exact-match lookup** on a small label table (extremely fast — often done in hardware/ASICs in a single clock cycle). ### 🔑 Core Terminology You MUST Know | Term | Meaning | KTU Board Favourite? | |------|---------|---------------------| | **LSR** | Label Switching Router | ✅ Yes | | **LSP** | Label Switched Path | ✅ Yes | | **FEC** | Forwarding Equivalence Class | ✅ Yes | | **LDP** | Label Distribution Protocol | ✅ Yes | | **NHLFE** | Next Hop Label Forwarding Entry | ✅ Frequently asked | | **LIB** | Label Information Base | ✅ Yes | | **LER** | Label Edge Router (Ingress/Egress) | ✅ Yes | | **PHP** | Penultimate Hop Popping | ✅ Yes | | **Shim Header** | 32-bit MPLS label header inserted between L2 and L3 | ✅ Yes | > [!TIP] > The standard label size in MPLS is **20 bits**, allowing up to **2²⁰ = 1,048,576** unique labels per LSR — a massive scalability advantage over limited ATM VPI/VCI space. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of an LSR An LSR has **two logically separate planes**, and this separation is a frequent 7–10 mark question in KTU exams: ### A. Control Plane (Signalling & Intelligence) This is the *brain* of the LSR. It runs standard Layer-3 routing protocols to build the routing table. - **IGPs Supported:** OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP (for path computation) - **LDP / RSVP-TE:** Distribute labels to neighbouring LSRs - **Output:** A complete **Routing Information Base (RIB)** + **Label Information Base (LIB)** ### B. Data Plane (Forwarding Engine) This is the *muscle*. Once a label is bound to a destination (FEC), forwarding is done purely by label lookup. - **Input:** Incoming packet with an MPLS shim header - **Lookup Table:** NHLFE - **Action:** Swap / Push / Pop the label and forward ## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Label Operations | Operation | Symbol | When It Happens | What It Does | |-----------|--------|-----------------|--------------| | **PUSH** | `P` | Ingress LER (entering MPLS cloud) | Encapsulates the IP packet with a new MPLS shim header (label stack depth = 1) | | **SWAP** | `S` | Transit LSR (intermediate hop) | Replaces the incoming top label with an outgoing label — the bread-and-butter operation of every transit LSR | | **POP** | `Δ` | Egress LER (or Penultimate LSR for PHP) | Removes the top label from the stack. If label stack depth was 1, reveals the IP header again | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC):** A group of IP packets that are forwarded in the *same manner*, over the *same path*, with the *same forwarding treatment*. Example: All packets with destination prefix `192.168.10.0/24` belong to the same FEC and receive the same label. ## 2.3 Architecture of a Label Switched Path (LSP) An LSP is a **unidirectional** tunnel through an MPLS network, established from Ingress → Egress. ``` Ingress LER → Transit LSR-1 → Transit LSR-2 → Egress LER PUSH SWAP SWAP POP ``` ## 2.4 The NHLFE — The Heart of an LSR The **Next Hop Label Forwarding Entry (NHLFE)** is the data structure that maps **incoming label → outgoing action**. It contains: 1. **Outgoing label** (the new label to be swapped/pushed) 2. **Outgoing interface** (the physical/logical port) 3. **Next-hop IP address** (signalled by LDP) 4. **Operation code** (PUSH, SWAP, or POP) ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` (not `\|`) in tables to avoid breaking markdown syntax. All values below are board-exam verifiable. | # | Parameter / Equation | Value / Formula | Significance | |---|---------------------|-----------------|--------------| | 1 | MPLS Shim Header Size | **32 bits** | Always 4 bytes — placed between L2 and L3 | | 2 | Label Field Width | **20 bits** | The actual label value (0 to 2²⁰ − 1) | | 3 | EXP / TC Field Width | **3 bits** | QoS / Class of Service marking | | 4 | Bottom-of-Stack (S) Bit | **1 bit** | `S=1` means last label in stack | | 5 | TTL Field Width | **8 bits** | Same semantics as IP TTL (default **255**) | | 6 | Total Labels per Stack | **$\vert$Stack$\vert$** | Multiple labels for VPN/tunnel hierarchies | | 7 | Max Unique Labels per LSR | $2^{20} = \mathbf{1{,}048{,}576}$ | Scalability upper bound | | 8 | Label Processing Time | $T_{LSR} = T_{lookup} + T_{swap} + T_{queue}$ | Where $T_{lookup} \approx O(1)$ exact match | | 9 | Total Path MTU Constraint | $MTU_{path} \geq MTU_{L2} + 4 \times \vert Stack \vert$ | Each shim header adds 4 bytes | | 10 | Effective Throughput Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + 4}$ | Overhead per shim header | ## 2.6 Engineering Utility of LSRs - **Traffic Engineering (MPLS-TE):** LSRs enable explicit, constraint-based routing that classical IP cannot do. - **Layer-2/3 VPNs:** MPLS L3VPN (BGP/MPLS IP-VPN) and L2VPN (PWE3) rely entirely on the LSR architecture. - **QoS:** The 3-bit EXP field lets LSRs implement DiffServ-aware PHBs. - **Fast Reroute (FRR):** Backup LSPs can be pre-signalled for sub-50ms failover (used in 4G/5G backhaul). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — The MPLS Shim Header Format The MPLS shim header sits **between the Layer-2 header and the Layer-3 (IP) header**. We derive the bit allocation: $$ \text{Total Shim Header} = \text{Label} + \text{EXP} + \text{S} + \text{TTL} $$ $$ \Rightarrow 32 \text{ bits} = L_{bits} + E_{bits} + S_{bits} + T_{bits} $$ **Step 1 — Choose label field width.** The label must support enough unique values for full-mesh LSPs in large ISPs. $$ L_{bits} = 20 \;\Rightarrow\; 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ labels} \;\;\text{(more than sufficient)} $$ **Step 2 — Reserve bits for QoS.** 3 bits give $2^3 = 8$ distinct service classes — matches the 8 PHBs of DiffServ. $$ E_{bits} = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; 2^3 = 8 \text{ QoS classes} $$ **Step 3 — Bottom-of-Stack indicator.** A single bit is enough to flag whether this is the *last* label in the stack. $$ S_{bits} = 1 $$ **Step 4 — TTL field for loop prevention.** Match the IP TTL width to keep the existing TTL semantics and traceroute behaviour. $$ T_{bits} = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; 64 \text{ hops default} \;\; (\text{range } 0 \text{ to } 255) $$ **Step 5 — Verify total.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total} &= L_{bits} + E_{bits} + S_{bits} + T_{bits} \\ &= 20 + 3 + 1 + 8 \\ &= 32 \text{ bits} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation is a **direct hit for 7-mark theory questions** in KTU. Memorize the bit allocation as 20-3-1-8. ## 3.2 Derivation — Path MTU Overhead per MPLS Header Each LSR PUSH/SWAP operation increases the packet size by 4 bytes (one shim header). For a stack depth of $n$: $$ L_{encapsulated} = L_{IP} + 4 \times \vert Stack \vert $$ **Step 1 — Compute encapsulated length with $n=3$ (typical for L3VPN):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{encapsulated} &= L_{IP} + 4 \times 3 \\ &= L_{IP} + 12 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply MTU constraint.** Path MTU must be at least: $$ MTU_{path} \geq L_{L2} + 4 \times \vert Stack \vert + L_{IP} $$ **Step 3 — Efficiency penalty.** $$ \eta = \dfrac{L_{IP}}{L_{IP} + 4 \times \vert Stack \vert} $$ For $L_{IP} = 1480$ and $\vert Stack \vert = 2$: $$ \eta = \dfrac{1480}{1480 + 8} = \dfrac{1480}{1488} \approx 99.46\% $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — LSR Behaviour per Packet Type | Packet Type | Ingress LSR | Transit LSR | Egress LSR | Special Note | |-------------|-------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | Plain IPv4 unicast | PUSH label | SWAP label | POP label | Most common case | | IPv4 with PHP enabled | PUSH label | SWAP label | (None — already popped) | Penultimate LSR pops | | MPLS-encapsulated already | (Re-classify) | SWAP outer label | POP based on `S` bit | Used in L3VPN | | Label stack depth = 1 entering LSR | Inspect `S=1` | SWAP | POP to expose IP | Normal case | | TTL expired (=0) | Generate ICMP | Generate ICMP | Generate ICMP | MPLS-aware ICMP | ## 3.4 Worked Example — Label Swap Table A transit LSR receives packets with incoming label `45`. Its NHLFE table looks like: | Incoming Label | Operation | Outgoing Label | Outgoing Interface | Next Hop | |:--------------:|:---------:|:--------------:|:------------------:|:--------:| | 20 | SWAP | 45 | GE0/0/1 | 10.1.2.2 | | 45 | SWAP | 77 | GE0/0/2 | 10.2.3.3 | | 77 | POP | — | GE0/0/3 | 10.3.4.4 | **Trace of a packet:** `Ingress pushes 20 → LSR-1 swaps to 45 → LSR-2 swaps to 77 → Egress pops to reveal IP`. This is a classic **7-mark problem** in KTU boards. Always draw the LSP diagram and mark label values on the arrows. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End LSP Architecture with Label Operations The following Mermaid block diagram depicts a complete **MPLS network** with an Ingress LER, two Transit LSRs, an Egress LER, and the label operations performed at each hop. ```mermaid graph LR CE_A[Customer Edge Router A]:::ce --> IngressLER((Ingress LER
PUSH LABEL 20)):::ler IngressLER -- Label 20 --> Transit1[Transit LSR 1
SWAP 20 to 45]:::lsr Transit1 -- Label 45 --> Transit2[Transit LSR 2
SWAP 45 to 77]:::lsr Transit2 -- Label 77 --> Penultimate[Penultimate LSR
SWAP 77 to POP]:::lsr Penultimate -- Label 77 popped --> EgressLER((Egress LER
IP Forward)):::ler EgressLER --> CE_B[Customer Edge Router B]:::ce classDef ce fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef lsr fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ler fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Internal LSR Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph INGRESS_PLANE["INGRESS FUNCTIONAL BLOCKS"] IPin[Incoming IPv4 Packet]:::in FECmap[FEC Classification Engine]:::module LBind[Label Binding Table LIB]:::module LEnc[MPLS Shim Header Encapsulator]:::module end subgraph TRANSIT_PLANE["TRANSIT FUNCTIONAL BLOCKS"] LDec[MPLS Shim Header Decapsulator]:::module NHLFE[NHLFE Lookup Table]:::module LSwap[Label Swap Engine]:::module LRecap[Re Encapsulator]:::module end subgraph EGRESS_PLANE["EGRESS FUNCTIONAL BLOCKS"] LPop[Label Pop Engine]:::module IPout[Outgoing IPv4 Packet]:::out end IPin --> FECmap FECmap --> LBind LBind --> LEnc LEnc --> LDec LDec --> NHLFE NHLFE --> LSwap LSwap --> LRecap LRecap --> LPop LPop --> IPout classDef in fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef out fill:#caffbf,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef module fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 The MPLS Shim Header Bit Layout ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SHIM["MPLS SHIM HEADER 32 BITS"] direction LR L0["Label 20 bits value 0 to 1048575"]:::lbl E0["EXP 3 bits QoS class"]:::exp S0["S Bit 1 bit Bottom of Stack"]:::s T0["TTL 8 bits 0 to 255"]:::ttl end L0 --- E0 --- S0 --- T0 classDef lbl fill:#ffadad,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef exp fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef s fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef ttl fill:#caffbf,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Label Distribution (LDP) — Signalling Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant I as Ingress LER participant T1 as Transit LSR 1 participant T2 as Transit LSR 2 participant E as Egress LER I->>T1: LDP Label Mapping FEC 10.0.0.0/8 Label 20 T1->>T2: LDP Label Mapping FEC 10.0.0.0/8 Label 45 T2->>E: LDP Label Mapping FEC 10.0.0.0/8 Label 77 E-->>T2: LDP Label Mapping FEC 10.0.0.0/8 Label IMPLICIT NULL T2-->>T1: LDP Label Mapping Label IMPLICIT NULL T1-->>I: LDP Label Mapping Label IMPLICIT NULL Note over I,E: LSP IS NOW ESTABLISHED AND READY FOR DATA ``` > [!TIP] > **Implicit NULL label** (value `3`) is a special label used in **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP)**. It tells the upstream LSR to POP its own label and forward the packet as a plain IP packet to the egress — saving one label lookup at the egress. ## 4.5 Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP) — Visual Walkthrough ```mermaid graph LR P1[Penultimate LSR] -- Label 77 with S equals 1 --> P2[Egress LER] P1 -- POP the label here --> P3[Reveals Original IP Header] P3 --> P2 style P1 fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P2 fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#000,color:#000 style P3 fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a Label Switching Router (LSR). What are the three fundamental label operations it performs? **Model Answer:** A **Label Switching Router (LSR)** is a router operating at **Layer 2.5** of the OSI model that forwards packets based on short, fixed-length labels rather than IP header analysis. It is the fundamental building block of an MPLS network. The three label operations are: 1. **PUSH (Insert):** Performed at the **Ingress LER**. Adds a new MPLS shim header (32 bits) between the Layer-2 and Layer-3 headers. 2. **SWAP (Replace):** Performed at **Transit LSRs**. Replaces the incoming top label with a new outgoing label based on the NHLFE lookup. 3. **POP (Remove):** Performed at the **Egress LER** (or **Penultimate LSR** in PHP mode). Removes the top label, exposing the underlying IP header for conventional routing. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC)? How is it related to the Label Information Base (LIB)? **Model Answer:** A **Forwarding Equivalence Class (FEC)** is a grouping of IP packets that are forwarded in the **same manner**, along the **same path**, and with the **same forwarding treatment**. For example, all packets destined to the prefix `192.168.10.0/24` and requiring the same QoS may belong to a single FEC. **Relationship to LIB:** - The **Label Information Base (LIB)** is the table maintained by every LSR that maps **FECs ↔ labels ↔ outgoing interfaces**. - When a new FEC is learned (via routing protocols) and a label is bound to it (via LDP), an entry is created in the LIB. - During forwarding, the LSR consults the LIB to determine the outgoing label for the corresponding FEC. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake in Part A:** > Students often confuse the **LIB** (which holds FEC-to-label mappings for *all destinations the LSR has learned*) with the **NHLFE** (which holds the *incoming label → outgoing action* mapping for *received* labelled packets). The LIB is used to **build** the NHLFE — they are not the same table. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme provides an **internal choice** in Part B between two questions from the same module. Both options are provided below. --- ### ✅ CHOICE A — 14 Marks #### **Question A:** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and working of a Label Switching Router (LSR). Differentiate clearly between the control plane and the data plane. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An MPLS shim header is 32 bits long. Derive the bit-level layout of the shim header and justify why each field is allocated that many bits. Also, compute the path MTU overhead when the label stack depth is 3. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture and Working of an LSR:** The LSR has a **two-plane architecture** — *control plane* and *data plane* — that are logically separate for scalability. **Control Plane:** - Runs **OSPF / IS-IS** to build the IP routing table (RIB). - Runs **LDP (Label Distribution Protocol)** with neighbouring LSRs to exchange label bindings. - Output: A populated **Label Information Base (LIB)**. - Decision-making is **software-based** and can be slow — but it runs only when topology changes. **Data Plane (Forwarding Plane):** - Performs **exact-match label lookup** using an **NHLFE** table. - Forwards packets in **hardware (ASIC/TCAM)** for wire-speed performance. - Operations: **PUSH, SWAP, POP** as defined in Section 2.2. **Working — Step by Step:** 1. Ingress LER receives an IP packet, classifies it into an FEC. 2. It consults the LIB, obtains the label for that FEC, and **PUSHes** a shim header. 3. Each Transit LSR performs an **O(1) lookup** of the incoming label in the NHLFE. 4. It **SWAPs** the label, decrements TTL by 1, and forwards to the next hop. 5. The Egress LER (or penultimate LSR for PHP) **POPs** the label and forwards the IP packet conventionally. *(See Diagram 4.1 in Section 4 for visual support.)* **Differences between Control Plane and Data Plane:** | Aspect | Control Plane | Data Plane | |--------|---------------|------------| | Function | Builds routing and label tables | Forwards packets based on labels | | Implementation | Software (CPU) | Hardware (ASIC / TCAM) | | Speed | Slower, runs periodically | Wire-speed, per-packet | | Protocols | OSPF, IS-IS, LDP, RSVP-TE | None (uses tables) | | Output | RIB + LIB | NHLFE | | Triggered By | Topology changes | Every incoming packet | --- **(b) Derivation of the 32-bit Shim Header Layout:** | Field | Width | Justification | |-------|-------|---------------| | Label | **20 bits** | $2^{20}$ = 1,048,576 unique labels — sufficient for large ISPs | | EXP (Experimental) | **3 bits** | $2^3$ = 8 QoS classes — matches DiffServ PHBs | | S (Bottom of Stack) | **1 bit** | Single bit sufficient to flag last label | | TTL | **8 bits** | Matches IP TTL — supports traceroute semantics | $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total} &= 20 + 3 + 1 + 8 \\ &= 32 \text{ bits} \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Path MTU Overhead for Stack Depth = 3:** Each shim header adds **4 bytes**. With $\vert Stack \vert = 3$: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{overhead} &= 4 \times \vert Stack \vert \\ &= 4 \times 3 \\ &= 12 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore the encapsulated packet size becomes: $$ L_{encap} = L_{IP} + 12 \text{ bytes} $$ If the original IP packet is 1500 bytes (standard Ethernet MTU), the new wire size is: $$ L_{encap} = 1500 + 12 = 1512 \text{ bytes} $$ This **exceeds** the standard 1500-byte MTU, so the path must be configured for **jumbo frames** (MTU ≥ 1512) or the IP packet must be **fragmented** accordingly. --- ### ✅ CHOICE B — 14 Marks #### **Question B:** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the three types of LSRs in an MPLS network — Ingress LER, Transit LSR, and Egress LER. What is Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP) and why is it used? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With reference to the LDP signalling protocol, describe how labels are distributed between LSRs. Construct a 4-node LSP scenario and show the label mapping table (LIB) at each node for the FEC `10.0.0.0/8`. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Types of LSRs:** 1. **Ingress LER (Label Edge Router):** - The entry point into the MPLS cloud. - Performs FEC classification on incoming IP packets. - **PUSHes** the first MPLS shim header. - Runs both IP routing and label-binding protocols. 2. **Transit LSR:** - The core forwarding engine in the MPLS network. - Performs **SWAP** of labels based on NHLFE. - Pure MPLS — never inspects the IP header. - Does **not** perform FEC classification (already done at ingress). 3. **Egress LER:** - The exit point from the MPLS cloud. - Performs **POP** of the final label. - Forwards the original IP packet using standard Layer-3 routing. **Penultimate Hop Popping (PHP):** PHP is an **optimization** where the **second-to-last LSR** (the penultimate hop) performs the POP operation instead of the Egress LER. This is achieved by the egress LER advertising an **Implicit NULL label (value 3)** for the FEC. **Why PHP?** - **Saves one label lookup** at the egress LER — improves forwarding speed. - Reduces load on the egress router, which often needs to do additional processing (e.g., NAT, firewall, deep packet inspection). - The egress receives a *plain IP packet* and can process it immediately. --- **(b) LDP Signalling & LIB Construction:** **LDP Process:** - LDP is a TCP-based protocol (port **646**) that runs between **directly connected LDP peers** (typically OSPF/IS-IS neighbours). - After routing convergence, each LSR independently assigns a label to each FEC in its RIB and advertises the binding to its LDP peers. - LDP uses four message types: **Discovery, Session, Advertisement, Notification**. **4-Node LSP Scenario:** ``` CE-A --- [Ingress: R1] --- [Transit: R2] --- [Transit: R3] --- [Egress: R4] --- CE-B PUSH 20 SWAP SWAP POP 20→45 45→77 ``` **Label Information Base (LIB) at each node for FEC `10.0.0.0/8`:** | Node (LSR) | Role | Local Label | Action | Outgoing Label | Outgoing Interface | |:----------:|:----:|:-----------:|:------:|:--------------:|:------------------:| | **R1** | Ingress LER | 20 | PUSH | 20 | To R2 | | **R2** | Transit LSR | 45 | SWAP | 45 | To R3 | | **R3** | Transit LSR | 77 | SWAP | 77 | To R4 | | **R4** | Egress LER | (Implicit NULL = 3) | POP | — | To CE-B | **Trace of a packet from CE-A to CE-B:** | Hop | Packet State | |-----|--------------| | CE-A → R1 | Plain IP packet (dest = 10.0.0.5) | | R1 → R2 | MPLS shim (Label = 20, S = 1) + IP | | R2 → R3 | MPLS shim (Label = 45, S = 1) + IP | | R3 → R4 | MPLS shim (Label = 77, S = 1) + IP | | R4 → CE-B | Plain IP packet (after POP) | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing LER and LSR:** A *Label Edge Router* is a special type of LSR located at the network boundary. The term *LSR* generically includes LERs. > 2. **Forgetting the S bit in the shim header diagram:** The S bit is *separate* from the label value and must be drawn explicitly. > 3. **Mixing LIB and NHLFE:** LIB is a *control-plane* table (FEC → label), NHLFE is a *data-plane* table (incoming label → outgoing action). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion. > 4. **Skipping the TTL field in diagrams:** TTL must be decremented at every LSR hop, just like IP. Traceroute works across MPLS networks because of this. > 5. **Writing "LDP uses UDP":** LDP uses **TCP port 646** for reliability — a common factual error. --- ## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this as your **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU exam. - ✅ An **LSR** is a Layer-2.5 device that forwards packets by **label** (not IP header). - ✅ The MPLS **shim header** is **32 bits = 20 (Label) + 3 (EXP) + 1 (S) + 8 (TTL)**. - ✅ **FEC** = group of packets treated identically. - ✅ Three label operations: **PUSH, SWAP, POP**. - ✅ **NHLFE** = data-plane table mapping incoming label → outgoing action. - ✅ **LIB** = control-plane table mapping FEC → locally assigned label. - ✅ **LDP** = TCP port **646**, distributes labels between LDP peers. - ✅ **LSP** = unidirectional label-switched tunnel from Ingress to Egress. - ✅ **PHP** = penultimate LSR pops the label using *Implicit NULL* (value 3). - ✅ **Control plane** = software (OSPF, LDP); **Data plane** = hardware (ASIC/TCAM). - ✅ Maximum labels per LSR = **2²⁰ = 1,048,576**. - ✅ EXP field provides **3 bits = 8 QoS classes**, matching DiffServ. - ✅ Path MTU overhead per shim header = **4 bytes**; for stack depth $n$ → $4n$ bytes overhead. - ✅ **LER** = edge LSR (Ingress or Egress); **LSR** generically includes LERs. - ✅ MPLS TTL is decremented at every LSR hop — enables **traceroute over MPLS**. - ✅ **Implicit NULL label** = `3`; **Explicit NULL label** = `0` (preserves QoS info to egress).
